first_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/day PO (max 1g) for 5-7 days; preferred for uncomplicated
75-80 mg/kg/day IV (max 2.5g) for 10-14 days; severe or inpatient

second_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg PO bid (max 500 mg/dose) for 7-10 days; if susceptible, age >=1yr
60 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 3g/day); XDR typhoid

counselling: Complete the full antibiotic course — stopping early leads to relapse and drug resistance
Give plenty of fluids and a soft, easily digestible diet during fever
Wash hands thoroughly and ensure safe drinking water to prevent spread to family
The child can pass typhoid to others through stool — maintain strict hand hygiene after toilet
Typhoid vaccination (typhoid conjugate vaccine) is recommended for prevention
referral_criteria: Refer if suspected intestinal perforation, GI hemorrhage, encephalopathy, XDR typhoid, or no defervescence after 5 days of appropriate antibiotics
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for persistent high fever not responding to oral antibiotics, suspected complications (perforation, hemorrhage, encephalopathy), XDR typhoid requiring IV therapy
notes: Azithromycin is preferred empirically due to rising multidrug resistance. For travelers from Pakistan/Iraq, suspect XDR and use azithromycin or carbapenem.
guideline_changes: XDR typhoid (Pakistan, Iraq) requires azithromycin or carbapenems empirically

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-75 mg/kg/day IV OD or BD, dilute in 100 ml NS over 30-60 min, x 7-10 days
10-15 mg/kg/dose IV 6 hourly for fever control
0.1-0.15 mg/kg/dose IV 8 hourly for vomiting
Maintenance rate per Holliday-Segar formula (~60-80 ml/hr adjust clinically), maintain hydration and correct electrolytes

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg/day IV over 1-2 hours x 5-7 days, add-on for moderate-severe/resistant cases
8 mg/kg/day div BD PO, step-down oral antibiotic after afebrile 48-72 hours, complete 10-14 days total

investigations.name: Blood culture
investigations.indication: Gold standard for diagnosis, before starting antibiotics

counselling: Typhoid requires a full course of antibiotics - do not stop early even if fever resolves
Expected hospital stay is 3-5 days until fever-free
Give soft easily digestible diet: khichdi, curd, buttermilk, banana, coconut water
Avoid spicy and oily food during recovery
Complete the full course of oral antibiotics after discharge
Follow-up blood tests may be needed to confirm cure
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Abdominal distension or severe abdominal pain
Signs of GI bleeding such as blood in stool or black stools
Persistent high fever despite treatment for more than 5 days
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric infectious disease specialist if no defervescence after 5 days of appropriate antibiotics, suspected MDR typhoid, or complications (perforation, encephalopathy, myocarditis)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all moderate-severe enteric fever. Also admit if: persistent high fever, toxic appearance, vomiting with inability to take oral antibiotics, suspected complications, age <2 years, or immunocompromised child
notes: Indoor protocol. Ceftriaxone is first-line IV drug. IV fluids (DNS/NS) as per Holliday-Segar maintenance. Monitor temperature 4-hourly, pulse/BP 4-hourly, strict I/O charting, daily sensorium and abdominal exam. Step-down to oral antibiotics when afebrile 48-72 hrs. Complete 10-14 days total therapy. Watch for intestinal perforation, GI bleeding, encephalopathy, shock.
source: IAP 2024, WHO Typhoid Guidelines, AIIMS Protocol
guideline_changes: WHO/CDC 2024: azithromycin elevated as co-first-line with ceftriaxone due to rising XDR S. Typhi (especially South Asia); meropenem recommended for XDR strains; blood culture before antibiotics strongly emphasized; TCV single-dose conjugate vaccine now in routine immunisation in endemic countries.

first_line_drugs.notes: 12 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg), then 6 mg/kg/day PO days 2-5 (max 250 mg)
50 mg/kg/day IV/IM q24h (max 2 g) x5 days for resistant or severe cases

second_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg PO BID (max 500 mg/dose) x3 days if susceptible
8 mg/kg/day TMP PO divided BID x5 days if susceptible
20 mg/kg PO QID x5 days (max 400 mg/dose); alternative per WHO

investigations.name: Stool culture and sensitivity
investigations.indication: Confirm Shigella and guide antibiotic therapy — resistance is common

counselling: Your child has Shigella infection — this is a serious bacterial diarrhea that needs antibiotics
Give the full course of antibiotics even if the child improves before finishing
Maintain hydration with ORS — the child may have bloody stools which is common in this infection
Strict hand hygiene is essential — Shigella spreads very easily from person to person
Come back if: blood in stools increases, child develops high fever, or stops urinating
referral_criteria: Infectious disease for XDR Shigella or treatment failure. Nephrology if HUS develops. GI if chronic carrier state. ICU if septic shock or toxic megacolon
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe dehydration. Toxic appearance (high fever, altered mental status, lethargy). Bloody diarrhea with hemodynamic instability. Age <6 months. Suspected HUS or toxic megacolon. Immunocompromised child
notes: Always obtain stool culture and susceptibility before or concurrent with empiric therapy. XDR strains increasing; ceftriaxone preferred if resistance suspected.
guideline_changes: CDC 2023 alert on increasing XDR Shigella strains; azithromycin resistance rising

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 ml/kg after each loose stool, frequent small sips, life-saving
8-10 mg/kg/day div BD PO for 5-7 days
<6 months: 10 mg/day; >=6 months: 20 mg/day PO for 14 days, reduces severity and recurrence
10 mg/kg/dose TDS PO for 5 days, for suspected amoebic or mixed infection
Once or twice daily PO for 5 days, probiotic to restore gut flora and reduce diarrhea duration
Age/weight appropriate dose TDS PO, short-term, for abdominal pain/cramps

second_line_drugs.notes: Consider for persistent diarrhea or resistant infection, not routine first-line

investigations.name: Stool culture and sensitivity
investigations.indication: Severe or non-responding cases to identify organism

counselling: ORS is the most important treatment - give after every loose stool
Continue normal feeding, do not stop food - give curd, khichdi, rice, banana
Give zinc syrup daily for 14 days even after diarrhea stops
Complete full course of antibiotic as prescribed
Wash hands before feeding and after toilet to prevent spread
Come immediately if blood in stool increases, persistent vomiting, no urine, child becomes lethargic or severely dehydrated
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Persistent vomiting or unable to drink
No urine output for more than 6 hours
Signs of severe dehydration such as sunken eyes or dry mouth
referral_criteria: Refer if no improvement in 48 hours on antibiotics, suspected hemolytic uremic syndrome, recurrent dysentery, or suspected resistant organism
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe dehydration, unable to drink or retain ORS, bloody stool with hemodynamic instability, persistent vomiting, lethargy/altered sensorium, or age <2 months
notes: ORS + Zinc = cornerstone of therapy. Continue normal feeding. Antibiotic indicated for bloody dysentery. Preferred diet: curd, buttermilk, khichdi, rice, banana, coconut water. Avoid junk food and sugary drinks.
source: IAP Diarrhea Guidelines, WHO Diarrheal Disease Management, CDC Shigella Treatment Guidelines 2024, IDSA Infectious Diarrhea Guidelines
guideline_changes: WHO 2024: Rising antimicrobial resistance in Shigella globally - azithromycin resistance increased significantly; CDC 2023: Alert on extensively drug-resistant (XDR) Shigella strains; ceftriaxone or azithromycin recommended where ciprofloxacin resistance is high; stool culture and sensitivity recommended for non-responding cases; IAP 2024: ORS + Zinc remains cornerstone; cefixime preferred empiric oral antibiotic for pediatric bloody dysentery in India

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg/day PO daily x5 days (max 500 mg/day) for Campylobacter/Shigella
50-75 mg/kg/day IV/IM q24h (max 2 g) for invasive Salmonella or severe cases
Rehydration first-line; antibiotics only for invasive/toxic presentations

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg PO BID (max 500 mg/dose) x5-7 days if susceptible
8 mg/kg/day TMP PO divided BID x5 days if susceptible
30 mg/kg/day PO divided TID x10 days for C. difficile

investigations.name: Renal function and CBC
investigations.indication: Screen for HUS if STEC suspected (Hb, platelets, creatinine)

counselling: Your child has a bacterial gut infection — antibiotics are given only if the infection is serious or specific bacteria are found
Give ORS after every loose stool to prevent dehydration
Continue normal feeding — stopping food does not help and slows recovery
Practice strict hand hygiene — wash hands with soap before meals and after toilet
Ensure safe drinking water — boil or filter water before giving to the child
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI if diarrhea persists >14 days. Infectious disease for resistant organisms or immunocompromised patients. Surgery if toxic megacolon or perforation suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe dehydration. Systemic toxicity (high fever, altered mental status). Bloody diarrhea with hemodynamic instability. Immunocompromised child with invasive disease. Suspected HUS (after STEC infection)
notes: Most bacterial gastroenteritis is self-limited; antibiotics reserved for invasive disease, immunocompromised, or specific pathogens. Always check local resistance patterns.
guideline_changes: 2024 ESPGHAN/NASPGHAN H. pylori update emphasizes susceptibility-guided therapy

first_line_drugs.notes: 35-50 mg/kg/day PO divided tid (max 2.25g/day) for 10 days; invasive disease
25-35 mg/kg/day PO divided tid for 7 days; follow metronidazole to clear cysts

second_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg/day PO once daily (max 2g) for 3-5 days; age >=3yr
30-40 mg/kg/day PO divided tid (max 1.95g/day) for 20 days; luminal agent

investigations.name: Stool antigen test for E. histolytica

counselling: Complete the full course of metronidazole AND the follow-up luminal agent (paromomycin)
The child may feel nauseous with metronidazole — give with food
Ensure safe drinking water — boil or filter water at home
Wash hands thoroughly before eating and after using the toilet
Follow up with stool test after completing treatment to confirm parasite clearance
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric gastroenterologist or surgeon if liver abscess detected, or if fulminant colitis suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for amoebic liver abscess, toxic megacolon, intestinal perforation, severe dehydration, or dysentery with systemic toxicity
notes: Always follow tissue-active agent (metronidazole/tinidazole) with a luminal agent to eradicate intraluminal cysts. Tinidazole has better tolerability and shorter course.

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg/day PO divided tid (max 750 mg/day) for 5-7 days; giardiasis
50 mg/kg PO single dose (max 2g); giardiasis, age >=3yr

second_line_drugs.notes: Age 1-3yr: 100 mg PO bid; 4-11yr: 200 mg PO bid; for 3 days
25-35 mg/kg/day PO divided tid for 7 days; pregnancy-safe option

counselling: Give metronidazole with food to reduce stomach upset
The medicine may cause a metallic taste — this is normal and temporary
Ensure the child drinks only boiled or filtered water
Wash hands thoroughly before eating and after toilet use
Follow up with stool test after treatment to confirm the parasite is cleared
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric GI if recurrent giardiasis despite treatment (consider IgA deficiency), or if chronic diarrhea with malabsorption
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe dehydration from diarrhea, significant malnutrition, or Cryptosporidium in immunocompromised child
notes: Tinidazole single-dose has 80-100% cure rate for giardiasis with better tolerability than metronidazole. Nitazoxanide also covers Cryptosporidium.

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mL/kg PO over 3-4h for mild-mod dehydration
10 mg/day PO (<6mo) or 20 mg/day PO (>6mo) x10-14 days
0.15 mg/kg PO/IV single dose (max 4 mg) for persistent vomiting

second_line_drugs.notes: 1.5 mg/kg PO TID x5-7 days; antisecretory adjunct
250-750 mg/day PO x5-7 days; adjunctive probiotic

investigations.name: Stool rotavirus/norovirus rapid test
investigations.indication: Identify specific virus — helps epidemiological tracking

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes
investigations.indication: If moderate-severe dehydration — guide fluid therapy

investigations.name: Blood glucose
investigations.indication: Hypoglycemia risk in young children with poor intake

counselling: Your child has a viral stomach infection — it usually gets better on its own in 3-5 days
No antibiotics are needed — viruses do not respond to antibiotics
Give ORS sip by sip and continue feeding — this is the most important treatment
Wash hands thoroughly and keep the child's utensils separate to prevent spread to other family members
Rotavirus vaccine can prevent the most severe forms of viral diarrhea — ask about it at next visit
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI if diarrhea persists >14 days (consider post-infectious lactose intolerance). Immunology if recurrent or severe viral GI infections suggesting immunodeficiency
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe dehydration requiring IV fluids. Failed oral rehydration despite ondansetron. Age <3 months with moderate-severe dehydration. Seizures from electrolyte imbalance
notes: No antiviral therapy available; treatment is entirely supportive. Rotavirus vaccination remains the most effective prevention strategy.

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mL/kg PO over 3-4h for mild-mod dehydration
10 mg/day PO (<6mo) or 20 mg/day PO (>6mo) for 10-14 days
0.15 mg/kg PO/IV single dose (max 4 mg) for persistent vomiting

second_line_drugs.notes: 1.5 mg/kg PO TID for up to 7 days; antisecretory
250-750 mg/day PO for 5-7 days; adjunctive probiotic
10 billion CFU/day PO for 5-7 days; adjunctive probiotic

investigations.name: Stool routine and microscopy
investigations.indication: WBCs, RBCs, parasites — differentiate inflammatory vs non-inflammatory

investigations.name: Stool culture
investigations.indication: If bloody diarrhea, high fever, or antibiotic use planned

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: If systemic infection or dehydration suspected

counselling: Your child has a stomach infection causing loose stools — ORS and zinc are the main treatment
Give ORS after every loose stool: under 2 years = 50-100mL, over 2 years = 100-200mL
Continue normal feeding — do not starve the child. Breast milk is the best food during illness
Give zinc syrup for 10-14 days as prescribed — it reduces the duration and severity of diarrhea
Wash hands with soap before feeding and after toilet — this prevents spread of infection
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI if bloody diarrhea persisting >5 days, or diarrhea >14 days (persistent diarrhea). Infectious disease if unusual pathogen or immunocompromised child. Surgical consultation if toxic megacolon suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe dehydration requiring IV fluids. Failed oral rehydration. Bloody diarrhea with systemic toxicity (high fever, altered mental status). Age <3 months with moderate-severe dehydration. Suspected surgical abdomen
notes: Antibiotics not routinely indicated; reserve for specific pathogens or toxic/invasive presentations. Early refeeding with regular diet improves outcomes.
guideline_changes: 2023 WHO update reinforces zinc supplementation and reduced-osmolarity ORS

diagnosis_name: Respiratory tuberculosis, bacteriologically and histologically confirmed

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 300 mg); give with pyridoxine 1-2 mg/kg/day
10-20 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 600 mg); take on empty stomach
30-40 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 2 g); used in intensive phase (first 2 months)
15-25 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 2.5 g); used in intensive phase; monitor visual acuity

second_line_drugs.notes: Isoniazid + rifapentine + pyrazinamide + moxifloxacin daily x4 months; for adolescents >=12 yr
15-20 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 750 mg); for drug-resistant TB or ethambutol intolerance
15-30 mg/kg/day IV/IM daily (max 1 g); for MDR-TB regimens; monitor audiology

investigations.name: Sputum/gastric aspirate for AFB and GeneXpert
investigations.indication: Confirm TB and detect rifampicin resistance

investigations.name: LFT baseline
investigations.indication: Before starting hepatotoxic drugs (INH, RIF, PZA)

investigations.name: HIV test
investigations.indication: TB-HIV coinfection screening per NACO guidelines

counselling: Tuberculosis is curable but treatment takes 4-6 months - completing all medicines is critical
All medicines must be taken daily without missing any dose; directly observed therapy is recommended
Common side effects include orange urine (rifampicin) - this is normal and harmless
Contact screening: all family members especially children should be tested for TB
Your child can return to school after 2 weeks of treatment if feeling well and cough improving
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric TB specialist if drug-resistant TB, extrapulmonary TB, HIV co-infection, or treatment failure
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe or miliary TB, TB meningitis, respiratory distress, or when directly observed therapy initiation is needed
notes: Standard regimen: 2 months HRZE then 2-4 months HR (4-month total for non-severe, 6-month for severe/cavitary). Directly Observed Therapy (DOT) is recommended.
guideline_changes: ATS/CDC/ERS/IDSA 2025 update: 4-month regimen (2HRZE/2HR) now standard for non-severe pediatric TB
snomed_display: Respiratory tuberculosis, bacteriologically and histologically confirmed

diagnosis_name: Respiratory tuberculosis, not confirmed bacteriologically or histologically

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 300 mg); give with pyridoxine 1-2 mg/kg/day
10-20 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 600 mg); take on empty stomach
30-40 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 2 g); intensive phase (first 2 months)

second_line_drugs.notes: 15-25 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 2.5 g); add to intensive phase if drug resistance suspected
Isoniazid + rifapentine + pyrazinamide + moxifloxacin daily x4 months; adolescents >=12 yr
15-20 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 750 mg); for drug-resistant TB or intolerance

investigations.name: Gastric aspirate or induced sputum for GeneXpert

counselling: Your child is being treated for TB even though the test did not grow the bacteria - this is common in children
Treatment is the same and just as important; complete all 4-6 months of medicines
Give medicines on empty stomach in the morning as directed
Watch for side effects: jaundice (yellow eyes), persistent vomiting, severe itching - report immediately
All close contacts should be screened for TB; keep follow-up appointments
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric TB specialist if not improving after 2 months of treatment, drug-resistant contacts, or extrapulmonary involvement
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe disease, miliary/meningeal TB, respiratory distress, or for treatment initiation in unreliable home setting
notes: Clinically diagnosed TB (smear/culture negative) in children is treated identically to confirmed TB. The 4-month regimen is non-inferior for non-severe disease per SHINE trial.
guideline_changes: 4-month regimen (2HRZ(E)/2HR) validated for clinically diagnosed non-severe pediatric TB per SHINE trial

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO (max 300 mg); for 12 months total
20-30 mg/kg/day PO (max 600 mg); higher dose for CNS penetration, 12 months
30-40 mg/kg/day PO (max 2g); intensive phase 2 months
15-25 mg/kg/day PO (max 2.5g); intensive phase 2 months

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.6 mg/kg/day IV for 2-4 wk, then taper over 4 wk; adjunctive for TBM
15-20 mg/kg/day PO (max 1g); good CNS penetration, for resistant TB

investigations.name: CSF analysis
investigations.indication: Lymphocytic pleocytosis, high protein, low glucose

counselling: TB meningitis needs 12 months of treatment — stopping early can be fatal
The child will also receive steroids initially to reduce brain swelling
Give pyridoxine daily with isoniazid
Watch for worsening headache, vomiting, drowsiness, or seizures and rush to hospital
Hearing and vision tests are recommended during and after treatment
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric neurologist and TB specialist; neurosurgery if hydrocephalus requiring VP shunt
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all cases of TB meningitis; ICU for altered consciousness, seizures, or raised ICP
notes: TB meningitis requires 12 months therapy and adjunctive corticosteroids to reduce mortality. Higher-dose rifampicin improves CNS penetration.
guideline_changes: ATS/CDC/ERS/IDSA 2024 update on drug-resistant TB regimens

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO (max 300 mg)
15-20 mg/kg/day PO (max 600 mg)
30-40 mg/kg/day PO (max 2g); intensive phase 2 months
15-25 mg/kg/day PO (max 2.5g); intensive phase 2 months

second_line_drugs.notes: 15-20 mg/kg/day PO (max 750 mg); MDR-TB or drug intolerance
10 mg/kg PO/IV bid (max 600 mg bid); MDR/XDR-TB

counselling: Complete the full 6-month TB treatment course — even if the child feels better after 2 months
Give all medicines together on empty stomach in the morning
Give pyridoxine (vitamin B6) daily to prevent side effects of isoniazid
Report jaundice, persistent vomiting, vision changes, or joint pain immediately
The child can attend school after 2 weeks of treatment if feeling well
referral_criteria: Refer to TB specialist if drug resistance suspected, extrapulmonary sites requiring surgical intervention (spine, lymph node), or treatment failure
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for spinal TB with cord compression, pericardial TB, renal TB with obstructive uropathy, or severe systemic illness
notes: Standard 2HRZE/4HR for 6 months; non-severe TB in children 3mo-16yr may use 4-month regimen per WHO 2022. Add pyridoxine supplementation with isoniazid.
guideline_changes: 4-month regimen (2HRZ(E)/2HR) for non-severe TB in children 3mo-16yr per WHO 2022

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO (max 300 mg); for 9-12 months total
15-20 mg/kg/day PO (max 600 mg); for 9-12 months total
30-40 mg/kg/day PO (max 2g); intensive phase 2 months
15-25 mg/kg/day PO (max 2.5g); intensive phase 2 months

second_line_drugs.notes: 15-20 mg/kg/day PO (max 750 mg); MDR-TB or ethambutol intolerance
15-30 mg/kg/day IV/IM (max 1g); MDR-TB intensive phase

investigations.name: Sputum/gastric aspirate for AFB and GeneXpert
investigations.indication: Confirm TB and check rifampicin resistance

investigations.indication: Supportive evidence (may be negative in miliary TB)

investigations.name: Fundoscopy
investigations.indication: Choroidal tubercles pathognomonic for miliary TB

counselling: Miliary TB is a serious form of TB requiring 9-12 months of daily medicine — never skip doses
Give all 4 medicines together on empty stomach in the morning
Give pyridoxine (vitamin B6) daily to prevent nerve damage from isoniazid
Watch for jaundice (yellow eyes), rash, or vision changes and report immediately
The child is not infectious after 2 weeks of effective treatment — can return to school
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist or TB specialist; urgent referral if CNS involvement or drug resistance suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe respiratory distress, CNS involvement (TB meningitis), initiation of therapy in sick child, or drug-resistant TB
notes: Miliary TB requires 9-12 months of therapy (2HRZE + 7-10HR). Add pyridoxine 1-2 mg/kg/day to prevent isoniazid neuropathy.
guideline_changes: ATS/CDC/ERS/IDSA 2024 update supports fluoroquinolone-containing regimens for drug-resistant TB

first_line_drugs.notes: 250,000 U/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 24MU/day) for 7 days; severe disease
2-4 mg/kg/day PO divided bid (max 200 mg/day) for 7 days; mild-moderate

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-75 mg/kg IV once daily (max 2g) for 7 days; severe disease alternative
10 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg), then 5 mg/kg/day for 2 more days; age <8yr

counselling: Complete the full antibiotic course even if the child starts feeling better
Leptospirosis comes from contaminated water — avoid playing in stagnant water or floods
Watch for jaundice, dark urine, reduced urine output, or bleeding and seek immediate care
The child should rest until fever resolves completely
After flooding or heavy rains, wear footwear and avoid wading in dirty water
referral_criteria: Refer to hospital with ICU if Weil disease (jaundice + renal failure + hemorrhage), pulmonary hemorrhage, or myocarditis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe leptospirosis (Weil disease), acute kidney injury, pulmonary hemorrhage, hepatitis, or hemodynamic instability
notes: Early antibiotic therapy reduces severity and duration. Avoid doxycycline in children <8yr due to dental staining; use azithromycin or amoxicillin instead.

first_line_drugs.notes: 500 IU IM as single dose (2 separate sites); neutralizes unbound toxin
15 mg/kg IV load, then 7.5 mg/kg IV q12h x7-10 days; preferred over penicillin
0.1-0.3 mg/kg IV q1-4h PRN for spasms; or infusion 5-15 mg/kg/day

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO/IV divided q6h as adjunct for spasm control
50 mg/kg IV loading then 30-50 mg/kg/day infusion for autonomic dysfunction

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis
investigations.indication: Tetanus is diagnosed clinically; no specific lab test

counselling: Baby has tetanus infection from the umbilical cord; this is a serious illness
Treatment involves antitoxin, antibiotics, and medicines to control muscle spasms
Baby will need to be in a quiet, dark room as noise and light trigger spasms
Baby may need help with breathing during the illness
This is preventable with maternal tetanus vaccination; ensure all future pregnancies are covered
referral_criteria: Refer to NICU/PICU immediately; may need mechanical ventilation for severe spasms
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require ICU admission; life-threatening condition
notes: Neonatal tetanus is preventable with maternal TT vaccination. Monitor respiratory function closely; intubation may be needed for severe spasms.

first_line_drugs.notes: 20,000-120,000 IU IV based on severity; administer ASAP on clinical suspicion
40-50 mg/kg/day PO/IV divided q6h (max 2g/day) for 14 days
25,000-50,000 U/kg/day IM divided q12h (max 1.2MU/day) for 14 days

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-12 mg/kg/day PO (max 500 mg/day) for 14 days

investigations.name: Throat swab culture on Loeffler/tellurite medium
ECG
investigations.indication: Screen for myocarditis (ST changes, heart block)

counselling: Diphtheria is a serious but treatable infection — early treatment is critical
The child must complete the full 14-day antibiotic course
All family members and close contacts need prophylactic antibiotics and vaccination check
Watch for breathing difficulty, drooling, or neck swelling and seek emergency care immediately
After recovery, the child needs to complete DPT vaccination as per schedule
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to hospital with ICU facility; ENT referral if airway compromise
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all suspected diphtheria cases; ICU if airway compromise, myocarditis, or neurological involvement
notes: Antitoxin must be given early; antibiotics eliminate the organism but do not neutralize toxin. Contacts require prophylaxis and booster vaccination.

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg), then 5 mg/kg/day days 2-5 (max 250 mg)
40-50 mg/kg/day PO divided q6h (max 2g/day) for 14 days

second_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg/day PO divided bid (max 1g/day) for 7 days
8 mg/kg/day TMP PO divided bid (max 320 mg TMP/day) for 14 days; macrolide allergy

counselling: Whooping cough coughing spells can last 6-10 weeks even after treatment — this is normal
Antibiotics prevent spread to others but may not shorten cough duration if started late
Keep infants upright after feeding to reduce vomiting from coughing spells
All household contacts need prophylactic antibiotics regardless of vaccination status
Ensure all family members are up to date on pertussis (DPT/Tdap) vaccination
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist or ICU if infant <6 months, apnea spells, cyanotic episodes, or pneumonia develops
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all infants <6 months with confirmed/suspected pertussis, any child with apnea, cyanosis, respiratory distress, or dehydration from vomiting
notes: Azithromycin is preferred for infants <1 month due to lower pyloric stenosis risk vs erythromycin. Antibiotics are most effective in the catarrhal phase.

first_line_drugs.notes: 250 mg PO bid-tid (<27 kg) or 500 mg bid-tid (>=27 kg) for 10 days
50 mg/kg/day PO once daily or divided bid (max 1g/day) for 10 days

second_line_drugs.notes: 12 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg), then 6 mg/kg/day days 2-5
40 mg/kg/day PO divided bid (max 1g/day) for 10 days; non-anaphylactic PCN allergy
20 mg/kg/day PO divided tid (max 1.8g/day) for 10 days

investigations.name: Throat culture
investigations.indication: Gold standard if rapid test negative

counselling: Complete the full 10-day antibiotic course to prevent rheumatic fever — this is very important
The child can return to school 24 hours after starting antibiotics
Give soft, cool foods if the throat is sore; avoid spicy and acidic foods
The rash feels like sandpaper and will peel — this is normal and not contagious after treatment
Watch for joint pain, chest pain, or dark urine in the weeks after recovery and report immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist if signs of rheumatic fever develop (joint pain, cardiac murmur, chorea) within 2-4 weeks
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if unable to swallow, severe dehydration, peritonsillar abscess, or signs of rheumatic fever
notes: Group A strep has no reported penicillin resistance. Full 10-day course is essential to prevent rheumatic fever.

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg/day IV divided q12h (max 4g/day) for 7 days
200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 12g/day) for 7 days

second_line_drugs.notes: 300,000 U/kg/day IV divided q4-6h (max 24MU/day); if susceptible
120 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 6g/day); severe allergy to cephalosporins

investigations.name: CSF analysis (if safe to LP)
investigations.indication: Cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture

counselling: Meningococcal disease is a medical emergency — the child is receiving urgent treatment
All close contacts (household, school) need prophylactic antibiotics within 24 hours
Watch for rash that does not fade on pressing (glass test) — this is a danger sign
After recovery, the child should receive meningococcal vaccine
Report the case to local public health authorities
referral_criteria: Refer immediately to pediatric ICU if septic shock, DIC, or meningitis with altered sensorium
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all cases — meningococcal disease is a medical emergency requiring IV antibiotics and close monitoring
notes: Begin empiric therapy immediately; meningococcal disease progresses rapidly. Close contacts require chemoprophylaxis with rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone.

first_line_drugs.notes: 250,000-400,000 U/kg/day IV divided q4-6h (max 24MU/day)
100 mg/kg/day IV divided q12h (max 4g/day)

second_line_drugs.notes: 40 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 2.7g/day); add for toxin suppression in GAS
60 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 4g/day); severe penicillin allergy

counselling: Streptococcal sepsis is a serious infection requiring IV antibiotics in hospital
The child will be closely monitored — report any worsening symptoms to the nurses immediately
All prescribed antibiotics must be completed — incomplete treatment risks relapse
After discharge, watch for recurrent fever, joint pain, or skin changes
Good wound care and hygiene prevent reinfection
referral_criteria: Refer to PICU for streptococcal toxic shock syndrome, necrotizing fasciitis, or hemodynamic instability
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all cases of streptococcal sepsis; ICU for toxic shock syndrome, necrotizing fasciitis, or multiorgan dysfunction
notes: Add clindamycin to penicillin for streptococcal toxic shock syndrome to suppress toxin production. Group A strep remains universally penicillin-susceptible.

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg IV day 1 (max 4g), then 50-100 mg/kg/day; community-acquired
50 mg/kg IV q8h (max 2g/dose); if Pseudomonas concern or nosocomial
60 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 4g/day); add if MRSA suspected

second_line_drugs.notes: 60-120 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 6g/day); ESBL or resistant organisms
300 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 16g/day pip component)

investigations.name: Lactate
investigations.indication: Tissue perfusion marker; elevated in shock

counselling: Sepsis is a life-threatening condition — the child is receiving emergency treatment
IV antibiotics must be given on time every time — do not delay or miss doses
Watch for any deterioration: increased drowsiness, poor feeding, fast breathing
Once the child improves, antibiotics may be switched to oral form to complete the course at home
Follow-up blood cultures will confirm the infection has cleared
referral_criteria: Refer immediately to pediatric ICU if septic shock, multiorgan dysfunction, or need for vasopressors/ventilation
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all cases of sepsis; ICU admission for septic shock, respiratory failure, or multiorgan dysfunction
notes: Administer antibiotics within 1 hour for septic shock, within 3 hours for sepsis without shock. De-escalate based on cultures at 48 hours.

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg IV day 1 (max 4g), then 50-100 mg/kg/day; broad empiric coverage
60 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 4g/day); add for MRSA coverage

second_line_drugs.notes: 60-120 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 6g/day); multidrug-resistant concern
7.5 mg/kg IV once daily; neonatal sepsis empiric with ampicillin
200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h; neonatal sepsis with gentamicin

counselling: Your child has a serious bloodstream infection requiring hospital treatment
Antibiotics will be adjusted once blood test results identify the exact germ
Report any worsening: high fever spikes, cold hands/feet, drowsiness, or rash
The child needs to complete the full antibiotic course even after feeling better
After discharge, follow up within 1 week for repeat blood tests
referral_criteria: Refer to PICU if hemodynamic instability, need for vasopressors, respiratory support, or multiorgan failure
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all cases; PICU for septic shock, altered consciousness, respiratory failure, or DIC
notes: Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics when possible without delaying therapy. Fluid resuscitation with 10-20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid boluses for shock.

first_line_drugs.notes: 45-90 mg/kg/day PO divided bid-tid (max 3g/day); mild-moderate infections
45-90 mg/kg/day PO divided bid (max 3g/day amoxicillin component)
50-75 mg/kg IV/IM once daily (max 2g/dose); moderate-severe

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg), then 5 mg/kg/day days 2-5
30-40 mg/kg/day PO/IV divided tid (max 1.8g/day); penicillin allergy

counselling: Complete the full course of antibiotics even if the child feels better after 2-3 days
Give probiotics (curd/yogurt) alongside antibiotics to prevent loose stools
Ensure adequate fluid intake and nutrition during the illness
Return if fever does not improve within 48-72 hours of starting antibiotics
Wash hands frequently and maintain hygiene to prevent spread
referral_criteria: Refer if no improvement after 48-72 hours of appropriate antibiotics, suspected deep-seated infection, or immunocompromised child
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for sepsis signs, toxic appearance, inability to tolerate oral medications, or immunocompromised status
notes: Narrow antibiotic spectrum once culture and sensitivity results are available. Always obtain cultures before starting empiric therapy when feasible.

first_line_drugs.notes: 2.2 mg/kg PO/IV bid (max 100 mg/dose) for 7 days
10 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg), then 5 mg/kg/day for 4 more days; age <8yr

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-75 mg/kg/day PO/IV divided q6h (max 4g/day) for 7 days; if resistant
10-20 mg/kg/day PO (max 600 mg); resistant scrub typhus

counselling: Scrub typhus is spread by mite bites — fever usually improves within 48 hours of starting doxycycline
Look for a painless black scab (eschar) on the body — this helps confirm the diagnosis
Complete the full antibiotic course even after fever resolves
Wear full-sleeve clothes and use insect repellent when going into bushy or grassy areas
Watch for severe headache, confusion, or breathing difficulty and seek immediate care
referral_criteria: Refer to ICU if ARDS, multiorgan dysfunction, myocarditis, meningoencephalitis, or no response to doxycycline within 48 hours
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for organ involvement (hepatitis, AKI, pneumonitis), altered consciousness, hemodynamic instability, or infant with suspected scrub typhus
notes: Doxycycline is first-line for all ages; AAP supports short courses in children <8yr. Fever typically resolves within 48h of appropriate therapy.
guideline_changes: NEJM 2023 trial showed IV doxycycline + IV azithromycin combination superior for severe scrub typhus

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 mL IM on days 0, 3, 7, 14 (deltoid or anterolateral thigh in children)
20 IU/kg infiltrated into wound site; remainder IM at site distant from vaccine

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 mL IM on days 0, 3, 7, 14, 28; immunocompromised patients
40 IU/kg infiltrated into wound; where HRIG unavailable (skin test first)

investigations.name: Clinical assessment of wound category
investigations.indication: WHO category I/II/III determines PEP regimen

investigations.name: No pre-treatment diagnostic test needed
investigations.indication: Treatment is based on exposure, not lab confirmation

counselling: Rabies is nearly 100% fatal once symptoms appear — post-exposure vaccination is the ONLY lifesaving treatment
Wash the wound thoroughly with soap and running water for 15 minutes immediately after the bite
Get the first vaccine dose on the same day as the bite — do not delay
Complete all 4 vaccine doses on the scheduled days (0, 3, 7, 14)
Do not apply turmeric, chili, or any home remedies to the wound — only soap, water, and povidone-iodine
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to anti-rabies clinic or hospital for category III exposure (deep bite, multiple bites, bites on head/face/neck) — RIG is mandatory
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if wound needs surgical debridement, category III exposure requiring RIG infiltration, bite on face/neck, or symptomatic rabies (palliative care)
notes: Post-exposure prophylaxis is nearly 100% effective if administered promptly. HRIG is given only once with the first vaccine dose; never repeat HRIG.

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO q6-8h for headache and fever; max 40 mg/kg/day
15 mg/kg PO q4-6h for analgesia/antipyresis; max 75 mg/kg/day
Maintenance fluids; monitor for SIADH; restrict if serum Na <135 mEq/L

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg IV/PO q8h; max 4 mg/dose; for nausea/vomiting
10 mg/kg IV q8h if HSV-2 meningitis confirmed or immunocompromised patient

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis
investigations.indication: Lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, mildly elevated protein

investigations.name: CSF enterovirus PCR
investigations.indication: Most common cause of viral meningitis in children

investigations.name: CBC, CRP
investigations.indication: Normal/mildly elevated CRP helps distinguish from bacterial

counselling: Child has a viral infection of the brain covering; this is usually milder than bacterial meningitis
Treatment is mainly supportive: rest, fluids, and pain medicine
Most children recover completely within 1-2 weeks
Keep child comfortable; give pain medicine regularly for headache
Return to hospital if child becomes more drowsy, has seizures, or symptoms worsen
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist if altered consciousness, seizures, or not improving within expected timeframe
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit neonates, immunocompromised, altered mental status, or if unable to differentiate from bacterial meningitis
notes: Most cases are self-limited and resolve in 1-2 weeks with supportive care. Admit neonates, immunocompromised, or those with altered mental status or seizures for observation.
guideline_changes: WHO launched first-ever meningitis diagnosis/treatment/care guidelines April 2025

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg PO q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4g/day); fever and pain control
ORS or isotonic fluids; maintain hydration, monitor daily from day 3

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-7 mL/kg/hr for 1-2h, then reduce; for dehydration or rising hematocrit
5-7 mL/kg/hr for 1-2h, then reduce; alternative crystalloid

counselling: Give ONLY paracetamol for fever — do NOT give ibuprofen, aspirin, or nimesulide
Ensure the child drinks plenty of fluids — ORS, coconut water, fruit juices
Monitor daily from day 3 to day 7 — this is the critical period
Watch for warning signs: severe belly pain, persistent vomiting, bleeding, drowsiness
Use mosquito nets even during the day — dengue mosquito bites in daytime
referral_criteria: Refer urgently if warning signs: persistent vomiting, abdominal pain, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, hematocrit rising rapidly
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for warning signs, hematocrit rise >20%, platelet <20,000 with bleeding, severe dehydration, or altered consciousness
notes: Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin due to bleeding risk. Monitor platelet count and hematocrit daily from day 3 until 1-2 days after defervescence.
guideline_changes: Dengvaxia and TAK-003 (Qdenga) vaccines available; treatment remains supportive

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 mL/kg bolus over 15-30 min; repeat as needed for shock resuscitation
15 mg/kg PO/PR q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day); avoid NSAIDs/aspirin

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 mL/kg IV; if no response to crystalloid after 2 boluses
10 mL/kg IV; only for severe bleeding with platelet <10,000 or active hemorrhage
5-10 mL/kg IV; for significant hemorrhage with Hgb drop

investigations.name: Chest X-ray/USG abdomen
investigations.indication: Pleural effusion, ascites in plasma leakage

counselling: Severe dengue is a medical emergency — the child is receiving critical care
No ibuprofen or aspirin — only paracetamol for fever
IV fluids are carefully monitored — both too little and too much fluid are dangerous
The critical phase (day 3-7) requires close monitoring of blood pressure and blood counts
After recovery, do not skip the follow-up visit — platelet count and recovery need to be confirmed
referral_criteria: Refer immediately to PICU if dengue shock syndrome, severe hemorrhage, organ failure, or massive plasma leakage
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all cases of dengue hemorrhagic fever; ICU for shock, massive hemorrhage, organ impairment, or rapid hematocrit rise
notes: Severe dengue requires ICU admission. Judicious fluid management is critical: overhydration causes fluid overload and pulmonary edema during recovery phase.

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg PO q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4g/day); first-line for fever/pain
10 mg/kg PO q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); ONLY after dengue is ruled out

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-7 mg/kg PO bid (max 1g/day); persistent arthralgia after excluding dengue
ORS ad libitum; prevent dehydration during febrile phase

counselling: Chikungunya is a viral illness — antibiotics will not help
Give only paracetamol for fever until dengue is ruled out (no ibuprofen/aspirin initially)
Joint pain may persist for weeks to months — this is common in chikungunya
Ensure adequate fluid intake; ORS, coconut water, and soups are helpful
Use mosquito nets and repellents to prevent spread to other family members
referral_criteria: Refer if severe joint involvement causing immobility, persistent arthritis >3 months, encephalitis, or neonatal chikungunya
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for encephalitis, myocarditis, severe dehydration, neonatal chikungunya, or hemorrhagic manifestations
notes: Use only acetaminophen until dengue is ruled out (co-endemic areas). NSAIDs may be added for persistent arthralgia once dengue is excluded.
guideline_changes: FDA approved Ixchiq (live attenuated vaccine) Nov 2023 for adults >=18yr; no pediatric approval yet

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg PO qid (max 400 mg/dose) for 7-14 days; mucocutaneous HSV
20 mg/kg IV q8h for 14-21 days; neonatal HSV or encephalitis

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg PO bid (max 1g/dose); children >=12yr, recurrent HSV
40-60 mg/kg IV q8h; acyclovir-resistant HSV in immunocompromised

counselling: HSV infection can recur — triggers include fever, sun exposure, and stress
Keep the sores clean; wash hands after touching them to prevent spread
Complete the full course of acyclovir even if sores start healing
Do not kiss the child on the mouth or share utensils when sores are active
For neonatal herpes, hospital treatment is essential — it can be life-threatening in newborns
referral_criteria: Refer to infectious disease specialist for neonatal HSV, HSV encephalitis, or recurrent severe episodes; ophthalmologist for eye involvement
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all neonatal HSV cases, HSV encephalitis, disseminated HSV, eczema herpeticum, or immunocompromised child with HSV
notes: Neonatal HSV requires high-dose IV acyclovir for 14-21 days. Ensure adequate hydration to prevent acyclovir crystalluria.

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg PO qid (max 800 mg/dose) for 5 days; within 24h of rash onset
10-20 mg/kg IV q8h for 7-10 days; immunocompromised or severe disease

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg PO tid (max 1g/dose) for 5 days; age >=12yr, better bioavailability
Apply topically prn for pruritus relief

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis (usually sufficient)
investigations.indication: Classic pruritic vesicular rash in different stages

counselling: Chickenpox usually resolves in 7-10 days — keep the child comfortable and hydrated
Do not use aspirin for fever — use paracetamol only
Trim fingernails short and keep sores clean to prevent bacterial superinfection
Calamine lotion can help with itching; oatmeal baths are soothing
The child is contagious until all blisters have crusted over (usually 5-7 days after rash onset) — keep home from school
referral_criteria: Refer if immunocompromised, neonatal varicella, encephalitis, pneumonitis, or severe skin superinfection
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit immunocompromised child with varicella, varicella pneumonia, encephalitis, neonatal varicella, or severe bacterial superinfection
notes: AAP does not recommend routine acyclovir for uncomplicated varicella in healthy children. Reserve antiviral for immunocompromised, adolescents, or complicated disease.

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg PO qid (max 800 mg/dose) for 7-10 days
10-20 mg/kg IV q8h for 7-10 days; for immunocompromised

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg PO tid (max 1g/dose) for 7 days; age >=12yr
Limited pediatric data; 500 mg PO tid for adolescents >=12yr

investigations.name: HIV test
investigations.indication: Consider if zoster in young child without prior varicella

counselling: Herpes zoster (shingles) in children is usually milder than in adults
Give acyclovir as prescribed — best results when started within 72 hours of rash
Keep the rash clean and dry; do not apply any home remedies
The child is contagious to those who have not had chickenpox — keep away from pregnant women and newborns
Pain and itching usually resolve within 2-3 weeks as the rash heals
referral_criteria: Refer to ophthalmologist if eye involvement (herpes zoster ophthalmicus), or to infectious disease specialist if immunocompromised
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if immunocompromised child, disseminated zoster, CNS involvement, or severe pain requiring IV therapy
notes: Immunocompromised children require IV acyclovir. Treat within 72h of rash onset for best efficacy.
source: AAP Red Book 2024, NIH OI Guidelines

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg PO q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day); fever management

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); age >=6mo, antipyretic/analgesic
Amoxicillin 45-90 mg/kg/day PO divided bid for bacterial pneumonia/otitis

counselling: There is no cure for measles — treatment focuses on managing symptoms and preventing complications
Give Vitamin A as prescribed — it reduces complications and death from measles
Keep the child isolated from others for 4 days after the rash appears
Watch for fast breathing, ear discharge, or drowsiness — these suggest complications
Ensure the child stays hydrated with ORS and nutritious foods
referral_criteria: Refer if pneumonia, encephalitis, severe malnutrition, or immunocompromised child with measles
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for measles pneumonia, encephalitis, severe dehydration, croup, severe malnutrition, or immunocompromised status
notes: No specific antiviral therapy. Vitamin A reduces mortality and complication rates, especially in malnourished or hospitalized children. Isolate for 4 days after rash onset.
guideline_changes: CDC 2025 updated guidance on vitamin A: recommended for hospitalized children

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg PO q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day); fever and arthralgia
10 mg/kg PO q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); joint pain, age >=6mo

second_line_drugs.notes: ORS to maintain hydration during febrile illness

counselling: Rubella in children is usually mild and self-limiting — no specific treatment needed
Give paracetamol if the child has fever or joint pain
Keep the child away from pregnant women for 7 days after rash onset — rubella can harm the unborn baby
The rash typically lasts 3 days and resolves without scarring
Ensure the child has received MMR vaccination to prevent future episodes
referral_criteria: Refer if congenital rubella syndrome suspected (cataracts, heart defects, deafness), thrombocytopenia, or encephalitis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for congenital rubella syndrome management, thrombocytopenic purpura, or encephalitis
notes: No specific therapy for rubella or congenital rubella syndrome. Postnatal rubella is usually mild and self-limiting. Isolate for 7 days after rash onset.

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg PO/PR q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day); fever control
10 mg/kg PO q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); age >=6mo

second_line_drugs.notes: ORS ad libitum; maintain hydration during high fever phase (3-4 days)

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis (usually sufficient)
investigations.indication: Classic pattern: high fever 3-4 days then rash

counselling: Roseola causes high fever for 3-4 days followed by a rash once fever breaks — the rash is harmless
Give paracetamol for fever and ensure plenty of fluids
Febrile seizures can occur due to high fever — if it happens, place child on side and seek emergency care
The rash means the illness is almost over — no treatment needed for the rash
The child can return to daycare/school once fever-free for 24 hours
referral_criteria: Refer if febrile seizures occur, encephalitis suspected, or immunocompromised child with HHV-6 infection
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for prolonged febrile seizure, encephalitis, hepatitis, or immunocompromised child with severe HHV-6 disease
notes: Self-limiting illness caused by HHV-6. High fever (up to 40.5C) resolves in 3-4 days followed by characteristic rash. No antiviral therapy needed in immunocompetent children.
snomed_display: Human herpes virus 6 infection of skin

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg PO q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4g/day); fever and oral pain
10 mg/kg PO q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); age >=6mo, fever and pain

second_line_drugs.notes: Equal parts Maalox/diphenhydramine/lidocaine 2%; swish-and-spit for oral pain
ORS ad libitum; prevent dehydration from poor intake due to oral ulcers

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis (usually sufficient)
investigations.indication: Characteristic oral ulcers + vesicles on hands/feet

counselling: HFMD is a viral illness that resolves on its own in 7-10 days — no antibiotics needed
Give cold fluids, ice pops, and soft foods — avoid spicy, salty, or acidic foods that sting the mouth sores
Watch for dehydration: fewer wet diapers, dry mouth, or no tears when crying
Keep the child home from school/daycare until fever resolves and sores are healing
Wash hands frequently — the virus spreads through saliva, blister fluid, and stool
referral_criteria: Refer if signs of neurological involvement (myoclonus, ataxia, encephalitis), cardiopulmonary compromise, or dehydration not responding to oral rehydration
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe dehydration, encephalitis, pulmonary edema (EV-A71 complication), myocarditis, or inability to maintain oral intake
notes: Self-limiting illness (7-10 days). Focus on maintaining hydration as oral ulcers may cause feeding refusal. No antiviral therapy is indicated.

first_line_drugs.notes: Rest, adequate hydration, balanced nutrition. No specific antiviral therapy available
10-15 mg/kg PO q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day) for fever/pain; AVOID if hepatotoxicity concern
NS/D5NS maintenance if unable to tolerate PO; correct dehydration from vomiting

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg IV/PO q8h (max 4 mg/dose) for nausea/vomiting
240 mg/kg/day PO divided TID (max 4 g/dose) for severe pruritus

investigations.name: PT/INR
investigations.indication: Liver synthetic function — coagulopathy suggests severe disease

counselling: Your child has hepatitis A — this is a viral infection of the liver that usually heals on its own
Give plenty of fluids and a balanced diet — no special dietary restrictions are needed
Avoid giving paracetamol in high doses — it can stress the liver. Use only as directed by the doctor
The child is contagious for about 2 weeks — maintain strict hand hygiene and do not share utensils
Hepatitis A vaccine can protect other family members — ask about vaccination
referral_criteria: Hepatology/pediatric GI if signs of liver failure (coagulopathy, encephalopathy, persistent jaundice >6 weeks). Liver transplant center if fulminant hepatitis develops
hospitalisation_criteria: Persistent vomiting with dehydration. INR >1.5 (coagulopathy). Hepatic encephalopathy (altered behavior, confusion). Severe jaundice with liver synthetic failure
notes: Self-limited in children with excellent prognosis; fulminant hepatitis rare (<1%). Avoid hepatotoxic medications; no dietary restrictions needed. HAV vaccination prevents disease.

first_line_drugs.notes: Rest, hydration, nutrition. Most acute HBV is self-limited in older children
NS maintenance for dehydration; monitor glucose, coagulation, LFTs serially

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.015 mg/kg PO QD (max 0.5 mg) for severe/protracted acute HBV or fulminant hepatitis
8 mg/kg PO QD (max 300 mg) age >=2yr; for severe acute HBV (off-label)

investigations.name: HBsAg, HBV DNA, HBeAg
investigations.indication: Confirm acute HBV; viral load for severity

investigations.name: Liver function tests
investigations.indication: AST, ALT, bilirubin for severity assessment

counselling: Your child has hepatitis B — most older children recover fully, but monitoring is important
Blood tests will be done over the next 6 months to make sure the virus clears completely
If the virus does not clear, long-term antiviral treatment may be needed
All family members should be tested and vaccinated against hepatitis B
For newborns: HBIG and HBV vaccine within 12 hours of birth prevents mother-to-child transmission
referral_criteria: Pediatric hepatology/GI for all cases. Infectious disease for chronic HBV requiring antivirals. Liver transplant center if fulminant hepatitis. Obstetrics for pregnant mothers (HBV-positive) for prevention of vertical transmission
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe acute HBV with coagulopathy (INR >1.5). Hepatic encephalopathy. Fulminant hepatic failure. Severe dehydration from persistent vomiting. Newborn of HBV-positive mother for HBIG + vaccine
notes: Acute HBV rarely needs antivirals; interferon is contraindicated in acute phase. Neonates require HBIG + HBV vaccine within 12h of birth to prevent vertical transmission.
guideline_changes: 2024 WHO expanded treatment criteria for chronic HBV; EASL 2025 updated management guidance

first_line_drugs.notes: Weight-based: <17kg 150/37.5mg, 17-<30kg 200/50mg, >=30kg 400/100mg PO QD x12wk
Weight-based granules or tablets PO QD x8wk; pangenotypic, age >=3yr

second_line_drugs.notes: Weight-based PO QD x12wk; for genotypes 1,4,5,6; age >=3yr
No DAAs approved <3yr; observe for spontaneous clearance (high in infants)

investigations.name: HCV RNA (quantitative PCR)
investigations.indication: Confirm viremia and measure viral load

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Baseline; rule out cytopenias from chronic disease

counselling: Your child has hepatitis C — new oral medicines can cure this infection in 8-12 weeks
The medicines must be taken every day at the same time — do not miss doses
Treatment is available for children aged 3 years and above with very high cure rates (>95%)
Hepatitis C spreads through blood — avoid sharing razors, toothbrushes, or needles
After completing treatment, a blood test at 12 weeks will confirm if the virus is cured
referral_criteria: Pediatric hepatology/GI mandatory for DAA therapy initiation. Infectious disease for complex cases. Hematology if acquired through blood products. Liver transplant if cirrhosis develops
hospitalisation_criteria: Fulminant hepatitis (rare). Decompensated cirrhosis. Severe liver failure. Generally DAA treatment is outpatient
notes: Treat all children >=3yr with chronic HCV; spontaneous clearance high in infants so defer treatment. SVR rates >95% with current DAA regimens in children.
guideline_changes: Pan-genotypic DAAs now FDA-approved for children >=3yr; child-friendly formulations available

diagnosis_name: Acute Severe Viral Hepatitis (with risk of Acute Liver Failure / Hyperammonemia)

first_line_drugs.notes: IV infusion regimen: Loading 125 mg/kg over 1 hour, then 50 mg/kg over 4 hours, then 125 mg/kg over 19 hours; Day 2 onward: 150 mg/kg over 24 hours, repeat next 24 hours as required. Preparation: Add 1000 mg NAC (2 ampoules of 5 ml each) in 40 ml of 5% DEXTROSE; adjust total volume and rate as per weight-based dose and infusion duration
10 mg IM once daily for 3 days for coagulopathy correction
5 mg/kg/dose PO every 12 hours for 21 days for cholestasis and bile flow improvement
1 ml/kg/dose PO 2–3 times daily; titrate to achieve 2–3 soft stools/day for prevention/treatment of hepatic encephalopathy
250–500 mg/kg/day PO in 4 divided doses every 6 hours for hyperammonemia management
Maintenance IV fluids as per Holliday-Segar calculation; avoid fluid overload; add MVI as per standard pediatric dosing

second_line_drugs.notes: 10–15 mg/kg/day PO in 2 divided doses; use if persistent or worsening hepatic encephalopathy despite lactulose

investigations.name: BLOOD GLUCOSE
investigations.indication: Monitor for hypoglycemia in acute liver failure

investigations.name: SERUM AMMONIA
investigations.indication: Assess and monitor hyperammonemia and encephalopathy

investigations.name: PT / INR
investigations.indication: Assess severity of liver dysfunction and coagulopathy

investigations.name: LIVER FUNCTION TEST (LFT)
investigations.indication: Monitor disease progression and hepatic injury

investigations.name: RENAL FUNCTION TEST (RFT)
investigations.indication: Assess renal involvement and hepatorenal syndrome risk

counselling: Child has severe liver infection which can affect brain and blood clotting; close monitoring is required — बच्चे को गंभीर लिवर संक्रमण है जो दिमाग और खून के थक्कों को प्रभावित कर सकता है; नज़दीकी निगरानी ज़रूरी है
Strict medication adherence is essential; do not miss doses especially lactulose and NAC — दवाइयाँ समय पर लेना बहुत जरूरी है; खासकर लैक्टुलोज़ और NAC की खुराक न छोड़ें
Avoid outside food, ensure adequate carbohydrate intake and hydration — बाहर का खाना न दें, पर्याप्त कार्बोहाइड्रेट और पानी दें
Do NOT give PARACETAMOL, antiemetics, or any over-the-counter drugs without doctor advice — डॉक्टर की सलाह के बिना पैरासिटामोल, उल्टी की दवा या कोई भी दवा न दें
Immediate follow-up required if child becomes drowsy, vomits repeatedly, or stops feeding — अगर बच्चा सुस्त हो जाए, बार-बार उल्टी करे या दूध न पिए तो तुरंत डॉक्टर को दिखाएं
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric gastroenterologist or liver transplant center if INR >2, rising ammonia, worsening encephalopathy, or no improvement within 24–48 hours
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases of severe hepatitis with coagulopathy, encephalopathy, persistent vomiting, hypoglycemia, or rising bilirubin/ammonia require admission (preferably ICU monitoring)
notes: Acute severe viral hepatitis can progress to acute liver failure; early NAC improves outcomes even in non-paracetamol liver injury. Strict drug avoidance (especially hepatotoxic drugs) is critical.
guideline_changes: NAC recommended even in non-acetaminophen acute liver failure (updated global practice)
expected_course: Initial worsening may occur over 3–5 days; recovery may take 2–4 weeks; close monitoring required for early detection of liver failure
key_clinical_points: NAC improves survival even in viral hepatitis-related liver failure
Lactulose is essential to prevent hepatic encephalopathy
Avoid hepatotoxic drugs including PARACETAMOL
Monitor INR and ammonia closely – key prognostic markers

severity_assessment.mild: Jaundice with stable vitals, no coagulopathy
severity_assessment.moderate: Jaundice with mild coagulopathy and elevated enzymes
severity_assessment.severe: Coagulopathy (INR >1.5), encephalopathy, hypoglycemia, rising ammonia

first_line_drugs.notes: Weight-band PO once daily + 2 NRTIs; preferred INSTI for all ages >=4 wk

Weight-band PO once daily; children >=3yr and >=14 kg

investigations.name: HIV viral load

counselling: ART medicines must be given every day at the same time — never skip doses
The child can live a normal, healthy life with consistent treatment
Regular blood tests (CD4, viral load) every 3-6 months are essential to monitor treatment
Keep the child's HIV status confidential — disclosure should be age-appropriate and gradual
Ensure all routine vaccinations are given (avoid live vaccines if severely immunosuppressed)
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric ART center for initiation; refer urgently for treatment failure (rising viral load), opportunistic infections, or IRIS
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe opportunistic infections (PCP, TB meningitis, cryptococcal meningitis), severe malnutrition, or WHO stage 4 conditions

guideline_changes: Dolutegravir-based regimens now preferred first-line for all ages including neonates

investigations.name: CMV PCR (urine or saliva)

Get regular blood tests (CBC) as the medicine can lower blood counts temporarily
Hearing tests should be done every 6 months until age 3 years even after treatment

Report any rash, unusual bleeding, or feeding difficulty during treatment

Maintenance rate isotonic crystalloid; if unable to tolerate PO due to trismus

investigations.indication: Bilateral parotid swelling in unvaccinated child

counselling: Mumps is a viral infection — antibiotics are not needed
Give soft foods and plenty of fluids; avoid sour/acidic foods that stimulate saliva and worsen pain
Apply warm or cold compresses to swollen cheeks for pain relief
The child is contagious from 2 days before to 5 days after swelling appears — keep home from school
Watch for severe headache, stiff neck, or scrotal swelling in boys and seek medical care immediately

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for meningitis/encephalitis, severe pancreatitis, dehydration from inability to eat, or bilateral orchitis

Rest is important; the child will feel tired for 2-4 weeks
Avoid contact sports for at least 3-4 weeks due to risk of spleen injury
Do not give amoxicillin/ampicillin — it causes a rash in mono patients
The sore throat and fever should resolve in 1-2 weeks; fatigue may last longer

For tinea capitis (scalp ringworm), oral medicine is essential - cream alone will not work
Do not share combs, towels, caps, or pillows to prevent spread

If family members or pets have similar patches, they need treatment too

notes: Tinea capitis always requires systemic antifungal therapy; topical agents alone are insufficient. Monitor for T. indotineae resistance if poor response to terbinafine.
guideline_changes: Rising T. indotineae antifungal resistance reported; CDC alert 2024

Apply antifungal shampoo as body wash; leave on for 5-10 minutes before rinsing
The white/brown patches may take months to match normal skin colour even after treatment
Use antifungal shampoo once monthly as maintenance to prevent recurrence
The condition commonly recurs in hot humid weather - preventive treatment before summer helps

hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not needed for superficial mycoses
notes: Pityriasis versicolor has high recurrence; discuss monthly prophylactic antifungal wash. Dyspigmentation may persist for months after successful treatment.

first_line_drugs.notes: 100,000-400,000 U PO qid swish-and-swallow for 7-14 days; oropharyngeal
6 mg/kg PO/IV day 1, then 3 mg/kg/day (max 400 mg) for 7-14 days

counselling: For oral thrush, apply nystatin drops after feeds — do not feed immediately after
Sterilize bottle nipples and pacifiers daily by boiling
If breastfeeding, treat the mother's nipples simultaneously to prevent reinfection
Watch for white patches that do not wipe off in the mouth — this is thrush
Complete the full course of antifungal even if the white patches clear early

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for invasive/systemic candidiasis, candidemia, esophageal candidiasis with inability to swallow, or immunocompromised child with disseminated disease
notes: Nystatin is first-line for oropharyngeal thrush; fluconazole is preferred for esophageal or systemic candidiasis. Neonates <2 weeks require adjusted dosing intervals.

counselling: Oral thrush is common and easily treatable. — मुंह का छाला आम है और आसानी से ठीक हो जाता है।
It is not due to poor care. — यह खराब देखभाल की वजह से नहीं होता।
Maintain proper hygiene of feeding equipment. — दूध पिलाने के बर्तन साफ़ रखें।
Complete full course of medication even if symptoms improve early. — लक्षण कम होने पर भी दवाई पूरी करें।
Consult doctor if baby develops fever, feeding refusal or spreading lesions. — बुखार, दूध न पीना, या छाले बढ़ना दिखे तो डॉक्टर को दिखाएं।
warning_signs: Feeding refusal or excessive crying during feeds — दूध पीने से मना करना या दूध पीते समय बहुत रोना
Spreading lesions beyond mouth — छाले मुंह से बाहर फैलना
Poor weight gain — वज़न न बढ़ना
Persistent or recurring infection despite treatment — इलाज के बावजूद बार-बार होना
Rash in diaper area — डायपर वाली जगह पर दाने
expected_course: Oral thrush in neonates usually responds well to topical antifungal therapy (clotrimazole or nystatin) within 7-14 days. Treatment should continue for 48 hours after resolution of lesions. Recurrence is common if hygiene measures are not followed or if maternal nipple candidiasis is untreated.
key_clinical_points: White adherent plaques that do not wipe off easily are the hallmark of oral thrush.
Milk residue is easily wiped away and does not leave erythema underneath.
Clotrimazole 1% mouth paint or Nystatin oral suspension are first-line treatments.
Always treat concurrent maternal nipple candidiasis to prevent reinfection.
Sterilize all feeding equipment (bottles, nipples, pacifiers) daily.
Systemic fluconazole is reserved for refractory or systemic candidiasis under specialist guidance.

severity_assessment.mild: Few white patches, feeding unaffected, no systemic symptoms — topical antifungal and hygiene advice.
severity_assessment.moderate: Multiple patches, some feeding difficulty or irritability — topical antifungal, close follow-up.
severity_assessment.severe: Extensive lesions, feeding refusal, failure to thrive, systemic symptoms — consider systemic therapy, rule out immunodeficiency.

Apply to affected area daily for 2-4 weeks; alternative topical antifungal

hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed for cutaneous candidiasis; admit if disseminated candidiasis suspected (immunocompromised child), or extensive erosive lesions in neonate

2.4 mg/kg IV at 0, 12, 24h, then daily until oral tolerated (min 24h)

counselling: P. falciparum is the most dangerous type of malaria — take all medicines exactly as prescribed
Take ACT (artemether-lumefantrine) with fatty food for better absorption
Watch for danger signs: confusion, seizures, very dark urine, jaundice, severe weakness
Return if fever comes back within 28 days — blood test will need to be repeated
Prevent future malaria: use insecticide-treated bed nets every night

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe malaria, hyperparasitemia >5%, any danger sign (prostration, seizures, respiratory distress, bleeding), or age <6 months
notes: P. falciparum is the most lethal species; always assess for severity. Children <25 kg need minimum DHA 2.5 mg/kg/day per WHO 2025 guidelines.

second_line_drugs.notes: 2.4 mg/kg IV at 0h, 12h, 24h then daily, severe malaria first-line

counselling: Complete the full 3-day course of ACT tablets even if fever goes away
Give the medicine with food or milk for better absorption
Use mosquito nets (treated with insecticide) every night and mosquito repellent

Watch for danger signs: repeated vomiting, dark urine, jaundice, confusion - rush to hospital

guideline_changes: IV artesunate is now unambiguous first-line for severe malaria, replacing IV quinine. Artesunate-pyronaridine added as new ACT.

Single-dose radical cure; approved >=16yr only, requires G6PD normal

G6PD test must be done before starting primaquine to avoid a serious side effect

Treat ALL family members and close contacts at the same time even if they have no symptoms
Wash all clothes, bed sheets, and towels in hot water (>60C) on treatment day
Itching may continue for 2-4 weeks after treatment — this does not mean treatment failed
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if crusted (Norwegian) scabies, refractory to two rounds of treatment, or immunocompromised child
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for crusted (Norwegian) scabies requiring isolation and intensive topical + oral treatment, or secondary bacterial infection with sepsis
notes: Treat all household members and close contacts simultaneously. Launder clothing/bedding at >=60C. Itching may persist 2-4 weeks post-treatment (not treatment failure).
guideline_changes: Oral ivermectin safety data expanding for children <15 kg per Lancet 2024 trial

diagnosis_name: Malignant neoplasm of bone and articular cartilage

first_line_drugs.notes: High-dose methotrexate 12 g/m2 IV + doxorubicin 37.5 mg/m2 IV x2d + cisplatin 60 mg/m2 IV x2d; 8-10wk
Limb-salvage surgery preferred if feasible; wide excision with negative margins after neoadjuvant chemo
Continue MAP regimen post-op x12-29 weeks; modify if <90% tumor necrosis

second_line_drugs.notes: Ifosfamide 1.8 g/m2 IV x5d + etoposide 100 mg/m2 IV x5d; add for poor histologic response
MTX 12 g/m2 IV over 4h with leucovorin rescue 15 mg/m2 q6h x10 doses starting at 24h post-MTX

investigations.name: MRI of primary tumor
investigations.indication: Assess local extent and skip lesions

counselling: Bone cancer (osteosarcoma) treatment involves chemotherapy and surgery — limb-salvage is usually possible
Treatment is long (9-12 months) but cure rates are around 65% for localized disease
Report new or worsening pain, fever, or swelling immediately
Physiotherapy after surgery is essential for limb function recovery
Emotional support and counselling for the child and family is strongly recommended
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric orthopedic oncology center with chemotherapy and limb-salvage surgery capability
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for neoadjuvant chemotherapy, surgical resection, and management of chemotherapy complications
notes: Osteosarcoma is most common; 5-year OS 62-65% for localized disease. Assess histologic response (>90% necrosis = good) to guide adjuvant therapy.

diagnosis_name: Malignant neoplasm of connective and soft tissue

first_line_drugs.notes: Vincristine 1.5 mg/m2 IV wkly, actinomycin D 0.045 mg/kg IV, cyclophosphamide 2.2 g/m2 IV q3wk
Complete excision if feasible; delayed surgery after chemo for initially unresectable tumors
40-50 Gy to tumor bed; for microscopic or gross residual disease after surgery

second_line_drugs.notes: VAC alternating with vincristine + irinotecan (VCR 1.5 mg/m2 + irinotecan 50 mg/m2 x5d); less toxicity
Ifosfamide 3 g/m2 IV x3d + doxorubicin 30 mg/m2 IV x2d for non-rhabdo soft tissue sarcomas

counselling: Rhabdomyosarcoma is the most common soft tissue cancer in children — it responds well to treatment
Treatment involves chemotherapy, surgery, and sometimes radiation over many months
Report any new lumps, swelling, or worsening pain immediately
Maintain good nutrition during treatment to support the child's recovery
Regular follow-up imaging is needed even after treatment completion
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric oncology center with multidisciplinary sarcoma team
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for chemotherapy initiation, surgical resection, neutropenic fever, or tumor-related emergencies (obstruction, compression)
notes: Rhabdomyosarcoma is the most common pediatric STS. Low-risk RMS achieves >90% FFS with VAC. Multimodal therapy (chemo, surgery, RT) is standard.
guideline_changes: EpSSG 2025 released European standard care recommendations for childhood rhabdomyosarcoma

diagnosis_name: Malignant neoplasm of kidney, except renal pelvis

first_line_drugs.notes: Radical nephrectomy with lymph node sampling; COG approach is surgery first then chemo
Actinomycin D 0.045 mg/kg IV + VCR 1.5 mg/m2 IV wkly x18 weeks for stage I-II FH
Add doxorubicin 1.5 mg/kg IV to VA for stage III-IV FH; regimen DD-4A per COG protocol

second_line_drugs.notes: Actinomycin D + VCR x4-6 weeks pre-op; SIOP approach for large/bilateral tumors
Cyclophosphamide, etoposide + vincristine, doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide alternating; for anaplastic histology

investigations.name: CT or MRI abdomen with contrast

counselling: Wilms tumor (kidney cancer) has an excellent cure rate — over 90% for early-stage disease
Surgery to remove the kidney is the main treatment, followed by chemotherapy
The child can live a normal life with one kidney — protect the remaining kidney from injury
Report abdominal swelling, blood in urine, or fever immediately
Follow-up imaging (ultrasound/CT) will continue for several years after treatment
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric oncology center with pediatric surgical capability
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for nephrectomy and chemotherapy initiation; admit urgently for tumor rupture, hemorrhage, or bilateral tumor management
notes: Wilms tumor has >90% overall survival for favorable histology. LOH at 1p/16q indicates worse prognosis and need for more intensive chemotherapy.

first_line_drugs.notes: Neurosurgical resection targeting gross total removal; residual <1 cm x 1 cm if possible
CSI 23.4 Gy (std risk) or 36 Gy (high risk) + tumor bed boost to 54-55.8 Gy for medulloblastoma
Post-radiation chemo for MB: cisplatin 75 mg/m2, VCR 1.5 mg/m2 weekly, cyclophosphamide 1.5 g/m2

second_line_drugs.notes: Carboplatin 175 mg/m2 IV wkly x4 + VCR 1.5 mg/m2 IV wkly for low-grade glioma; 10-wk cycles
150-200 mg/m2 PO x5 days q28 days for recurrent/progressive high-grade glioma
10 mg/kg IV q2wk for recurrent high-grade glioma; anti-VEGF targeted therapy

investigations.name: CT head (if MRI not immediately available)

counselling: Brain tumors in children often have good outcomes with proper treatment at a specialized center
Surgery is usually the first step; chemotherapy and/or radiation may follow depending on tumor type
Watch for headaches, vomiting (especially morning), balance problems, or vision changes
The child may need rehabilitation (physio, OT, speech) after surgery
Long-term follow-up is essential — regular MRI scans will be needed for years
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric neurosurgery and neuro-oncology center
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit urgently for raised intracranial pressure, new neurological deficits, posterior fossa syndrome, or for surgical planning
notes: Treatment is tumor-type specific (medulloblastoma, glioma, ependymoma). Avoid CSI in children <3y; use chemotherapy-only protocols for infants.
guideline_changes: TTFields (tumor-treating fields) emerging for pediatric brain tumors. WNT-subtype MB trials de-escalating therapy

first_line_drugs.notes: Doxorubicin 25 mg/m2, bleomycin 10 U/m2, vinblastine 6 mg/m2, dacarbazine 375 mg/m2 IV q2wk; 4-6 cycles
BV 1.8 mg/kg IV d1 + doxorubicin, vincristine, etoposide, pred, cyclophosphamide q3wk x5 cycles
Low-dose 21 Gy ISRT for slow early responders or bulky disease; response-adapted approach

second_line_drugs.notes: 3 mg/kg IV q3wk for R/R HL; used with brentuximab +/- bendamustine
1.8 mg/kg IV q3wk for up to 16 cycles for R/R HL
Ifosfamide, carboplatin, etoposide for relapsed HL prior to autologous HSCT

counselling: Hodgkin lymphoma has a cure rate above 90% in children with proper treatment
Treatment involves chemotherapy cycles given every few weeks for several months
Side effects like nausea, hair loss, and tiredness are temporary and manageable
Maintain good nutrition and report fever or breathing difficulty immediately
Long-term follow-up after cure is important to monitor for late effects of treatment
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric oncology center; urgent if compressive symptoms (SVC syndrome, airway compromise)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for initiation of chemotherapy, SVC syndrome, airway compromise, neutropenic fever, or severe treatment toxicity
notes: Cure rate 90-95%. BV+AVEPC achieved 92% 3-year EFS vs 82.5% with standard chemo. Minimize radiation to reduce late effects in children.
guideline_changes: Brentuximab vedotin + AVEPC is new standard for high-risk pediatric HL (FDA approved 2022)

first_line_drugs.notes: Rituximab 375 mg/m2 IV + cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisone per BFM/LMB protocol
ALL-like regimen: induction, consolidation, maintenance x2 years total with VCR, pred, Asp, MTX, 6-MP

second_line_drugs.notes: Short intensive pulses q3-4wk x6-8 months; French LMB protocol with rituximab for B-NHL
1.8 mg/kg IV q3wk for CD30+ anaplastic large cell lymphoma; up to 16 cycles

counselling: NHL in children responds well to chemotherapy — cure rates exceed 80%
Treatment is intensive and requires close hospital follow-up
Watch for rapid swelling, breathing difficulty, or abdominal pain and seek emergency care
Good nutrition and hydration are critical during treatment
The child may need to miss school during intensive phases — coordinate with the school
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric oncology center; emergency if airway/bowel obstruction or tumor lysis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit at diagnosis for chemotherapy initiation, emergency admission for tumor lysis syndrome, bowel obstruction, or respiratory compromise
notes: NHL subtype determines protocol: Burkitt/DLBCL use short intensive chemo, LBL uses ALL-like 2-year regimen. Cure rate exceeds 80% overall.
guideline_changes: Rituximab now routinely added to chemotherapy for mature B-cell NHL in children
