first_line_drugs.notes: Vincristine 1.5 mg/m2 IV wkly, dexamethasone 6 mg/m2/day PO, PEG-asparaginase, daunorubicin 25 mg/m2
15 mcg/m2/day continuous IV infusion x28 days per cycle; now standard for B-ALL consolidation
Age-adjusted IT triple therapy for CNS prophylaxis; frequency per protocol risk group
6-mercaptopurine 75 mg/m2 PO daily + methotrexate 20 mg/m2 PO weekly x2 years total

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.2-5x10^6 CAR-T cells/kg IV single dose for R/R B-ALL <=25y; requires lymphodepletion
Approved 2024 for R/R CD22+ B-ALL >=1y; given IV per cycle per protocol
650 mg/m2 IV daily x5 days for T-ALL; added to consolidation in T-ALL protocols

investigations.name: Bone marrow aspirate with flow cytometry

counselling: ALL is the most common childhood cancer and has a very high cure rate (>90%) with proper treatment
Treatment lasts about 2-3 years — maintaining regular hospital visits is critical
Watch for fever, unusual bleeding, or bone pain and report immediately
The child will be immunosuppressed — avoid crowded places and sick contacts
Good nutrition and emotional support are essential throughout treatment
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric oncology center with ALL protocol capability
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit at diagnosis for induction chemotherapy, for neutropenic fever, tumor lysis syndrome, or any life-threatening complication
notes: Risk stratification by genetics, MRD, and response guides therapy intensity. Blinatumomab in consolidation improved 3-year DFS to 96% (AALL1731).
guideline_changes: Blinatumomab added to frontline consolidation (COG AALL1731, 2024). Inotuzumab approved 2024 for R/R ALL

first_line_drugs.notes: Ara-C 100-200 mg/m2/day CI x7 days + daunorubicin 50 mg/m2 IV x3 days (7+3); 2 cycles
100-150 mg/m2/day IV x5 days; added to induction as ADE regimen
3 mg/m2 IV (max 4.5 mg) on days 1,4,7 of induction; for CD33+ AML
Age-adjusted IT Ara-C for CNS prophylaxis; given during each chemotherapy cycle

second_line_drugs.notes: First unit dose on days 1,3,5 IV; COG AAML1421 showed 81% response; for high-risk AML
Under investigation in APAL2020D trial for R/R AML; promising early results in children
For high-risk AML or poor response after induction; MSD or MUD donor preferred

investigations.name: Bone marrow aspirate with flow cytometry

counselling: AML treatment is intensive but most children enter remission after the first phase
The child will need to stay in the hospital for weeks during each chemotherapy cycle
Infections are the biggest risk — strict hand hygiene and avoiding sick contacts is critical
Report fever immediately — even 38C needs urgent medical attention during treatment
Emotional and psychological support for the child and family is very important
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric oncology center with AML protocol capability and HSCT access
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit at diagnosis for induction chemotherapy; admit for neutropenic fever, bleeding, or any complication during treatment
notes: Over 85% of children enter remission after induction. Risk stratification by cytogenetics and MRD guides consolidation and HSCT decisions.
guideline_changes: Venetoclax being evaluated in frontline pediatric AML (APAL2020D trial). CPX-351 showed 81% ORR in COG study

first_line_drugs.notes: 3-6 mg/kg/day elemental iron PO divided 1-2 doses on empty stomach; continue 2-3 months after Hb normalizes
3-6 mg/kg/day elemental iron PO divided 1-2 doses; take with vitamin C to enhance absorption

second_line_drugs.notes: 3-5 mg/kg/day elemental iron PO with meals in 1-2 doses; better GI tolerance
15 mg/kg IV single dose (max 750 mg); for oral iron failure or malabsorption

investigations.name: Serum ferritin
investigations.indication: Confirm iron deficiency (low ferritin is diagnostic)

investigations.name: Stool for occult blood
investigations.indication: Rule out GI blood loss in older children

investigations.name: Hb electrophoresis
investigations.indication: Rule out thalassemia trait if not responding to iron

counselling: Give iron drops/syrup on empty stomach with orange juice or lemon water for better absorption
Avoid giving milk, tea, or calcium with iron — wait at least 2 hours
Iron may cause black stools and mild stomach upset — this is normal
Continue iron for 2-3 months after hemoglobin normalizes to replenish stores
Include iron-rich foods: jaggery, green leafy vegetables, eggs, ragi, dates
referral_criteria: Refer to hematologist if no rise in Hb after 4-6 weeks of adequate oral iron, or if thalassemia trait or chronic blood loss suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if Hb <5 g/dL, signs of heart failure, severe pallor with tachycardia, or need for blood transfusion
notes: Avoid milk/calcium with iron doses. Treatment typically 3-6 months total to replenish stores; recheck ferritin at 3 months.
guideline_changes: 2024 EHA consensus recommends lower-frequency dosing (alternate-day) for better absorption

first_line_drugs.notes: 1000 mcg IM x1, then 1000 mcg weekly x6 weeks; infants <2y: 1000 mcg IM then 250 mcg/wk x6wk
1000 mcg PO daily x4 months for mild-moderate nutritional deficiency

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg IM 3x/week x2 weeks then 1 mg IM every 3 months; preferred in some guidelines
1000 mcg sublingual daily; alternative to oral in children with poor compliance

investigations.name: Serum methylmalonic acid
investigations.indication: More sensitive marker for B12 deficiency

counselling: B12 injections are important — do not skip scheduled doses
Include B12-rich foods: milk, curd, eggs, paneer, and fortified cereals
Strict vegetarian families should give B12 supplements to children regularly
Neurological symptoms (tingling, weakness) should improve within weeks of treatment
If the cause is pernicious anemia, lifelong B12 injections will be needed
referral_criteria: Refer to hematologist if neurological symptoms present, if anemia does not respond to B12 within 4 weeks, or if pernicious anemia suspected (rare in children)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe anemia (Hb <5 g/dL), neurological compromise, or severe pancytopenia
notes: Start B12 at least 10-14 days before folic acid if combined deficiency to prevent neurological deterioration. Lifelong therapy if pernicious anemia.

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg PO daily for 4 months; infants 0.1 mg/day, children 1-3y 0.15 mg/day, >4y up to 1 mg/day
Increase folate-rich foods: green leafy vegetables, legumes, fortified cereals

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg IM/IV/SC daily if oral absorption impaired; same duration as oral
5 mg PO daily for 4 months if antiepileptic drug-induced deficiency

investigations.name: CBC with reticulocyte count
investigations.indication: Confirm megaloblastic anemia and monitor response

investigations.name: Peripheral blood smear
investigations.indication: Look for megaloblastic changes, hypersegmented neutrophils

counselling: Give folic acid tablet daily on empty stomach for best absorption
Include green leafy vegetables (palak, methi), dal, and fortified cereals in diet
Improvement in energy and appetite expected within 2-3 weeks
Complete the full 4-month course even if the child feels better
Follow up with blood test at 1 month and 3 months to check progress
referral_criteria: Refer to hematologist if anemia does not respond to folic acid after 4 weeks, or if pancytopenia or hemolysis suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if Hb <5 g/dL with cardiac symptoms, or if severe pancytopenia requiring transfusion
notes: Rule out B12 deficiency before starting folate to avoid masking neurological damage. Reticulocytosis expected by day 3-4, anemia resolves in 4-8 weeks.

first_line_drugs.notes: 5-25 mg PO daily for B6 deficiency anemia; higher doses if INH-induced
0.1 mg/kg/day PO elemental copper for copper-deficiency anemia; max 3 mg/day

second_line_drugs.notes: 100-300 mg PO daily for scurvy-related anemia; give with iron if coexistent deficiency
Age-appropriate multivitamin PO daily when multiple nutritional deficiencies suspected

investigations.name: Serum B6, copper, vitamin C levels

counselling: Give the prescribed vitamin/mineral supplement daily as directed
Include a variety of foods: fruits, vegetables, milk, eggs, and whole grains
If the child is a strict vegetarian, supplementation is especially important
Improvement in appetite and energy expected within 2-4 weeks
Complete the full course and get a follow-up blood test as advised
referral_criteria: Refer to hematologist if anemia does not respond to supplementation in 4-6 weeks, or if multiple cytopenias present
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe anemia (Hb <5 g/dL), neurological symptoms from B12/copper deficiency, or need for parenteral supplementation
notes: D53 includes anemia from B6, copper, and other nutrient deficiencies. Identify specific deficiency with targeted labs before supplementing.

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mL/kg PRBC IV q2-4wk to maintain pre-transfusion Hb 9-10 g/dL in TDT
20-40 mg/kg/day PO once daily for iron chelation; start after 20-25 transfusions or ferritin >1000
1 mg PO daily to support erythropoiesis

second_line_drugs.notes: 20-40 mg/kg/day SC infusion over 8-12h, 5-7 days/wk; gold standard chelator, poor compliance
75-100 mg/kg/day PO divided TID; best for cardiac iron overload; risk of agranulocytosis
Curative; best outcomes if MSD donor and low Pesaro risk class; perform early in disease
Gene therapy; one-time autologous HSCT with CRISPR-edited cells; approved >=12y TDT

investigations.name: Cardiac MRI T2*
investigations.indication: Detect cardiac iron loading (annually from age 10)

counselling: Regular blood transfusions every 2-4 weeks are essential to maintain adequate hemoglobin
Iron chelation medicine must be taken daily to prevent iron overload in the heart and liver
Avoid iron-rich supplements and iron-fortified foods — the child already has excess iron from transfusions
Annual cardiac MRI (T2*) and liver iron monitoring are critical from age 10
Genetic counselling is important for the family — both parents are carriers
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric hematologist at a thalassemia center; refer for HSCT evaluation if matched sibling donor available
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for transfusion reactions, cardiac decompensation from iron overload, severe infection, or HSCT
notes: Iron chelation is critical; cardiac iron overload is the leading cause of death in TDT. MRI-guided chelation (T2*) recommended for monitoring.
guideline_changes: Casgevy (CRISPR gene therapy) approved Jan 2024 for TDT. Luspatercept under pediatric trial

first_line_drugs.notes: Start 20 mg/kg/day PO from age 9 months; escalate to MTD ~25 mg/kg/day; monitor CBC q4-8wk
<3y: 125 mg PO BID; 3-5y: 250 mg PO BID; pneumococcal prophylaxis from 2 months
1 mg PO daily to support erythropoiesis

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mL/kg PRBC q3-4wk to maintain HbS <30%; for stroke prevention or severe disease
5-15 g PO BID based on weight; FDA-approved >=5y to reduce vaso-occlusive crises
Gene therapy; one-time autologous HSCT with CRISPR-edited CD34+ cells; approved >=12y
Gene therapy; one-time autologous HSCT with lentiviral-modified CD34+ cells; approved >=12y

investigations.name: Hb electrophoresis/HPLC
investigations.indication: Diagnostic confirmation and Hb F monitoring

investigations.name: Transcranial Doppler (TCD)
investigations.indication: Annual screening for stroke risk, ages 2-16

counselling: Give hydroxyurea daily as prescribed — it significantly reduces pain crises and complications
Keep the child well-hydrated at all times; offer water frequently especially in hot weather
Avoid extreme cold, dehydration, high altitude, and vigorous exhausting exercise
Seek emergency care immediately for fever >38.5C, severe pain, chest pain, or sudden weakness
Penicillin prophylaxis must be given daily until at least age 5 — do not skip doses
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric hematologist at a sickle cell center; urgently for stroke symptoms, acute chest syndrome, or priapism
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for vaso-occlusive crisis not responding to home management, fever with ANC <500, acute chest syndrome, stroke, splenic sequestration, or aplastic crisis
notes: Hydroxyurea is disease-modifying first-line for all children with SCD from age 9 months. Voxelotor was withdrawn in 2024 due to safety concerns.
guideline_changes: Voxelotor withdrawn Sept 2024 (safety signal). Gene therapies Casgevy and Lyfgenia approved Dec 2023

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg PO daily for chronic hemolysis (hereditary spherocytosis, elliptocytosis)
10-15 mL/kg PRBC IV for Hb <7-8 g/dL or symptomatic anemia; minimize transfusion burden

second_line_drugs.notes: Consider for moderate-severe HS after age 6; vaccinate against encapsulated organisms pre-op
For neonatal jaundice in HS; exchange transfusion if bilirubin reaches critical levels

investigations.name: CBC with reticulocyte count
investigations.indication: Baseline and monitor for aplastic crisis

counselling: Hereditary hemolytic anemia is a lifelong condition but manageable with regular monitoring
Give folic acid daily to help the body make new red blood cells
Watch for sudden pallor, dark urine, or jaundice — this may mean a hemolytic crisis
Parvovirus B19 infection can cause aplastic crisis — seek care for sudden worsening of pallor
Annual ultrasound checks for gallstones are recommended from school age onwards
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric hematologist for diagnosis confirmation, splenectomy evaluation, and management of severe disease
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for aplastic crisis (parvovirus B19), severe hemolytic crisis with Hb <7 g/dL, symptomatic gallstones, or splenectomy
notes: Hereditary spherocytosis is the most common cause; severity ranges from mild to transfusion-dependent. Annual monitoring with CBC and ultrasound for gallstones.

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) for warm AIHA; taper slowly over 6-12 months after response
1 mg PO daily to support erythropoiesis during active hemolysis

second_line_drugs.notes: 375 mg/m2 IV weekly x4 doses for steroid-refractory AIHA; 80-90% response rate
600 mg/m2 PO BID as steroid-sparing agent for relapsed warm AIHA
1 g/kg IV x2 days for acute severe hemolysis as temporizing measure
Consider in children >5y refractory to rituximab; effective in ~66% of warm AIHA

investigations.name: Peripheral blood smear
investigations.indication: Spherocytes in warm AIHA, agglutination in cold AIHA

counselling: Your child's body is destroying its own red blood cells — this needs careful treatment
Take prednisolone exactly as prescribed; do not stop suddenly — it must be tapered slowly
Watch for dark urine (cola-colored), increasing pallor, or jaundice and report immediately
Folic acid supports new red blood cell production — give daily
Follow-up blood tests are needed every 1-2 weeks initially to monitor response
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric hematologist for all cases; urgent if Hb dropping rapidly or steroid-refractory
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if Hb <7 g/dL, rapidly falling Hb, cardiac symptoms, or need for transfusion/IVIG
notes: Warm AIHA responds to steroids in 70-85% of children. Transfuse least-incompatible RBCs for life-threatening anemia; do not withhold transfusion.

first_line_drugs.notes: 40 mg/kg/day IV x4 days; premedicate with methylprednisolone and diphenhydramine
<12y: 7.5 mg/kg/dose PO q12h; >=12y: 5 mg/kg/dose PO q12h; target trough 150-250 ng/mL
>=12y: 150 mg PO daily; 6-11y: 75 mg daily; 2-5y: 2.5 mg/kg/day; start day 1 with ATG x6 months
First-line if matched sibling donor available; BM graft preferred over PBSC

second_line_drugs.notes: If no MSD and IST fails or age <20y; use BM graft with ATG conditioning
Consider if no MSD/MUD; post-transplant cyclophosphamide-based GvHD prophylaxis

investigations.name: Bone marrow aspirate and biopsy
investigations.indication: Confirm aplastic anemia, rule out MDS/leukemia

counselling: This is a serious condition affecting the bone marrow — regular hospital visits are essential
Avoid contact sports, crowded places, and anyone with infections
Report fever immediately — even a mild fever needs urgent medical attention
The child may need blood and platelet transfusions regularly
Treatment with IST or transplant offers good outcomes in children — discuss options with the hematologist
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric hematologist/oncologist at a tertiary center with HSCT capability
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for neutropenic fever, active bleeding, Hb <7 g/dL requiring transfusion, initiation of ATG therapy, or HSCT
notes: Horse ATG is superior to rabbit ATG for pediatric SAA. Add eltrombopag to IST based on recent trial data showing improved response rates.
guideline_changes: 2024 Delphi consensus recommends horse ATG over rabbit ATG; eltrombopag added to frontline IST

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mL/kg IV for Hb <7 g/dL or symptomatic anemia; irradiated if immunocompromised
10-15 mL/kg IV for platelets <10,000/mcL or active bleeding; single donor preferred
Cefepime 50 mg/kg IV q8h or piperacillin-tazobactam 100 mg/kg IV q8h for neutropenic fever

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-10 mcg/kg/day SC for severe neutropenia (ANC <500); continue until ANC recovery
0.4 g/kg IV monthly if hypogammaglobulinemia with recurrent infections

investigations.name: Viral panel (EBV, CMV, HIV, Parvovirus B19)

counselling: Pancytopenia means all blood counts are low — the child is at risk for infection and bleeding
Avoid crowded places, raw/uncooked food, and contact with sick people
Report fever, unusual bleeding, or bruising immediately
Blood product transfusions are supportive while the cause is investigated
The treatment depends on identifying the underlying cause — investigations are essential
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric hematologist — bone marrow biopsy is mandatory to determine cause
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all cases for workup; urgently if neutropenic fever, active bleeding, or Hb <7 g/dL
notes: Pancytopenia requires urgent bone marrow biopsy to determine etiology. Supportive care with transfusions and infection management while awaiting diagnosis.

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mg PO daily trial for sideroblastic anemia; assess response at 2-3 months
10-15 mL/kg PRBC IV for symptomatic anemia (Hb <7 g/dL); transfuse over 2-4 hours

second_line_drugs.notes: 20-40 mg/kg/day SC infusion over 8-12h, 5 days/week for iron overload from chronic transfusions
50-300 IU/kg SC 3x/week for anemia of chronic disease/renal failure; titrate to Hb 10-12 g/dL

investigations.name: CBC with reticulocyte count
investigations.indication: Classify anemia and assess marrow response

counselling: This type of anemia needs careful investigation to find the exact cause
Blood transfusions may be needed temporarily until the cause is treated
Report any new symptoms: fever, bleeding, bone pain, or increasing weakness
Keep all follow-up appointments — blood counts need regular monitoring
If on long-term transfusions, iron overload screening will be done
referral_criteria: Refer to hematologist for all cases — specialized evaluation required to determine etiology and guide therapy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if Hb <7 g/dL with symptoms, active bleeding, or need for urgent transfusion or bone marrow biopsy
notes: D64 covers heterogeneous anemias; identify underlying cause before treatment. Pyridoxine trial warranted for all sideroblastic anemias.

first_line_drugs.notes: Prophylaxis: 25-40 IU/kg IV 3x/week; target trough >3-5%; dose=wt(kg)x desired rise(%)x0.5
3 mg/kg SC weekly x4 weeks loading, then 1.5 mg/kg SC weekly; for severe hemophilia A
Minor: 10-15 IU/kg IV; moderate: 25 IU/kg IV; severe: 50 IU/kg IV to reach 80-100% level

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.3 mcg/kg IV/SC (max 20 mcg) for mild hemophilia A; test dose to confirm response
25 mg/kg PO TID or 10 mg/kg IV TID as adjunct for mucosal bleeding
300 mg SC loading then 150 mg SC weekly; approved 2024 for >=12y without inhibitors

investigations.name: aPTT
investigations.indication: Screening test — prolonged in hemophilia A

counselling: Hemophilia requires lifelong management — build a strong relationship with the hemophilia treatment center
Avoid aspirin and NSAIDs; use paracetamol for pain
Apply RICE (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation) for joint/muscle bleeds and infuse factor early
The child should wear a medical alert bracelet at all times
Encourage swimming and cycling; avoid contact sports like football and wrestling
referral_criteria: Refer to hemophilia treatment center for all newly diagnosed cases; urgent referral for inhibitor development or life-threatening bleed
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for head injury/intracranial bleed, GI bleed, airway bleed, compartment syndrome, or any bleed not responding to home factor infusion
notes: Start prophylaxis before age 3 to prevent arthropathy. Children have shorter FVIII half-life and may need more frequent dosing than adults.
guideline_changes: ISTH 2024 now recommends emicizumab as alternative first-line prophylaxis alongside FVIII concentrates

first_line_drugs.notes: Prophylaxis: 25-40 IU/kg IV 2x/week; dose=wt(kg)x desired rise(%)x1.4; target trough >3-5%
50-75 IU/kg IV every 7-14 days; higher trough levels at lower frequency
Minor: 20-30 IU/kg IV; moderate: 40-60 IU/kg IV; severe: 100-140 IU/kg IV

second_line_drugs.notes: 25 mg/kg PO TID or 10 mg/kg IV TID as adjunct for mucosal bleeding
2 mg SC monthly; anti-antithrombin siRNA for hemophilia B with/without inhibitors

investigations.name: aPTT
investigations.indication: Screening test — prolonged in hemophilia B

counselling: Hemophilia B is lifelong — regular prophylaxis prevents joint damage
Give factor IX infusion on schedule even if the child seems well
Avoid aspirin; use paracetamol for pain relief
Encourage non-contact physical activities to keep joints healthy
Always carry the hemophilia card and inform teachers, dentists, and doctors about the condition
referral_criteria: Refer to hemophilia treatment center for diagnosis, prophylaxis planning, and inhibitor monitoring
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for intracranial, GI, or airway bleeds, surgery, or factor IX infusion for major bleeds not manageable at home
notes: Children <15y require higher FIX doses due to faster clearance (use 1.4 IU/kg correction factor). Avoid DDAVP as it is ineffective in hemophilia B.
guideline_changes: Extended half-life FIX products (rIX-FP) now preferred allowing once-weekly prophylaxis
snomed_display: Hereditary factor IX deficiency disease with inhibitor

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mL/kg IV for acute bleeding with prolonged PT/aPTT; repeat q8-12h as needed
1-2 units/10 kg IV for fibrinogen <100 mg/dL; each unit raises fibrinogen ~50-100 mg/dL
1-5 mg IV/PO for vitamin K-dependent factor deficiency; neonates 1 mg IM at birth

second_line_drugs.notes: 25 mg/kg PO TID or 10 mg/kg IV TID for mucosal bleeding as antifibrinolytic adjunct
10-15 mL/kg IV if concurrent thrombocytopenia with DIC; target platelets >50,000 if bleeding

counselling: This condition causes the blood to not clot properly — report any unusual bleeding immediately
Avoid trauma-prone activities and contact sports until the doctor advises otherwise
If the child has a bleeding episode, apply pressure and seek immediate medical help
Take all medications exactly as prescribed — vitamin K is important if prescribed
Follow up regularly for blood clotting tests
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric hematologist for definitive diagnosis and management of all coagulation defects
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for active significant bleeding, DIC, need for FFP/cryoprecipitate infusion, or surgical procedures
notes: Treat the underlying cause (e.g., sepsis, liver disease) as the cornerstone of DIC management. FFP should only be given for active bleeding, not isolated lab derangements.

first_line_drugs.notes: Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO q6-8h PRN for joint pain; rest and hydration; self-limiting in 94%
15 mg/kg PO q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day) for mild pain if NSAIDs contraindicated

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) x1-2 weeks then taper; for severe abdominal pain or nephritis
600 mg/m2 PO BID for IgAV nephritis refractory to steroids; specialist supervision

counselling: HSP is usually a self-limiting illness that resolves in 4-6 weeks
The rash on legs and buttocks looks alarming but is not dangerous by itself
Give the child plenty of fluids and rest during the active phase
Watch for belly pain, bloody stools, or swollen joints and report immediately
Monthly urine tests are needed for 6 months to check for kidney involvement
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist if proteinuria >1 g/day, nephrotic-range proteinuria, rising creatinine, or persistent hematuria beyond 3 months
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe abdominal pain (intussusception risk), GI bleeding, nephrotic syndrome, or inability to tolerate oral intake
notes: Self-resolves in most children within 4-6 weeks. Monitor urinalysis monthly for 6 months for renal involvement; nephritis is the main long-term concern.

first_line_drugs.notes: Watchful waiting for mild ITP (no significant bleeding); treat based on symptoms not platelet count
0.8-1 g/kg IV single dose for significant bleeding; raises platelets within 24-48h
2-4 mg/kg/day PO (max 120 mg) x4 days or 1-2 mg/kg/day x1-2 weeks then taper

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-10 mcg/kg SC weekly; start 1 mcg/kg, titrate by 1 mcg/kg/wk to target plt >50,000
1-5y: 25 mg PO daily; 6-17y: 50 mg PO daily; titrate to maintain plt >50,000
375 mg/m2 IV weekly x4 doses for chronic/refractory ITP; response in 60-80%

investigations.name: CBC with peripheral smear
investigations.indication: Confirm isolated thrombocytopenia, rule out pseudothrombocytopenia

investigations.name: Direct Coombs test
investigations.indication: Rule out concurrent autoimmune hemolytic anemia

counselling: Most children with ITP recover fully within a few months without treatment
Avoid contact sports, rough play, and activities that risk head injury while platelets are low
Do not give aspirin or ibuprofen — use paracetamol only for pain/fever
Watch for unusual bleeding: nosebleeds lasting >20 min, blood in stool/urine, or new large bruises
Monthly blood tests will be needed to track platelet count recovery
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric hematologist if platelets remain <20,000 beyond 3 months, significant bleeding despite treatment, or chronic ITP (>12 months)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for life-threatening bleeding (intracranial, GI), mucosal bleeding with platelets <20,000, or for IVIG/steroid initiation in severe bleeding
notes: Most pediatric ITP resolves within 12 months. Splenectomy is rarely indicated and only after age 5 in life-threatening refractory cases.
guideline_changes: 2024 AIEOP recommends TPO-RAs (romiplostim) as preferred second-line over rituximab

first_line_drugs.notes: Congenital: 5-10 mcg/kg/day SC; cyclic/idiopathic: 1-5 mcg/kg/day SC; adjust to ANC >1000
Cefepime 50 mg/kg IV q8h for neutropenic fever (ANC <500 + temp >=38.3C); start empirically

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg SC single dose (max 6 mg) for chemotherapy-induced neutropenia; one dose per cycle
0.4 g/kg IV monthly for autoimmune neutropenia with recurrent severe infections

investigations.name: Bone marrow aspirate
investigations.indication: If congenital or persistent severe neutropenia

counselling: Low neutrophil count makes the child vulnerable to infections — avoid crowded places
Wash hands frequently and maintain good oral hygiene
Seek emergency care immediately for any fever above 38C — do not wait
Avoid raw/uncooked food and unpasteurized milk during severe neutropenia
G-CSF injection helps the body make more white blood cells — give as directed
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric hematologist if congenital neutropenia suspected, ANC persistently <500, or recurrent serious infections
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for neutropenic fever (ANC <500 + temp >=38.3C), severe oral/perianal infection, or suspected serious bacterial infection
notes: Monitor CBC twice weekly during G-CSF initiation. Congenital severe neutropenia (Kostmann) requires long-term G-CSF; watch for MDS/AML transformation.

diagnosis_name: Hyperleukocytosis with / without Tumor Lysis Syndrome

first_line_drugs.notes: Aggressive hydration to maintain urine output and reduce blood viscosity / tumor lysis risk. In asymptomatic hyperleukocytosis: start 2 times maintenance IV fluids. In symptomatic hyperleukocytosis / TLC >2-3 lakhs/cc: start 3-4 times maintenance or approximately 3000 mL/m2/day. Monitor for fluid overload.
For tumor lysis prophylaxis in hyperleukocytosis / high uric acid risk. Dose as per institutional pediatric oncology protocol; start early with hydration.
Use in high-risk tumor lysis syndrome or significant hyperuricemia; preferred when uric acid is elevated or rapid control is needed.
Avoid routine PRBC transfusion before cytoreduction if hyperviscosity is present. Use platelet transfusion for severe thrombocytopenia / bleeding as clinically indicated.

second_line_drugs.notes: Use according to leukemia subtype and oncology protocol for leukoreduction if symptomatic hyperleukocytosis or rapidly rising counts are present.
Urgent leukapheresis or exchange transfusion may be considered in symptomatic hyperleukocytosis with leukostasis or exchange-transfusion-responsive situations.
Consider if tumor lysis syndrome causes refractory hyperkalemia, severe hyperphosphatemia, symptomatic hypocalcemia, fluid overload, or acute kidney injury.

investigations.name: Complete Blood Count with Differential and Platelets
investigations.indication: Diagnosis of hyperleukocytosis and baseline monitoring

investigations.name: Peripheral Smear / Bone Marrow / Flow Cytometry as indicated
investigations.indication: Confirm leukemia subtype and guide cytoreduction

investigations.name: Serum Uric Acid
investigations.indication: Tumor lysis syndrome diagnosis and monitoring

investigations.name: Serum Potassium, Phosphorus, Calcium, Creatinine, Urea
investigations.indication: Cairo-Bishop laboratory TLS assessment and AKI monitoring

investigations.name: Chest X-ray / Neuroimaging as clinically indicated

counselling: This is an oncologic emergency and needs urgent hospital treatment. — यह कैंसर से जुड़ी आपातकालीन स्थिति है और तुरंत अस्पताल में इलाज जरूरी है।

Very high white cell count can block blood flow and affect lungs, brain, kidneys, and heart. — बहुत ज्यादा सफेद रक्त कोशिकाएं खून के बहाव को रोक सकती हैं और फेफड़ों, दिमाग, किडनी और दिल को नुकसान पहुंचा सकती हैं।
Strong hydration and repeated blood tests are required to prevent tumor lysis complications. — ट्यूमर लाइसिस की जटिलताओं से बचने के लिए पर्याप्त पानी और बार-बार खून की जांच जरूरी है।
Breathlessness, drowsiness, seizures, reduced urine output, or chest symptoms must be reported immediately. — सांस फूलना, सुस्ती, दौरे, पेशाब कम होना या सीने में तकलीफ होने पर तुरंत बताएं।
Treatment may include cytoreduction, ICU care, and specialist oncology management. — इलाज में कोशिका कम करने की प्रक्रिया, ICU देखभाल और कैंसर विशेषज्ञ का इलाज शामिल हो सकता है।
warning_signs: Sudden weakness, visual symptoms, or coma
referral_criteria: Immediate referral to pediatric oncologist / intensivist for any hyperleukocytosis with symptoms of leukostasis, TLS, neurological signs, respiratory distress, arrhythmia, or renal dysfunction.
hospitalisation_criteria: All patients with hyperleukocytosis, suspected leukemia with TLC >100000/uL, clinical leukostasis, laboratory TLS, or need for IV hydration / cytoreduction / ICU monitoring require admission.
notes: Hyperleukocytosis is usually defined as WBC/TLC >100000/uL. Leukostasis symptoms may involve pulmonary, CNS, genitourinary, gastrointestinal, and cardiovascular systems. Cairo-Bishop laboratory TLS is present if 2 or more occur within 3 days before or 7 days after therapy: uric acid >=8 mg/dL or 25% rise, potassium >=6 mEq/L or 25% rise, phosphorus >=6.5 mg/dL or 25% rise, calcium <=7 mg/dL or 25% fall. Clinical TLS includes creatinine >=1.5 times upper limit normal, cardiac arrhythmia / sudden death, or seizures.
source: Provided pediatric hyperleukocytosis and tumor lysis syndrome protocol
expected_course: Stabilization depends on early hydration, cytoreduction, and prevention of TLS. Lab abnormalities may worsen over the first few days of treatment and require frequent reassessment.
key_clinical_points: Hyperleukocytosis with leukostasis is an emergency requiring rapid hydration and oncology input.
Tumor lysis syndrome can occur before or after therapy and must be anticipated early.
Avoid unnecessary PRBC transfusion before leukoreduction in severe hyperviscosity unless clearly indicated.

severity_assessment.mild: High TLC without symptoms of leukostasis or TLS; hemodynamically stable.
severity_assessment.moderate: Hyperleukocytosis with rising uric acid / electrolytes or mild respiratory / neurological symptoms.
severity_assessment.severe: Symptomatic leukostasis, TLC often >2-3 lakhs/cc, CNS or pulmonary compromise, arrhythmia, AKI, or clinical tumor lysis syndrome.

monitoring_parameters.parameter: Uric acid, potassium, phosphorus, calcium, creatinine
Fluid balance and signs of overload

first_line_drugs.notes: 400-600 mg/kg IV q3-4wks; individualize trough target based on infection frequency
100-200 mg/kg SC weekly; preferred for home therapy; fewer systemic side effects
20 mg/kg/day PO for sinopulmonary infections pending Ig replacement initiation

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO weekly (max 500mg) for recurrent lower respiratory infections
HyQvia: rHuPH20 + IgG 10% SC monthly; allows higher volumes per site
Airway clearance techniques for established bronchiectasis; non-pharmacologic

counselling: Your child's body does not make enough antibodies so regular immunoglobulin replacement is needed
IVIG is given monthly at hospital or SCIG can be given weekly at home
Your child can attend school and play normally; inform school about the condition
Report all infections promptly for early treatment
Annual lung function tests help detect any lung damage early
referral_criteria: All cases managed by pediatric immunologist; pulmonology referral if recurrent respiratory infections or bronchiectasis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe or unusual infections, initial IVIG therapy, or complications such as pneumonia or sepsis
notes: Antibody deficiency is the most common primary immunodeficiency group. Regular monitoring of lung function and CT chest for bronchiectasis is essential.
guideline_changes: 2024 review supports individualized trough targets rather than fixed 500mg/dL threshold

first_line_drugs.notes: Definitive cure; best outcomes if <3.5mo age; HLA-matched sibling preferred; ~97% survival
400-600 mg/kg IV q3-4wks; maintain trough IgG >500mg/dL; bridge to HSCT
5 mg/kg/day TMP PO divided BID for PJP prophylaxis until immune reconstitution

second_line_drugs.notes: Autologous CD34+ cell modification; for ADA-SCID and SCID-X1 without matched donor
3-6 mg/kg/day PO daily for fungal prophylaxis pre-transplant
20 mg/kg/dose IV q8h or 20 mg/kg/dose PO QID for HSV/VZV prophylaxis

investigations.name: Lymphocyte subsets (CD3, CD4, CD8, CD19, CD16/56)
investigations.indication: Confirm T-cell, B-cell, NK-cell deficiency pattern

investigations.name: Genetic testing (SCID gene panel)
investigations.indication: Identify specific gene defect for treatment planning

investigations.name: CMV PCR
investigations.indication: Screen for CMV viremia before HSCT

counselling: Your baby has a serious immune deficiency that needs urgent specialized treatment
Keep your baby away from sick people, crowded places, and avoid live vaccines
Hand hygiene is critical for everyone who touches your baby
HSCT (bone marrow transplant) is the cure and best done as early as possible
Your baby will need to take preventive antibiotics and antifungals until the immune system is restored
referral_criteria: Urgent referral to pediatric immunologist and HSCT center at diagnosis; this is a pediatric emergency
hospitalisation_criteria: Most SCID patients require hospitalization from diagnosis through HSCT for protective isolation and infection management
notes: SCID is a pediatric emergency; early HSCT before 3.5 months yields best survival. Avoid live vaccines and use irradiated, CMV-negative blood products.
guideline_changes: Gene therapy trials showing >90% survival for SCID-X1 and ADA-SCID

diagnosis_name: Immunodeficiency associated with other major defects

first_line_drugs.notes: 400-600 mg/kg IV q3-4wks for antibody deficiency component; target trough >500mg/dL
Definitive cure for WAS and severe DiGeorge; HLA-matched donor preferred; early referral
5 mg/kg/day TMP PO divided BID for PJP and bacterial infection prophylaxis

second_line_drugs.notes: For complete DiGeorge (T-cell absent); cultured thymus tissue allograft
Lentiviral CD34+ cell transduction for WAS without matched donor; investigational
For WAS with severe thrombocytopenia and bleeding; irradiated products only
Weight-based dosing PO daily for WAS thrombocytopenia; off-label in children

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Cardiac defects in DiGeorge and other syndromes

counselling: Your child has an immune deficiency combined with other medical problems that need coordinated care
Multiple specialists will be involved in your child's care - keep all appointments
Preventive antibiotics and immunoglobulin may be needed to prevent infections
Avoid live vaccines unless specifically cleared by the immunologist
Bone marrow transplant may be recommended for certain conditions and should be discussed early
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric immunologist at diagnosis; genetics for syndrome-specific evaluation; cardiology for DiGeorge
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for serious infections, pre-HSCT conditioning, thymus transplant, or acute complications of the underlying syndrome
notes: Syndrome-specific management is essential; DiGeorge requires cardiac and endocrine care alongside immunology. WAS patients should receive early curative therapy.
guideline_changes: Gene therapy for Wiskott-Aldrich showing promising long-term outcomes in trials
snomed_display: [X]Immunodeficiency associated with other specified major defects

first_line_drugs.notes: 400-600 mg/kg IV q3-4wks; target trough IgG >500mg/dL (>700 if lung disease)
100-200 mg/kg SC weekly; equivalent efficacy to IVIG; fewer systemic reactions
20 mg/kg/day PO daily for breakthrough sinopulmonary infections if needed

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO weekly (max 500mg) for recurrent respiratory infections
Enteric-coated 3-9mg PO daily for CVID-associated enteropathy
375 mg/m2 IV weekly x4 for CVID-associated autoimmune cytopenias

investigations.name: HRCT chest
investigations.indication: Screen for bronchiectasis and granulomatous disease

counselling: CVID means your child's body cannot make enough antibodies to fight infections
Monthly immunoglobulin infusions (IVIG or weekly SCIG) are needed lifelong
Your child can attend school and lead a normal life with regular treatment
Report any new infections, diarrhea, enlarged lymph nodes, or unexplained weight loss promptly
Annual blood tests and periodic lung scans are needed to monitor for complications
referral_criteria: All cases should be managed by pediatric immunologist; pulmonology for lung complications; gastroenterology for CVID enteropathy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe infections despite replacement therapy, CVID-related autoimmune complications, or for initial IVIG loading doses
notes: Lifelong immunoglobulin replacement is standard of care. Monitor for granulomatous disease, autoimmune cytopenias, and lymphoid malignancy.
guideline_changes: 2024 review supports SCIG as preferred over IVIG for replacement therapy in stable patients

first_line_drugs.notes: 400-600 mg/kg IV q3-4wks if hypogammaglobulinemia confirmed; monitor trough levels
5 mg/kg/day TMP PO divided BID for patients at risk of opportunistic infections
Discontinue/reduce offending drug if secondary; nutritional repletion if malnourished

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO weekly (max 500mg) for recurrent sinopulmonary infections
3-6 mg/kg/day PO daily for fungal prophylaxis in combined defects
5-10 mcg/kg/day SC for congenital or severe neutropenia component

counselling: Your child has an immune problem that makes infections more common or severe
Finding and treating the underlying cause is the first step
Preventive antibiotics or immunoglobulin may be needed depending on the specific deficiency
Good nutrition is important for immune function; ensure a balanced diet
Report any new or unusual infections to your doctor promptly
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric immunologist for evaluation and management plan; address underlying cause
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for serious or opportunistic infections, or for diagnostic workup of complex immunodeficiency
notes: Identify and treat reversible secondary causes first. Monitor immune function q6-12mo and initiate IgG replacement if recurrent infections persist.

first_line_drugs.notes: Neonates: 10-15 mcg/kg/day PO; 1y: 4-6 mcg/kg/day; adolescents: 2-4 mcg/kg/day; take on empty stomach

second_line_drugs.notes: Same mcg/kg dosing as tablet; use liquid formulation in neonates/infants unable to swallow tablets

investigations.name: TSH and free T4
investigations.indication: Primary diagnosis — high TSH, low FT4 confirms hypothyroidism

investigations.name: Thyroid scintigraphy or ultrasound
investigations.indication: Determine etiology in congenital hypothyroidism (ectopic, aplastic, dyshormonogenesis)

investigations.name: Anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies
investigations.indication: Hashimoto thyroiditis — most common cause in older children

investigations.name: Bone age
investigations.indication: Assess degree of hypothyroidism and delayed skeletal maturation

counselling: Your child needs thyroid medicine (levothyroxine) every day — give it on an empty stomach in the morning
Give the tablet crushed in a small amount of water for infants — avoid mixing with soy formula or iron
Never stop the medicine without asking the doctor — stopping can cause developmental delays
Blood tests (TSH, T4) are needed 4-6 weeks after each dose change, then every 3-6 months
For congenital hypothyroidism, early treatment within 2 weeks of birth prevents intellectual disability
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology for all congenital hypothyroidism. Re-evaluation at age 3 (trial off levothyroxine to confirm permanent vs transient). Genetics if syndromic. Developmental pediatrics if developmental concerns
hospitalisation_criteria: Myxedema coma (extremely rare in children) — IV levothyroxine, ICU admission. Severe hypothyroidism with pericardial effusion or cardiac compromise. Newborn with very high TSH >100 requiring urgent treatment
notes: Start treatment within 2 weeks of life for congenital hypothyroidism to prevent cognitive impairment. Recheck TSH/FT4 at 4-6 weeks after any dose change.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1.0 mg/kg/day PO divided TID initially; maintenance 0.2 mg/kg/day; treat >=3 years
0.5-2 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for symptom control (tachycardia, tremor) while awaiting MMI effect

second_line_drugs.notes: Dose calculated per thyroid size; for children >10y failing ATD or with significant adverse effects
Near-total thyroidectomy by experienced surgeon; for large goiter, non-compliance, or ATD failure

investigations.name: TSH, free T4, free T3
investigations.indication: Diagnosis and monitoring — TSH suppressed, FT4/FT3 elevated

investigations.name: CBC with differential
investigations.indication: Baseline before methimazole — monitor for agranulocytosis

counselling: Your child has an overactive thyroid — medicine (methimazole) will be given for at least 3 years
Never use propylthiouracil (PTU) in children — it can cause liver damage
Report immediately if the child develops: fever with sore throat, jaundice, or rash — stop methimazole and come to hospital
Blood tests (TSH, T3, T4, CBC) are needed every 4-6 weeks initially, then every 3 months
Propranolol for fast heart rate is temporary — it will be stopped once thyroid levels normalize
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology mandatory. Nuclear medicine if RAI therapy considered (age >10). Experienced thyroid surgeon if surgery needed. Ophthalmology if Graves ophthalmopathy present
hospitalisation_criteria: Thyroid storm (fever, tachycardia, altered mental status, severe thyrotoxicosis). New-onset atrial fibrillation. Severe heart failure from thyrotoxicosis. Agranulocytosis from methimazole (fever + sore throat + neutrophils <500)
notes: Methimazole is first-line; never use propylthiouracil (PTU) in children due to hepatotoxicity risk. Treatment duration of 3-5+ years recommended; euthyroid usually achieved in 4-6 weeks.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.05-0.1 U/kg/hr IV; start after 1h fluids and K+ >3.0; do NOT bolus
10-20 mL/kg IV bolus over 20-30 min; then replace deficit over 36h with added KCl 40 mmol/L

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.15 U/kg SC q1-2h; for mild DKA without IV access in resource-limited settings
0.5-1 mmol/kg IV over 6h; if phosphate <1 mg/dL; use with KCl to provide total K+ replacement

investigations.name: Venous blood gas (q2-4h)
investigations.indication: Monitor pH and bicarbonate for resolution

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes (q2-4h)
investigations.indication: Monitor potassium — must be >3.0 before starting insulin

investigations.name: HbA1c
investigations.indication: Assess pre-existing glycemic control; distinguishes new vs established T1DM

counselling: Your child has diabetes with a complication called DKA — this is treatable but serious
Insulin and fluids will be given through IV to correct the problem slowly over 24-48 hours
After recovery, your child will need insulin injections every day for life
You will be trained on insulin injection, blood sugar monitoring, and diet management
Never stop insulin on your own — even during illness, insulin must continue (may need to adjust dose)
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology/diabetology mandatory. PICU if severe DKA (pH <7.1) or altered mental status. Neurosurgery immediately if signs of cerebral edema (sudden headache, vomiting, altered consciousness during treatment)
hospitalisation_criteria: All DKA episodes require hospitalisation. Severe DKA (pH <7.1, bicarbonate <5) requires PICU. New-onset T1DM for stabilization and diabetes education. Any DKA in children <5 years (higher cerebral edema risk)
notes: Start insulin only after initial fluid resuscitation and confirmed K+ >3.0 mmol/L. Monitor for cerebral edema especially in children <5y; avoid rapid glucose drops >5 mmol/L/hr.
guideline_changes: ISPAD 2024 updated DKA chapter; low-dose insulin 0.05 U/kg/hr now preferred initial rate

first_line_drugs.notes: Initial 4 hours IV fluids; bolus 10–20 ml/kg over 30–60 min only if shock; then continue deficit + maintenance over 36–48 hrs
Switch after initial 4 hours if available; continue rehydration over 36–48 hrs
Start after 1 hour of fluids at 0.05–0.1 U/kg/hr; no bolus; prepare 0.5–1 U/ml solution and infuse via pump
Add 40 mEq/L once urine output established and K+ normal; if hypokalemia use up to 80 mEq/L with ECG monitoring
Add when blood glucose <250 mg/dL; increase to D10 if glucose <150 mg/dL while acidosis persists

second_line_drugs.notes: Avoid routine use; consider only if severe acidosis (pH <6.9) with hemodynamic compromise
For suspected cerebral edema: Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg or 3% saline 5 ml/kg IV

counselling: Child is critically ill and requires ICU care — बच्चा गंभीर रूप से बीमार है और ICU में इलाज जरूरी है
Insulin and fluids will correct the condition gradually — इंसुलिन और तरल पदार्थ से हालत धीरे-धीरे सुधरेगी
Frequent monitoring is required to prevent complications — जटिलताओं से बचने के लिए बार-बार जांच जरूरी है
Do not stop treatment early even if sugar improves — शुगर ठीक होने पर भी इलाज जल्दी बंद न करें
Long-term diabetes management will be needed — लंबे समय तक मधुमेह (डायबिटीज) का इलाज चलेगा
warning_signs: Sudden drop in heart rate or BP
referral_criteria: Refer to PICU/endocrinologist if severe DKA, altered sensorium, or complications
hospitalisation_criteria: All DKA cases require admission; ICU if moderate–severe DKA
notes: Correct dehydration slowly over 36–48 hrs. Avoid rapid fluid correction to prevent cerebral edema. Do not give insulin bolus.
source: ISPAD Guidelines 2022/2024, IAP Pediatric Endocrine Guidelines
guideline_changes: Lower insulin dose (0.05 U/kg/hr) preferred in young children to reduce cerebral edema risk
expected_course: Improvement in 24–48 hrs; full correction of acidosis within 36–48 hrs
key_clinical_points: Fluid therapy is first priority before insulin
Never give insulin bolus in pediatric DKA
Watch closely for cerebral edema

severity_assessment.mild: pH 7.2–7.3, mild dehydration
severity_assessment.moderate: pH 7.1–7.2, moderate dehydration, tachypnea
severity_assessment.severe: pH <7.1, severe dehydration, altered sensorium

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.2-0.4 U/kg/day SC once daily at bedtime
0.05-0.1 U/kg/dose SC pre-meal, lispro or aspart

second_line_drugs.notes: Automated insulin delivery, preferred for all ages where available - ISPAD 2022

investigations.name: HbA1c
investigations.indication: Every 3 months to assess glycaemic control

counselling: Insulin injections are lifelong - never stop without doctor advice even if child feels well
Check blood sugar at least 4 times daily; use CGM if available
Always carry fast-acting sugar (glucose tablets, juice) for low sugar episodes
Learn to count carbohydrates in food and adjust insulin dose accordingly
Regular follow-up every 3 months for HbA1c; annual eye and kidney screening from 5 years of diabetes
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric endocrinologist for all new diagnoses, recurrent DKA, HbA1c persistently >9%, suspected celiac or thyroid comorbidity, or transition to insulin pump/CGM
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if diabetic ketoacidosis (pH <7.3, bicarbonate <18), new-onset T1D for stabilisation and education, severe hypoglycaemia with seizure or loss of consciousness, or intercurrent illness with inability to manage at home
notes: HbA1c target under 7% for all ages. CGM strongly recommended. TIR 70-180 mg/dL over 70% is a co-primary target.
guideline_changes: Time-in-range over 70% added as co-primary target. Hybrid closed-loop systems now recommended as preferred delivery for all ages.

first_line_drugs.notes: Neonates: 2 ml/kg IV bolus (200 mg/kg), then start infusion
Children: 2–4 ml/kg IV bolus (max D25 preferred), repeat if needed after 5–10 min
Start GIR 4–6 mg/kg/min, titrate up to 8–12 mg/kg/min (up to 20 mg/kg/min in hyperinsulinism)

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.03–0.1 mg/kg IM/IV (max 1 mg) in refractory hypoglycemia or no IV access
2–5 mg/kg IV if adrenal insufficiency suspected

counselling: Child needs urgent glucose correction and monitoring — बच्चे को तुरंत शुगर (ग्लूकोज़) ठीक करना और निगरानी ज़रूरी है।
Ensure frequent feeding after stabilization — स्थिति ठीक होने के बाद बार-बार थोड़ा-थोड़ा दूध/खाना दें।
Avoid prolonged fasting — बच्चे को लंबे समय तक भूखा न रखें।
Give medicines as advised strictly — दवाइयाँ समय पर और बताई गई मात्रा में दें।
Follow-up for cause evaluation is important — कारण की जाँच के लिए फॉलो-अप ज़रूरी है।
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric intensivist/endocrinologist if persistent or recurrent hypoglycemia
hospitalisation_criteria: Symptomatic hypoglycemia, seizures, need for IV glucose infusion, recurrent episodes
notes: Always treat hypoglycemia immediately before confirming cause. Avoid delays.
source: IAP Guidelines 2024, WHO Emergency Care, Pediatric Endocrine Protocols
guideline_changes: Higher GIR targets recommended in hyperinsulinemic states
expected_course: Rapid improvement within minutes after correction; recurrence depends on underlying cause
key_clinical_points: Hypoglycemia is a medical emergency
Check glucose in all sick children
Start treatment immediately without waiting for reports

severity_assessment.mild: Asymptomatic low glucose
severity_assessment.moderate: Irritability, sweating, poor feeding
severity_assessment.severe: Seizures, coma, apnea

diagnosis_name: Congenital adrenogenital disorders associated with enzyme deficiency

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/m2/day PO divided TID; titrate to lowest dose controlling androgens and allowing growth
0.1-0.2 mg PO daily for salt-wasting forms; add NaCl 2-4 g/day in infants

second_line_drugs.notes: 25-100 mg PO BID based on weight (>=4y); adjunct to GC to reduce steroid dose
2-4 mg/m2/day PO; only in post-pubertal patients when HC is insufficient

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes (Na, K)
investigations.indication: Hyponatremia and hyperkalemia in salt-wasting form

investigations.name: Karyotype
investigations.indication: Determine genetic sex in ambiguous genitalia

counselling: Your child has CAH — lifelong steroid medicine is needed to replace missing hormones
Give hydrocortisone 3 times a day at the same times every day
During illness or fever, TRIPLE the dose — this is very important to prevent crisis
Salt supplements may be needed in infancy — continue as directed
Regular blood tests (17-OHP, electrolytes) are needed every 3-4 months to adjust doses
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology mandatory for all cases. Pediatric surgery/urology for genital surgery timing in affected females. Genetics for family counselling and prenatal diagnosis in future pregnancies
hospitalisation_criteria: Salt-wasting crisis (hyponatremia <125, hyperkalemia, dehydration, shock). Any infant with ambiguous genitalia pending workup. Adrenal crisis with vomiting preventing oral medication
notes: Hydrocortisone is first-line in growing children; avoid dexamethasone pre-puberty. Crinecerfont (2024) enables GC dose reduction by blocking excess ACTH-driven androgen production.
guideline_changes: Crinecerfont (Crenessity) FDA-approved Dec 2024 as first-in-class CRF1 antagonist adjunct for CAH age >=4y

first_line_drugs.notes: 8 mg/m2/day PO divided TID; stress dose: triple during illness; max 20 mg/m2/day
0.05-0.2 mg PO daily for mineralocorticoid replacement; monitor renin and electrolytes

second_line_drugs.notes: 2-3 mg/m2/day PO daily; not preferred in children due to growth suppression
50-100 mg/m2 IV bolus for adrenal crisis then 50-100 mg/m2/24h continuous infusion

investigations.name: ACTH stimulation test (Synacthen test)
investigations.indication: Gold standard for diagnosis — cortisol <500 nmol/L post-stimulation is diagnostic

investigations.name: Serum ACTH
investigations.indication: Differentiate primary (high ACTH) vs secondary (low ACTH)

counselling: Your child needs to take steroid medicine every day for life — never miss a dose
During fever, vomiting, or any illness, TRIPLE the steroid dose (sick-day rules)
Always carry an emergency steroid injection kit and a medical alert bracelet
Teach all caregivers (school, grandparents) the sick-day rules and how to give emergency injection
Follow up every 3-6 months with the endocrinologist for dose adjustment as the child grows
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology for all cases. Emergency referral if adrenal crisis (hypotension, hypoglycemia, hyperkalemia not responding to stress dose steroids)
hospitalisation_criteria: Adrenal crisis: shock, severe dehydration, altered mental status, refractory hypoglycemia, hyperkalemia with ECG changes. Any patient unable to tolerate oral steroids during illness
notes: Avoid long-acting glucocorticoids in growing children; use lowest dose allowing adequate growth. All patients need emergency injection training and steroid sick-day rules.
guideline_changes: NICE 2024 guideline published; Khindivi oral HC solution FDA-approved 2025

first_line_drugs.notes: 7.5-15 mg IM q28d (1-mo depot) or 11.25-30 mg IM q3mo; US doses 200-300 mcg/kg/28d
50 mg SC implant replaced annually; avoids repeat injections; minor procedure under local anesthesia

second_line_drugs.notes: 22.5 mg IM q24wk (6-mo depot, Triptodur); or 3.75 mg IM q28d
45 mg SC q6mo; small-volume injection; FDA-approved for CPP

investigations.name: Bone age (left wrist X-ray)
investigations.indication: Advanced bone age confirms premature maturation

investigations.name: LH, FSH (basal and GnRH-stimulated)
investigations.indication: Stimulated LH >5 IU/L confirms central precocious puberty

investigations.name: MRI brain with pituitary focus
investigations.indication: Mandatory to rule out CNS lesion (especially in boys and girls <6 years)

investigations.name: Pelvic ultrasound (girls)
investigations.indication: Uterine enlargement, ovarian cysts — supportive of CPP

counselling: Your child is showing signs of puberty earlier than expected — treatment can help preserve adult height
The monthly/3-monthly injection stops early puberty and allows normal growth to continue
The child may initially have a brief increase in symptoms after the first injection — this is temporary
Provide emotional support — the child may feel different from same-age peers
Regular follow-up every 3-6 months with bone age X-ray is important to track progress
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology mandatory. MRI brain if onset before age 6 (girls) or 9 (boys) to rule out CNS pathology. Genetics if associated with NF1 or other syndromes
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Admit only if: CNS tumor discovered on MRI, acute complication of underlying condition, or need for GnRH stimulation test not available outpatient
notes: GnRH agonists are standard of care for central precocious puberty to preserve adult height potential. Monitor for adequate LH suppression and growth velocity deceleration.
guideline_changes: 6-month depot formulations (Fensolvi SC, Triptodur IM) now widely used reducing injection burden

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mg IM monthly for 3-6 months for pubertal induction; start at chronological age >=14y and BA >=12y
0.25-0.5 mg PO daily (or 5 mcg/kg/day) for pubertal induction; start at chronological age >=13y and BA >=11y

second_line_drugs.notes: Start 6.25 mcg (1/8 of 50 mcg patch) nightly; increase q6mo; more physiologic than oral
2.5 mg PO daily for 6 months in boys; aromatase inhibitor to boost growth and pubertal tempo

investigations.name: Bone age (left wrist X-ray)
investigations.indication: Assess skeletal maturity — delayed bone age suggests constitutional delay

investigations.name: LH, FSH, estradiol/testosterone
investigations.indication: Distinguish hypogonadotropic (low LH/FSH) vs hypergonadotropic (high LH/FSH)

investigations.name: Karyotype
investigations.indication: Turner syndrome (45,X) in girls; Klinefelter (47,XXY) in boys

investigations.name: MRI brain with pituitary focus
investigations.indication: Rule out CNS pathology if hypogonadotropic

investigations.name: TSH, free T4, IGF-1
investigations.indication: Screen for hypothyroidism and growth hormone deficiency

counselling: Your child's puberty is delayed — in most cases, this is a normal variant and nothing to worry about
A short course of hormone medicine may be given to help start puberty
The child may feel different from peers — provide emotional support and reassurance
Height and development will be monitored every 3-6 months
Follow up with the endocrinologist to determine if further treatment is needed
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology for all cases of delayed puberty. Genetics if Turner syndrome or Klinefelter syndrome suspected. Ophthalmology/neurosurgery if pituitary pathology on MRI
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Admit if: adrenal crisis in undiagnosed panhypopituitarism, severe psychological crisis, or need for dynamic hormone testing not available outpatient
notes: Distinguish constitutional delay from permanent hypogonadism with clinical follow-up and labs. Low-dose sex steroid trial for 3-6 months can help differentiate and provide psychological benefit.
guideline_changes: 2024 JCRPE review updated delayed puberty management perspectives including aromatase inhibitor use

first_line_drugs.notes: 25-50 mcg/kg/day SC daily for GHD; up to 67 mcg/kg/day for ISS; titrate by IGF-1 levels

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.16 mg/kg SC once weekly; long-acting GH approved for pediatric GHD; reduces injection burden
0.66 mg/kg SC once weekly; alternative long-acting GH for pediatric GHD

investigations.name: Bone age (left wrist X-ray)
investigations.indication: Assess growth potential — compare bone age to chronological age

investigations.name: Thyroid function (TSH, free T4)
investigations.indication: Hypothyroidism is a treatable cause of short stature

counselling: Your child is shorter than expected — growth hormone injections may be needed to help the child grow taller
The injection is given every day (or once weekly with newer medicine) under the skin — usually at bedtime
Growth will be monitored every 3-6 months — bring the child for regular height and weight measurements
Good nutrition, adequate sleep (8-10 hours), and regular physical activity also help growth
Treatment works best when started early — continue until the doctor says growth plates have closed
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology mandatory. Genetics if syndromic short stature (Turner, Noonan, Prader-Willi). GI if celiac or IBD contributing. Psychology if significant psychological impact
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Admit only for: GH stimulation testing if not available outpatient, newly diagnosed pituitary tumor, or severe GH deficiency with hypoglycemia in infants
notes: Individualize dosing based on growth response and IGF-1 levels; stop GH when growth velocity falls below 2.5 cm/year. Once-weekly formulations (2024) improve adherence.
guideline_changes: Somapacitan (Sogroya) once-weekly GH approved for new pediatric indications (2024)

first_line_drugs.notes: 75 kcal/100 mL; stabilization phase days 1-7; feed q2-3h; low protein/Na to prevent refeeding syndrome
150-200 kcal/kg/day in rehab phase; 10-15 kg total over 6-8 wk; for age >=6mo without complications
40 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for 5-7 days; routine antibiotic per WHO SAM protocol

second_line_drugs.notes: 100 kcal/100 mL; transition/rehabilitation phase; increase feeds by 10 mL/feed until desired intake
5 mL/kg IV bolus for hypoglycemia (glucose <54 mg/dL); then feed immediately

investigations.name: Blood glucose
investigations.indication: Hypoglycemia screening — common and life-threatening in SAM

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes (Na, K, Mg, PO4)
investigations.indication: Refeeding syndrome prevention — check BID for 4 days

investigations.name: Blood culture
investigations.indication: Occult sepsis is common in SAM children

investigations.name: HIV test
investigations.indication: All SAM children should be tested per WHO

counselling: Your child is severely malnourished and needs careful treatment in the hospital first, then at home
Give the therapeutic food (RUTF) as prescribed — it has all the nutrients your child needs
Feed small amounts frequently — do not force-feed large quantities at once
Continue breastfeeding along with therapeutic food if the child is under 2 years
Come for weekly follow-up until the child's weight reaches the target — missing visits is dangerous
referral_criteria: Nutrition rehabilitation center if available. Pediatric gastroenterology if underlying GI disease. Social services for food insecurity. Infectious disease if TB or HIV suspected. Metabolic evaluation if malnutrition unexplained by dietary history
hospitalisation_criteria: All complicated SAM (with medical complications: dehydration, sepsis, hypothermia, hypoglycemia, severe edema, poor appetite). WHZ <-3 SD or MUAC <115mm. Bilateral pitting edema. Failed appetite test (RUTF refusal)
notes: Start refeeding cautiously with F-75 to avoid refeeding syndrome; monitor electrolytes (K, Mg, PO4) BID for 4 days. Treat hypothermia, hypoglycemia, and dehydration before addressing nutrition.
guideline_changes: WHO 2023 guideline consolidated wasting and oedema management; community-based RUTF approach strengthened
snomed_display: Severe protein-calorie malnutrition (Gomez: less than 60 percent of standard weight)

diagnosis_name: Protein-energy malnutrition of moderate and mild degree

first_line_drugs.notes: Energy-dense supplementary food 250-500 kcal/day added to usual diet; attend checkup q2wk for 4-6 wk
Multivitamin/mineral supplement per WHO; include iron, zinc, folate, vitamin A per local protocol

second_line_drugs.notes: 92 g/day sachet (~500 kcal) for moderate acute malnutrition in 6-59 month olds
1-1.5 kcal/mL enteral formula for older children; increase by 10-20% q2-3d as tolerated

investigations.name: Urinalysis
investigations.indication: Screen for UTI as underlying cause

counselling: Your child is underweight — more nutritious food is needed every day
Give energy-rich foods like dal, ghee, peanuts, eggs, banana, and milk at every meal
Feed the child 5-6 times a day — 3 meals and 2-3 snacks
Continue breastfeeding if the child is under 2 years — it is the best nutrition
Come for weight check-up every 2 weeks until the child's weight is back on track
referral_criteria: Pediatric gastroenterology if no improvement after 4-6 weeks of supplementary feeding. Dietitian for detailed meal planning. Social services if food insecurity identified. Investigate for underlying disease if weight not improving
hospitalisation_criteria: Failure to respond to outpatient supplementary feeding after 4-6 weeks. Development of complications (severe dehydration, electrolyte imbalance). Coexisting severe illness requiring treatment. Weight-for-height z-score dropping to <-3 SD (now SAM)
notes: Counsel on age-appropriate feeding practices and complete immunizations. Monitor weight-for-height z-score every 2 weeks; refer if no improvement after 4-6 weeks.
guideline_changes: WHO 2023 guideline expanded to include moderate wasting management in 6-59 month age group

first_line_drugs.notes: Start at 50-80% estimated caloric needs for current weight; advance 10-20%/day if electrolytes stable
100-300 mg IV/PO daily before and during refeeding to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy
Check K, Mg, PO4 BID for >=4 days; replace aggressively; do not advance feeds if abnormal

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 mmol/kg/day IV or PO if PO4 low; critical to prevent refeeding syndrome cardiac complications
Polymeric 1 kcal/mL formula via NG if unable to eat; advance slowly per refeeding protocol

investigations.name: Serum phosphate, potassium, magnesium
investigations.indication: Refeeding syndrome monitoring — check BID for 4 days

investigations.name: ECG
investigations.indication: Cardiac monitoring during refeeding (QTc prolongation risk)

counselling: Your child needs gradual refeeding — starting with small amounts and increasing slowly is safer
Do not try to feed large amounts quickly — it can cause a dangerous condition called refeeding syndrome
The child will need frequent blood tests during the first few days to monitor salt levels
Once stable, follow the nutritional plan given by the doctor and dietitian
Follow up weekly for weight monitoring until nutritional goals are met
referral_criteria: Dietitian for refeeding protocol. Pediatric gastroenterology for underlying malabsorption. Psychiatry if eating disorder suspected. Social services for food insecurity or neglect
hospitalisation_criteria: Any malnourished patient at refeeding risk (>10 days poor intake). Electrolyte instability (low phosphate, potassium, magnesium). Cardiac symptoms during refeeding. BMI <14 kg/m2 in adolescents
notes: High refeeding syndrome risk in any child malnourished >10 days; start calories low and advance slowly. Correct electrolytes before advancing nutritional support.

first_line_drugs.notes: <6mo: 50,000 IU PO; 6-12mo: 100,000 IU PO; >12mo: 200,000 IU PO; day 1, day 2, then repeat at 2 wk

second_line_drugs.notes: 100,000 IU PO q4-6mo for ages 6-11mo; 200,000 IU PO q4-6mo for 12-59mo in endemic areas

investigations.name: Ophthalmologic examination
investigations.indication: Bitot spots, corneal xerosis, keratomalacia grading

investigations.name: Serum albumin
investigations.indication: Protein status — VAD often coexists with PEM

counselling: Your child has vitamin A deficiency — high-dose vitamin A drops will be given on 3 occasions
Eye involvement (night blindness, dry eyes) is a sign of severe deficiency — report immediately
Feed the child orange/yellow fruits and vegetables (carrot, papaya, mango), green leafy vegetables, and eggs
Vitamin A supplementation every 6 months is recommended for all children 6-59 months in India
Come back if the child develops eye redness, whiteness on the eye, or difficulty seeing at night
referral_criteria: Ophthalmology urgently if corneal involvement (xerophthalmia stage X2-X3B — corneal ulcer/keratomalacia). Pediatric nutrition for severe malnutrition associated with VAD. GI if malabsorption identified
hospitalisation_criteria: Corneal xerophthalmia (X2 or higher — corneal xerosis, ulceration, keratomalacia). Measles with vitamin A deficiency. Severe malnutrition with VAD. Corneal perforation requiring emergency treatment
notes: Active corneal xerophthalmia is a medical emergency requiring immediate high-dose vitamin A. Also give vitamin A with measles treatment per WHO protocol regardless of deficiency status.

first_line_drugs.notes: <1y: 2000 IU/day PO x12wk; >1y: 3000-6000 IU/day PO x8-12wk; recheck 25-OH-D after course
30-75 mg/kg/day elemental calcium PO divided TID for rickets with low Ca or inadequate dietary intake

second_line_drugs.notes: 50,000 IU PO weekly x6-8wk for adolescents; then maintenance 600-1000 IU/day
0.01-0.05 mcg/kg/day PO for vitamin D-dependent rickets type I or renal disease

investigations.name: Serum 25-OH vitamin D
investigations.indication: Primary diagnostic test — <20 ng/mL is deficiency, <12 is severe

investigations.name: Serum calcium, phosphorus, ALP
investigations.indication: Low Ca, low PO4, high ALP in nutritional rickets

investigations.name: Parathyroid hormone (PTH)
investigations.indication: Secondary hyperparathyroidism from vitamin D deficiency

investigations.name: Wrist X-ray
investigations.indication: Rachitic changes: cupping, fraying, widening of metaphysis

counselling: Your child has vitamin D deficiency — this can cause weak bones and rickets in children
Give vitamin D drops/tablets as prescribed every day — complete the full 8-12 week course
All breastfed babies should get 400 IU vitamin D daily from birth until weaning to fortified milk
Encourage 15-20 minutes of sunlight exposure daily (morning sun is best)
Include calcium-rich foods: milk, curd, paneer, ragi, sesame seeds in the child's diet
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology if vitamin D-resistant or dependent rickets suspected. Orthopedics if severe skeletal deformities (bowing, fractures). Nephrology for renal rickets. GI if malabsorption
hospitalisation_criteria: Hypocalcemic seizures from severe vitamin D deficiency. Symptomatic hypocalcemia (tetany, laryngospasm). Pathological fractures. Severe rickets requiring IV calcium supplementation
notes: All breastfed infants should receive 400 IU/day vitamin D prophylaxis from birth. Double treatment dose in patients with obesity, malabsorption, or dark skin pigmentation.
source: AAP 2008, Indian Academy of Pediatrics 2021, Endocrine Society 2024

first_line_drugs.notes: Neonates: 0.5-1 mg IM at birth; VKDB treatment: 1-5 mg IV/SC; preterm: 0.3-0.5 mg/kg IM
1000 mcg PO daily x4mo for nutritional deficiency; or 1000 mcg IM weekly x4wk then monthly

second_line_drugs.notes: 100-300 mg PO daily for scurvy treatment; continue for 1-3 months until stores replete
25-200 IU/day PO for deficiency; higher doses in cholestasis or fat malabsorption

investigations.name: CBC with peripheral smear
investigations.indication: Macrocytic anemia in B12/folate deficiency; assess bleeding risk

investigations.name: Serum homocysteine and methylmalonic acid
investigations.indication: Elevated in B12 deficiency — more sensitive than serum B12

counselling: Your child has a vitamin deficiency that can be corrected with proper supplementation
Give vitamin supplements as prescribed — complete the full course even if the child looks better
Feed the child a balanced diet with fruits, vegetables, eggs, milk, and green leafy vegetables
All newborns must receive Vitamin K injection at birth — never refuse it
Follow up for blood tests after completing the treatment course to confirm recovery
referral_criteria: Hematology if severe anemia from B12/folate deficiency. Neurology if neurological symptoms from B12 deficiency. GI if malabsorption suspected as cause. Dietitian for dietary counselling
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe bleeding from vitamin K deficiency (VKDB). Severe megaloblastic anemia (Hb <5 g/dL) from B12 deficiency. Scurvy with hemorrhagic complications. Neurological symptoms from B12 deficiency requiring urgent replacement
notes: Vitamin K prophylaxis at birth prevents hemorrhagic disease of newborn. Identify and treat underlying cause of deficiency (dietary, malabsorption, or chronic disease).

first_line_drugs.notes: Intensive health behavior and lifestyle treatment (26+ contact hours); diet, physical activity, behavioral therapy
SC weekly; titrate 0.25mg to 2.4mg over 16wk; age >=12y with BMI >=95th percentile
SC daily; titrate 0.6mg to 3.0mg over 4wk; age >=12y (6-12y per recent trial data)

second_line_drugs.notes: 120 mg PO TID with meals; age >=12y; add multivitamin due to fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption
Per weight-based dosing PO daily; age >=12y; monitor HR and mood

investigations.name: Fasting lipid profile
investigations.indication: Dyslipidemia screening — all obese children >=2 years

investigations.name: Liver function tests (ALT)
investigations.indication: NAFLD/MASLD screening — screen all obese children

investigations.name: TSH
investigations.indication: Rule out hypothyroidism as secondary cause

counselling: Your child's weight needs to be managed with diet changes, more physical activity, and less screen time
Limit sugar-sweetened drinks (cola, juice, packaged drinks) — switch to water and buttermilk
Ensure at least 60 minutes of active play/exercise every day — whole family should participate
Reduce screen time to less than 2 hours per day for children above 5 years
Regular follow-up every 3 months to track weight, blood sugar, and cholesterol is important
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology if secondary cause suspected or pharmacotherapy considered (age >=12). Dietitian for structured meal planning. Psychology for binge eating or emotional eating. Bariatric surgery team if BMI >=40 or >=35 with comorbidities (age >=13)
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Consider admission for: severe obstructive sleep apnea, hypertensive emergency from obesity, bariatric surgery, or psychiatric crisis (eating disorder with medical instability)
notes: Semaglutide achieves up to 17% BMI reduction, the most potent of approved agents. Pharmacotherapy is adjunct to, not replacement for, intensive lifestyle modification.
guideline_changes: Semaglutide FDA-approved Dec 2022 for age >=12y; liraglutide expanded to ages 6-12y (2024 trial data)

first_line_drugs.notes: Limit phenylalanine intake using Phe-free amino acid formula; target blood Phe 120-360 umol/L
10-20 mg/kg/day PO once daily; for BH4-responsive patients (~25-50%); trial response required

second_line_drugs.notes: SC injection; approved for adults/adolescents >=16y; titrate from 2.5 mg to 20-40 mg daily; anaphylaxis risk
Supplement PO to compete with Phe transport across BBB; adjunct in patients with poor dietary compliance

investigations.name: BH4 loading test
investigations.indication: Determine BH4 responsiveness — 25-50% of patients respond

investigations.name: Plasma amino acids (full profile)
investigations.indication: Ensure adequate tyrosine and other amino acid levels

investigations.name: MRI brain
investigations.indication: If developmental regression or poor metabolic control

counselling: Your child needs a special low-protein diet for life — this prevents brain damage
Use the special PKU formula as the main source of nutrition — it provides essential nutrients without phenylalanine
Blood phenylalanine levels must be checked weekly (infants) to monthly (older children)
Avoid high-protein foods: meat, fish, eggs, dairy, nuts, soy — your dietitian will give you a detailed list
If the child is unwell or not eating, contact the metabolic team immediately
referral_criteria: Metabolic genetics/biochemical genetics mandatory. Developmental pediatrics for cognitive monitoring. Dietitian specializing in PKU. Maternal-fetal medicine for females with PKU planning pregnancy
hospitalisation_criteria: Phenylalanine level >900 umol/L with neurological symptoms. New diagnosis in neonate for initiation of diet. Metabolic crisis during intercurrent illness. Non-compliance with dangerously high levels
notes: Newborn screening enables early dietary treatment preventing intellectual disability. All patients should undergo BH4 responsiveness testing; diet remains the cornerstone of therapy.
guideline_changes: ACMG 2024 evidence-based guideline published Aug 2024; sapropterin conditionally recommended in pregnancy

diagnosis_name: Disorders of branched-chain amino-acid metabolism and fatty-acid metabolism

first_line_drugs.notes: Restrict leucine to age-appropriate tolerance; use MSUD-specific formula to maintain Leu <200 umol/L (<5y)
High-calorie IV glucose (8-10 mg/kg/min) during acute decompensation to promote anabolism

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-300 mg/day PO trial for thiamine-responsive MSUD variants; assess response over 3 weeks
50-100 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for fatty acid oxidation defects; max 3 g/day
1-2 g/kg/day PO for long-chain FAO defects; avoid fasting >4-6h in infants

investigations.name: Plasma amino acids (quantitative)
investigations.indication: Diagnosis and monitoring — leucine, isoleucine, valine levels

investigations.name: Urine organic acids
investigations.indication: Characteristic pattern for MSUD and FAO disorders

investigations.name: Acylcarnitine profile
investigations.indication: Specific pattern for fatty acid oxidation defects

counselling: Your child has a metabolic disorder that requires a special diet for life
Follow the dietary restrictions strictly — even small deviations can cause crisis
During fever, vomiting, or illness, take the child to hospital immediately — do not wait
Never let the child fast for more than 4-6 hours (infants) or 8 hours (older children)
Regular blood tests to check amino acid levels are needed every 1-3 months
referral_criteria: Metabolic genetics/biochemical genetics specialist mandatory. Liver transplant center for classic MSUD if eligible. Dietitian specializing in metabolic disorders. Neurology for developmental monitoring
hospitalisation_criteria: Acute metabolic crisis (encephalopathy, vomiting, metabolic acidosis). Leucine >750 umol/L. Intercurrent illness with poor oral intake. New diagnosis requiring stabilization
notes: Acute metabolic crises require emergency IV glucose and protein restriction; hemodialysis if leucine >1500 umol/L. Liver transplant is curative for classic MSUD.

first_line_drugs.notes: 100-500 mg/day PO trial; ~50% of CBS-deficient HCU patients are B6-responsive
Use met-free amino acid formula; target plasma homocysteine <50 umol/L
