second_line_drugs.notes: 50-125 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for B6-nonresponsive HCU; max 6 g/day in children
Folate 1-5 mg/day PO + B12 1 mg/day PO as adjunct to lower homocysteine

investigations.name: Pyridoxine responsiveness test
investigations.indication: 50% of CBS-deficient patients respond — determines treatment approach

counselling: Your child needs a special diet low in methionine and may need lifelong vitamin supplements
Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) responsiveness will be tested — if responsive, it greatly simplifies treatment
Blood tests for homocysteine level are needed every 3-6 months
Report any sudden vision changes, leg pain, or swelling immediately — these are emergencies
Ensure the child takes betaine and folate as prescribed — these help lower homocysteine
referral_criteria: Metabolic genetics mandatory. Ophthalmology for lens dislocation screening. Hematology if thromboembolic event. Cardiology for cardiovascular risk monitoring
hospitalisation_criteria: Thromboembolic event (stroke, DVT, PE). Lens dislocation requiring surgery. Acute metabolic decompensation. New diagnosis requiring stabilization and dietary initiation
notes: Test all patients for pyridoxine responsiveness before committing to lifelong dietary restriction. Early treatment prevents intellectual disability, thromboembolism, and lens dislocation.
guideline_changes: Novel enzyme replacement therapies (pegtibatinase, pegtarviliase) in clinical trials for CBS-deficient HCU

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 g/kg PO q4-6h (older children); maintains euglycemia in GSD I/III; avoid in <1y
Complex carbs q2-4h daytime; continuous NG glucose infusion overnight for infants

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for hyperuricemia in GSD I; max 800 mg/day
1-2 g/kg PO at bedtime; provides longer glucose release than raw cornstarch; age >5y

investigations.name: Serum lactate
investigations.indication: Elevated in GSD I during fasting

counselling: Your child must eat regularly and never skip meals — fasting can cause dangerous low blood sugar
Cornstarch mixed in cool water is a key medicine — give at prescribed times without fail
Night-time feeds are critical for infants — set alarms to avoid long gaps
Carry glucose tablets or sugar whenever travelling in case of emergency
Regular blood tests for sugar, lactate, uric acid, and liver size checks are needed every 3-6 months
referral_criteria: Metabolic genetics mandatory. Hepatology for liver adenoma surveillance in GSD I. Nephrology for renal complications. Dietitian specializing in metabolic disorders for cornstarch regimen
hospitalisation_criteria: Hypoglycemic episode requiring IV dextrose. Lactic acidosis. Intercurrent illness with poor oral intake. New diagnosis requiring initiation of feeding regimen
notes: Frequent feeding and cornstarch are the mainstay for hepatic GSD; avoid fasting >4h in infants. Monitor lactate, uric acid, lipids, and liver size regularly.

diagnosis_name: Sphingolipidosis and other lipid storage disorders

first_line_drugs.notes: 30-60 U/kg IV every 2 weeks; for Gaucher type 1; adjust based on clinical response
Gaucher: 100 mg PO TID (adults); NPC: substrate reduction therapy per weight-based dosing

second_line_drugs.notes: PO per weight-based dosing for Niemann-Pick type C; first FDA-approved NPC therapy (2024)
PO weight-based dosing for NPC; second FDA-approved NPC therapy (Sep 2024)
60 U/kg IV every 2 weeks for Gaucher type 1 (age >=4y); alternative to imiglucerase

investigations.name: Enzyme activity assay (specific to disorder)
investigations.indication: Confirmatory diagnosis — glucocerebrosidase for Gaucher, etc.

investigations.name: Genetic testing
investigations.indication: Confirm mutation, family screening, prenatal diagnosis

counselling: Your child has a rare storage disorder — treatment depends on the specific type
Enzyme infusions (if applicable) must be given regularly — usually every 2 weeks
Watch for bone pain, easy bruising, enlarged belly, or difficulty walking and report immediately
Some types affect the brain — developmental monitoring is important
Genetic counselling for the family is recommended to understand the inheritance pattern
referral_criteria: Metabolic genetics mandatory. Hematology for Gaucher disease. Neurology for neuronopathic forms. Hepatology for liver involvement. HSCT team for eligible patients. Psychiatry for NPC (behavioral changes)
hospitalisation_criteria: First dose of ERT (monitoring for infusion reactions). Acute neurological deterioration. Splenic crisis (Gaucher). Severe anemia or thrombocytopenia requiring transfusion
notes: ERT is effective for Gaucher type 1 but not for neuronopathic forms. Two new NPC therapies approved in 2024 represent major advances for this previously untreatable condition.
guideline_changes: Arimoclomol (Miplyffa) and levacetylleucine (Aqneursa) both FDA-approved Sep 2024 for Niemann-Pick C

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.58 mg/kg (100 U/kg) IV weekly over 3-4h; premedicate with antipyretic/antihistamine
0.5 mg/kg IV weekly over 1-3h; monitor for infusion reactions in ~20% patients

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg/kg IV weekly over 4h; for Maroteaux-Lamy syndrome
Hematopoietic stem cell transplant; preferred for severe MPS I (Hurler) if <2.5y with DQ >70

investigations.name: Enzyme activity assay (specific to MPS type)
investigations.indication: Confirmatory — alpha-L-iduronidase for MPS I, iduronate-2-sulfatase for MPS II

counselling: Your child has a storage disorder that causes substances to build up in the body over time
Enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) is given as a weekly IV infusion — do not miss sessions
The child may have a mild reaction during infusion (fever, rash) — premedication is given to prevent this
Regular eye, ear, and heart checkups are needed as the condition can affect multiple organs
Early treatment gives the best results — please keep all follow-up appointments
referral_criteria: Metabolic genetics mandatory. HSCT team referral for MPS I Hurler (age <2.5y). Orthopedics for skeletal complications. ENT for airway management. Cardiology for valve disease. Ophthalmology for corneal clouding
hospitalisation_criteria: Initiation of ERT (first dose monitoring). Acute airway compromise. Cardiac decompensation. HSCT admission. Spinal cord compression (odontoid hypoplasia). Hydrocephalus
notes: ERT does not cross the blood-brain barrier; HSCT is preferred for MPS I Hurler phenotype if done early. Early diagnosis via newborn screening improves outcomes substantially.
guideline_changes: Gene therapy trials advancing for MPS I/II; intrathecal ERT for CNS involvement under study

first_line_drugs.notes: For symptomatic hypocalcemia: 1 mL/kg/dose IV (provides about 9-18 mg/kg elemental calcium), maximum 10 mL, dilute with equal volume of D5W or distilled water and infuse slowly over 10 minutes under ECG monitoring
For recurrent symptomatic hypocalcemia: continuous infusion starting at 10-30 mg/kg/hour elemental calcium equivalent, titrate based on serial calcium levels and symptoms; ECG monitoring mandatory
For asymptomatic hypocalcemia or after stabilization: 100-200 mg/kg/day in 2-3 divided doses
For asymptomatic hypocalcemia/supportive therapy: 400-800 IU/day orally

second_line_drugs.notes: If hypomagnesemia is present or hypocalcemia is refractory: 25-50 mg/kg slow IV infusion; approximately 0.05-0.1 mL/kg of 50% solution; may repeat every 6 hours for 2-3 doses
Use in hypoparathyroidism: 20-60 ng/kg/day orally

investigations.name: Serum Total Calcium
investigations.indication: Diagnosis and serial monitoring of hypocalcemia

investigations.name: Ionized Calcium
investigations.indication: Best indicator in symptomatic child or critical illness

investigations.name: ECG
investigations.indication: Symptomatic hypocalcemia, arrhythmia, prolonged QT, or IV calcium therapy

investigations.name: Serum Magnesium
investigations.indication: Refractory hypocalcemia or suspected combined deficiency

investigations.name: Serum Phosphate, Alkaline Phosphatase, Vitamin D, PTH
investigations.indication: Etiology workup for persistent or recurrent hypocalcemia

counselling: Low calcium can cause twitching, spasms, seizures, or heart rhythm changes. — कम कैल्शियम से मांसपेशियों में ऐंठन, झटके, दौरे या दिल की धड़कन बिगड़ सकती है।
Symptomatic low calcium needs urgent treatment and monitoring. — लक्षण वाले कम कैल्शियम का तुरंत इलाज और निगरानी ज़रूरी है।
After stabilization, oral calcium and vitamin D may be needed for several days or longer. — स्थिति ठीक होने के बाद भी कुछ दिनों तक मुँह से कैल्शियम और विटामिन डी लेना पड़ सकता है।
Regular blood tests are important to ensure calcium becomes normal and stays normal. — नियमित ब्लड टेस्ट कराएं ताकि कैल्शियम सामान्य बना रहे।
Seek urgent care if the child develops twitching, stridor, seizures, or poor feeding. — अगर बच्चे को झटके, साँस में आवाज़, दौरे या दूध न पीना हो तो तुरंत डॉक्टर को दिखाएं।
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric intensivist/endocrinologist if symptomatic hypocalcemia, recurrent episodes, arrhythmia, seizures, suspected hypoparathyroidism, or refractory hypocalcemia.
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if symptomatic hypocalcemia, seizures, laryngospasm, prolonged QT, arrhythmia, ionized calcium significantly low, or need for IV calcium infusion.
notes: Corrected calcium formula: Corrected calcium = Serum calcium + 0.8 x (4.0 - serum albumin). Symptoms usually occur when ionized calcium falls below about 0.8-0.9 mmol/L. ECG monitoring is required during IV calcium administration.
expected_course: Symptoms often improve quickly after IV calcium, while biochemical normalization may require continued oral calcium and vitamin D with repeat monitoring over days to weeks depending on cause.
key_clinical_points: Symptomatic hypocalcemia is a medical emergency.
Always check magnesium in refractory or recurrent hypocalcemia.
Continuous ECG monitoring is essential during IV calcium infusion.

severity_assessment.mild: Low serum calcium without symptoms; child stable and tolerating oral therapy.
severity_assessment.moderate: Low calcium with irritability, mild twitching, or biochemical abnormality needing close monitoring.
severity_assessment.severe: Tetany, carpopedal spasm, laryngospasm, seizures, prolonged QT, or arrhythmia.

monitoring_parameters.frequency: 6-12 hourly during acute correction, then daily once stable
Repeat as indicated in refractory cases
Hourly if laryngospasm or severe symptoms

first_line_drugs.notes: Moderate to severe hypercalcemia: start hydration to correct volume depletion; for severe hypercalcemia give 10-20 mL/kg IV bolus over 1 hour, then continue IV saline as needed (often 2-3 times maintenance initially depending on hydration and cardiac status)
1-2 mg/kg IV after adequate hydration to enhance calciuresis; avoid before volume status is corrected
0.5-2 mg/kg diluted in 30 mL normal saline, IV over 4 hours; use in severe or persistent hypercalcemia
2-4 units/kg subcutaneous every 12 hours for severe symptomatic hypercalcemia requiring faster calcium reduction

second_line_drugs.notes: 5 mg/kg orally twice daily as a bisphosphonate option where appropriate
2 mg/kg IV every 6 hours when indicated, especially where reduced intestinal calcium absorption is desired or steroid-responsive cause suspected

investigations.name: Serum Total Calcium
investigations.indication: Diagnosis and serial monitoring of severity

investigations.name: Renal Function Tests
investigations.indication: Assess dehydration, renal injury, and treatment safety

investigations.name: Serum Electrolytes (Na, K, Mg, Phosphate)
investigations.indication: Associated electrolyte abnormalities and monitoring during therapy

investigations.name: ECG
investigations.indication: Symptomatic hypercalcemia, arrhythmia, or severe hypercalcemia

investigations.name: PTH, Vitamin D, Urinary Calcium
investigations.indication: Etiology workup in persistent or recurrent hypercalcemia

counselling: High calcium can cause vomiting, constipation, excessive urine, irritability, and weakness. — ज्यादा कैल्शियम से उल्टी, कब्ज, ज्यादा पेशाब, चिड़चिड़ापन और कमजोरी हो सकती है।
Mild cases may only need fluids and removal of triggering medicines or excess calcium intake. — हल्के मामलों में सिर्फ पानी पिलाना और कैल्शियम बढ़ाने वाली दवाएं बंद करना काफी हो सकता है।
Severe cases need admission, IV fluids, and close monitoring. — गंभीर मामलों में भर्ती, IV तरल पदार्थ और करीबी निगरानी जरूरी है।
Avoid thiazide diuretics, lithium, and unnecessary calcium supplements unless specifically advised. — डॉक्टर की सलाह के बिना थायज़ाइड, लिथियम या कैल्शियम सप्लीमेंट न दें।
Follow-up blood tests are important because calcium may rise again depending on the cause. — फॉलो-अप खून की जांच जरूरी है क्योंकि कारण के अनुसार कैल्शियम फिर बढ़ सकता है।
warning_signs: Polyuria / reduced urine output after dehydration
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric endocrinologist/nephrologist/intensivist if calcium is >14 mg/dL, symptoms are significant, renal dysfunction is present, medical therapy fails, or recurrent/unclear etiology is suspected.
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if total serum calcium >14 mg/dL, symptomatic moderate hypercalcemia, dehydration, poor oral intake, arrhythmia, altered sensorium, or need for IV therapy/serial monitoring.
notes: Mild hypercalcemia (10-12 mg/dL) usually needs no specific drug therapy beyond hydration and avoiding offending agents. Moderate hypercalcemia (12-14 mg/dL) may not need immediate drug treatment unless symptomatic. Severe hypercalcemia (>14 mg/dL) requires urgent stepwise treatment.
expected_course: Symptoms improve as hydration is corrected and serum calcium falls. Mild cases may normalize with conservative care, while severe cases may take 24-72 hours or longer depending on cause and response to bisphosphonate therapy.
key_clinical_points: Hydration is the first step in treatment of clinically significant hypercalcemia.
Furosemide should be used only after correcting volume depletion.
Persistent or severe hypercalcemia may require bisphosphonate, calcitonin, steroid, or dialysis-based management.

severity_assessment.mild: Total serum calcium 10-12 mg/dL, usually minimally symptomatic or asymptomatic.
severity_assessment.moderate: Total serum calcium 12-14 mg/dL, may have nausea, vomiting, constipation, polyuria, irritability, or fatigue.
severity_assessment.severe: Total serum calcium >14 mg/dL or significant symptoms such as dehydration, neurological changes, arrhythmia, or poor feeding.

monitoring_parameters.frequency: 6-12 hourly initially, then daily once improving

first_line_drugs.notes: Age 6-11y <30kg: ELX50/TEZ25/IVA37.5 AM + IVA75 PM; >=30kg or >=12y: ELX100/TEZ50/IVA75 AM + IVA150 PM; with fat
2.5 mg nebulized daily; for age >=6y to improve mucus clearance and lung function

second_line_drugs.notes: For gating mutations; age 1mo-5y: 25-75 mg PO BID by weight; >=6y: 150 mg PO q12h with fat
500-2500 lipase U/kg/meal PO; max 10,000 U/kg/day; for pancreatic insufficiency
4 mL nebulized BID; for age >=6y; improves mucociliary clearance

investigations.name: Sweat chloride test
investigations.indication: Gold standard for CF diagnosis — Cl >60 mmol/L diagnostic

investigations.name: CFTR genetic testing
investigations.indication: Identify mutation — guides CFTR modulator eligibility

investigations.name: Pulmonary function tests (spirometry)
investigations.indication: FEV1 monitoring — baseline and q3-6 months (age >=6)

investigations.name: Sputum culture
investigations.indication: Identify respiratory pathogens — Pseudomonas, Staph aureus

counselling: Your child has cystic fibrosis — daily chest physiotherapy and medicines are essential for life
Give the CFTR modulator (Trikafta) with fatty food every day at the same time
Do airway clearance (chest physiotherapy) twice a day — even when the child seems well
Pancreatic enzyme capsules must be taken with every meal and snack — before eating
Call the CF team immediately if the child has fever, increased cough, or colored sputum
referral_criteria: Pediatric pulmonology (CF center) mandatory for all cases. Gastroenterology for pancreatic insufficiency. Endocrinology for CF-related diabetes. ENT for nasal polyps. Lung transplant evaluation if FEV1 <30% predicted
hospitalisation_criteria: Pulmonary exacerbation requiring IV antibiotics. Newly diagnosed CF for initiation of care. Hemoptysis. Pneumothorax. Meconium ileus equivalent. Distal intestinal obstruction syndrome
notes: Trikafta has transformed CF care with significant lung function improvement and reduced mortality. Start CFTR modulators at earliest eligible age to attenuate disease progression.
guideline_changes: Trikafta approved down to age 2y (2023); Canadian consensus recommends starting CFTR modulators at youngest age possible

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mL/kg PO over 3-4h; 5 mL q5min initially, increase as tolerated
20 mL/kg IV bolus for severe dehydration; repeat up to 3 boluses if needed
20 mL/kg IV bolus for severe dehydration; alternative to NS

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg PO/IV single dose (max 4 mg) to facilitate oral rehydration
Nasogastric ORS at 20 mL/kg/h if PO fails and IV not available

investigations.name: Blood urea and creatinine
investigations.indication: Assess renal function, severity of dehydration

investigations.name: Blood glucose
investigations.indication: Hypoglycemia risk especially in young children

counselling: Your child is dehydrated — give ORS (oral rehydration solution) in small sips every few minutes
Do not give fruit juice, cola, or sweetened drinks — they can worsen diarrhea
Continue breastfeeding — it is the best fluid for a dehydrated baby
If the child vomits, wait 10 minutes and try again with smaller sips
Come back immediately if: child becomes very drowsy, eyes look sunken, no tears when crying, or no urine for 6 hours
referral_criteria: Pediatric emergency if severe dehydration with shock. GI referral if chronic/recurrent dehydration suggesting underlying disease. Endocrinology if diabetes insipidus or adrenal insufficiency suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe dehydration (>10% weight loss, altered mental status, hemodynamic instability). Failed oral rehydration. Persistent vomiting preventing ORS intake. Infant <3 months with moderate-severe dehydration
notes: Oral rehydration is first-line for mild-moderate dehydration; IV reserved for severe or failed ORT. Continue breastfeeding; avoid sugary fluids.
guideline_changes: Emphasis on hypoosmolar ORS and early refeeding confirmed in 2023 updates

first_line_drugs.notes: Plan A: <2 yrs: 50–100 ml after each loose stool; 2–10 yrs: 100–200 ml; >10 yrs: as tolerated
Plan B: 75 ml/kg over 4 hours (for some dehydration), reassess after 4 hrs
Plan C: 100 ml/kg IV (30 ml/kg rapid then 70 ml/kg over 2.5–5 hrs depending on age)
<6 months: 10 mg OD for 14 days; >6 months: 20 mg OD for 14 days

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg/dose PO/IV if persistent vomiting interfering with ORS
Optional; may reduce duration of diarrhea

counselling: Give ORS frequently in small amounts — ORS बार-बार थोड़ी-थोड़ी मात्रा में पिलाएं।
Continue breastfeeding and normal diet — माँ का दूध और सामान्य खाना जारी रखें।
Avoid sugary drinks and packaged juices — मीठे पेय और पैकेट वाले जूस न दें।
Maintain hand hygiene to prevent spread — हाथों की सफ़ाई रखें ताकि बीमारी न फैले।
Follow-up if no improvement in 2–3 days — 2-3 दिन में सुधार न हो तो दोबारा डॉक्टर को दिखाएं।
referral_criteria: Refer if severe dehydration, shock, persistent vomiting, or no improvement after ORS therapy
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe dehydration, inability to drink, repeated vomiting, shock, altered sensorium
notes: ORS is cornerstone therapy. Avoid unnecessary antibiotics unless dysentery or specific infection suspected.
guideline_changes: Low osmolar ORS recommended universally; early feeding emphasized
expected_course: Diarrhea improves in 3–5 days; hydration improves within 24–48 hours
key_clinical_points: ORS is lifesaving and first-line treatment
Assess dehydration clinically before treatment
Reassess after 4 hours in Plan B

severity_assessment.mild: No signs of dehydration, normal drinking
severity_assessment.moderate: Restless, sunken eyes, thirsty, reduced skin turgor
severity_assessment.severe: Lethargic, unable to drink, weak pulse, delayed capillary refill

first_line_drugs.notes: Correct slowly over 24-84 hours depending on initial serum sodium. Use calculated fluid plan: Total fluid deficit + free water deficit + maintenance sodium. Avoid rapid correction.
Use first if child is in shock to restore intravascular volume before hypernatremia correction. Bolus as per shock protocol.

first_line_drugs.drug: Half Normal Saline based dextrose fluid (e.g., N/2, N/5, N/6 with dextrose as per calculated sodium requirement)
first_line_drugs.notes: Choose fluid strength according to calculated sodium requirement and day-wise correction plan; usual target sodium fall not more than 10-12 mEq/L in 24 hours or about 0.5 mEq/L/hr.

second_line_drugs.notes: Only if seizures occur during correction due to overly rapid sodium fall or cerebral edema from hypotonic correction; use to acutely raise serum sodium under ICU monitoring.
Add only after urine output is established and potassium status is known, if clinically indicated.

investigations.name: Serum Sodium
investigations.indication: Diagnosis and serial monitoring during correction

investigations.name: Serum Potassium, Chloride, Bicarbonate
investigations.indication: Baseline electrolyte assessment and monitoring during IV correction

investigations.name: Blood Glucose
investigations.indication: Baseline metabolic assessment and before selecting dextrose-containing fluids

investigations.name: Renal Function Tests
investigations.indication: Assess dehydration severity, renal perfusion, and ongoing monitoring

investigations.name: Urine output monitoring
investigations.indication: Guide fluid therapy and assess renal perfusion

investigations.name: Weight
investigations.indication: Needed for calculating free water deficit and maintenance fluids

counselling: High sodium is usually due to water loss more than salt loss and needs slow correction. — ज्यादा सोडियम आमतौर पर शरीर में पानी की कमी से होता है और धीरे-धीरे ठीक करना जरूरी है।
Do not give plain water or change IV fluids abruptly without medical advice. — डॉक्टर की सलाह के बिना सादा पानी न दें या IV तरल पदार्थ अचानक न बदलें।
Treatment is usually gradual over 1 to 3 days depending on sodium level. — इलाज आमतौर पर 1 से 3 दिन में धीरे-धीरे किया जाता है।
Strict monitoring of urine output, sensorium, and repeat sodium tests is essential. — पेशाब, होश और सोडियम जांच की सख्त निगरानी जरूरी है।
Come back immediately or alert staff if the child becomes drowsy, irritable, or has seizures. — अगर बच्चा सुस्त, चिड़चिड़ा हो जाए या दौरे पड़ें तो तुरंत स्टाफ को बताएं।
warning_signs: Reduced urine output or inability to drink
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric intensivist/nephrologist if serum sodium is markedly elevated, correction is complex, there is shock, neurological symptoms, persistent sodium rise, or associated renal dysfunction.
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if serum sodium >145 mEq/L with dehydration needing IV fluids, neurological symptoms, shock, inability to tolerate oral fluids, or need for serial biochemical monitoring.
notes: Hypernatremia correction must be slow. If child is in shock, restore circulation first with isotonic fluid. Acute hypernatremia can be corrected over 24-36 hours; chronic hypernatremia usually over 48 hours or longer. Rapid reduction can cause cerebral edema.
expected_course: Serum sodium should fall gradually, generally not more than 10-12 mEq/L per 24 hours. Clinical improvement parallels rehydration and normalization of sensorium over 24-84 hours depending on severity.
key_clinical_points: Treat shock first with isotonic saline before starting planned correction.
Target sodium fall should not exceed about 0.5 mEq/L/hr.
Seizures during correction suggest overly rapid sodium fall and need urgent reassessment.

severity_assessment.mild: Serum sodium 146-150 mEq/L, mild thirst or dehydration, stable vitals.
severity_assessment.moderate: Serum sodium 151-169 mEq/L with clinical dehydration, irritability, poor feeding, or reduced urine output.
severity_assessment.severe: Serum sodium >=170 mEq/L, shock, neurological symptoms, seizures, or significant dehydration.

first_line_drugs.notes: Symptomatic (Na <125 with seizures/encephalopathy): 4–6 ml/kg IV over 30 min; may repeat 2 ml/kg over 15 min if seizures persist; target rise 5–10 mEq/L
Asymptomatic (Na <125): correct slowly using calculated Na deficit over 4–24 hrs
50–75% of maintenance fluids in SIADH-related hyponatremia

second_line_drugs.notes: Used in hypovolemic hyponatremia to restore volume
1 mg/kg IV in selected cases (SIADH with fluid overload)

counselling: Low sodium levels can affect brain function — सोडियम कम होने से दिमाग की कार्यप्रणाली प्रभावित हो सकती है।
Treatment will be done slowly to avoid complications — इलाज धीरे-धीरे किया जाएगा ताकि कोई दिक्कत न हो।
Fluid intake may be restricted as advised — डॉक्टर की सलाह अनुसार पानी/तरल पदार्थ सीमित रखें।
Regular blood tests are required for monitoring — निगरानी के लिए नियमित ब्लड टेस्ट ज़रूरी हैं।
Follow-up is important to identify underlying cause — असली कारण पता लगाने के लिए फॉलो-अप ज़रूरी है।
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric intensivist if symptomatic hyponatremia or rapid sodium fall
hospitalisation_criteria: Na <125 mEq/L, neurological symptoms, seizures, or need for IV correction
notes: Avoid rapid correction. Increase Na should not exceed 10–12 mEq/L/day or 0.5 mEq/L/hr to prevent osmotic demyelination.
source: IAP Guidelines 2024, Pediatric Electrolyte Management Protocols
guideline_changes: Emphasis on controlled correction rate and early use of 3% saline in symptomatic cases
expected_course: Improvement within 24–48 hrs with proper correction
key_clinical_points: Treat symptoms first (especially seizures)
Correct sodium slowly
Always identify and treat underlying cause (e.g., SIADH)

severity_assessment.mild: Na 130–134 mEq/L, no symptoms
severity_assessment.moderate: Na 125–129 mEq/L, mild symptoms (nausea, irritability)
severity_assessment.severe: Na <125 mEq/L with seizures, coma, encephalopathy

diagnosis_name: Metabolic Acidosis / Indications for Sodium Bicarbonate Therapy

first_line_drugs.notes: Indicated in selected severe metabolic acidosis: pH <7.1 and/or bicarbonate <8-10 mEq/L and/or base excess >10 mmol/L. Dose based on HCO3 deficit: HCO3 needed (mEq) = 0.3 x weight (kg) x base deficit (or 0.3 x weight x (24 - serum HCO3)). Give half of calculated dose over 60 minutes, then reassess blood gases.

Prepare by mixing sodium bicarbonate solution with equal volume of sterile water or compatible solution. Administer as slow IV infusion over 30-60 minutes. In emergency, may be given as bolus 1 mEq/kg.

second_line_drugs.notes: Examples: shock correction, sepsis treatment, insulin for DKA, antidote/toxin management, ventilation support, renal support depending on etiology.

investigations.name: Arterial / Venous Blood Gas
investigations.indication: Confirm severe metabolic acidosis and monitor response to bicarbonate

investigations.name: Serum Electrolytes including HCO3, Na, K, Cl
investigations.indication: Assess severity, base deficit, associated electrolyte abnormalities

investigations.name: Renal Function Tests
investigations.indication: Assess renal cause or consequence of acidosis

investigations.name: Lactate / Anion Gap / Cause-specific tests
investigations.indication: Evaluate high anion gap metabolic acidosis and underlying etiology

counselling: Sodium bicarbonate is not used routinely in all acidosis cases. — सोडियम बाइकार्बोनेट हर एसिडोसिस में नहीं दिया जाता।
It is reserved for selected severe situations after doctor assessment. — यह केवल गंभीर स्थितियों में डॉक्टर की जांच के बाद दिया जाता है।
Treatment of the underlying cause is more important than bicarbonate alone. — मूल कारण का इलाज बाइकार्बोनेट से ज्यादा जरूरी है।
Frequent blood gas and electrolyte monitoring is needed during therapy. — इलाज के दौरान बार-बार ब्लड गैस और इलेक्ट्रोलाइट जांच जरूरी है।
Inform staff immediately if breathing worsens, irritability increases, or seizures occur. — सांस बिगड़ने, चिड़चिड़ापन बढ़ने या दौरे पड़ने पर तुरंत स्टाफ को बताएं।
warning_signs: Worsening acidosis on repeat blood gas
referral_criteria: Refer to PICU/intensivist if severe metabolic acidosis, high anion gap acidosis with hemodynamic compromise, cardiopulmonary resuscitation setting, toxin-related acidosis, or refractory acidosis.
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if pH <7.2, bicarbonate markedly low, shock, altered sensorium, need for IV bicarbonate, suspected toxin/metabolic disease, or need for serial blood gas monitoring.
notes: Routine use in older children is not recommended unless specific indications are present. Common indications include severe normal anion gap acidosis, selected severe high anion gap acidosis, hyperkalemia, TCA poisoning, beta-blocker poisoning, and some peri-resuscitation situations. Ensure adequate ventilation before giving bicarbonate.
source: Provided sodium bicarbonate indication and administration protocol
guideline_changes: 2024 real-world study shows NaHCO3 benefit only when Cl >=113 mmol/L; harmful when Cl <107
expected_course: pH and bicarbonate improve gradually after infusion if the underlying cause is also corrected. Repeat reassessment is required within 1-2 hours or sooner in unstable patients.
key_clinical_points: Do not use sodium bicarbonate routinely in all acidosis.
Correct severe acidosis only in selected indications and reassess with blood gas.
Ensure IV line patency, compatibility, and adequate ventilation before administration.

severity_assessment.mild: Mild acidosis without hemodynamic compromise; usually treat underlying cause only.
severity_assessment.moderate: Biochemical acidosis with clinical illness but no clear bicarbonate indication yet.
severity_assessment.severe: pH <7.1 and/or HCO3 <8-10 mEq/L, significant base deficit, shock, cardiac dysfunction, or selected toxic/metabolic indication.

monitoring_parameters.frequency: After first infusion and then 1-2 hourly as needed

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg/dose IV (approximately 1 mL/kg/dose) over 5-10 minutes for ECG changes or severe hyperkalemia; membrane stabilizing; onset 1-3 min; duration about 30 min
1-2 mEq/kg/dose IV over 5-10 minutes; especially useful if associated acidosis; onset 10-30 min; duration about 2 hours
Glucose 0.5 g/kg (approximately 2 mL/kg of 25% dextrose) plus regular insulin 0.1 U/kg IV over 15-30 minutes; shifts potassium intracellularly; onset about 30 min; duration 2-4 hours
2.5 mg if <25 kg, 5 mg if >25 kg, nebulized over 10 minutes; promotes intracellular shift of potassium; onset about 120 min

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 g orally or rectally; enhances gastrointestinal potassium elimination; onset 60-120 min; duration 4-6 hours
1-2 mg/kg IV if urine output is adequate; enhances urinary potassium excretion; onset 15-30 min; duration 4-6 hours

investigations.name: 12-lead ECG
investigations.indication: All suspected or confirmed hyperkalemia; assess ECG changes and severity

investigations.name: Serum Potassium
investigations.indication: Diagnosis and serial monitoring during treatment

investigations.name: Renal Function Tests
investigations.indication: Assess renal cause and need for dialysis

investigations.name: Urine Output Monitoring
investigations.indication: Guide diuretic use and assess renal perfusion

counselling: High potassium can affect the heart and may become life-threatening. — ज्यादा पोटैशियम दिल को प्रभावित कर सकता है और जानलेवा हो सकता है।
Continuous monitoring is needed until potassium becomes safe. — पोटैशियम सामान्य होने तक लगातार निगरानी जरूरी है।
All potassium-containing oral feeds, IV fluids, and medicines should be reviewed and stopped if advised. — पोटैशियम वाले खाने, तरल पदार्थ और दवाइयाँ बंद करें जैसा डॉक्टर कहें।
Do not use over-the-counter supplements or salt substitutes unless approved. — डॉक्टर की मंजूरी के बिना कोई सप्लीमेंट या नमक का विकल्प न लें।
Seek urgent care if weakness, palpitations, or reduced urine output develops. — कमजोरी, धड़कन बढ़ना या पेशाब कम होने पर तुरंत डॉक्टर को दिखाएं।
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric intensivist/nephrologist if ECG changes are present, serum potassium is markedly elevated, medical treatment fails, renal failure is suspected, or dialysis may be required.
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if serum potassium >5.5 mEq/L with symptoms, ECG changes, renal dysfunction, ongoing potassium load, or need for IV therapy/cardiac monitoring.
notes: Obtain ECG and place on cardiac monitor immediately. Eliminate potassium from diet and parenteral fluids. Stop drugs that can worsen hyperkalemia, such as ACE inhibitors and beta blockers. Dialysis is recommended if medical measures are unsuccessful.
guideline_changes: 2024 Janjua review updated pediatric hyperkalemia management algorithm
expected_course: Cardiac stabilization is immediate after calcium therapy, while potassium shift and elimination occur over minutes to hours. Serial reassessment is required until potassium normalizes and ECG changes resolve.
key_clinical_points: Calcium stabilizes myocardium but does not lower total body potassium.
Insulin with glucose, bicarbonate, and salbutamol shift potassium intracellularly.
Definitive potassium removal may require resin, diuresis, or dialysis.

severity_assessment.mild: Serum K+ 5.5-6.5 mEq/L; may show tall peaked T waves and prolonged PR interval.
severity_assessment.moderate: Serum K+ 6.5-8 mEq/L; may show loss of P waves, QRS widening, ST changes, ectopic beats, or escape rhythm.
severity_assessment.severe: Serum K+ >8 mEq/L or any level with severe ECG changes such as sine wave pattern, AV block, ventricular arrhythmias, bradycardia, or asystole.

monitoring_parameters.frequency: 30-60 min after insulin, then as needed

first_line_drugs.notes: For severe hypokalemia (serum K+ <2.5 mEq/L) or hypokalemia with ECG changes: prepare stock solution with 90 mL Normal Saline + 10 mL KCl; infuse at 1.5-2.5 mL/kg/hour to deliver 0.3-0.5 mEq/kg/hour under strict continuous ECG monitoring; continue until serum K+ is at least 3 mEq/L or ECG changes normalize
For mild to moderate hypokalemia without ECG changes and when oral route is tolerated; dose as per potassium deficit and repeat serum potassium monitoring

second_line_drugs.notes: Use in refractory hypokalemia or suspected magnesium deficiency: 25-50 mg/kg slow IV infusion; approximate volume 0.05-0.1 mL/kg of 50% solution; may repeat every 6 hours for total 2-3 doses

investigations.name: Serum Potassium
investigations.indication: Diagnosis and repeat monitoring during correction

investigations.name: ECG
investigations.indication: Severe hypokalemia, symptoms, or suspected arrhythmia

investigations.name: Renal Function Tests
investigations.indication: Assess renal handling and safety of potassium replacement

counselling: Low potassium can affect muscle strength and heart rhythm. — पोटैशियम कम होने से मांसपेशियों में कमज़ोरी और दिल की धड़कन बिगड़ सकती है।
Severe low potassium may need IV correction and heart monitoring. — गंभीर कमी में नस द्वारा दवा और दिल की निगरानी ज़रूरी है।
Do not stop treatment once symptoms improve; repeat blood tests are important. — लक्षण ठीक होने पर भी इलाज बंद न करें; दोबारा ब्लड टेस्ट करवाएं।
Vomiting, diarrhea, poor intake, and some medicines can worsen potassium loss. — उल्टी, दस्त, कम खाना और कुछ दवाइयों से पोटैशियम और कम हो सकता है।
Return urgently if weakness, palpitations, breathing difficulty, or persistent vomiting develops. — कमज़ोरी, धड़कन बढ़ना, साँस में तकलीफ़ या लगातार उल्टी हो तो तुरंत आएं।
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric intensivist/nephrologist if serum K+ <2.5 mEq/L, ECG changes, arrhythmia, respiratory weakness, refractory hypokalemia, or suspected renal/tubular disorder.
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if serum K+ <2.5 mEq/L, ECG changes, symptomatic hypokalemia, inability to tolerate oral replacement, ongoing losses, or need for IV correction.
notes: Mild hypokalemia is often asymptomatic. Severe hypokalemia can present with muscle weakness, paralytic ileus, respiratory weakness, hypotonia, areflexia, ECG changes, and arrhythmia. Correct slowly and monitor closely.
expected_course: Clinical improvement usually occurs as serum potassium rises; ECG changes and muscle symptoms improve after adequate correction, but repeated monitoring is required until stable.
key_clinical_points: Severe hypokalemia or ECG changes require urgent IV correction.
Continuous ECG monitoring is needed during rapid IV potassium replacement.
Always consider magnesium deficiency in refractory hypokalemia.

severity_assessment.mild: Serum K+ 2.5-3.5 mEq/L, usually asymptomatic or nonspecific symptoms only.
severity_assessment.moderate: Serum K+ around 2.5-3.0 mEq/L with weakness, nausea, vomiting, or ECG changes beginning to appear.
severity_assessment.severe: Serum K+ <2.5 mEq/L or any potassium level with significant ECG changes, arrhythmia, respiratory weakness, or paralysis.

monitoring_parameters.frequency: 4-6 hourly during IV correction, then daily once stable
Repeat as indicated in refractory cases

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-30 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for mitochondrial disorders; higher doses not necessarily more effective
50-100 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for carnitine deficiency or secondary deficiency; max 3 g/day

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-400 mg/day PO for Complex I/II defects and MADD; assess response over 3 months
5-10 mg/kg/day PO as mitochondrial antioxidant adjunct

investigations.name: Serum lactate and pyruvate
investigations.indication: Elevated lactate:pyruvate ratio suggests mitochondrial disease

investigations.name: Plasma acylcarnitine profile
investigations.indication: Screen for fatty acid oxidation defects

investigations.name: Genetic testing (mitochondrial panel/WES)
investigations.indication: Identify specific mutation for diagnosis and prognosis

investigations.name: Echocardiogram
investigations.indication: Screen for cardiomyopathy in mitochondrial disease

counselling: Your child has a metabolic disorder that affects energy production in the body's cells
Give the vitamin and supplement cocktail prescribed (CoQ10, carnitine, riboflavin) daily without fail
Avoid prolonged fasting and strenuous exercise — both can trigger a crisis
During illness or fever, bring the child to hospital early — do not wait for things to worsen
Regular follow-up every 3-6 months with the metabolic team is essential
referral_criteria: Metabolic genetics specialist mandatory. Neurology for seizures or regression. Cardiology for cardiomyopathy. Ophthalmology for retinopathy. Audiology for sensorineural hearing loss
hospitalisation_criteria: Acute metabolic crisis (lactic acidosis, seizures, stroke-like episodes). Cardiac decompensation. Intercurrent illness with poor oral intake. New diagnosis requiring evaluation
notes: Metabolic disorders require individualized cocktail therapy targeting specific enzyme defects. Evidence for mitochondrial supplements is limited but CoQ10 and carnitine are widely used.
guideline_changes: FDA granted Rare Pediatric Disease designation for BPM31510IV for primary CoQ10 deficiency (2024)

diagnosis_name: Sleep disorders not due to a substance or known physiological condition

first_line_drugs.notes: Consistent bedtime routine; limit screens 1 hr before bed; cool dark room; regular wake time
Graduated extinction or bedtime fading; stimulus control; for ages 0-5; parent-directed
Sleep restriction, stimulus control, cognitive restructuring; 4-8 sessions; ages 7+

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 mg initial (ages 2-5), 1-3 mg (ages 6-12), 3-5 mg (adolescents); PO 30-60 min before bed
2 mg PO at bedtime; may increase to 5 mg; pediatric-licensed formulation for ASD/Smith-Magenis
0.05-0.1 mg PO at bedtime; off-label for sleep-onset delay; monitor BP; ages 6+
0.5-1 mg/kg PO at bedtime; max 50 mg; short-term use for sleep-onset difficulty; antihistamine

investigations.name: Polysomnography
investigations.indication: If obstructive sleep apnea or parasomnia suspected

investigations.name: Ferritin
investigations.indication: Low ferritin associated with restless legs/poor sleep

counselling: Set a fixed bedtime and wake time — even on weekends
No screens (TV, phone, tablet) for at least 1 hour before bedtime
Keep the bedroom cool, dark, and quiet — use a nightlight if needed
Avoid caffeinated drinks (cola, tea, chocolate) after afternoon
A calm bedtime routine (bath, story, lullaby) signals the brain to sleep
referral_criteria: Refer to sleep specialist or child psychiatrist if sleep disorder persists beyond 3 months despite behavioral interventions, or if sleep apnea/parasomnia suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation rarely needed; admit only if severe sleep deprivation causing safety concerns or for polysomnography in complex cases
notes: Behavioral interventions are first-line for all pediatric insomnia. Use pharmaceutical-grade melatonin only; OTC products have inconsistent dosing per FDA/IPSA warnings.
guideline_changes: 2025 IPSA consensus: melatonin only after behavioral interventions fail; pharmaceutical-grade preferred

first_line_drugs.notes: Individualized education program; modified curriculum; resource room or inclusion support; IDEA-mandated
1-3 sessions/week; targeting functional communication, social language, and literacy
Positive behavior support; functional communication training; social skills groups; ongoing
1-2 sessions/week; adaptive daily living skills, fine motor, vocational readiness

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.3 mg/kg/dose BID-TID; for comorbid ADHD; start low, titrate cautiously; monitor closely
0.25-0.5 mg/day initial, titrate to 0.5-2 mg/day; for severe aggression/irritability; short-term
5-10 mg/day initial, titrate to 10-20 mg/day; for comorbid anxiety or depression; ages 8+

investigations.name: Hearing assessment (BERA/OAE)
investigations.indication: Rule out hearing loss contributing to delays

investigations.name: MRI brain
investigations.indication: If dysmorphic features or focal neurological signs

counselling: Your child can learn and grow — focus on their strengths and what they can do
Enroll in special education or inclusive school with resource room support
Practice daily living skills (dressing, brushing teeth) at home with repetition and praise
Join a parent support group for guidance and emotional support
Regular follow-up every 6 months to reassess developmental progress and therapies
referral_criteria: Refer to developmental pediatrician or child psychiatrist if comorbid ADHD, anxiety, seizures, or behavioral crisis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit only for acute behavioral crisis with self-harm/aggression not manageable outpatient, or for inpatient evaluation of seizures
notes: No medication treats intellectual disability itself; pharmacotherapy targets comorbid psychiatric conditions. Most with mild ID achieve functional independence with support.
guideline_changes: Increased focus on functional communication training to reduce challenging behaviors

first_line_drugs.notes: Self-contained or supported classroom; functional academics; life skills curriculum; IDEA-mandated
2-5 sessions/week; augmentative/alternative communication devices; functional communication goals
2-3 sessions/week; self-care, feeding, dressing skills; sensory integration; adaptive equipment
10-25 hrs/week; for adaptive skill building and challenging behavior reduction; individualized

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.25 mg/day initial, titrate to 0.5-2 mg/day; for severe aggression/self-injury; FDA-approved 5-16y
0.3 mg/kg/dose BID; for comorbid ADHD; lower efficacy in moderate ID; monitor closely
0.5-3 mg PO 30-60 min before bed; for comorbid sleep-onset insomnia; common in ID
10-15 mg/kg/day initial; for comorbid epilepsy (common in moderate ID); monitor levels/LFTs

investigations.name: EEG
investigations.indication: If seizures suspected (common in moderate ID)

investigations.name: Metabolic screen (TMS, urine GCMS)
investigations.indication: Rule out inborn errors of metabolism

counselling: Your child will benefit greatly from structured routines and visual schedules at home
Speech therapy and AAC devices can help your child communicate better — practice at home daily
Teach self-care skills step by step with patience — dressing, eating, toileting
Monitor for seizures (present in 15-30%) and report any unusual episodes
Connect with local disability support organizations for government benefits and school placement
referral_criteria: Refer to child psychiatrist for severe aggression/self-injury, neurologist for seizures, or genetics if cause unidentified
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for uncontrolled seizures, severe self-injurious behavior, or acute medical illness where cognitive impairment complicates outpatient management
notes: Individuals with moderate ID typically achieve supported semi-independent living. Treat comorbid epilepsy (present in ~15-30%) and psychiatric conditions proactively.
guideline_changes: AAC (augmentative/alternative communication) increasingly emphasized as early intervention

diagnosis_name: Specific developmental disorders of speech and language

first_line_drugs.notes: Structured parent training (e.g. Heidelberg model) for expressive language delay; start by age 2-3
Direct child-centered therapy by SLP; 1-3 sessions/wk; phonological and language-focused
Targeted phonological or integrated phonological treatment for speech sound disorders

second_line_drugs.notes: Picture exchange, speech-generating devices for severe expressive impairment
Motor approach for isolated phonetic speech sound disorders

investigations.name: Hearing assessment (OAE/BERA)
investigations.indication: Mandatory for all children with speech delay; hearing loss is leading treatable cause

investigations.name: Developmental assessment
investigations.indication: Evaluate for global developmental delay vs isolated language delay

investigations.name: Autism screening (M-CHAT)
investigations.indication: Rule out ASD which commonly presents with speech delay

counselling: Child has delayed speech or language development; early therapy gives the best results
Talk to your child frequently; narrate daily activities in simple language
Reduce screen time; face-to-face interaction is best for language learning
Read picture books together daily; let child point and name objects
Speech therapy should be regular (1-3 times per week); practice exercises at home too
referral_criteria: Refer to speech-language pathologist by age 2 if not using words; audiology for all speech-delayed children
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalization not required for speech/language disorders
notes: Begin intervention by age 2-3 as only ~1/3 of late talkers catch up spontaneously. For multilingual children, provide therapy in all languages when possible.
guideline_changes: 2024 interdisciplinary CPG published with updated intervention recommendations for DLD

diagnosis_name: Specific developmental disorders of scholastic skills

first_line_drugs.notes: Orton-Gillingham-based phonics instruction; 4-5 sessions/week; 30-60 min; individualized
School-based specialized instruction; legally mandated accommodations under IDEA; annual review
1-3 sessions/week; phonological awareness, reading fluency; for language-based learning disorders

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 sessions/week; handwriting, visual-motor integration; for dysgraphia component
Text-to-speech, audiobooks, speech-to-text software; classroom accommodations
Structured math tutoring for dyscalculia; multisensory approaches; 3-5 sessions/week

investigations.name: Psychoeducational assessment (IQ + achievement testing)
investigations.indication: Diagnose specific learning disability — discrepancy between ability and achievement

investigations.name: Hearing assessment
investigations.indication: Rule out hearing loss affecting reading/language development

investigations.name: Vision assessment
investigations.indication: Rule out visual problems affecting reading

investigations.name: ADHD screening (Conners/Vanderbilt)
investigations.indication: ADHD comorbid in 30-50% of children with learning disabilities

counselling: Your child has a learning disability — this is NOT due to low intelligence or laziness
Structured reading programs (like Orton-Gillingham) are the most effective treatment for dyslexia
The child has a legal right to accommodations in school — an IEP (individualized education program) should be set up
Extra time on tests, use of audiobooks, and typing instead of handwriting can all help
Early identification and intervention (before age 7) gives the best outcomes — start therapy now
referral_criteria: Educational psychologist for formal psychoeducational testing. Learning disability specialist for structured literacy intervention. School for IEP evaluation. Speech-language pathologist for language-based learning issues. Psychiatry if comorbid ADHD/anxiety
hospitalisation_criteria: Not applicable — learning disabilities do not require hospitalisation. Admit only for severe comorbid psychiatric crisis (suicidality from academic stress/bullying)
notes: No pharmacologic treatment for learning disabilities; medications only for comorbid ADHD/anxiety. Early identification before age 7 yields best outcomes.
guideline_changes: Emphasis on structured literacy and Orton-Gillingham approaches for dyslexia intervention

first_line_drugs.notes: Physical therapy with task-specific motor training; evidence-based per APTA CPG; 1-3x/wk
Activity-based intervention targeting daily living skills and fine motor tasks
Structured home exercises and environmental modifications per therapist guidance

second_line_drugs.notes: Cognitive Orientation to Occupational Performance; child-directed problem solving for motor tasks
0.3-1 mg/kg/day PO for comorbid ADHD if present; start low and titrate

investigations.name: Movement ABC-2
investigations.indication: Standardized motor assessment for DCD diagnosis

investigations.name: Vision screening
investigations.indication: Rule out visual problems affecting coordination

investigations.name: Cognitive assessment
investigations.indication: Rule out intellectual disability; identify learning needs

counselling: Child has difficulty with coordination and motor skills; this is called DCD (developmental coordination disorder)
Occupational therapy and physical therapy are the main treatments
Practice motor skills through fun activities and play at home
This is not laziness; child is trying hard but their brain processes movement differently
Many children improve significantly with consistent therapy; be patient and encouraging
referral_criteria: Refer to occupational therapist and physiotherapist; screen for ADHD and learning disabilities
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalization not typically required
notes: Screen for comorbid ADHD, ASD, and language disorders which are common. No pharmacotherapy for DCD itself; intervention focuses on task-oriented motor skill training.

diagnosis_name: Pervasive developmental disorders / autism spectrum disorders

first_line_drugs.notes: 25-40 hrs/week intensive or 10-25 hrs/week focused; individualized; ages 2+; gold standard
1-5 sessions/week; targeting functional communication; may include AAC devices
1-3 sessions/week; sensory integration, fine motor, self-care skills; individualized
0.25 mg/day initial (<20kg) or 0.5 mg/day (>20kg); titrate to 0.5-3 mg/day; FDA-approved 5-16y for irritability

second_line_drugs.notes: 2 mg/day initial, titrate to 5-15 mg/day; FDA-approved ages 6-17 for irritability in ASD
1 mg/day initial, titrate to 1-4 mg/day; for hyperactivity/impulsivity in ASD; off-label
0.3 mg/kg/dose initial, titrate to max 1 mg/kg/dose; for comorbid ADHD symptoms; lower doses in ASD
2.5-5 mg/day initial, titrate to 10-40 mg/day; for anxiety/repetitive behaviors in ASD; off-label

investigations.name: Standardized autism assessment (ADOS-2, CARS-2, ISAA in India)
Hearing assessment (OAE/BERA)
investigations.indication: Mandatory — rule out hearing loss mimicking ASD

investigations.name: Chromosomal microarray
investigations.indication: Genetic cause found in 10-20% — recommended for all newly diagnosed

investigations.name: Fragile X testing
investigations.indication: Most common inherited cause of intellectual disability with ASD features

investigations.name: Lead level
investigations.indication: Pica behavior in ASD children increases lead exposure risk

counselling: Your child has autism spectrum disorder — early intensive behavioral therapy is the most important treatment
ABA therapy (applied behavior analysis) for 25-40 hours per week gives the best outcomes
Speech therapy will help your child communicate — even non-verbal children can learn alternative communication
Create a structured routine at home — children with ASD thrive with predictability
Medicines do not cure autism but can help with associated problems like irritability, hyperactivity, or anxiety
referral_criteria: Developmental pediatrics/child psychiatrist for diagnosis and management. ABA therapy provider. Speech-language pathologist. Occupational therapist. School for IEP/special education placement. Genetics (chromosomal microarray, Fragile X) for all newly diagnosed children
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Consider if: severe self-injurious behavior not manageable at home, acute behavioral crisis with risk of harm, medication adjustment requiring close monitoring, comorbid medical condition requiring admission
notes: Behavioral and developmental therapies are primary treatment; medications target co-occurring symptoms, not core ASD. Monitor weight at baseline and quarterly on antipsychotics.
guideline_changes: 2024 guidelines expand pharmacologic options for co-occurring irritability and emphasize early ABA

first_line_drugs.notes: Start 5mg BID for age 6yr+, titrate by 5mg/week, max 60mg/day
18-54mg once daily AM, Concerta or Ritalin LA

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5 mg/kg/day for 4 weeks then 1.2 mg/kg/day (max 100mg), non-stimulant
1mg/day HS, titrate to 1-4mg/day, non-stimulant alpha-2 agonist

investigations.name: Vanderbilt Assessment Scale (parent and teacher)
investigations.indication: Standardised ADHD rating for diagnosis and monitoring

investigations.name: Vision and hearing screening
investigations.indication: Rule out sensory deficits mimicking inattention

investigations.indication: If clinical suspicion of thyroid disorder
