investigations.indication: Before starting stimulants if family history of cardiac disease or sudden death

counselling: ADHD is a medical condition, not bad behaviour - the child needs support, not punishment
Medicine works best when combined with behavioural strategies and structured routines
Give methylphenidate in the morning; monitor appetite and sleep
Regular follow-up every 3 months to check weight, height, blood pressure and heart rate
Inform school teachers about the condition; structured classroom seating and shorter task durations help
referral_criteria: Refer to child psychiatrist/developmental pediatrician for initial diagnosis, comorbid conditions (anxiety, learning disability, autism), medication non-response, or significant side effects
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation rarely needed; consider if acute suicidal ideation, severe aggression with safety concerns, or need for inpatient behavioural assessment
notes: Behaviour therapy first-line for age 4-5yr. Stimulants first-line for age 6yr+. Monitor growth, BP, HR.

first_line_drugs.notes: Structured behavioral therapy; weekly sessions for 12-16 weeks; caregiver-focused
Individual or group CBT; weekly sessions targeting anger management and problem-solving skills
0.25-0.5 mg/day initial, titrate to 0.5-3 mg/day in 2 divided doses; ages 5-17

second_line_drugs.notes: 300 mg BID-TID; target serum level 0.6-1.2 mEq/L; monitor renal/thyroid; ages 7+
2 mg/day initial, titrate to 5-15 mg/day; ages 6-17; monitor metabolic
Intensive community-based family therapy; 3-5 months duration; for severe cases

investigations.name: Standardized behavioral rating scales (CBCL, Conners)
investigations.indication: Quantify behavior problems, screen for ADHD, monitor treatment response

investigations.name: Psychoeducational testing
investigations.indication: Screen for comorbid learning disability (50% comorbidity)

investigations.name: Lead level
investigations.indication: Lead exposure associated with behavioral problems in Indian children

counselling: Your child has behavior problems that need structured treatment — this is a medical condition, not just bad behavior
Parent management training (PMT) is the most effective first step — learning consistent strategies at home
Set clear rules and consistent consequences — positive reinforcement works better than punishment
Medicine may be needed if behavioral therapy alone is not enough — your doctor will guide you
Screen time, peer group, and family stress all affect behavior — address these alongside treatment
referral_criteria: Child psychiatrist for medication management. Clinical psychologist for CBT/PMT. School counselor for academic support. Social services if family dysfunction or abuse contributing. Developmental pediatrics to screen for comorbid ADHD/learning disability
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Consider if: risk of self-harm or harm to others, acute psychotic features, failed outpatient treatment with escalating aggression, need for psychiatric stabilization
notes: Always treat comorbid ADHD/depression first. Pharmacotherapy only after psychosocial interventions fail; short-term antipsychotic use recommended.
guideline_changes: Network meta-analysis confirms SGAs most efficacious for disruptive behaviors

diagnosis_name: Emotional disorders with onset specific to childhood

first_line_drugs.notes: Individual or group CBT with exposure; 12-16 weekly sessions; ages 6-18; CAMS-supported
10 mg/day initial, titrate to max 20 mg/day; ages 7+; for separation/generalized anxiety
25 mg/day initial (6-12y), 50 mg/day (12+); titrate to max 200 mg/day; off-label for anxiety

second_line_drugs.notes: 25 mg/day initial, titrate to 50-200 mg/day; FDA-approved for pediatric OCD ages 8+
30 mg/day initial, max 60-120 mg/day; FDA-approved for GAD ages 7+
5 mg/day initial, titrate to 10-20 mg/day; ages 12+; off-label for anxiety
1-3 mg/kg/day in divided doses; last-line for refractory separation anxiety; ECG monitoring

investigations.name: Standardized anxiety scales (SCARED, GAD-7)
investigations.indication: Quantify anxiety severity and monitor treatment response

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Rule out anemia (fatigue mistaken for anxiety/depression)

counselling: Your child has anxiety that is stronger than normal — this is very treatable with therapy and sometimes medicine
CBT (cognitive behavioral therapy) is the gold standard — it teaches the child to manage anxiety
Do not force the child into feared situations suddenly — gradual exposure works much better
Avoid excessive reassurance — teach the child to cope rather than avoiding all anxious situations
Combination of therapy + medicine (if needed) gives the best results — up to 80% improvement
referral_criteria: Child psychiatrist if SSRI needed. Clinical psychologist for CBT. School counselor for school-based accommodations. Developmental pediatrics for comorbid ADHD or learning issues
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Consider if: school refusal >4 weeks not responding to outpatient treatment, suicidality, severe self-harm, panic disorder with agoraphobia causing inability to function, comorbid depression
notes: Combination CBT+SSRI yields ~81% improvement vs 55% SSRI alone (CAMS trial). Benzodiazepines not recommended for pediatric anxiety.
guideline_changes: 2020 AACAP guideline recommends combined CBT+SSRI for moderate-severe anxiety

diagnosis_name: Disorders of social functioning with onset specific to childhood and adolescence

first_line_drugs.notes: Graduated exposure-based CBT; weekly sessions 12-20 weeks; for selective mutism and social anxiety
For reactive attachment disorder (F94.1); 12-25 sessions; caregiver involvement essential
Primary intervention for RAD/DSED; establish safe consistent caregiving environment

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-10 mg/day initial, titrate to 10-40 mg/day; off-label for selective mutism; ages 5+
12.5-25 mg/day initial, titrate to 50-200 mg/day; off-label for social anxiety; ages 6+
Child-centered or directive play therapy; weekly sessions; for younger children unable to do CBT

investigations.name: Developmental assessment
investigations.indication: Comprehensive evaluation to rule out ASD, intellectual disability

investigations.name: Hearing assessment (audiometry)
investigations.indication: Rule out hearing loss as cause of social withdrawal

investigations.name: Standardized anxiety scales
investigations.indication: Screen for anxiety (comorbid in selective mutism)

investigations.name: Thyroid function
investigations.indication: Rule out thyroid disorder affecting behavior

counselling: Your child has difficulty with social interactions — this responds best to therapy, not medicine
A stable, consistent, and loving home environment is the most important factor for improvement
CBT with gradual exposure helps children with selective mutism speak in social situations
Be patient — progress may be slow but steady. Avoid forcing the child to speak
School coordination is important — teachers should understand the condition and provide accommodations
referral_criteria: Child psychiatrist if medication needed (SSRI for selective mutism). Clinical psychologist for CBT/play therapy. School coordination for accommodations. Social services for foster care/placement stability in RAD
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Consider if: severe self-harm risk, acute attachment crisis with danger, comorbid psychiatric emergency (suicidality, psychosis)
notes: No FDA-approved medications for RAD; stable caregiving is cornerstone. For selective mutism, CBT with graduated exposure is first-line; SSRIs adjunctive.
guideline_changes: Updated AACAP anxiety guideline now covers selective mutism under anxiety spectrum

first_line_drugs.notes: Habit reversal + relaxation training; 8 sessions over 10 weeks; ages 9+; AAN Level B
0.05 mg/day initial, titrate to 0.1-0.4 mg/day in divided doses; monitor BP; ages 6+
0.5 mg/day initial, titrate to 1-4 mg/day; extended-release preferred; monitor BP

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/day initial, titrate to 2-20 mg/day; for moderate-severe tics; monitor metabolic
0.5-1 mg/day initial, titrate to 1.5-10 mg/day; typical antipsychotic; monitor for EPS
25 mg/day initial, titrate to 50-200 mg/day; AAN Level C evidence; monitor cognition
12.5 mg/day initial, titrate to 25-75 mg/day; for refractory tics; monitor for depression

investigations.name: Clinical assessment with Yale Global Tic Severity Scale (YGTSS)
investigations.indication: Quantify tic severity and track treatment response

investigations.name: Screening for ADHD and OCD (rating scales)
investigations.indication: Comorbid conditions — present in >50% of Tourette patients

investigations.name: Throat culture (RADT/ASO titer)
investigations.indication: If sudden onset tics — rule out PANDAS/PANS

investigations.name: TSH
investigations.indication: Thyroid disease can cause movement abnormalities

counselling: Tics are involuntary movements or sounds — your child cannot control them, so do not scold or punish
Tics tend to come and go — they may increase with stress and decrease during focused activities
CBIT (behavioral therapy) is the first treatment — it teaches the child techniques to manage tics
Treat comorbid ADHD or OCD first — these usually cause more problems than the tics themselves
Most children with tics improve significantly by adulthood — reassurance is important
referral_criteria: Pediatric neurologist for diagnosis and medication management. Clinical psychologist for CBIT. Psychiatry if comorbid OCD/ADHD needs treatment. School for accommodations (extra time, breaks)
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Consider only for: tic status (severe continuous tics causing injury or exhaustion), malignant Tourette syndrome unresponsive to all treatments, deep brain stimulation evaluation (refractory severe cases)
notes: Treat comorbid ADHD/OCD first as they often cause more impairment than tics. Stimulants generally safe and do not worsen tics.
guideline_changes: 2025 reaffirmation emphasizes CBIT as first-line behavioral intervention for ages 9+

first_line_drugs.notes: Moisture-sensing alarm; use nightly for 12-16 weeks minimum; best sustained response; 66% cure rate
120 mcg melt tablet SL 30-60 min before bed; may increase to 240-360 mcg; restrict fluids 8 hrs

second_line_drugs.notes: For refractory cases; alarm nightly + desmopressin 120-240 mcg SL at bedtime; 12-16 weeks
5 mg PO at bedtime; for enuresis with daytime urgency/frequency; anticholinergic; ages 5+
25 mg PO at bedtime (ages 6-12); max 50 mg (ages 12+); last-line due to cardiac risk; ECG required

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: If daytime symptoms or recurrent UTI

counselling: Bedwetting is not the child's fault — never punish or shame them
Restrict fluids 2 hours before bedtime and ensure the child urinates before sleeping
Use a waterproof mattress cover and keep spare sheets ready
Be patient — alarm therapy takes 12-16 weeks to show full effect
Reward dry nights with a star chart to motivate the child
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist if daytime wetting persists, recurrent UTIs, abnormal voiding pattern, or no response after 6 months of alarm + desmopressin therapy
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation rarely needed; admit only if secondary to spinal cord pathology or if invasive urodynamic studies are required
notes: Active treatment from age 6. Treat constipation and daytime incontinence first. Structured desmopressin withdrawal over weeks reduces relapse.
guideline_changes: 2025 IPSA consensus recommends structured desmopressin withdrawal to improve cure rates

diagnosis_name: Encopresis not due to a substance or known physiological condition

first_line_drugs.notes: Disimpaction: 1-1.5 g/kg/day PO x3-6 days; Maintenance: 0.4-0.8 g/kg/day PO; mix in 6-8 oz fluid
Scheduled sitting 5-10 min after meals; positive reinforcement; stool diary; ongoing

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-3 mL/kg/day PO in divided doses; osmotic laxative; for PEG-intolerant patients
Ages 2-6: 2.5-5 mL/day PO; Ages 6-12: 5-10 mL/day PO; stimulant laxative; short-term
1-3 mL/kg/day PO; max 240 mL/day; ages 1+; not for children at aspiration risk
1 pediatric suppository PR daily as needed; for acute relief; ages 2+

investigations.name: Abdominal X-ray
investigations.indication: Assess fecal loading and response to disimpaction

investigations.name: Celiac screen (tTG-IgA)
investigations.indication: Celiac disease can present as constipation

counselling: Encopresis (soiling) is usually caused by chronic constipation — it is NOT the child's fault
First, the impacted stool must be cleared (disimpaction) — then daily stool softener prevents re-impaction
The child should sit on the toilet for 5-10 minutes after each main meal — make it a relaxed routine
Praise and reward the child for sitting on the toilet — never punish for accidents
Treatment takes 6-12 months — do not stop the stool softener too early or the problem will return
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI if treatment failure after 6 months or red flags (blood in stool, failure to thrive, delayed meconium at birth). Psychology if significant behavioral component. Surgery if Hirschsprung suspected (delayed meconium, refractory to treatment)
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Consider if: massive fecal impaction requiring inpatient disimpaction. Suspected Hirschsprung disease needing biopsy. Severe associated behavioral/psychological crisis
notes: Disimpaction must precede maintenance therapy. Taper laxatives by 25% every 2-3 months over 6-12 months once behavioral modification established.
guideline_changes: PEG 3350 dosing optimized in 2024 studies confirming 0.4-0.8 g/kg/day maintenance range

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg IV day 1 then 50 mg/kg IV q12h; max 4 g/day; 10-14 days
15 mg/kg IV q6h; target trough 15-20 mcg/mL; add empirically for DRSP coverage
0.15 mg/kg IV q6h x 2-4 days; give 10-20 min before or with first antibiotic dose

second_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg IV q6h; max 12 g/day; alternative to ceftriaxone
40 mg/kg IV q8h; max 6 g/day; for resistant organisms or penicillin allergy

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis
investigations.indication: Cell count, protein, glucose, gram stain, culture; do not delay antibiotics if LP delayed

investigations.name: Brain CT/MRI
investigations.indication: If focal signs, papilledema, or clinical deterioration

counselling: Child has a serious bacterial infection of the brain lining; strong IV antibiotics are needed immediately
Treatment requires at least 10-14 days of IV antibiotics in hospital
Steroid medicine is given to reduce brain swelling and protect hearing
Hearing test must be done before discharge as hearing loss can occur
Vaccinations (pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib) are the best prevention for close contacts and future protection
referral_criteria: Immediate referral to pediatric ICU; neurology consultation; audiology before discharge
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require emergency admission; ICU if altered consciousness, shock, or seizures
notes: Do not delay antibiotics for LP; start empiric therapy immediately if meningitis suspected. Adjunctive dexamethasone reduces hearing loss especially in H. influenzae meningitis.
guideline_changes: NICE updated meningitis guidelines March 2024 with revised empiric antibiotic recommendations

diagnosis_name: Meningitis due to other and unspecified causes

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg IV day 1 then 50 mg/kg IV q12h; empiric until etiology clarified; max 4 g/day
15 mg/kg IV q6h empirically; de-escalate once organism identified
10 mg/kg IV q8h empirically if viral (HSV) etiology possible; pending PCR

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg IV q6h x 2-4 days if bacterial etiology suspected
50 mg/kg IV q6h; add for Listeria coverage in neonates and immunocompromised

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis
investigations.indication: Cell count, protein, glucose, gram stain, culture, viral PCR

investigations.name: Brain MRI
investigations.indication: If atypical course or focal signs

counselling: Child has an infection of the brain covering; cause is being investigated
Antibiotics and antiviral medicines are started until the exact cause is known
Blood and spinal fluid tests will identify whether bacteria, virus, or other cause is responsible
If it is viral meningitis, it usually gets better on its own in 1-2 weeks
Watch for worsening headache, neck stiffness, drowsiness, or seizures and come immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist and infectious disease; ICU if clinical deterioration
hospitalisation_criteria: All suspected meningitis cases require admission until etiology is established
notes: Start empiric antibiotics plus acyclovir until CSF culture, PCR, and gram stain clarify etiology. Up to 92% of aseptic meningitis cases receive empirical antibiotics pending workup.
guideline_changes: NICE 2024 guidelines updated empiric treatment recommendations

first_line_drugs.notes: Neonates: 20 mg/kg IV q8h x 21 days; Children >3mo: 10 mg/kg IV q8h x 14-21 days
100 mg/kg IV day 1 then 50 mg/kg IV q12h empirically until bacterial meningitis excluded

second_line_drugs.notes: 20-30 mg/kg/day IV x 3-5 days for autoimmune/ADEM; max 1 g/day
2 g/kg IV over 2-5 days for autoimmune encephalitis or ADEM refractory to steroids
5 mg/kg IV q12h for CMV encephalitis in immunocompromised

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis
investigations.indication: Lymphocytic pleocytosis; HSV PCR, enterovirus PCR

investigations.name: Brain MRI
investigations.indication: Temporal lobe involvement suggests HSV; diffuse ADEM pattern

investigations.name: EEG
investigations.indication: Seizure detection; periodic lateralized discharges in HSV

investigations.name: Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
investigations.indication: Autoimmune encephalitis panel if viral causes excluded

counselling: Child has inflammation of the brain which can be caused by virus or immune system problems
IV antiviral medicine (acyclovir) is started immediately and continued until tests confirm the cause
Child may be confused, have seizures, or behave differently during the illness
Recovery can take weeks to months; rehabilitation may be needed
Follow-up with neurologist is important for long-term cognitive and developmental monitoring
referral_criteria: Immediate referral to pediatric neurology and infectious disease; ICU if altered consciousness
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require admission; ICU for seizures, altered consciousness, or need for intubation
notes: Start acyclovir empirically in all suspected encephalitis; do not wait for HSV PCR results. Autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDAR) increasingly recognized and requires immunotherapy.

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg/day IV divided q12h; max 4 g/day; for aerobic gram-negative and streptococcal coverage
30 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h; max 4 g/day; excellent anaerobic coverage and CNS penetration
60 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h; target trough 15-20 mcg/mL; for MRSA/resistant strep coverage

second_line_drugs.notes: 120 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h; max 6 g/day; for resistant organisms or Pseudomonas coverage
Stereotactic aspiration for abscess >2.5 cm or causing mass effect; culture-directed therapy
0.5-1 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h short-term for significant perilesional edema and mass effect

investigations.name: MRI brain with contrast
investigations.indication: Confirm abscess, assess size, ring enhancement, edema

investigations.name: Stereotactic aspiration culture
investigations.indication: Culture-directed antibiotic therapy if surgical drainage done

counselling: Your child has a serious infection inside the brain that needs long-term antibiotics through IV
Treatment usually takes 4-6 weeks or longer - completing the full course is critical
Surgery may be needed to drain the infection if medicines alone are not enough
Regular CT or MRI scans will be done to check if the infection is shrinking
After treatment, your child will need follow-up to watch for seizures or other complications
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurosurgeon for all brain abscesses; infectious disease specialist for antibiotic guidance; ENT if sinogenic or otogenic source
hospitalisation_criteria: All intracranial abscesses require hospitalization for IV antibiotics; urgent neurosurgical intervention for abscess >2.5 cm, mass effect, or clinical deterioration
notes: Empiric triple therapy (cephalosporin + metronidazole + vancomycin) covers most pathogens. Duration is 4-6 weeks post-surgery or 6-8 weeks for medically managed abscesses.
guideline_changes: ESCMID 2023 published first comprehensive brain abscess guidelines for children and adults

first_line_drugs.notes: Start 1 mg/day PO; increase 1 mg q3-5 days to 2 mg TID; max 30 mg TID; 51% response rate
1-5 mg/kg/day levodopa PO divided TID; diagnostic trial for dopa-responsive dystonia first
Focal injection into dystonic muscles; dose varies by muscle group; repeat q3 months

second_line_drugs.notes: Start 5 mg/day PO; increase 5 mg q3-5 days; max 40-80 mg/day divided TID; 10% response rate
GPi DBS for severe generalized dystonia refractory to medical therapy; best for DYT1+
ITB pump for severe dystonia-spasticity; test dose 25-100 mcg intrathecal
0.01-0.03 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID; adjunctive for dystonia with myoclonus

counselling: Dystonia causes involuntary muscle contractions - your child is not doing this on purpose
Medicines take several weeks to show full effect; do not stop them suddenly
A trial of levodopa is important because some types of dystonia respond dramatically to it
Stress and fatigue can worsen symptoms; ensure adequate rest and a calm environment
Regular follow-up is needed to adjust medicine doses as your child grows
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric movement disorder specialist for classification and genetic testing; neurosurgery referral for DBS evaluation if refractory to medications
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for status dystonicus (life-threatening), acute dystonic storm with respiratory compromise, or DBS implantation
notes: Always trial levodopa first to rule out dopa-responsive dystonia (Segawa disease). Anticholinergics (trihexyphenidyl) are most effective first-line oral agents in children.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.02-0.05 mg/kg/dose IM/IV for acute dystonic reaction; then 1-2 mg PO BID maintenance
1.25 mg/kg IV/IM for acute EPS; then 5 mg/kg/day PO divided QID; max 300 mg/day
Start 1 mg PO daily; titrate by 1 mg q3-5 days; for chronic movement disorders

second_line_drugs.notes: VMAT2 inhibitor for tardive dyskinesia; limited pediatric data; FDA-approved adults only
12.5 mg PO daily; titrate to 12.5 mg TID; for chorea/hyperkinetic disorders; monitor for depression
1-4 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for essential/action tremor

counselling: If your child developed these movements after starting a new medicine, inform the doctor immediately
Do not stop any prescribed medicine without consulting the doctor first
These movements are often treatable once the cause is identified
Keep a diary of when movements are worst and what triggers them
Ensure adequate sleep as tiredness can worsen movement problems
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist if movement disorder persists beyond 2 weeks, etiology unclear, or not responding to initial treatment
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe acute dystonic reaction with airway compromise, tardive dyskinesia with functional impairment, or diagnostic workup requiring prolonged monitoring
notes: Identify and discontinue offending medication for drug-induced movement disorders first. Treatment depends on specific movement disorder type; anticholinergics for acute EPS, VMAT2 inhibitors for tardive syndromes.
guideline_changes: VMAT2 inhibitors emerging for tardive dyskinesia; novel gene therapies under investigation
snomed_display: Other extrapyramidal disease and abnormal movement disorders

first_line_drugs.notes: 15-30 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID; first-line for generalized epilepsy; monitor LFTs; avoid in pregnancy
10-30 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; titrate over 2-4 wks; first-line for focal and generalized seizures
10-20 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID; first-line for focal seizures; check HLA-B*1502 in at-risk groups
20 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; first-line for childhood absence epilepsy; max 40 mg/kg/day

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.3-1 mg/kg/day PO; titrate slowly over 8 wks to 5-15 mg/kg/day; first-line for focal/generalized
8-10 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; titrate to 30-46 mg/kg/day; for focal seizures
1-3 mg/kg/day PO; titrate to 5-9 mg/kg/day; adjunctive or monotherapy for focal/generalized
Starting dose 12.5 mg/day PO; titrate slowly; for drug-resistant focal epilepsy >=18 yrs; peds trials ongoing

investigations.name: EEG (awake and sleep)
investigations.indication: Classify epilepsy syndrome and guide medication choice

investigations.name: Drug levels
investigations.indication: Therapeutic drug monitoring for valproate, carbamazepine, phenytoin

counselling: Epilepsy medicine must be taken every day at the same time - never skip doses
Do not stop the medicine suddenly even if seizures have stopped - this is dangerous
Keep a seizure diary noting date, time, duration, and description of each episode
Your child can attend school normally but inform the teacher about the condition and first aid
Avoid swimming alone and heights; ensure adequate sleep as lack of sleep triggers seizures
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist if seizures not controlled with first medication, diagnostic uncertainty, or consideration of epilepsy surgery
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for status epilepticus, cluster seizures, new-onset seizures with altered consciousness, or for EEG monitoring
notes: Choice of ASM depends on seizure type and epilepsy syndrome; monotherapy is preferred. Avoid valproate in females of childbearing potential due to teratogenicity per ILAE/NICE guidance.
guideline_changes: ILAE updated initial monotherapy recommendations 2022; cenobamate emerging for refractory epilepsy

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg IV over 2 min; max 4 mg; repeat x1 after 5 min if seizure persists; first-line
0.2 mg/kg IM/IN (max 10 mg) if no IV access; or 0.1 mg/kg IV; faster onset via IM than IV diazepam
0.2-0.3 mg/kg IV (max 10 mg) or 0.5 mg/kg PR (max 20 mg); repeat x1 after 5 min

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg PE/kg IV at 3 mg PE/kg/min; max 1500 mg PE; 2nd-line if benzodiazepine fails
60 mg/kg IV over 15 min; max 4500 mg; 2nd-line per ESETT trial; equivalent efficacy to fosphenytoin
20-40 mg/kg IV over 5-10 min; max 3000 mg; 2nd-line; avoid in <2 yrs or liver disease
Load 0.2 mg/kg IV then 0.05-2 mg/kg/hr for refractory SE; titrate to EEG burst suppression

investigations.name: Continuous EEG
investigations.indication: Monitor for ongoing subclinical seizures in refractory SE

investigations.name: CT brain
investigations.indication: If first presentation or focal signs suggesting structural cause

investigations.name: Antiepileptic drug levels
investigations.indication: Check therapeutic levels in known epilepsy patients

counselling: Status epilepticus is a medical emergency - your child needed emergency treatment for prolonged seizures
If seizure lasts more than 5 minutes at home, give the emergency medicine and call an ambulance
Keep rescue medicine (midazolam nasal spray or diazepam rectal) with you at all times
After discharge, regular follow-up with the neurologist is essential to prevent recurrence
Ensure your child takes daily seizure medicines on time and never misses a dose
referral_criteria: All status epilepticus cases should be managed by or in consultation with pediatric neurologist; refer for refractory SE or if underlying cause unclear
hospitalisation_criteria: All status epilepticus requires emergency hospitalization; ICU admission for refractory status epilepticus, respiratory compromise, or need for continuous infusions
notes: Benzodiazepines must be given within 5 minutes of seizure onset; underdosing is common and increases morbidity. ESETT trial showed levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, and valproate equally effective as second-line agents.
guideline_changes: ESETT trial confirmed equivalent efficacy of levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, and valproate as 2nd-line

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO at onset; max 400 mg; first-line acute treatment
5 mg PO if <40 kg, 10 mg if >=40 kg; FDA-approved for pediatric migraine age >=6
5-20 mg intranasal; FDA-approved >=12 yrs for acute migraine
1-3 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for prophylaxis; only FDA-approved preventive in children 12-17

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.25-1 mg/kg PO qHS for prophylaxis; max 1 mg/kg/day; limited evidence vs placebo
1-4 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for prophylaxis; monitor HR and BP
6.25-12.5 mg PO once; FDA-approved >=12 yrs for acute migraine
Combined cognitive behavioral therapy with amitriptyline shown beneficial in CHAMP trial

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis (ICHD-3 criteria)
investigations.indication: Migraine is a clinical diagnosis; no specific test needed

investigations.name: Brain MRI
investigations.indication: Only if atypical features, aura prolonged >60 min, or focal deficits

investigations.name: Vision screening
investigations.indication: Rule out refractive error contributing to headaches

counselling: Migraine is a recurring headache condition; it can be managed but not cured
Take pain medicine within 30 minutes of headache onset for best effect
Identify and avoid triggers: skipped meals, poor sleep, stress, bright lights, certain foods
If headaches are frequent (>4/month), preventive medicine may be needed
Relaxation techniques and regular exercise help reduce migraine frequency
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist if >4 headaches/month, not responding to acute treatment, or atypical features
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for status migrainosus (>72h), associated stroke-like symptoms, or intractable vomiting
notes: Acute treatment should be given early at headache onset for best effect. Evidence for preventive medications is limited; CBT combined with medication may be superior to medication alone.
guideline_changes: AAN acute and preventive guidelines reaffirmed Oct 2025

first_line_drugs.notes: First-line surgical treatment for pediatric OSA; refer to ENT for evaluation
Titrated via polysomnography; for children not candidates for surgery or with persistent OSA post-surgery
Fluticasone 1 spray/nostril daily or mometasone 1 spray/nostril daily for mild OSA

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-5 yr: 4 mg PO daily; 6-14 yr: 5 mg PO daily; for mild residual OSA
Dietary and lifestyle interventions for obese children with OSA
Orthodontic device for children with maxillary constriction and OSA

investigations.name: Polysomnography (sleep study)
investigations.indication: Gold standard for diagnosing and grading OSA severity

investigations.name: Lateral neck X-ray
investigations.indication: Assess adenoid size if PSG not available

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: If severe OSA to rule out cor pulmonale

counselling: Your child stops breathing briefly during sleep which reduces oxygen to the brain and body
Enlarged tonsils and adenoids are the most common cause in children and surgery usually cures it
If your child snores loudly, breathes through the mouth, or is restless during sleep, these are important signs
Overweight children should be encouraged to eat healthy and exercise to help reduce symptoms
After surgery, a follow-up sleep study may be needed to confirm the problem is resolved
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT for adenotonsillectomy evaluation; sleep medicine if OSA persists post-surgery or child not a surgical candidate
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for adenotonsillectomy (day surgery or overnight for high-risk); severe OSA in young children (<3 yrs) or those with comorbidities may need overnight observation post-op
notes: Up to 40% of children have persistent OSA after adenotonsillectomy. Polysomnography is recommended for diagnosis and post-surgical reassessment.
guideline_changes: ATS 2024 guideline on persistent post-adenotonsillectomy OSA management

first_line_drugs.notes: Consistent bedtime routine, limit screens 1h before bed; graduated extinction; first-line always
Start 0.5 mg PO 30-60 min before bedtime; titrate weekly to max 5 mg; for sleep-onset insomnia

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.05-0.1 mg PO qHS for sleep-onset difficulty with comorbid ADHD; max 0.4 mg/day
3-6 mg/kg/day elemental iron PO if ferritin <50 ng/mL and restless legs/PLMD suspected
1-2 mg/kg PO qHS for sleep-maintenance insomnia; limited pediatric data; off-label use

investigations.name: Serum ferritin
investigations.indication: Low ferritin (<50 ng/mL) associated with restless legs/PLMD

investigations.name: Polysomnography
investigations.indication: If sleep-disordered breathing or periodic limb movements suspected

counselling: Good sleep habits are the most important treatment - set a fixed bedtime and wake time every day
Remove all screens (TV, tablet, phone) from the bedroom and stop screen use 1 hour before bed
Create a calm bedtime routine: bath, story, dim lights, then sleep
Avoid caffeinated drinks (cola, tea, coffee, chocolate) especially after lunch
If using melatonin, give it 30-60 minutes before the desired bedtime
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric sleep specialist if behavioral measures and melatonin fail after 4-6 weeks, suspected sleep-disordered breathing, or narcolepsy
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalization rarely needed; consider admission for polysomnography if severe obstructive symptoms or for safety if parasomnia causing injury
notes: Behavioral interventions should always precede and accompany any pharmacotherapy for pediatric insomnia. Melatonin not recommended under age 2; avoid in typically developing children without clear clinical indication.
guideline_changes: 2025 IPSA expert consensus on melatonin use in typically developing children published

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.4 g/kg/day IV x 5 days (total 2 g/kg); preferred in children; can also give 1 g/kg/day x 2 days
Monitor respiratory function q4h; intubate if FVC <20 mL/kg; DVT prophylaxis; pain management

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-1.5 plasma volumes x 5-6 sessions over 10-14 days; for children >10-15 kg; requires central line
5-10 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for neuropathic pain; titrate to effect

investigations.name: Nerve conduction study/EMG
investigations.indication: Confirm diagnosis and classify subtype (AIDP vs AMAN)

counselling: GBS causes weakness that usually starts in the legs and moves upward - this is expected
Most children recover fully but recovery can take weeks to months
If your child has difficulty breathing or swallowing, rush to the hospital immediately
Physiotherapy during and after recovery is very important to regain strength
Pain is common in GBS and should be reported to the doctor for treatment
referral_criteria: All GBS cases should be managed by or in consultation with pediatric neurologist; ICU referral if respiratory function declining
hospitalisation_criteria: All suspected GBS cases require hospitalization for monitoring; ICU admission if FVC <20 mL/kg, bulbar weakness, rapid progression, or autonomic instability
notes: IVIG preferred over plasmapheresis in children due to easier administration and fewer side effects. Monitor for respiratory failure and autonomic instability closely; ICU admission for severe cases.

diagnosis_name: Myasthenia gravis and other myoneural disorders

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg/kg PO q4-6h; start 30 mg TID then titrate to max 120 mg QID based on symptoms
1-2 mg/kg/day PO; start low and increase slowly to minimize transient worsening; taper once stable

second_line_drugs.notes: 2-3 mg/kg/day PO as steroid-sparing agent; check TPMT before starting; onset 3-6 months
600 mg/m2 PO BID; max 2 g/day; steroid-sparing; onset 2-6 months
2 g/kg IV over 2-5 days for exacerbations or pre-thymectomy
Weight-based IV q2wk for AChR-Ab+ generalized MG refractory to standard therapy; FDA-approved peds

investigations.name: CT chest
investigations.indication: Evaluate for thymoma or thymic hyperplasia

investigations.name: Repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS)
investigations.indication: Electrophysiological confirmation of neuromuscular junction disorder

investigations.name: Pulmonary function tests
investigations.indication: Baseline and serial monitoring of respiratory muscle strength

counselling: Myasthenia gravis causes muscle weakness that worsens with activity and improves with rest
Give medicines exactly on time as even small delays can increase weakness
Learn the emergency signs: difficulty breathing, unable to swallow, or very weak voice
Avoid very hot weather and overexertion as these worsen symptoms
Inform any doctor or dentist about myasthenia before any procedure or new medicine
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist for all new diagnoses; thoracic surgery referral for thymectomy evaluation in generalized MG
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure, severe dysphagia), cholinergic crisis, or pre-thymectomy preparation
notes: Children with ocular MG may achieve spontaneous remission; start with pyridostigmine alone initially. Thymectomy should be considered for generalized MG in children; vaccinate against meningococcus before eculizumab.
guideline_changes: FDA approved eculizumab expanded indication for pediatric generalized MG

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.75 mg/kg/day PO for Duchenne MD; reduces strength loss; delays cardiomyopathy; long-term use
0.9 mg/kg/day PO for DMD; FDA-approved; possibly less weight gain than prednisone
Regular stretching, strengthening, and cardiopulmonary rehab; prevent contractures; ongoing

second_line_drugs.notes: 40 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for DMD with nonsense mutations; EMA-approved
30 mg/kg IV weekly for DMD amenable to exon 51 skipping; FDA-approved
ACE inhibitor (enalapril 0.1 mg/kg/day) or beta-blocker started by age 10 for cardiomyopathy prevention
Gene therapy for DMD age 4-5; single IV dose 1.33x10^14 vg/kg; FDA-approved 2023

investigations.name: Genetic testing (dystrophin gene)
investigations.indication: Confirm specific mutation for treatment eligibility

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Baseline cardiac function; annual from age 10

counselling: Muscular dystrophy is a genetic condition - steroid medicine slows the progression significantly
Daily steroid medicine must not be stopped suddenly; your doctor will adjust the dose
Regular physiotherapy and stretching prevents stiff joints and maintains mobility longer
Watch for signs of heart or breathing problems: getting tired easily, cough at night, swollen feet
Genetic counseling is available for family planning and testing other family members
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist and geneticist at diagnosis; cardiology referral by age 10 or at diagnosis for cardiac monitoring
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for acute respiratory failure, pneumonia, cardiac decompensation, fractures in osteoporotic bone, or post-surgical care
notes: Corticosteroids remain the cornerstone of DMD treatment and should be initiated when motor function plateaus. Gene therapies are emerging; specific genetic testing guides exon-skipping and gene therapy eligibility.
source: AAN 2016 (DMD corticosteroids), French Soc Ped Neuro 2024
guideline_changes: 2024 French guidelines on corticosteroid compound choice and side effect management in DMD

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg); taper over 6-12 months once response achieved
15 mg/m2/wk PO or SC (max 25 mg/wk); preferred steroid-sparing agent; give with folic acid

second_line_drugs.notes: 2 g/kg IV monthly for refractory disease or severe dysphagia; as adjunct to immunosuppression
375 mg/m2 IV x 4 weekly doses for treatment-resistant IIM
600 mg/m2 PO BID; max 2 g/day; for skin-predominant or steroid-intolerant disease
500 mg/m2 IV monthly x 6 for severe refractory IIM with lung involvement

investigations.name: CK (creatine kinase)
investigations.indication: Marker of muscle damage and disease activity

investigations.name: MRI muscles (thighs)
investigations.indication: Assess extent of muscle inflammation and guide biopsy

counselling: Your child has muscle inflammation that needs long-term treatment to control
Steroid medicines are essential but have side effects - do not stop them without doctor advice
Protect your child from sun exposure with sunscreen and clothing as skin is very sensitive
Regular gentle exercise and physiotherapy help maintain muscle strength
Blood tests are needed regularly to monitor disease activity and medicine side effects
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric rheumatologist for all inflammatory myopathies; dermatology for skin-predominant disease
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe muscle weakness affecting swallowing or breathing, pulse IV steroid therapy, or disease flare not responding to outpatient treatment
notes: Juvenile IIM should be managed by pediatric rheumatology specialist. Exercise and physiotherapy across all disease activity levels improves outcomes and quality of life.
guideline_changes: 2025 PRES/CARRA treat-to-target recommendations published for juvenile dermatomyositis

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.3 mg/kg/day PO divided TID; titrate weekly to 0.75-2 mg/kg/day; max 40 mg/day <8 yrs
OnabotulinumtoxinA 10-15 U/kg per session for focal spasticity; max 340 U total per session
Ongoing PT/OT; goal-directed task-specific training; minimum 2-3 sessions/wk
0.12-0.8 mg/kg/day PO divided TID-QID for generalized spasticity; short-term use preferred

second_line_drugs.notes: ITB pump: test dose 25-100 mcg intrathecal; for severe generalized spasticity refractory to oral meds
0.05 mg/kg PO qHS; titrate to 0.05 mg/kg TID; max 0.1 mg/kg TID
0.5 mg/kg PO BID; titrate to max 3 mg/kg QID or 400 mg/day; monitor LFTs
Surgical option for children with spastic diplegia age 3-8 yrs with good selective motor control

investigations.name: MRI brain
investigations.indication: Determine etiology and extent of brain injury

investigations.name: Swallowing assessment (VFSS)
investigations.indication: If feeding difficulties or recurrent pneumonia

counselling: Cerebral palsy is a lifelong condition but therapy can greatly improve your child's abilities
Regular physiotherapy and occupational therapy at home is as important as hospital sessions
Medicines for stiffness need to be given on time every day and dose changes should only be done by the doctor
Watch for difficulty swallowing or frequent choking during meals and report to doctor
Your child can attend school with appropriate support - ask about inclusive education options
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist for classification, botulinum toxin planning, or if seizures develop; orthopedic referral for progressive contractures or hip surveillance
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe aspiration pneumonia, status epilepticus, post-surgical care (SDR, ITB pump placement), or acute hip dislocation
notes: Multimodal approach combining physical therapy, orthotics, and pharmacology is standard. BoNT-A is preferred for focal/segmental spasticity; oral agents for generalized spasticity.
guideline_changes: 2024 China neurorestorative therapy CPG published with updated rehabilitation recommendations

first_line_drugs.notes: OnabotulinumtoxinA 10-15 U/kg per session into affected limb muscles; repeat q3-6 months
CIMT: restrain unaffected limb 2-6 hrs/day for 2-8 wks to promote affected side use
Task-specific upper and lower limb rehabilitation; 3-5 sessions/wk during intensive programs

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.3 mg/kg/day PO divided TID; titrate to 0.75-2 mg/kg/day for associated spasticity
Short leg cast changed weekly x 4-6 wks for ankle equinus contracture after BoNT-A
ITB pump for severe refractory spasticity; test dose 25-100 mcg intrathecal

counselling: Encourage your child to use the affected hand/leg during daily activities like eating and dressing
Home exercises prescribed by the therapist should be done daily for best results
Botulinum toxin injections help reduce stiffness and need to be repeated every 3-6 months
Use proper footwear and ankle braces as recommended to prevent falls
Your child can participate in most activities with appropriate adaptations
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist for etiology workup; orthopedic surgeon for progressive contractures or gait abnormalities
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for intensive rehabilitation programs, post-surgical recovery (tendon transfers, SDR), or acute neurological deterioration
notes: BoNT-A combined with intensive PT or CIMT gives best functional outcomes for hemiplegic CP. Address both upper and lower limb involvement for optimal rehabilitation.

first_line_drugs.notes: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt; use antibiotic-impregnated catheter (Level I); programmable valve preferred
Endoscopic third ventriculostomy for obstructive hydrocephalus; lower infection/reoperation rates

second_line_drugs.notes: ETV with choroid plexus cauterization; reduces CSF production; emerging evidence in infants
25-100 mg/kg/day PO divided TID as temporizing measure; max 2 g/day; monitor electrolytes
For post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus; removes intraventricular clots; may reduce shunt need (Level III)

investigations.name: Shunt series X-ray
investigations.indication: Assess shunt continuity if malfunction suspected

counselling: Learn the danger signs of shunt blockage: sudden headache, vomiting, drowsiness, vision changes
Bring the child immediately to hospital if these signs appear at any time
Regular follow-up with neurosurgeon is essential even if the child seems well
Keep the shunt identification card with you at all times
Inform any doctor treating your child about the VP shunt before procedures or medications
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurosurgeon for all new diagnoses; ophthalmology for papilledema monitoring
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for acute raised ICP, shunt malfunction or infection, new-onset hydrocephalus, or pre-surgical workup
notes: Both CSF shunts and ETV are acceptable options (Level II evidence). Shunt infection prevention with antibiotic catheters and strict protocols is a priority.
guideline_changes: 2021 CNS systematic review update reinforced antibiotic-impregnated shunt use
snomed_display: (Child cerebral degeneration) or (hydrocephalus) or (senile brain degeneration)

diagnosis_name: Anoxic brain damage, not elsewhere classified

first_line_drugs.notes: Cool to 33.5-34.5°C within 6h of birth x 72h then rewarm over 6-12h; for mod-severe HIE >=36wk GA
20 mg/kg IV loading dose for seizures; then 3-5 mg/kg/day maintenance; first-line neonatal ASM
Maintain normoglycemia, normocarbia, avoid hypotension; target MAP for gestational age

second_line_drugs.notes: 40-60 mg/kg IV loading then 20-30 mg/kg/day divided BID for neonatal seizures; preferred if cardiac
1000 U/kg IV x 3-5 doses adjunct to cooling; investigational neuroprotectant; mixed trial results

counselling: Your baby's brain was affected by low oxygen - cooling treatment helps protect the brain
Your baby will be closely monitored in the NICU for several days
Seizures are common after brain injury and will be treated with medicines
Long-term developmental follow-up is very important even if your baby seems to recover well
Early intervention therapy (physiotherapy, occupational therapy) should start as soon as recommended
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist for all moderate-severe HIE; developmental pediatrician for long-term follow-up
hospitalisation_criteria: All moderate-severe HIE requires NICU admission for therapeutic hypothermia, seizure management, and multiorgan support
notes: Therapeutic hypothermia is standard of care for moderate-severe neonatal HIE within 6 hours of birth. Late cooling (6-24h) may still provide modest benefit per updated 2026 AAP guidance.
guideline_changes: AAP released updated clinical report on therapeutic hypothermia for neonatal HIE Feb 2026

first_line_drugs.notes: 15-25 mg/kg/day PO divided TID; titrate up to max 100 mg/kg/day (max 2 g/day); monitor electrolytes
Target 5-10% weight loss if overweight/obese; dietary counseling; primary intervention in obese teens

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-3 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; has carbonic anhydrase activity; also aids weight loss
1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; adjunct to acetazolamide if insufficient response
Surgical; for progressive visual loss refractory to medical therapy
Lumboperitoneal or VP shunt for refractory headache with visual loss despite medical therapy

investigations.name: MRI brain with MRV

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture with opening pressure
investigations.indication: Confirm elevated CSF pressure (>28 cmH2O in children)

investigations.name: Visual field perimetry
investigations.indication: Baseline and serial monitoring of visual field loss

counselling: Your child has increased pressure inside the head that can affect vision if not treated
Take the medicine regularly even if headaches improve - stopping too soon causes relapse
Regular eye checkups are very important to protect your child's vision
If your child is overweight, losing weight is one of the most important treatments
Report any worsening headache, vision changes, or double vision immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to ophthalmologist urgently for visual field and fundus assessment; neurosurgeon if vision deteriorating despite medical therapy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for acute vision loss, severe papilledema with visual field loss, need for therapeutic lumbar puncture, or surgical intervention
notes: Acetazolamide is first-line pharmacotherapy; monitor for metabolic acidosis and electrolyte abnormalities. Ophthalmology follow-up is essential to monitor papilledema and visual fields.
source: Alder Hey IIH Guideline 2024, IIH Consensus 2018
guideline_changes: 2024 Alder Hey hospital published updated pediatric IIH clinical guideline

first_line_drugs.notes: Maintain airway, normoglycemia, normothermia; treat raised ICP; target MAP for age; GCS monitoring
0.1 mg/kg IV (max 4 mg) for seizures; may repeat x1 in 5 min; first-line anticonvulsant
10 mg/kg IV q8h empirically pending HSV PCR if infectious etiology suspected

second_line_drugs.notes: 20-30 mg/kg/day IV x 3-5 days for suspected autoimmune/inflammatory encephalopathy; max 1 g/day
2 g/kg IV over 2-5 days for autoimmune encephalopathy refractory to steroids
0.25-1 g/kg IV over 20 min for raised ICP; can repeat q4-6h; monitor serum osmolality

investigations.name: CT brain (urgent)
investigations.indication: Rapid screen for mass lesion, hemorrhage, edema

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis
investigations.indication: Rule out meningitis/encephalitis (cell count, protein, glucose, HSV PCR)

investigations.name: EEG
investigations.indication: Assess for subclinical seizures and encephalopathy severity

counselling: Your child's brain is not functioning normally - the cause needs to be found and treated
Your child will need close monitoring in the hospital, possibly in the ICU
Many causes of encephalopathy are treatable and children can recover well
Report any seizures, change in alertness, or breathing difficulty to the nursing staff immediately
After recovery, developmental follow-up may be needed to check for any lasting effects
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist urgently for all encephalopathy cases; infectious disease if infection suspected; metabolic genetics if metabolic cause suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: All children with encephalopathy require hospitalization; ICU admission for GCS <8, seizures, hemodynamic instability, or need for airway protection
notes: Identify and treat the underlying cause (metabolic, infectious, autoimmune, toxic). PICU admission required for GCS <8, raised ICP, or hemodynamic instability.

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 firm downward strokes over lacrimal sac bid-tid; parents taught technique; age <12mo
Apply 1cm ribbon bid-tid for acute dacryocystitis/purulent discharge episodes
Apply warm moist compress to inner canthus for 5-10min tid-qid to relieve obstruction

second_line_drugs.notes: Office or OR procedure if CNLDO persists >12mo; 85-95% success rate; under brief GA
Probing with balloon dilation for failed initial probing; higher success in older children
For recurrent obstruction after probing; tube left in place 3-6mo then removed
Surgical bypass of NLD; reserved for refractory cases after failed less invasive procedures

investigations.name: Clinical examination
investigations.indication: Diagnosis is clinical; fluorescein dye disappearance test

investigations.name: Dacryocystography
investigations.indication: If complex obstruction suspected before surgery

counselling: Lacrimal massage technique is the most important treatment: press firmly downward over inner corner of eye 10 times, 2-3 times daily
Wipe away discharge with clean wet cotton; the blocked tear duct itself is not infected
More than 90% of blocked tear ducts open on their own by age 12 months
If there is no improvement by 12 months, a simple probing procedure can open the duct
Antibiotic eye drops are only needed during active discharge episodes, not continuously
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric ophthalmologist if CNLDO persists beyond 12 months, acute dacryocystitis (red swollen lump at inner corner), or suspected dacryocystocele in neonate
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if acute dacryocystitis with preseptal cellulitis, dacryocystocele with respiratory distress (bilateral, neonatal), or probing under GA
notes: Congenital NLDO affects 6-20% of newborns; >90% resolve spontaneously by 12 months with massage. Probing should be deferred until after 12 months to allow spontaneous resolution.

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply 1cm ribbon to lower conjunctival sac qid x5-7d; all ages including neonates
1 drop in affected eye q3h while awake x7d; first-line for bacterial conjunctivitis ≥2mo
1-2 drops q2-4h PRN for viral/allergic conjunctivitis; supportive care

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 drop in affected eye tid x7d; age ≥1yr; broader coverage for severe cases
1 drop bid x2d then 1 drop daily x5d; age ≥1yr; good compliance profile
1 drop in each eye bid for allergic conjunctivitis; age ≥3yr
1 drop in each eye bid-tid for allergic conjunctivitis; age ≥3yr

investigations.name: Conjunctival swab culture
investigations.indication: If neonatal, severe, or not responding to empirical treatment

investigations.name: Gram stain
investigations.indication: If hyperacute purulent conjunctivitis (rule out gonococcal)

investigations.name: Chlamydia PCR
investigations.indication: Neonatal conjunctivitis at 5-14 days of life

counselling: Wash hands frequently and do not touch or rub the eyes
Use separate towels and washcloths from other family members
Clean eye discharge with wet cotton, wiping from inner to outer corner
Do not share eye drops between family members; replace bottle after infection
Child can return to school after 24 hours on antibiotic drops or when discharge stops
referral_criteria: Refer to ophthalmologist if neonatal conjunctivitis (<30 days), conjunctivitis not improving after 1 week of treatment, suspected gonococcal (hyperacute, profuse), or associated corneal involvement
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if neonatal gonococcal conjunctivitis (IV ceftriaxone needed), orbital cellulitis, or corneal ulcer with conjunctivitis
notes: Bacterial conjunctivitis is most common in children; viral is self-limiting in 7-14d. Neonatal conjunctivitis (<30d) requires urgent evaluation to rule out gonococcal/chlamydial infection.

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 drop q1h around the clock x48h then taper to q2-4h based on response; age ≥1yr
Alternating q1h (each q2h) for severe/central ulcers; compounded drops
1 drop bid-tid for ciliary spasm/pain relief; adjunct to antibiotic therapy

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 drop q1h x48h then taper; alternative 4th-gen fluoroquinolone monotherapy; ≥1yr
1 drop q1-2h for fungal keratitis; first-line for filamentous fungi
1 drop q1h for severe fungal keratitis; compounded; for Candida/resistant fungi
Apply 5x/day x14-21d for HSV keratitis; add oral acyclovir 20mg/kg q5h if stromal

investigations.name: Corneal scraping for gram stain and culture
investigations.indication: Before starting antibiotics for all central/large ulcers

investigations.name: Fluorescein staining
investigations.indication: Assess corneal defect size and depth

investigations.name: Corneal confocal microscopy
investigations.indication: If Acanthamoeba keratitis suspected (contact lens wearer)

counselling: Keratitis is a serious eye infection that can cause vision loss if not treated promptly
Use eye drops exactly as prescribed - initially drops may be needed every hour
Do not wear contact lenses until completely healed and cleared by eye doctor
Attend all follow-up appointments; corneal healing needs close monitoring
If pain worsens or vision decreases further, return to eye doctor immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to ophthalmologist urgently for all suspected keratitis; refer to cornea specialist if central ulcer, fungal keratitis, or not responding to treatment
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe corneal ulcer threatening perforation, hypopyon, need for hourly fortified drops (compliance concern), or fungal/acanthamoeba keratitis
notes: Bacterial keratitis is an ocular emergency; obtain corneal cultures before starting empiric therapy if central/severe. Contact lens wearers need Pseudomonas coverage with fluoroquinolone monotherapy.

counselling: Screening is essential even if baby appears normal — बच्चा सामान्य दिखे तब भी आंखों की जांच ज़रूरी है।
Multiple follow-up visits are required — कई बार जांच करानी पड़ेगी।
Procedure is safe — जांच सुरक्षित है।
Early treatment can prevent blindness — जल्दी इलाज से अंधापन रोका जा सकता है।
Do not miss any scheduled eye examination — कोई भी आंख की जांच की तारीख़ न छोड़ें।
Premature babies need long-term eye follow-up — समय से पहले जन्मे बच्चों को लंबे समय तक आंखों की जांच चाहिए।
warning_signs: White reflex in pupil — आंख की पुतली में सफ़ेद चमक
Abnormal eye movements — आंखों की असामान्य हरकत
Baby not following objects with eyes — बच्चा आंखों से चीज़ों को न देखना
Missed screening appointment — जांच की तारीख़ छूटना
expected_course: ROP screening begins at 2-4 weeks postnatal age depending on gestational age. Most preterm infants will have immature retina that vascularizes normally. Type 2 ROP requires close monitoring. Type 1 ROP requires urgent treatment (laser therapy or anti-VEGF). Screening continues until full retinal vascularization or PMA >=45 weeks without active disease.
key_clinical_points: Screen all infants with GA <=34 weeks or birth weight <=2000 grams.
First screening by 30 days of life (earlier if very premature or sick).
Pupil dilation with Tropicamide 0.5% + Phenylephrine 2.5% before examination.
Document zone, stage, and presence of plus disease at each screening.
Type 1 ROP requires treatment within 48-72 hours of diagnosis.
Stop screening when full vascularization into Zone III or PMA >=45 weeks without active disease.

severity_assessment.mild: No ROP or Stage 1 without plus disease — routine follow-up every 1-2 weeks.
severity_assessment.moderate: Stage 2 or any stage with plus disease features — weekly monitoring, close ophthalmology follow-up.
severity_assessment.severe: Stage 3 or more, Type 1 ROP, aggressive posterior ROP — immediate referral for laser therapy or anti-VEGF treatment.

monitoring_parameters.frequency: Every 1-2 weeks until retina fully vascularized

first_line_drugs.notes: Correct full cycloplegic refraction; accommodative esotropia may fully resolve with glasses
Patch sound eye 2h/day for moderate amblyopia, 6h/day for severe; with near activities
1 drop in sound eye daily or weekend-only for moderate amblyopia; age <10yr

second_line_drugs.notes: Recession/resection of extraocular muscles; under GA; for deviations unresponsive to optical Rx
Inject into extraocular muscle under EMG guidance; for small-angle deviations or pre-surgical
Translucent filter on spectacle lens over sound eye; for moderate amblyopia; better compliance
Fresnel or ground-in prism for small residual deviations; for diplopia relief

investigations.name: Cycloplegic refraction
investigations.indication: Essential for all strabismus; accommodative component assessment

investigations.name: MRI brain
investigations.indication: If acquired non-accommodative esotropia, cranial nerve palsy, or neurological signs

counselling: Wearing glasses as prescribed is essential - it may fully correct accommodative squint
Patching or atropine for amblyopia treatment requires patience; results take weeks to months
Encourage the child to do close-up activities (drawing, reading) during patching for better results
Surgery for squint has good results but may need to be repeated; it aligns the eyes, does not replace glasses
Early treatment gives best outcomes; visual plasticity decreases significantly after age 7-9 years
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric ophthalmologist/strabismus specialist at first detection of squint at any age; urgent if new onset in child >6 months or associated with neurological signs
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for strabismus surgery (day procedure under GA); urgent if acquired squint with suspected neurological cause (cranial nerve palsy, brain tumor)
notes: Treat amblyopia first before surgical correction of strabismus. Visual plasticity is greatest before age 7-9; early detection and treatment yield best outcomes.

first_line_drugs.notes: Full cycloplegic refraction Rx; update q6-12mo; polycarbonate lenses for children
1 drop in each eye at bedtime daily for myopia control; age 4-12yr; monitor q6mo
For active children/sports; orthokeratology overnight lenses for myopia control option

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 drop each eye at bedtime daily for faster-progressing myopia; more side effects
Overnight rigid GP lenses reshape cornea; for myopia control; age ≥6yr; monitor closely
FDA-approved dual-focus soft lens for myopia control; age 8-12yr at initiation
For accommodative esotropia or high AC/A ratio; prescribed per binocular vision assessment

investigations.name: Cycloplegic refraction
investigations.indication: Accurate measurement of refractive error in children

investigations.name: Axial length measurement
investigations.indication: Monitor myopia progression in children on myopia control

investigations.name: Fundus examination
investigations.indication: High myopes need retinal screening for degenerative changes

counselling: Wear glasses consistently as prescribed - the eyes need clear images for proper development
Encourage 2 hours of outdoor play daily - this slows myopia progression
Limit screen time; follow the 20-20-20 rule (every 20 min, look 20 feet away for 20 seconds)
Eye check-ups every 6 months are needed as power changes during growth
If prescribed atropine drops for myopia control, use at bedtime; minor pupil dilation and light sensitivity are normal
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric ophthalmologist if amblyopia detected, rapid myopia progression (>-0.75D/year), high myopia (>-6D), or associated with systemic conditions (Marfan, Down syndrome)
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not needed for refractive errors; consider if complication of contact lens use (keratitis)
notes: Myopia control interventions (atropine, Ortho-K, MiSight) should be considered for progressive myopia in children. Ensure 120min outdoor time daily as modifiable protective factor against myopia progression.
guideline_changes: Low-dose atropine 0.01-0.05% now widely adopted for myopia control based on Phase 3 trial data

first_line_drugs.notes: Full cycloplegic refraction, dilated fundus exam, visual field if cooperative; age-appropriate
Prescribe optimal refraction first; reassess 4-6wk; may resolve functional visual disturbance
For amblyopia: 2h/day moderate, 6h/day severe; combine with 1h near activities daily

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 drop in sound eye daily or weekend-only for amblyopia; equivalent to 2h patching
VR-based dichoptic therapy 1h/day x12wk for amblyopia; FDA-approved age 4-7yr
If unexplained visual loss, papilledema, optic atrophy, or neurologic signs present
Magnifiers, large-print materials, assistive tech if best-corrected VA remains impaired

investigations.name: Comprehensive eye examination

investigations.name: MRI brain and orbits
investigations.indication: If optic neuritis, papilledema, or unexplained vision loss

investigations.name: Visual evoked potentials (VEP)
investigations.indication: Objective assessment of visual pathway function

counselling: Any change in your child vision needs prompt evaluation - early treatment is essential
If patching is prescribed for lazy eye, consistent use gives the best chance of improvement
Ensure good lighting for reading and close work
Regular eye examinations are needed to track progress
Report any sudden vision loss, flashing lights, or new headaches urgently to the eye doctor
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric ophthalmologist for all children with visual disturbances; urgent referral if acute vision loss, papilledema, or neurological signs
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if acute vision loss requiring urgent workup (optic neuritis, papilledema with raised ICP, retinal detachment), or neuroimaging needed urgently
notes: Visual disturbances in children require thorough evaluation to exclude retinal, optic nerve, or neurologic pathology. Functional/non-organic visual loss is a diagnosis of exclusion in the pediatric population.
guideline_changes: Digital therapeutic (Luminopia) FDA-approved 2021 as adjunct for amblyopia treatment in children 4-7yr

first_line_drugs.notes: Comprehensive exam by low vision specialist; prescribe magnifiers, contrast aids, lighting
Maximize residual vision with best refraction; update q6-12mo in growing children
Certified O&M specialist; begin early; white cane training when ambulatory

second_line_drugs.notes: CCTV, tablet-based magnifiers, screen readers; prescribe per functional assessment
JAWS, NVDA, or VoiceOver for school-age children; training by TVI specialist
Adaptive daily living skills; sensory integration; handwriting alternatives
Enroll by 6mo; developmental support, family training, per state EI program

investigations.name: Comprehensive eye examination
investigations.indication: Dilated fundus exam, refraction, visual field

investigations.name: MRI brain and orbits
investigations.indication: If optic nerve or neurological cause suspected

counselling: Start low vision rehabilitation early - it helps the child use remaining vision to the fullest
Ensure good lighting at home and school; reduce glare with tinted lenses if needed
Talk to the school about special seating (close to board) and learning aids
Teach the child orientation and mobility skills early for independence
Regular eye check-ups to detect any treatable causes and monitor progression
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric ophthalmologist for all cases of childhood visual impairment; refer to low vision centre for rehabilitation; refer to genetic counselling if hereditary cause
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed; admit if acute visual loss requiring urgent workup (retinal detachment, optic neuritis), or surgical intervention for treatable cause
notes: Low vision rehabilitation should begin early as a parallel ongoing treatment, not a last resort. Multidisciplinary team approach including TVI, O&M specialist, OT, and family support is essential.

first_line_drugs.notes: 4 drops in affected ear bid x7d; ≥6mo age; warm bottle in hand before use
5 drops (age 1-12yr) or 10 drops (≥13yr) in affected ear bid x7d
3-5 drops in affected ear tid-qid x7d; for mild OE or prophylaxis

3-4 drops in affected ear tid-qid x7-10d; only if TM intact (ototoxic risk)

investigations.indication: If not responding to first-line drops after 1 week

counselling: Keep the ear completely dry during treatment - use cotton with petroleum jelly during bathing
No swimming until treatment is complete and ear is healed
Warm the ear drops in your hand before putting them in to reduce discomfort
Use ear drops for the full 7 days even if ear feels better
Prevent recurrence: dry ears after swimming, avoid inserting objects, use swimmer ear drops
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if malignant (necrotizing) otitis externa suspected, fungal OE not responding to treatment, periauricular cellulitis, or recurrent OE needing canal evaluation
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if malignant otitis externa (diabetic/immunocompromised child), periauricular cellulitis spreading beyond canal, or suspected mastoiditis
notes: Topical fluoroquinolone-steroid combination is first-line; systemic antibiotics only if peri-auricular cellulitis or immunocompromised. Keep ear dry; use wick if canal is edematous to deliver drops.

first_line_drugs.notes: Instill 5-10 drops in affected ear, wait 15-30min, then irrigate; age ≥3yr
Body-temp water via syringe or electronic irrigator; gentle pressure; intact TM only
Under direct visualization with otoscope; by trained provider; preferred in young children

second_line_drugs.notes: Instill 3-5 drops in ear bid x3-5 days to soften wax before removal
Instill 3-5 drops in ear, wait 15min then irrigate; off-label cerumenolytic
Body-temp saline via bulb syringe or ear wash system; safe first-line alternative

investigations.name: Otoscopy
investigations.indication: Visualise wax and tympanic membrane status

investigations.name: Audiometry
investigations.indication: If hearing loss persists after wax removal

counselling: Do not put cotton buds or any objects into the ear canal - this pushes wax deeper
Ears are self-cleaning; wax normally moves out on its own
Use ear drops as directed to soften wax before the removal appointment
If ear feels blocked after swimming, tilt head to drain water; do not insert objects
Come back if ear pain, discharge, or hearing difficulty persists after wax removal
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if failed removal after 2 attempts in clinic, suspected foreign body behind wax, non-intact tympanic membrane, or child too uncooperative for office removal
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not needed for cerumen impaction; consider if removal under GA needed in very young or uncooperative child
notes: Cerumenolytics not recommended under age 3 or with non-intact TM. Ear candling is contraindicated and ineffective; refer to ENT if removal fails after 1-2 attempts.

first_line_drugs.notes: Observe 3mo from onset/diagnosis if no risk factors for speech/language delay
Obtain audiogram if OME persists ≥3mo or at any time if language delay suspected
Reassess q3-6mo with pneumatic otoscopy until effusion resolves

second_line_drugs.notes: Bilateral myringotomy with tube insertion if OME ≥3mo with hearing loss ≥20dB
Child inflates balloon via nostril bid-tid x4wk; age ≥3yr; modest benefit
Consider if age ≥4yr with persistent OME or getting 2nd set of tubes

investigations.name: Pneumatic otoscopy
investigations.indication: Primary diagnostic tool; assess TM mobility

investigations.name: Tympanometry
investigations.indication: Type B tracing confirms middle ear effusion

investigations.name: Audiometry
investigations.indication: If OME >3 months or speech/language concerns

counselling: Fluid behind the eardrum is very common after ear infections - it usually clears in 3 months
Antibiotics, decongestants, and antihistamines do NOT help clear this fluid
If your child seems to not hear well, speak face-to-face and reduce background noise
Hearing test is recommended if fluid persists for 3 months
Ear tubes may be needed if fluid persists and causes hearing loss or speech delay
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if OME persists >3 months, documented hearing loss >=20 dB, speech/language delay, or learning difficulties
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for tympanostomy tube insertion or adenoidectomy (day procedure under GA)
notes: Steroids, antibiotics, antihistamines, and decongestants are NOT recommended for OME per AAO-HNS guidelines. Tympanostomy tubes indicated when OME causes significant hearing loss or speech delay.

first_line_drugs.notes: 80-90mg/kg/day PO divided bid-tid x10d (<2yr) or 7d (2-5yr) or 5d (>5yr), max 3g/day
90mg/kg/day amox component PO divided bid x10d if failed amox or recent Abx use
5-10mg/kg PO q6-8h PRN pain/fever, max 40mg/kg/day
10-15mg/kg PO q4-6h PRN pain/fever, max 75mg/kg/day

second_line_drugs.notes: 50mg/kg IM x1-3 doses (1 dose for uncomplicated; 3 daily doses if failed oral)
14mg/kg/day PO once daily or divided bid x10d, max 600mg/day; PCN-allergic (non-severe)
10mg/kg PO day 1 then 5mg/kg/day x4d for severe PCN allergy, max 250mg/day
4 drops in ear bid x7d for AOM with otorrhea through tympanostomy tube

investigations.name: Pneumatic otoscopy
investigations.indication: Primary diagnostic assessment of middle ear

investigations.name: Tympanocentesis with culture
investigations.indication: If failed 2 antibiotic courses or immunocompromised

investigations.name: CT temporal bone
investigations.indication: If mastoiditis or intracranial complication suspected

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: If systemic toxicity or complicated AOM

counselling: Give the antibiotic for the full prescribed course - stopping early causes resistant bacteria
Paracetamol or ibuprofen is very important for ear pain relief; do not wait for antibiotic to work
Follow up in 48-72 hours if no improvement; antibiotic may need changing
Clean any ear discharge gently with cotton; do not insert anything deep into ear canal
Completed pneumococcal and influenza vaccines reduce ear infection frequency
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if recurrent AOM (>=3 in 6 months or >=4 in 12 months), failed 2 courses of antibiotics, suspected mastoiditis, or persistent otorrhoea >2 weeks
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if acute mastoiditis, intracranial complication (meningitis, brain abscess, lateral sinus thrombosis), toxic appearance, or need for IV antibiotics
notes: High-dose amoxicillin is first-line; watchful waiting may be appropriate for mild AOM in children >2yr with unilateral symptoms. Pain management is essential and should be addressed regardless of antibiotic decision.

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/dose 6-8 hourly PO SOS, pain relief is first priority
5-10 mg/kg/dose 6-8 hourly PO SOS, for pain and fever
40-45 mg/kg/day (amoxicillin component) div BD PO x 5-7 days, drug of choice
Age-appropriate dose TDS PO, short duration, nasal decongestant to improve Eustachian tube function

second_line_drugs.notes: 8-10 mg/kg/day div BD PO x 5-7 days, use if penicillin allergy or poor response
2-3 drops TDS in affected ear, only if external ear involvement or discharge; avoid if tympanic membrane perforation

counselling: Ear pain usually improves in 24-48 hours with treatment
Complete the full course of antibiotics even if pain resolves
Apply warm compress over the affected ear for pain relief
Keep the ear dry - avoid water entering the ear during bathing
If pain persists beyond 48 hours, high fever continues, ear discharge appears, or swelling behind the ear develops - return to hospital immediately
Follow up in 2-3 days to assess response to treatment
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Persistent ear pain beyond 48 hours despite treatment
High fever not responding to medication
Ear discharge or pus from the ear
Swelling behind the ear suggesting mastoiditis
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT specialist if no improvement after 48-72 hours on antibiotics, recurrent AOM (>3 episodes in 6 months or >4 in 1 year), suspected mastoiditis, hearing difficulty, or chronic ear discharge
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if suspected mastoiditis (post-auricular swelling/tenderness), intracranial complications (meningitis, brain abscess), toxic appearance, age <2 months with fever, or failed outpatient therapy with systemic signs
notes: Pain control is the priority. Amox-Clav is first-line antibiotic. Avoid dual antibiotic therapy (Amox-Clav + Cefpodoxime simultaneously not recommended). Ear drops only if external ear involvement or discharge (avoid if TM perforation unless appropriate drops). Non-pharmacological: warm compress over ear, adequate hydration, keep ear dry.
guideline_changes: AAP 2024 reaffirms watchful waiting for mild unilateral AOM in children >=2 yr; high-dose amoxicillin (80-90 mg/kg/day) remains first-line; amoxicillin-clavulanate for treatment failure or beta-lactamase producers; Italian intersociety 2024 consensus aligns with AAP on shortened 5-day courses for uncomplicated AOM in children >=2 yr.

first_line_drugs.notes: 5 drops in affected ear bid x7-10d; safe with perforated TM (non-ototoxic)
3-4 drops in affected ear bid x7-10d; safe with perforated TM
Cotton ball with petroleum jelly for bathing; avoid swimming until healed

second_line_drugs.notes: Office-based paper/gelfoam patch for small acute perforations to aid healing
Formal surgical repair with fascia graft if perforation persists >3mo; under GA
10-15mg/kg PO q4-6h PRN pain, max 75mg/kg/day or 4g/day

investigations.name: Otoscopy
investigations.indication: Document perforation size, location, and margin

investigations.name: Audiometry (pure tone)
investigations.indication: Assess hearing loss and conductive gap

investigations.name: CT temporal bone
investigations.indication: If cholesteatoma or ossicular erosion suspected

counselling: Keep the ear completely dry - use cotton with petroleum jelly during bathing, no swimming
Do not put any drops in the ear unless prescribed by doctor (some drops are harmful with perforation)
Most small perforations heal on their own within 1-3 months
Avoid blowing nose forcefully as air pressure can prevent healing
If hearing does not improve after healing, follow-up hearing test is needed
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if perforation not healed in 3 months, persistent hearing loss, recurrent ear infections through perforation, or cholesteatoma suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for tympanoplasty surgery (elective), or if acute perforation with vertigo, facial nerve palsy, or suspected inner ear injury
notes: Most acute TM perforations heal spontaneously within 1-3 months; avoid aminoglycoside otic drops (ototoxic through perforation). Surgical repair indicated for persistent perforation with hearing loss or recurrent infection.

first_line_drugs.notes: Fit bilateral behind-the-ear aids; trial 3-6mo; verify with real-ear measurements
For severe-profound bilateral SNHL (>70dB); FDA approved ≥9mo; implant ASAP ideally <12mo
Softband for conductive HL in infants; surgical implant (Baha/Ponto) at age ≥5yr

second_line_drugs.notes: For conductive HL from chronic OME; outpatient procedure under brief GA
Surgical reconstruction of ossicular chain for congenital/acquired conductive HL
Classroom amplification system; transmitter to teacher, receiver to child's hearing aid
Begin by 6mo of age per EHDI guidelines; ongoing per developmental milestones

investigations.name: Tympanometry
investigations.indication: Assess middle ear function; rule out conductive component

investigations.name: Temporal bone CT/MRI
investigations.indication: Assess cochlear anatomy before cochlear implant

investigations.name: Genetic testing (GJB2/connexin 26)
