investigations.indication: Most common cause of genetic SNHL

counselling: Early hearing aid fitting and speech therapy are critical - start by 6 months of age
Hearing aids should be worn during all waking hours for best speech development
Talk to your child face-to-face; reduce background noise during conversations
Regular audiological follow-up every 3-6 months is essential for adjustments
Cochlear implant can give hearing to profoundly deaf children if fitted early (before 12 months)
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT/audiologist immediately on newborn hearing screen failure; refer to cochlear implant centre if bilateral severe-profound SNHL not benefiting from hearing aids
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for cochlear implant surgery, tympanoplasty, or ossiculoplasty as planned procedures
notes: Early identification by 3mo and intervention by 6mo are critical per JCIH guidelines. Conductive HL often treatable medically/surgically; SNHL requires amplification or cochlear implantation.
guideline_changes: Cochlear implant candidacy expanded to include children 9-24mo with bilateral profound SNHL

first_line_drugs.notes: ABR/OAE for infants; behavioral audiometry for >6mo; complete by 3mo of age
BTE aids fitted per audiogram; 3-6mo trial; regular adjustment as child grows
Enroll by 6mo per EHDI; speech therapy, family training, developmental support

second_line_drugs.notes: If bilateral severe-profound SNHL with inadequate hearing aid benefit after trial
Softband from infancy for conductive/mixed HL; osseointegrated implant at ≥5yr
For school-age children; improves signal-to-noise ratio in classroom

investigations.name: Complete audiological evaluation (ABR, OAE, tympanometry)
investigations.indication: Define type and degree of hearing loss

investigations.name: CMV testing (urine/blood)
investigations.indication: Congenital CMV is leading non-genetic cause of SNHL; test within 3 weeks of birth

investigations.name: Genetic testing
investigations.indication: GJB2 mutation analysis for non-syndromic SNHL

investigations.name: Ophthalmology evaluation
investigations.indication: Usher syndrome screening; many HL syndromes have eye involvement

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Screen for associated renal anomalies (branchio-oto-renal syndrome)

counselling: Do not delay evaluation - early diagnosis and intervention prevents speech delay
Follow the 1-3-6 rule: screen by 1 month, diagnose by 3 months, intervene by 6 months
Hearing aids and speech therapy should start as soon as hearing loss is confirmed
Talk, read, and sing to your child regularly; face-to-face communication is very important
Ensure family members learn to communicate effectively with the child
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT/audiologist for all detected hearing loss; urgent referral for sudden hearing loss; genetic counselling for familial hearing loss
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for cochlear implant surgery or other surgical interventions (tympanoplasty, ossicular reconstruction)
notes: All newborns should be screened before 1 month; diagnosis by 3 months; intervention by 6 months (1-3-6 rule). Etiology workup includes genetics, CMV testing, imaging, and ophthalmology referral.

diagnosis_name: Acute rheumatic fever without mention of heart involvement

first_line_drugs.notes: 600,000 U IM x1 (<27kg) or 1.2 MU IM x1 (≥27kg); single dose for streptococcal eradication
10-15 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; first-line NSAID for arthritis; better safety profile than aspirin

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-60 mg/kg/day PO divided q6h; anti-inflammatory for arthritis; taper after 1-2 weeks of resolution
250mg PO q12h x10 days; oral alternative for streptococcal eradication if IM not feasible
12 mg/kg/day PO x5 days (max 500mg); for penicillin-allergic patients

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Assess for subclinical or clinical carditis (mitral/aortic regurgitation)

investigations.name: CRP and ESR
investigations.indication: Inflammatory markers; ESR >60 supports Jones criteria

counselling: Acute rheumatic fever is caused by untreated strep throat - always complete strep treatment
Monthly penicillin injection is needed for at least 5-10 years to prevent recurrence
Joint pain usually resolves in 2-4 weeks with treatment
Regular echocardiograms are needed to check if heart valves are affected
All sore throats in the family should be tested and treated for strep
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist if any cardiac involvement (murmur, echo abnormality); refer to pediatric rheumatologist if atypical presentation or chorea
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if carditis with heart failure, severe chorea, high fever not responding to treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty requiring echocardiography and workup
notes: Penicillin eradication does not alter the ARF course but prevents spread. Secondary prophylaxis with benzathine penicillin G q4wk is essential for at least 5 years or until age 21.
source: AHA 2009 ARF/RHD Guidelines, WHO 2004 RF Prevention
guideline_changes: Naproxen now recommended as first-line over aspirin for rheumatic arthritis

first_line_drugs.notes: 1.2 MU IM q3-4wk; secondary prophylaxis to prevent recurrent ARF and valve damage progression
0.1-0.5 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; afterload reduction for chronic mitral regurgitation
1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; diuretic for CHF symptoms from mitral valve dysfunction

second_line_drugs.notes: Conservative surgical repair preferred over replacement in children; avoids anticoagulation
For rheumatic mitral stenosis with favorable anatomy, MVA <1.5cm2, <moderate MR, no LA thrombus
Dose to target INR 2.5-3.5; for mechanical valve replacement or mitral stenosis with atrial fibrillation
5-10 mcg/kg/day PO; for rate control in atrial fibrillation or symptomatic HF

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Assess valve morphology, severity of MR/MS, LV and LA dimensions

investigations.name: ECG
investigations.indication: LA enlargement, atrial fibrillation, RV hypertrophy

counselling: Monthly penicillin injection must not be missed - it prevents further heart valve damage
Report any fever, joint pain, or new breathlessness immediately as it may indicate recurrence
Antibiotic prophylaxis before dental procedures is required lifelong
Maintain healthy weight and moderate activity as tolerated
Surgery may be needed if valve damage progresses - regular echo monitoring is essential
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist for ongoing valve monitoring; refer to cardiac surgery if severe MR/MS with symptoms, progressive LV dilation, or atrial fibrillation
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if acute heart failure, new atrial fibrillation, suspected recurrent ARF episode, planned percutaneous commissurotomy, or valve surgery
notes: Conservative valve repair is preferred over replacement in pediatric RHD to avoid lifelong anticoagulation. Strict secondary penicillin prophylaxis is critical to prevent recurrent valve injury.
source: AHA 2020 RHD Scientific Statement, ACC/AHA 2020 VHD

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.3 mg/kg/day (max 5-10mg) once daily, long-acting CCB
0.07 mg/kg/day (max 5mg) QD initially, preferred for CKD or proteinuria

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.7 mg/kg/day (max 50mg), alternative to ACE inhibitor
1-3 mg/kg/day (max 50mg), volume-dependent hypertension

investigations.name: Renal function tests (creatinine, BUN)
investigations.indication: Rule out renal cause of hypertension

investigations.name: Urinalysis with protein/creatinine ratio
investigations.indication: Screen for renal disease and proteinuria

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound with Doppler
investigations.indication: Screen for structural renal anomalies or renal artery stenosis

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Assess for left ventricular hypertrophy as end-organ damage

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes
investigations.indication: Baseline and monitor with ACEI/diuretic therapy

counselling: Reduce salt in cooking and avoid packaged/junk food which is high in sodium
Encourage daily physical activity for at least 60 minutes (walking, cycling, sports)
Maintain healthy weight - weight loss of even 5% can significantly lower blood pressure
Give medicine at the same time every day; do not skip doses
Regular blood pressure checks at home and at clinic visits are essential for monitoring
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist/cardiologist if stage 2 hypertension, secondary hypertension suspected (renal, endocrine), end-organ damage, or persistent hypertension despite 2 medications
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if hypertensive emergency (symptomatic: headache, seizures, visual changes, encephalopathy), BP >30 mmHg above 95th percentile, or evidence of end-organ damage (cardiac, renal, retinal)
notes: Beta-blockers NOT recommended as first-line in children. Lifestyle modification is essential first step.

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg/kg/dose PO q8h (ages 1-17yr); FDA-approved for WHO Group 1 PAH; titrate based on response
2 mg/kg/dose PO q12h (ages ≥3yr); ERA approved for pediatric PAH; monitor LFTs monthly
10-20 ppm via ventilator; first-line for acute PH crisis and PPHN; wean gradually

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 ng/kg/min IV continuous; titrate by 1-2 ng/kg/min q15min; for severe PAH (WHO FC III-IV)
1.25 ng/kg/min SC/IV; titrate slowly; prostacyclin analogue for advanced PAH
Dose per weight; dual ERA increasingly used in pediatric PAH; limited pediatric data
Oral IP receptor agonist; titrate from 200mcg q12h; emerging use in pediatric PAH

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Estimate PA pressure, assess RV function

investigations.name: Right heart catheterisation
investigations.indication: Confirm diagnosis, assess vasoreactivity, guide therapy

investigations.name: 6-minute walk test
investigations.indication: Functional capacity assessment in older children

counselling: Pulmonary hypertension requires lifelong medication - never stop without cardiologist guidance
Avoid high altitudes and air travel without supplemental oxygen
Avoid dehydration and excessive salt intake
Annual influenza vaccination and RSV prophylaxis are important
Report any worsening breathlessness, fainting, or blue discolouration immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to pulmonary hypertension centre for all confirmed PAH; escalation to prostacyclin therapy if WHO FC III-IV
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if acute RV failure, syncope, new arrhythmia, PAH crisis (acute PH with hemodynamic instability), oxygen desaturation <85%, or planned catheterisation
notes: Combination therapy (ERA + PDE5i) is increasingly standard for pediatric PAH. 2024 RCT showed sildenafil achieves faster pressure reduction than bosentan in neonatal PPHN.
source: AHA/ATS 2015 Pediatric PH Guidelines, ESC/ERS 2022 PH
guideline_changes: Sildenafil FDA-approved for pediatric PAH ages 1-17; 2024 RCT data on sildenafil vs bosentan in neonates

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg/dose PO q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); first-line NSAID; taper over 2-4 weeks after symptom resolution
Children ≤5yr: 0.5mg/day PO; >5yr: 1-1.5mg/day PO divided q12h; no loading dose; 3 months duration

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-10 mg/kg/dose PO q12h; alternative NSAID if ibuprofen not tolerated
0.25-0.5 mg/kg/day PO; only if NSAID+colchicine fail or contraindicated; slow taper over weeks
2-4 mg/kg/day SC (max 100mg); IL-1 receptor antagonist for refractory recurrent pericarditis
Loading 4.4 mg/kg SC then 2.2 mg/kg/wk; FDA-approved for recurrent pericarditis age ≥12yr

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Assess pericardial effusion size and hemodynamic impact

investigations.name: ECG
investigations.indication: Diffuse ST elevation, PR depression; low voltage if large effusion

investigations.name: CRP and ESR
investigations.indication: Inflammatory markers to guide duration of treatment

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Cardiomegaly if large effusion (water bottle sign)

counselling: Take ibuprofen with food to protect the stomach; colchicine is given for 3 months to prevent recurrence
Complete bed rest initially; no sports until symptoms resolve and CRP is normal
Watch for increasing chest pain, breathlessness, or dizziness - these need urgent evaluation
Avoid corticosteroid treatment if possible as it increases risk of recurrence
Follow-up echocardiogram is needed to ensure no pericardial effusion or constriction
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist if moderate-large pericardial effusion, recurrent pericarditis (>2 episodes), suspected purulent or tubercular pericarditis, or tamponade features
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if large pericardial effusion with hemodynamic compromise, cardiac tamponade, suspected purulent pericarditis, fever with myopericarditis, or high-risk features (troponin elevation)
notes: Colchicine added to NSAID halves recurrence rates. Avoid corticosteroids as first-line due to increased recurrence risk; exercise restriction until symptom and CRP resolution.
source: ACC 2025 Pericarditis Consensus, ESC 2015 Pericardial Diseases
guideline_changes: 2025 ACC Expert Consensus Statement on pericarditis diagnosis and management released

first_line_drugs.notes: Ampicillin 200-300 mg/kg/day IV divided q4-6h + Gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day IV q8h; empiric for native valve
40-60 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h; for MRSA or penicillin allergy; target trough 15-20 mcg/mL

second_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg/day IV once daily (max 4g); for viridans streptococci; 4-week course with or without gentamicin
200 mg/kg/day IV divided q4-6h; for MSSA endocarditis; 6-week course
Valve repair or replacement for refractory infection, large vegetations >10mm, embolic events, or HF

investigations.name: Blood cultures (3 sets)
investigations.indication: Before starting antibiotics; essential for organism identification

investigations.name: Echocardiography (TTE +/- TEE)
investigations.indication: Identify vegetations, assess valve function, abscess

investigations.name: Urinalysis
investigations.indication: Microscopic hematuria from immune complex deposition

counselling: Endocarditis requires 4-6 weeks of intravenous antibiotics - compliance is critical
Maintain excellent dental hygiene to prevent future episodes
The child may need a PICC line for prolonged IV antibiotics at home
Report any new fever, joint pain, or red spots on skin during treatment
Children with valve disease need antibiotic prophylaxis before dental procedures lifelong
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist and infectious disease specialist at diagnosis; refer to cardiac surgery if valve destruction, large vegetations >10mm, recurrent emboli, or refractory infection
hospitalisation_criteria: All infective endocarditis cases require hospitalisation for IV antibiotics, blood cultures, and monitoring; ICU if hemodynamic instability, acute valve failure, or stroke
notes: Treatment is 4-6 weeks IV antibiotics tailored to culture and sensitivity. Consult pediatric infectious disease; adjust doses based on renal function and drug levels.
source: AHA 2015 IE Update, ESC 2023 Endocarditis Guidelines
guideline_changes: 2023 ESC guidelines updated with refined surgical timing and diagnostic criteria

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; ACE inhibitor for LV dysfunction; titrate to 0.5 mg/kg/day
0.05 mg/kg/dose PO q12h; titrate to 0.3-0.5 mg/kg/dose; beta-blocker for symptomatic HF
1-2 mg/kg/dose IV/PO q6-12h; loop diuretic for fluid overload and pulmonary congestion
0.25-0.75 mcg/kg/min IV infusion; inodilator for cardiogenic shock with low cardiac output

second_line_drugs.notes: 2 g/kg IV over 10-12h single dose; immunomodulatory therapy; evidence mixed but commonly used
Extracorporeal membrane oxygenation; bridge to recovery or transplant for refractory cardiogenic shock
1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; aldosterone antagonist for chronic HF management

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Assess LV function, wall motion abnormalities, pericardial effusion

investigations.name: Cardiac MRI
investigations.indication: Gold standard for diagnosis; myocardial edema and late gadolinium enhancement

counselling: Your child has inflammation of the heart muscle - complete bed rest is essential
No sports or vigorous physical activity for at least 3-6 months after recovery
Give medicines regularly as prescribed - they help the heart recover
Watch for worsening breathlessness, chest pain, fast heartbeat, or swelling of feet
Regular follow-up echocardiograms are needed to monitor heart function recovery
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist immediately on suspicion; transfer to centre with ECMO capability if fulminant presentation
hospitalisation_criteria: All suspected myocarditis cases require hospitalisation; ICU admission if hemodynamically unstable, arrhythmia, or reduced ejection fraction <40%
notes: Supportive care is the mainstay; avoid NSAIDs and exercise restriction for 3-6 months. ECMO has shown good outcomes as bridge therapy in fulminant cases with recoverable myocardium.
source: AHA 2021 Pediatric Myocarditis Statement, ACC 2024 Myocarditis ECDP
guideline_changes: 2024 ACC Expert Consensus Decision Pathway for myocarditis diagnosis and management published

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.5 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; ACE inhibitor for DCM with systolic dysfunction; first-line GDMT
0.05-0.5 mg/kg/dose PO q12h; beta-blocker for DCM; titrate slowly over weeks
1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; loop diuretic for congestive symptoms

second_line_drugs.notes: ARNI; FDA-approved for children >1yr; dose by weight; emerging first-line for pediatric HFrEF
Cardiac myosin inhibitor for obstructive HCM; dose per protocol; new class in 2024 HCM guideline
1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; aldosterone antagonist adjunct for symptomatic HF
Loading 10-15 mg/kg/day PO x7-10d then 5 mg/kg/day; for ventricular arrhythmias in CM

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Assess chamber size, wall thickness, systolic and diastolic function

counselling: Cardiomyopathy is a chronic heart condition requiring lifelong medication and monitoring
Give all heart medicines at the same time every day; never skip doses
Restrict strenuous activity as advised; light activity may be allowed based on type
Monitor daily weight - sudden weight gain may indicate fluid retention
Genetic testing may be recommended for family screening in some types
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist at diagnosis; refer for transplant evaluation if refractory heart failure or EF <25% despite optimal GDMT
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if new-onset cardiomyopathy for workup, acute decompensated heart failure, ventricular arrhythmia, syncope, or need for inotropic support
notes: Treatment is guided by cardiomyopathy subtype: DCM needs afterload reduction, HCM needs negative inotropy. Sacubitril-valsartan is increasingly used in pediatric DCM based on FDA approval for age >1yr.
source: AHA 2023 Pediatric CM Statement, AHA/ACC 2024 HCM Guideline
guideline_changes: 2024 AHA/ACC HCM guideline adds cardiac myosin inhibitors for obstructive HCM

first_line_drugs.notes: Ice to face (infants) or Valsalva (older children); first-line non-pharmacologic intervention for SVT
0.1 mg/kg IV rapid push (max 6mg); if no response 0.2 mg/kg (max 12mg); flush with rapid NS bolus
1-3 mg/kg/day PO divided q8h; first-line prophylaxis for recurrent SVT in infants

second_line_drugs.notes: 2-4 mg/kg/day PO divided q8-12h; for SVT prophylaxis in structurally normal hearts; monitor ECG
5 mg/kg IV over 30-60min; for refractory SVT or hemodynamically unstable tachycardia
Radiofrequency or cryoablation of accessory pathway; curative for recurrent SVT; preferred in older children
0.5-1 J/kg; increase to 2 J/kg; for hemodynamically unstable SVT unresponsive to adenosine

investigations.name: 12-lead ECG (during and after episode)
investigations.indication: Capture tachycardia mechanism and check for pre-excitation

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Rule out structural heart disease; Ebstein anomaly with WPW

investigations.name: Event recorder
investigations.indication: For infrequent episodes; worn for weeks to capture events

counselling: SVT episodes can be scary but are usually not dangerous and can be stopped
Learn vagal maneuvers: ice pack on face for babies, Valsalva (blowing into blocked straw) for older children
If episode lasts more than 15 minutes or child becomes pale/unwell, go to emergency department
Take prophylactic medicine daily as prescribed to prevent episodes
Catheter ablation can permanently cure SVT in most older children - discuss with cardiologist
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric electrophysiologist for recurrent SVT, need for chronic medication, WPW pattern on ECG, or consideration for catheter ablation (typically >15 kg)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SVT with hemodynamic compromise, SVT not terminated by vagal maneuvers or single dose adenosine, wide complex tachycardia, or age <3 months with first SVT episode
notes: Adenosine is first-line pharmacotherapy for acute SVT termination; must be given as rapid push with immediate saline flush. Catheter ablation is curative and preferred for recurrent SVT in children >15kg.
source: AHA PALS 2020, EHRA/AEPC Pediatric Arrhythmia Consensus

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg IV rapid push (max 6mg); repeat at 0.2 mg/kg (max 12mg); first-line for SVT diagnosis/treatment
Loading 5 mg/kg IV over 30-60min; then 5-15 mcg/kg/min infusion; broad-spectrum antiarrhythmic

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-4 mg/kg/day PO divided q6-8h; beta-blocker for chronic arrhythmia suppression
2-8 mg/kg/day PO divided q8-12h; class III antiarrhythmic; monitor QTc closely
2-4 mg/kg/day PO divided q8-12h; class IC for SVT prophylaxis in structurally normal hearts
100-500 mcg/kg IV bolus then 50-500 mcg/kg/min infusion; ultra-short acting beta-blocker for acute use

investigations.name: 12-lead ECG
investigations.indication: Define arrhythmia type; essential first investigation

investigations.name: 24-hour Holter monitor
investigations.indication: Capture intermittent arrhythmias and assess daily burden

investigations.name: Thyroid function tests
investigations.indication: Hyperthyroidism as a cause of tachyarrhythmia

counselling: Your child has an abnormal heart rhythm that needs proper diagnosis with ECG and monitoring
Give anti-arrhythmic medicines exactly as prescribed; do not skip doses
Teach child and family to check pulse rate at home
Report any palpitations, dizziness, fainting, or chest pain immediately
Avoid excessive caffeine (energy drinks, colas) which can trigger abnormal rhythms
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric electrophysiologist for any sustained or symptomatic arrhythmia, WPW syndrome, or arrhythmia requiring chronic medication; catheter ablation evaluation
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if hemodynamically significant arrhythmia, SVT not responding to vagal maneuvers/adenosine, ventricular tachycardia, high-degree AV block, or syncope with arrhythmia
notes: Arrhythmia type must be defined by 12-lead ECG and Holter before chronic therapy. Centers should develop institution-specific pediatric antiarrhythmic protocols due to variable drug availability.
source: EHRA/AEPC 2013 Pediatric Arrhythmia Consensus, AHA PALS

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; titrate to 0.5 mg/kg/day; ACE inhibitor cornerstone of HFrEF GDMT
0.05 mg/kg/dose PO q12h; titrate to 0.35 mg/kg/dose; preferred beta-blocker in pediatric HF
1-2 mg/kg/dose PO/IV q6-12h; loop diuretic for volume overload; adjust to euvolemia
1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; aldosterone antagonist for symptomatic HFrEF

second_line_drugs.notes: ARNI; FDA-approved age >1yr; replacing ACE-I as preferred neurohormonal blockade in pediatric HFrEF
0.25-0.75 mcg/kg/min IV infusion; inodilator for acute decompensated HF with low output
5-10 mcg/kg/day PO; target level 0.5-1.0 ng/mL; for symptom relief in HFrEF

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Assess ejection fraction, chamber dimensions, valve function

investigations.name: BNP or NT-proBNP
investigations.indication: Diagnosis and monitoring of heart failure severity

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes and renal function
investigations.indication: Baseline and monitoring with diuretic/ACEI therapy

counselling: Heart failure medicines must be taken daily without missing; they help the heart pump better
Monitor weight daily - sudden increase of 0.5-1 kg may mean fluid retention
Restrict salt intake; avoid packaged foods, pickles, and papad
Watch for increasing breathlessness, leg swelling, or inability to lie flat - see doctor urgently
Some activity is beneficial; follow doctor guidance on safe exercise levels
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist for all new heart failure; refer for advanced HF/transplant evaluation if refractory to optimal GDMT
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if acute decompensated heart failure, new-onset HF for workup, need for IV diuretics or inotropes, arrhythmia, or pre-syncope/syncope
notes: Pediatric HF GDMT mirrors adult pillars: ACE-I/ARNI + beta-blocker + MRA + diuretic. SGLT2 inhibitors show promise but lack pediatric data; sacubitril-valsartan is rapidly becoming first-line.
source: AHA 2022 HF Guideline, AHA 2024 Pediatric CHD-HF Statement
guideline_changes: 2024 AHA scientific statement on chronic HF in children with CHD published

diagnosis_name: Common Cold (Acute Viral Upper Respiratory Infection)

first_line_drugs.notes: 10–15 mg/kg/dose PO every 6–8 hours SOS for fever >100°F, maximum 60 mg/kg/day, duration 3–5 days
2–3 drops in each nostril 4–6 times/day for nasal blockage relief and feeding improvement

second_line_drugs.notes: Oral drops as per weight (typically 2–6 drops TDS depending on age), use only if significant nasal discharge/congestion and child is symptomatic; avoid use >5 days and use cautiously in infants

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Persistent fever >3 days, toxic appearance, suspicion of bacterial infection

investigations.name: CRP
investigations.indication: To rule out bacterial infection if symptoms persist or worsen

counselling: This is a common viral illness and usually resolves on its own within few days — यह एक सामान्य वायरल बीमारी है, कुछ दिनों में अपने आप ठीक हो जाती है।
Continue breastfeeding and give adequate fluids; small frequent feeds are better — माँ का दूध जारी रखें और पर्याप्त तरल पदार्थ दें; थोड़ा-थोड़ा बार-बार पिलाएं।
Use medicines only as prescribed; avoid over-the-counter cough syrups in infants — केवल डॉक्टर की बताई दवा दें; छोटे बच्चों को बाज़ार की खांसी की दवा न दें।
Keep child warm, avoid cold exposure, and maintain hygiene — बच्चे को गर्म रखें, ठंड से बचाएं और साफ़-सफ़ाई रखें।
Follow-up after 2–3 days or earlier if symptoms worsen — 2-3 दिन बाद या लक्षण बिगड़ें तो पहले डॉक्टर को दिखाएं।
warning_signs: Poor feeding or refusal to feed
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatrician or higher center if no improvement after 3 days, recurrent episodes, or development of warning signs
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <92%, severe respiratory distress, poor feeding, dehydration, lethargy, or age <2 months with symptoms
notes: Viral upper respiratory infection; self-limiting illness lasting 5–7 days; antibiotics are not indicated routinely
guideline_changes: IAP 2024: Continued emphasis on no routine antibiotics or OTC cough/cold medicines in children; ACCP: Recommends against OTC cough suppressants in pediatric URI; No major guideline changes in 2024-2025 for common cold management
expected_course: Fever may last 2–3 days; nasal symptoms 5–7 days; cough may persist up to 7–10 days
key_clinical_points: Most common cause is viral; no role of antibiotics
Nasal saline is safest and most effective symptomatic relief in infants
Avoid strong cough syrups in children below 5 years
Maintain hydration and feeding is key to recovery

severity_assessment.mild: Runny nose, mild cough, no respiratory distress, feeding well
severity_assessment.moderate: Fever, irritability, reduced feeding, mild nasal blockage
severity_assessment.severe: Respiratory distress, hypoxia, poor feeding, lethargy

first_line_drugs.notes: 45-90 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 2 g/dose) for 10-14 days; standard first-line
90 mg/kg/day (amoxicillin) PO divided BID (max 2 g/dose) for 10-14d; severe or risk factors

second_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg IM/IV x1 dose (max 1 g); for vomiting or unable to tolerate oral; then switch to PO
30-40 mg/kg/day PO divided TID (max 1.8 g/day) for penicillin-allergic patients
Fluticasone 1 spray/nostril daily as adjunct to antibiotics; age >=2 yr

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis (no routine imaging)
investigations.indication: Diagnosis based on clinical criteria per AAP

investigations.name: CT sinuses
investigations.indication: Only if orbital or intracranial complications suspected

counselling: Sinusitis means infection of the sinuses (air spaces in the face bones)
Antibiotics need to be taken for the full 10-14 days even if your child feels better earlier
Saline nose drops or spray help clear the sinuses - use before meals and bedtime
Avoid smoke exposure and keep the house dust-free
Return if symptoms worsen after 48-72 hours of antibiotics or if high fever develops
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if complications (orbital/intracranial), recurrent acute sinusitis (>4 episodes/year), or progression to chronic sinusitis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for orbital complications (periorbital/orbital cellulitis), intracranial complications, or toxic appearance
notes: Diagnose bacterial sinusitis by clinical criteria (persistent >10d, worsening, or severe onset). Watchful waiting for 3 days may be appropriate for mild uncomplicated cases.

first_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg PO once daily (max 1000 mg) or 25 mg/kg BID (max 500 mg/dose) for 10 days
250 mg PO BID-TID (<27 kg) or 500 mg PO BID (>=27 kg) for 10 days for confirmed GAS
<27 kg: 600,000 U IM x1; >=27 kg: 1.2 MU IM x1; for adherence concerns

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/dose PO BID (max 500 mg/dose) for 10 days; non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy
12 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg) then 6 mg/kg/day x4 days; for anaphylactic PCN allergy
7 mg/kg/dose PO TID (max 300 mg/dose) for 10 days; for type 1 PCN allergy

investigations.name: Rapid strep test (throat swab)
investigations.indication: Detect Group A Streptococcus before prescribing antibiotics

investigations.name: Throat culture
investigations.indication: If rapid test negative but clinical suspicion high

counselling: Sore throat can be caused by virus or bacteria; a throat swab test will tell which one
If it is strep throat (bacterial), antibiotics for full 10 days are needed to prevent rheumatic fever
Give soft, warm foods and plenty of fluids; cold items like ice cream also help soothe the throat
Paracetamol or ibuprofen can be given for pain and fever
Your child can return to school 24 hours after starting antibiotics if feeling better
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if recurrent strep pharyngitis (>=7/year or >=5/year for 2 years) for tonsillectomy consideration; rheumatology if rheumatic fever develops
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for peritonsillar abscess, inability to swallow fluids, dehydration, or airway compromise
notes: Rapid strep test or throat culture should confirm GAS before starting antibiotics. Treatment prevents rheumatic fever and reduces symptom duration by 1-2 days.

first_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg/day (max 1g) once daily x10 days
Children 250mg BID-TID, adolescents 500mg BID-TID x10 days

second_line_drugs.notes: 12 mg/kg/day (max 500mg) x5 days, for penicillin allergy

investigations.name: Rapid Antigen Detection Test (RADT)
investigations.indication: First-line test for suspected strep pharyngitis

investigations.name: Throat culture
investigations.indication: If RADT negative but high clinical suspicion (McIsaac >=3)

investigations.name: ASO titre
investigations.indication: If suspected post-streptococcal complication (rheumatic fever, glomerulonephritis)

counselling: Full 10-day course of antibiotic is essential to prevent rheumatic fever - do not stop early
Child can return to school after 24 hours on antibiotics and no fever
Offer warm fluids and soft foods; cold items like ice cream may soothe throat
Replace toothbrush after 48 hours of antibiotics to prevent re-infection
Watch for joint pain, skin rash, or chest pain in the weeks after - see doctor immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if recurrent strep pharyngitis (>=7 episodes/year, >=5/year for 2 years, or >=3/year for 3 years) meeting Paradise criteria for tonsillectomy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if peritonsillar abscess suspected (trismus, uvula deviation, drooling), retropharyngeal abscess, severe dehydration from inability to swallow, or Lemierre syndrome signs
notes: 10-day course required to prevent acute rheumatic fever. Only treat confirmed positive RADT or culture.
guideline_changes: IDSA 2025: use Centor/McIsaac clinical scoring BEFORE testing - shift from 2012 universal testing approach.

first_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg PO once daily (max 1000 mg) or 25 mg/kg BID (max 500 mg/dose) for 10 days
250 mg PO BID-TID (<27 kg) or 500 mg PO BID (>=27 kg) for 10 days for confirmed GAS
<27 kg: 600,000 U IM x1; >=27 kg: 1.2 MU IM x1; for adherence concerns

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/dose PO BID (max 500 mg/dose) for 10 days; for non-anaphylactic PCN allergy
12 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg) then 6 mg/kg/day x4 days; for type 1 PCN allergy
7 mg/kg/dose PO TID (max 300 mg/dose) for 10 days; penicillin allergy alternative
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg q4-6h or ibuprofen 10 mg/kg q6-8h PRN for pain/fever

investigations.name: Throat culture
investigations.indication: If rapid test negative but GAS clinically suspected

investigations.name: Monospot test
investigations.indication: If EBV mononucleosis suspected (bilateral tonsillar enlargement, lymphadenopathy)

counselling: Tonsillitis is usually caused by a virus but strep bacteria also cause it
If strep test is positive, complete 10 days of antibiotics to prevent rheumatic fever and kidney problems
Warm salt water gargling (for children >6 years) and cold fluids help with pain
Your child can go back to school 24 hours after starting antibiotics
If tonsillitis keeps recurring (>7 times a year), surgery to remove tonsils may be recommended
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT for recurrent tonsillitis (>=7/yr, >=5/yr x2 yrs, or >=3/yr x3 yrs) or peritonsillar abscess
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for peritonsillar abscess drainage, airway compromise from severe tonsillar enlargement, inability to swallow, or dehydration
notes: Confirm GAS with rapid strep test or culture before prescribing antibiotics. Tonsillectomy is considered for recurrent GAS tonsillitis (>=7 episodes/yr).

first_line_drugs.notes: 8-10 mg/kg/day div BD PO for 5-7 days
10-15 mg/kg/dose PO 6-8 hourly SOS for fever/pain
5-10 mg/kg/dose PO 6-8 hourly SOS, for fever and throat pain
If child >5 years, adequate hydration, soft diet (khichdi, soups). Avoid cold items

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg/dose BD PO for 7-10 days, reserve drug for severe/non-responding/resistant cases only

counselling: Complete full course of antibiotic even if child feels better in 2-3 days
Give warm fluids and soft diet (khichdi, soups) to ease swallowing
Warm saline gargles help if child is older than 5 years
Paracetamol/ibuprofen will control fever and throat pain
Return if fever persists beyond 3 days, difficulty swallowing saliva, neck swelling, or breathing difficulty
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Persistent fever for more than 3 days
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if recurrent tonsillitis (>7 episodes/year or >5/year for 2 years), suspected peritonsillar abscess, or airway compromise
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if unable to swallow, severe dehydration, peritonsillar abscess, toxic appearance, or breathing difficulty
notes: Start antibiotic if high-grade fever with follicles or toxic child. Supportive: warm saline gargles (if child >5 years), adequate hydration, soft diet. Avoid cold items.
source: IAP Guidelines, IDSA Pharyngitis Guidelines 2012/2024 update, AHA Rheumatic Fever Prevention
guideline_changes: IDSA 2024: Continued emphasis on rapid antigen detection test (RADT) or culture before starting antibiotics for pharyngitis; Centor/McIsaac scoring to guide testing; Penicillin V or amoxicillin remain first-line for confirmed GAS; IAP 2024: Antibiotic stewardship - avoid empirical antibiotics for viral tonsillitis; reserve cephalosporins for penicillin-allergic patients

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.6 mg/kg PO/IM x1 dose (max 16 mg); onset 2-3h, lasts 24-48h; effective for all severity
L-epi 1:1000: 0.5 mL/kg (max 5 mL) or racemic epi 2.25%: 0.05 mL/kg (max 0.5 mL) neb
Cool mist therapy; limited evidence but commonly used for comfort; avoid steam (burn risk)

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) for 2-3 days; if dexamethasone unavailable
2 mg neb x1 dose; for patients unable to take PO dexamethasone or with persistent symptoms
70:30 or 80:20 helium-oxygen mixture via face mask; for severe croup with stridor at rest

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis (no routine investigations)
investigations.indication: Croup is diagnosed clinically by barking cough and stridor

investigations.name: Neck X-ray (AP view)
investigations.indication: Steeple sign if diagnosis uncertain (not routine)

counselling: Croup causes a barking cough and noisy breathing which sounds worse at night
A single dose of dexamethasone steroid medicine usually helps a lot within a few hours
Cool night air or cool mist can help ease the breathing - take the child near an open window
Most croup gets better in 3-5 days; the barking cough may last a bit longer
Rush to hospital if your child has noisy breathing at rest, drooling, or turns blue
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if recurrent croup (>3 episodes) to rule out subglottic stenosis or other airway abnormality
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if stridor at rest persists after nebulized epinephrine, severe respiratory distress, age <6 months, or toxic appearance (suspect bacterial tracheitis)
notes: A single dose of oral dexamethasone is effective for all croup severities including mild. Observe for 2-4 hours after nebulized epinephrine due to rebound potential.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.15-0.6 mg/kg single dose PO/IM/IV, max 16 mg; gold standard, reduces airway edema
0.5 ml/kg of 1:1000 solution (max 5 ml) nebulised, repeat after 20-30 min if needed; for moderate-severe cases with stridor at rest
If SpO2 < 92%, via nasal prongs or mask
Age/weight appropriate dose nebulised or PO, optional bronchial/cough support, not primary treatment for croup
Age-appropriate dose PO, only if allergic component present

investigations.name: Neck X-ray AP view
investigations.indication: Steeple sign if diagnosis uncertain or atypical

counselling: Croup causes a barking cough and noisy breathing - keep child calm as crying worsens symptoms
Use humidified air or steam in the room
Give adequate fluids and small frequent meals
Improvement expected within 24 hours after steroid dose
Full recovery usually in 3-5 days
If stridor persists at rest, child has severe breathing difficulty, SpO2 drops, or child becomes lethargic - come to hospital immediately
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Stridor at rest that does not improve
Poor oral intake or refusal to drink
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist or ENT if recurrent croup (>2 episodes), atypical presentation, suspected foreign body, or subglottic stenosis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if stridor at rest persists after dexamethasone and adrenaline, severe retractions, SpO2 <92%, lethargy/exhaustion, poor oral intake, or need for repeated adrenaline nebulisation
notes: Dexamethasone single dose is the single most effective treatment. Adrenaline nebulisation for emergency relief in moderate-severe cases - observe 2-4 hours after dose for recurrence. Keep child calm (crying worsens stridor). Humidified air, adequate fluids. Avoid unnecessary antibiotics. Severity: Mild = barking cough no stridor at rest; Moderate = stridor at rest with mild retractions; Severe = marked distress with hypoxia.
guideline_changes: 2024 cohort data: single-dose dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg) remains gold standard; repeat dosing in hospitalised children showed longer LOS without benefit on 30-day ED return; 0.15 mg/kg may suffice for mild croup; nebulised epinephrine observation period confirmed at 2-4 hr.

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg/dose PO q4-6h PRN (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4 g/day) for fever and discomfort
10 mg/kg/dose PO q6-8h PRN (max 40 mg/kg/day); age >=6 months for fever/pain
Saline drops (infants) or spray/rinse (older children) PRN for nasal congestion

second_line_drugs.notes: 2.5-10 mL PO at bedtime for nocturnal cough; age >1 yr only (botulism risk in infants)
Bedside humidifier at night for cough/congestion relief; keep device clean
0.6 mg/kg PO x1 (max 16 mg) if laryngotracheitis/croup component identified

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Only if fever prolonged >5 days to rule out bacterial infection

counselling: Upper respiratory infection (URI) is very common in children and caused by viruses
Antibiotics are NOT helpful and should not be used for viral URI
Keep your child hydrated with warm fluids; honey at bedtime for cough (>1 year only)
Do not give OTC cough and cold medicines to children under 4 years
See a doctor if fever lasts >5 days, breathing becomes fast, or ear pain develops
referral_criteria: Referral not usually needed; refer to ENT if recurrent URI with complications or suspected anatomical abnormality
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalization rarely needed; admit if febrile infant <3 months, severe dehydration, or complications develop
notes: Antibiotics are NOT indicated for viral URI. Avoid OTC cough/cold medications in children <4 years per AAP recommendation due to lack of efficacy and safety concerns.

diagnosis_name: Influenza due to identified influenza virus

first_line_drugs.notes: <1yr: 3 mg/kg PO bid x5 days; 1-12yr weight-based PO bid x5 days (max 75 mg bid)
Single PO dose: 20-<80 kg 40 mg; >=80 kg 80 mg; age >=5yr, within 48h

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg (2 inhalations) bid for 5 days; age >=7yr
12 mg/kg IV single dose (max 600 mg); age >=6mo, unable to take PO

investigations.name: Influenza PCR (nasopharyngeal)
investigations.indication: Confirm if rapid test negative but clinical suspicion high

counselling: Give oseltamivir (Tamiflu) within 48 hours of symptom onset for best results
The child should rest at home and drink plenty of fluids
Keep the child away from school for at least 24 hours after fever resolves
Wash hands frequently and cover coughs/sneezes with elbow
Annual influenza vaccination is the best prevention — get it every year before monsoon/winter season
referral_criteria: Refer to hospital if respiratory distress, dehydration, altered consciousness, or high-risk child (asthma, immunocompromised, cardiac disease)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for respiratory distress, hypoxia, encephalopathy, myocarditis, or severe dehydration
notes: Start antivirals within 48h of symptom onset; still consider beyond 48h in high-risk children. Oseltamivir is preferred due to widest age range and formulation options.

first_line_drugs.notes: <1yr: 3 mg/kg PO bid x5 days; 1-12yr weight-based PO bid x5 days (max 75 mg bid)
Single PO dose: 20-<80 kg 40 mg; >=80 kg 80 mg; age >=5yr, within 48h

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg (2 inhalations) bid for 5 days; age >=7yr
12 mg/kg IV single dose (max 600 mg); age >=6mo, unable to take PO

investigations.name: Respiratory viral panel PCR
investigations.indication: If influenza test negative but viral illness suspected

counselling: Start antiviral medicine (Tamiflu) as early as possible — even before test results return
Keep the child home, hydrated, and resting
Paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever and body aches
Do not give aspirin to children with viral illness (risk of Reye syndrome)
Annual flu vaccination recommended for all children above 6 months
referral_criteria: Refer to hospital if respiratory distress, high-risk child, or no improvement after 48 hours of antiviral therapy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for respiratory distress, hypoxia (SpO2 <92%), dehydration, seizures, or immunocompromised status
notes: Treat clinically suspected influenza empirically when testing is pending or unavailable. Same antiviral approach regardless of virus identification status.

first_line_drugs.notes: O2 if SpO2 <92%; IV fluids if dehydrated; antipyretics PRN; humidified air
<1 yr: 3 mg/kg PO BID; 1-12 yr: weight-based (30-75 mg BID); start within 48h x5 days

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-<12 yr, >=20 kg: weight-based single dose PO; >=12 yr & 40-80 kg: 40 mg x1; >80 kg: 80 mg
>=3.5 kg: 5 mg/kg IV day 1, then 2.5 mg/kg IV daily for 5 days; for hospitalized pts
Aerosolized: 6 g reconstituted in 300 mL via SPAG-2 x12-18 hr/day; severe immunocompromised RSV

counselling: Your child has a viral lung infection - antibiotics will NOT help as it is caused by a virus
Most viral pneumonia gets better on its own in 1-2 weeks with rest and fluids
Give paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever; ensure your child drinks plenty of fluids
If your child has influenza, antiviral medicine (oseltamivir) works best if started within 48 hours
Return if breathing worsens, fever persists beyond 5 days, or child stops eating/drinking
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if severe course, not improving as expected, or underlying lung/immune condition
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <92%, severe respiratory distress, dehydration, or high-risk child (immunocompromised, chronic lung disease)
notes: Most viral pneumonia in children is self-limiting and requires only supportive care. Oseltamivir should be started within 48 hours for confirmed or suspected influenza.

first_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 4 g/day) for 5-7 days; first-line oral therapy
150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 12 g/day) for hospitalized uncomplicated cases
200,000-250,000 U/kg/day IV divided q4-6h (max 24 MU/day) if penicillin-susceptible

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mg/kg/day IV/IM once daily (max 4 g) for penicillin-resistant strains (MIC >=2)
30-40 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 2.7 g/day) for penicillin-allergic patients
>=5 yr: 8-10 mg/kg/day PO/IV daily (max 750 mg) for beta-lactam allergy
40-60 mg/kg/day IV q6-8h (max 4 g/day) add if meningitis suspected or highly resistant

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Confirm pneumonia and check for complications

counselling: Your child has pneumonia caused by pneumococcus bacteria which responds well to antibiotics
Complete the full course of antibiotics even after your child starts feeling better
Ensure your child has received all pneumococcal vaccine doses (PCV) as per schedule
Give adequate fluids and rest; paracetamol for fever
Return if fever persists after 48 hours of antibiotics or breathing worsens
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if complicated pneumonia (empyema, necrotizing) or recurrent pneumococcal infections suggesting immunodeficiency
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <92%, unable to take oral medicines, moderate-severe respiratory distress, complicated pneumonia, or age <6 months
notes: High-dose amoxicillin (90 mg/kg/day) overcomes intermediate penicillin resistance in S. pneumoniae. Ensure PCV13/15/20 vaccination is up to date.

first_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day (amoxicillin) PO divided BID (max 4 g/day) for 7 days; covers beta-lactamase
50-100 mg/kg/day IV/IM once daily (max 4 g) for hospitalized patients

second_line_drugs.notes: 150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 12 g/day) alternative to ceftriaxone
10 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg) then 5 mg/kg/day x4 days for beta-lactam allergy
150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h; only if beta-lactamase negative confirmed
8-10 mg/kg/day (TMP) PO divided BID for 7-10 days as oral step-down alternative

investigations.name: Blood culture
investigations.indication: Confirm H. influenzae and susceptibility testing

counselling: Your child has pneumonia caused by Haemophilus bacteria - antibiotics will treat it
Complete the full antibiotic course as prescribed even if your child improves early
Ensure your child has received Hib vaccine doses as per schedule
Give plenty of fluids and paracetamol for fever
Return if fever does not improve in 48 hours or breathing worsens
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if complicated or recurrent pneumonia; immunology if recurrent H. influenzae infections
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <92%, severe respiratory distress, unable to take oral medicines, or complicated pneumonia
notes: 25-50% of H. influenzae produce beta-lactamase; do not use ampicillin empirically. Use amoxicillin-clavulanate or a cephalosporin for empiric coverage.

first_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID (max 4 g/day) for 5-7 days; first-line outpatient
150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 12 g/day) for hospitalized uncomplicated CAP
50-100 mg/kg/day IV/IM once daily (max 4 g) if not fully immunized or resistant organism

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg) then 5 mg/kg/day x4 days for atypical coverage
30-40 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 2.7 g/day) if MRSA or anaerobe suspected
6 mo-4 yr: 16-20 mg/kg/day IV/PO BID; >=5 yr: 8-10 mg/kg/day daily (max 750 mg)
150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 12 g/day) for hospitalized patients

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Confirm pneumonia and assess for complications

investigations.name: Blood culture
investigations.indication: Identify pathogen; obtain before antibiotics if possible

investigations.name: CRP/Procalcitonin
investigations.indication: Guide antibiotic therapy and monitor response

counselling: Your child has a bacterial lung infection (pneumonia) that needs antibiotics
Complete the full antibiotic course even if your child feels better after a few days
Give plenty of fluids and rest; keep your child in a well-ventilated room
Return to hospital if fever does not come down after 48 hours of antibiotics or breathing worsens
Make sure your child is up to date on pneumococcal and flu vaccines to prevent future pneumonia
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if complicated pneumonia (effusion, empyema, necrotizing), recurrent pneumonia, or immunocompromised host
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <92%, moderate-severe respiratory distress, unable to tolerate oral fluids/medicines, age <6 months, or complicated pneumonia
notes: Amoxicillin is first-line for outpatient CAP in immunized children. Viral pathogens cause most pneumonia in preschoolers; antibiotics not always needed.
guideline_changes: IDSA/PIDS 2026 updated CAP guidelines released Feb 2026 replacing 2011 version

first_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 4 g/day) for 5-7 days; outpatient empiric first-line
150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 12 g/day) for hospitalized children
O2 if SpO2 <92%; IV fluids if dehydrated; antipyretics (acetaminophen 15 mg/kg q4-6h)

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mg/kg/day IV/IM once daily (max 4 g) for under-immunized or severe illness
10 mg/kg PO day 1 then 5 mg/kg/day x4 days for school-age if atypical suspected
<=15 kg: 30 mg BID; 15-23 kg: 45 mg; 23-40 kg: 60 mg; >40 kg: 75 mg BID x5d if influenza
30-40 mg/kg/day IV q6-8h (max 2.7 g/day) add if necrotizing/MRSA suspected

counselling: Your child has a lung infection (pneumonia) - most cases in young children are caused by viruses
If antibiotics are prescribed, complete the full course; do not stop early
Give plenty of warm fluids, rest, and paracetamol/ibuprofen for fever
Watch for danger signs: very fast breathing, chest indrawing, refusing to eat, becoming very sleepy
Pneumococcal and influenza vaccines help prevent many types of pneumonia
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if complicated pneumonia, recurrent pneumonia (>2 episodes in 1 year), or underlying risk factors
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <92%, severe respiratory distress, unable to feed/drink, toxic appearance, or age <6 months with suspected bacterial pneumonia
notes: Viral causes predominate in children <5 years; antibiotics are not always required. Obtain CXR and labs to help differentiate bacterial vs viral etiology.
guideline_changes: IDSA/PIDS 2026 updated empiric CAP therapy recommendations

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg/dose PO q4-6h PRN (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4 g/day) for fever and discomfort
10 mg/kg/dose PO q6-8h PRN (max 40 mg/kg/day); age >=6 months for fever/pain
2.5-10 mL PO at bedtime for cough in children >1 yr; NOT for infants <1 yr (botulism risk)

second_line_drugs.notes: Bedside use at night for cough relief; keep clean to prevent mold
1-2 mg/kg/day divided q6-8h PO; age >4 yr only; limited evidence of efficacy
40-50 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for 2-4 wk if chronic wet cough >4 wk (PBB)

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Only if pneumonia or complications suspected

counselling: Acute bronchitis is usually caused by a virus and antibiotics are NOT needed
Cough may last 2-3 weeks even after the child is better - this is normal
Give plenty of warm fluids; honey at bedtime helps cough in children over 1 year
Avoid cough syrups in children under 4 years as they do not work and can be harmful
Return if fever persists beyond 5 days, or if your child develops difficulty breathing
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if wet cough persists >4 weeks (suspected protracted bacterial bronchitis) or recurrent bronchitis
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalization rarely needed; admit only if respiratory distress, inability to maintain hydration, or suspected complications (pneumonia)
notes: Antibiotics should NOT be prescribed for acute bronchitis as etiology is almost always viral. Consider protracted bacterial bronchitis only if wet cough persists >4 weeks.

first_line_drugs.notes: Via nasal cannula if SpO2 persistently <90%; titrate to maintain SpO2 >=90%
Gentle superficial suctioning of nares PRN; avoid deep suctioning
If unable to maintain oral hydration; NS or D5-NS at maintenance rate

second_line_drugs.notes: 2 L/kg/min (max 8-10 L/min in infants) if worsening on standard O2; FiO2 titrated
3% NaCl 4 mL neb q4-8h; may reduce hospital stay in admitted patients; not for ED
0.15 mg/kg neb x1 dose; consider in >12 mo with prior wheeze/atopy; stop if no response

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Only if diagnostic uncertainty or clinical deterioration (not routine)

counselling: Bronchiolitis is a viral chest infection very common in babies during winter months
Antibiotics do not help as this is a viral infection - supportive care is the main treatment
Keep your baby's nose clear by gentle suctioning before feeds
Feed small amounts frequently as babies tire easily while breathing fast
Come back to hospital if your baby stops feeding, breathes very fast, or turns blue
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if atypical course, recurrent bronchiolitis, or underlying lung/heart disease
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 persistently <90%, unable to maintain oral feeding, marked respiratory distress, age <3 months, or significant comorbidity (CHD, prematurity, immunodeficiency)
notes: Bronchodilators, corticosteroids, and antibiotics are NOT routinely recommended. Supportive care with oxygen and hydration is the mainstay of treatment.

first_line_drugs.notes: O2 if SpO2 <90%, nasal suctioning PRN, IV/NG fluids if poor oral intake; no routine meds
<5 kg: 50 mg IM x1; >=5 kg: 100 mg IM x1; before first RSV season; high-risk 2nd season: 200 mg
2 L/kg/min for infants with worsening respiratory distress despite standard O2

second_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg IM monthly x5 doses during RSV season; being phased out by Dec 2025
3% NaCl 4 mL neb q4-8h; for hospitalized patients; may reduce LOS
Aerosolized ribavirin reserved for severely immunocompromised with life-threatening RSV

investigations.name: RSV rapid antigen test or PCR
investigations.indication: Confirm RSV for cohorting and epidemiology

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Only if clinical deterioration or suspected complication

counselling: Your baby has an RSV infection which is the most common cause of bronchiolitis
Treatment is mainly supportive - oxygen and fluids if needed; medicines do not help much
Keep your baby away from other sick children and crowded places during illness
Handwashing is the most important way to prevent spreading the infection
Ask your doctor about nirsevimab injection to protect your baby before the next RSV season
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if prolonged course, underlying chronic lung disease, or recurrent RSV infections
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <90%, unable to feed, marked respiratory distress, age <6 weeks, or high-risk (preterm, CHD, immunodeficiency)
notes: Nirsevimab is now the preferred single-dose prophylaxis for RSV in infants entering their first RSV season. Treatment remains supportive; no antiviral has proven benefit in routine RSV bronchiolitis.
guideline_changes: Nirsevimab (2023) approved as single-dose RSV prophylaxis replacing monthly palivizumab for most infants

first_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 4 g/day) for 5 days if bacterial cause suspected
Supplemental O2 if SpO2 <92%; oral rehydration; antipyretics PRN

second_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day (amoxicillin) PO divided BID (max 4 g/day) if resistant organisms suspected
10 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg) then 5 mg/kg/day days 2-5 if atypical pathogen suspected
50-100 mg/kg/day IV/IM once daily (max 2 g) if hospitalized or unable to take PO

investigations.name: CRP
investigations.indication: Inflammatory marker to guide antibiotic use

counselling: Your child has a lower respiratory infection - it may be viral or bacterial
If antibiotics are prescribed, complete the full course even if your child feels better
Give plenty of fluids and rest; paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever
Watch for danger signs: fast breathing, chest indrawing, not feeding, becoming drowsy
Vaccination (pneumococcal, Hib, influenza) helps prevent many chest infections
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if recurrent LRTI, poor response to antibiotics, or underlying chronic condition
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <92%, severe respiratory distress, unable to feed, danger signs per WHO criteria, or failed outpatient treatment
notes: Most pediatric LRTIs are viral and require only supportive care. Reserve antibiotics for suspected bacterial infection based on clinical features and inflammatory markers.

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 puff each nostril BD, continue at least 4-6 weeks, regular use not SOS
6-12 yrs: 60-120 mg OD PO; >12 yrs: 120-180 mg OD PO, for 7-14 days or longer
2-3 drops each nostril 2-3 times/day, clears allergens and improves drug delivery
1 puff each nostril BD, for moderate-severe or persistent rhinitis, faster symptom relief

investigations.name: CBC with absolute eosinophil count
investigations.indication: Allergy indicator, elevated eosinophils suggest atopy

investigations.name: Allergy skin prick test / specific IgE
investigations.indication: Identify specific triggers in recurrent/severe cases

counselling: Use nasal spray regularly every day, not just when symptoms are bad
Avoid dust, smoke, perfumes, and cold exposure - these are common triggers
Saline nasal drops before steroid spray improve drug delivery
Improvement takes 3-5 days, full control in 2-3 weeks - do not stop early
Keep home dust-free, use cotton bedding, and avoid pets if allergic
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Severe nasal blockage causing difficulty breathing
Disturbed sleep or mouth breathing at night
Symptoms not improving despite regular medication
referral_criteria: Refer to allergist/ENT if poor response to 4-6 weeks of treatment, severe persistent symptoms, associated asthma requiring step-up therapy, or need for allergy testing
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation rarely needed. Consider if severe associated asthma exacerbation or complications like sinusitis requiring IV antibiotics
notes: ARIA classification: Intermittent (<4 days/week) vs Persistent (>4 days/week); Mild vs Moderate-Severe. Intranasal steroid is gold standard. Allergen avoidance is essential: dust, smoke, perfumes, cold exposure, pets, house dust mites.
source: ARIA-EAACI Guidelines 2024-2025, IAP Allergy Protocol, GINA
guideline_changes: ARIA-EAACI 2024-2025: Combined intranasal antihistamine + corticosteroid (INAH+INCS) recommended over monotherapy for moderate-severe AR; INCS preferred over INAH alone; digital shared decision-making algorithms introduced; pediatric considerations updated for preschool and school-age children; emphasis on step-up/step-down approach based on symptom control

first_line_drugs.notes: Fluticasone 1-2 sprays/nostril daily or mometasone 1 spray/nostril daily; age >=2 yr
Isotonic or hypertonic saline rinse 1-2 times daily via squeeze bottle or neti pot
45 mg/kg/day (amoxicillin) PO divided BID for acute exacerbations; max 875 mg/dose

second_line_drugs.notes: Surgical removal of adenoids; first-line surgical option when medical therapy fails
Reserved for refractory CRS after failed medical therapy and adenoidectomy
30-40 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for 21 days if anaerobes suspected; max 1.8 g/day

investigations.name: CT sinuses (non-contrast)
investigations.indication: Confirm chronic sinusitis and assess extent

investigations.name: Nasal endoscopy
investigations.indication: Visualize middle meatus, polyps, adenoid hypertrophy

investigations.name: Allergy testing (skin prick or specific IgE)
investigations.indication: Identify allergic component contributing to CRS

counselling: Chronic sinusitis means long-standing sinus infection lasting more than 12 weeks
Daily nasal saline rinse is very helpful - teach your child to do it morning and evening
Use the nasal spray medicine regularly as prescribed even when symptoms improve
Avoid exposure to cigarette smoke and dust as these worsen sinus problems
If your child has nasal polyps or does not improve with medicines, surgery may be needed
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if symptoms persist after 3 months of medical therapy, nasal polyps identified, or recurrent acute exacerbations
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for orbital or intracranial complications of sinusitis (orbital cellulitis, epidural abscess), or for endoscopic sinus surgery
notes: Chronic sinusitis requires symptoms >12 weeks without resolution. Routine systemic antibiotics are not recommended for uncomplicated chronic sinusitis in children.
guideline_changes: Emerging biologic therapies under investigation for pediatric CRS with nasal polyps

diagnosis_name: Chronic diseases of tonsils and adenoids

first_line_drugs.notes: For ≥7 episodes/1yr, ≥5/yr x2yr, or ≥3/yr x3yr of documented strep/tonsillitis; or OSA
50mg/kg/day PO divided bid-tid x10d for acute exacerbations of chronic tonsillitis
0.5mg/kg IV single dose intraop (max 10mg) for tonsillectomy; reduces PONV/pain

second_line_drugs.notes: 45mg/kg/day PO divided bid x10d for recurrent tonsillitis unresponsive to amoxicillin
20-30mg/kg/day PO divided tid x10d for chronic tonsillitis biofilm eradication; PCN allergy
5-10mg/kg PO q6-8h PRN x7-10d post-tonsillectomy; safe per AAO-HNS 2019 update
10-15mg/kg PO q4-6h PRN pain post-tonsillectomy; max 75mg/kg/day

investigations.name: Polysomnography (sleep study)
investigations.indication: If sleep-disordered breathing/OSA suspected before surgery

counselling: If surgery is recommended, it means your child meets criteria for frequent, severe infections
Post-surgery: soft foods and plenty of fluids for 10-14 days; avoid hard or crunchy foods
Pain after tonsillectomy peaks on day 3-5; give paracetamol and ibuprofen regularly, NOT codeine
Watch for post-operative bleeding (fresh blood from mouth/nose) - go to emergency immediately
The child may have bad breath and white patches in throat after surgery - this is normal healing
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if meeting Paradise criteria (>=7 episodes/1yr, >=5/yr x2yr, >=3/yr x3yr), sleep-disordered breathing/OSA, peritonsillar abscess, or suspected malignancy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for tonsillectomy (usually day surgery); emergency admission for peritonsillar abscess (drainage), post-tonsillectomy haemorrhage, or airway compromise from tonsillar hypertrophy
notes: Watchful waiting recommended if infection frequency is below Paradise criteria thresholds. Post-tonsillectomy: codeine is CONTRAINDICATED in children <12yr; ibuprofen and acetaminophen are mainstays of pain management.
snomed_display: [X]Anemia in other chronic diseases classified elsewhere

first_line_drugs.notes: Indicated for OSA/SDB with tonsillar hypertrophy; PSG recommended if <2yr, obese, or comorbid
1 spray per nostril daily trial x6wk for mild SDB before surgical decision; age ≥2yr
0.5mg/kg IV single dose intraop (max 10mg); reduces PONV and improves oral intake

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-10mg/kg PO q6-8h PRN x7-10d post-tonsillectomy; AAO-HNS confirms safety
10-15mg/kg PO q4-6h PRN pain post-tonsillectomy; max 75mg/kg/day
4mg (2-5yr) or 5mg (6-14yr) PO daily for persistent OSA post-surgery; FDA black box warning
If snoring without OSA/apnea and no impact on growth, behavior, or school performance

investigations.name: Polysomnography (sleep study)
investigations.indication: Document OSA severity before surgery; recommended if <2 years, obese, or comorbid

investigations.name: Lateral neck X-ray
investigations.indication: If unable to visualize tonsils or adenoid assessment needed

counselling: If your child snores loudly and stops breathing during sleep, a sleep study may be needed
Tonsillectomy has excellent results for sleep apnoea caused by large tonsils
Post-surgery: soft foods for 10-14 days; regular pain medicines (paracetamol + ibuprofen); NO codeine
Watch for bleeding from mouth/nose after surgery - go to emergency immediately
Keep child well hydrated after surgery; ice lollies and cold drinks can soothe the throat
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if tonsillar hypertrophy causing OSA/sleep-disordered breathing, dysphagia, failure to thrive from poor eating, or asymmetric tonsillar enlargement (rule out malignancy)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for tonsillectomy (day surgery for most; overnight if <3 years, OSA, or comorbidities); emergency for acute airway compromise or post-tonsillectomy haemorrhage
notes: Adenotonsillectomy is first-line for pediatric OSA with tonsillar hypertrophy; PSG should be obtained before surgery in high-risk groups. Codeine is contraindicated post-tonsillectomy in children <12yr due to fatal respiratory depression risk.

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 spray per nostril daily x6-12wk; age ≥2yr; 77% show improvement avoiding surgery
Isotonic saline 5-10mL per nostril bid via squeeze bottle or neti pot; all ages
Outpatient procedure under GA if medical therapy fails or severe obstruction/OSA/recurrent OME

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 spray per nostril daily x6-12wk; age ≥4yr; alternative intranasal steroid
4mg PO daily (2-5yr) or 5mg (6-14yr) as adjunct; FDA black box: neuropsychiatric risk
1.25mg PO daily (1-5yr), 2.5mg (6-11yr) if concurrent allergic rhinitis component
For mild symptoms without OSA; adenoid may involute with growth by age 7-10yr

investigations.name: Lateral X-ray nasopharynx
investigations.indication: Assess adenoid size and airway obstruction

investigations.name: Sleep study (polysomnography)
investigations.indication: If OSA suspected before surgical decision

investigations.name: Tympanometry
investigations.indication: If recurrent OME associated with adenoid hypertrophy

counselling: Nasal steroid spray is the first treatment to try - use for at least 6 weeks before deciding on surgery
Use correct spray technique: point nozzle towards outer wall of nose, not septum
Saline nasal wash helps clear mucus and reduces symptoms
Watch for mouth breathing, snoring, or disturbed sleep - these may indicate surgery is needed
Adenoids naturally shrink after age 7-10 years; some children may outgrow symptoms
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if nasal steroid spray fails after 6-12 weeks trial, sleep-disordered breathing, chronic sinusitis, recurrent OME with hearing loss, or significant nasal obstruction
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for adenoidectomy (day procedure under GA); urgent if severe OSA or acute upper airway obstruction
notes: Trial of intranasal corticosteroids for 6-12 weeks is recommended before proceeding to adenoidectomy for uncomplicated hypertrophy. Surgery indicated for OSA, chronic OME with hearing loss, or chronic rhinosinusitis unresponsive to medical therapy.
guideline_changes: Intranasal mometasone now established as first-line medical trial before adenoidectomy (S2k 2023)

first_line_drugs.notes: MDI + Spacer + Mask, 100 mcg/puff, 2 puffs QID (5-6 breaths per puff via spacer). Reliever - continue till symptoms controlled, stop 5 days after complete relief
100-200 mcg/puff MDI, 1 puff BD (q12h) via spacer. Controller - minimum 3 months, then step-down. Do NOT stop abruptly
NEB: <10 kg 0.63 mg, >=10 kg 1.25 mg, every 20 min x3 doses for acute relief, then q6-8h
1-2 mg/kg/day PO for 3-5 days, only for moderate episodes
Age-appropriate dose PO BD, if allergic component

investigations.name: Peak expiratory flow rate
investigations.indication: Children >5 years for severity and treatment response monitoring

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: If first episode or suspected complication

counselling: Inhaler is always better than syrup - faster, safer, fewer side effects
Use spacer with mask for proper drug delivery
Inhaler technique: shake, attach spacer, place mask tightly, press 1 puff, allow 5-6 breaths, repeat if needed
Controller inhaler (Budesonide) must be taken daily for minimum 3 months - do NOT stop suddenly
Reliever inhaler (Salbutamol) is for rescue use during symptoms only
Report immediately if severe chest retractions or SpO2 <92%
Report immediately if no response to inhaler
Report immediately if feeding difficulty
Follow up in 3-5 days, then regular follow-up for step-up/step-down assessment
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Feeding difficulty or unable to eat
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if requiring Step 3 or above, frequent exacerbations despite ICS, or diagnostic uncertainty
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe respiratory distress, SpO2 <92%, no response to inhaler therapy, or inability to feed
notes: Inhaler therapy is the GOLD STANDARD - faster action, fewer side effects than oral drugs. Spacer + Mask mandatory for proper drug delivery in infants and young children. GINA Stepwise: Step 1 SABA PRN, Step 2 low-dose ICS, Step 3 ICS+LABA, Step 4 specialist referral. ICS is preventive (not immediate relief). SABA is rescue drug. Early inhaler use reduces hospital admissions.
guideline_changes: GINA 2025: New diagnostic criteria for asthma in children <5 years; expanded role of T2 biomarkers (blood eosinophils, FeNO) in assessment; GINA 2024: MDI+Spacer remains preferred delivery for all ages; Step-down only after 3 months symptom-free on low-dose ICS

first_line_drugs.notes: NEB: <10 kg 0.63 mg, >=10 kg 1.25-2.5 mg, every 20 min x3 doses (first hour), then q1-2h, taper to q4-6h
1-2 mg/kg/dose IV STAT, then q6-8h. Mandatory early steroid. Reduces airway inflammation and prevents progression
Nasal cannula/mask, 2-6 L/min, target SpO2 >=94%
25-50 mg/kg IV in 30-50 ml NS over 20-30 min, for severe cases. Bronchodilator effect, reduces ICU need
250 mcg NEB q6h, add-on bronchodilator
0.01 mg/kg SC (1:1000), repeat every 15-20 min x2-3 doses. For severe bronchospasm / impending respiratory failure only

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Rule out pneumothorax, pneumonia, or other complications

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Baseline and to rule out infection

counselling: Child needs indoor admission for intensive monitoring and treatment
Oxygen and nebulization are the main treatment
Steroids are essential and will be given early to reduce inflammation
Report any worsening immediately: increased breathing difficulty, drowsiness, or bluish discoloration
After discharge, inhaler therapy (Salbutamol + Budesonide) will be started for long-term control
Learn proper inhaler technique before discharge
Follow up after 3-5 days of discharge
Avoid triggers: dust, smoke, cold exposure, strong smells
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Drop in oxygen saturation below 90% despite oxygen
referral_criteria: Refer to PICU if SpO2 <90% despite oxygen, silent chest, rising CO2 on ABG, exhaustion/drowsiness, or need for ventilatory support (HFNC/NIV/mechanical ventilation)
hospitalisation_criteria: Already indoor protocol. ICU transfer if: SpO2 <90% despite oxygen, silent chest, rising CO2 on ABG, exhaustion or drowsiness, HFNC failure
notes: Golden Hour: Oxygen + aggressive nebulization + systemic steroids. Monitoring: SpO2 continuous, RR/HR/work of breathing hourly, ABG if severe. Fluids 100 ml/kg/day, avoid overload. Antibiotics NOT routine - only if fever + infection suspected. Escalation: HFNC if moderate distress, NIV if worsening, mechanical ventilation as last resort. Step-down: transition to MDI+Spacer (Salbutamol reliever + Budesonide controller) and oral steroids before discharge.
source: GINA 2024/2025, IAP Guidelines 2024, AIIMS Protocol, NABH HCO 6th Edition
guideline_changes: GINA 2025: IV isotonic MgSO4 may be considered in children >=2 years with severe exacerbations; GINA 2024: Early systemic steroids within first hour remain critical; Ipratropium bromide add-on for severe episodes; Emphasis on step-down to MDI+Spacer before discharge with written action plan

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg nebulised q20min x3, min 2.5mg max 5mg
Budesonide-formoterol as both maintenance AND reliever at Steps 3-5

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day (max 40mg) x3-5 days, moderate-severe
0.3-0.6 mg/kg single dose, alternative to prednisolone course

investigations.name: Spirometry (FEV1/FVC)
investigations.indication: Baseline assessment and monitoring in children >6 years

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: If first episode, atypical presentation, or suspected complication

counselling: Always carry the reliever inhaler (blue puffer) wherever the child goes
Use spacer device with mask for children under 5 years for better medicine delivery
Identify and avoid triggers: dust, smoke, pets, cold air, strong smells
Do not stop controller inhaler even when child is well - it prevents attacks
Seek emergency care if child cannot speak in full sentences, lips turn blue, or reliever not helping
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if uncontrolled asthma despite Step 3 therapy, life-threatening exacerbation, diagnostic uncertainty, or suspected occupational/allergic triggers needing immunotherapy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe exacerbation not responding to 3 nebulizations in 1 hour, SpO2 <92% on room air, altered consciousness, silent chest, or need for IV magnesium/aminophylline
notes: GINA 2024: SABA alone without ICS not recommended for age 6yr+.
guideline_changes: ICS-formoterol preferred reliever at Steps 3-5. Tiotropium add-on for age 6yr+. No fixed FEV1/FVC cutoff.

first_line_drugs.notes: 5-10 kg: 7.5 mg/hr; 10-20 kg: 11.25 mg/hr; >20 kg: 15 mg/hr via continuous neb
Neb: 250-500 mcg q4-6h; give with albuterol in first hour q20min x3
1 mg/kg IV q6h (max 30 mg/dose) or 2 mg/kg load then 0.5 mg/kg q6h
50 mg/kg IV over 20-30 min (max 2 g); single dose for severe/refractory cases

second_line_drugs.notes: Load: 10 mcg/kg (max 400 mcg); infusion: 0.1-10 mcg/kg/min titrated to effect
0.01 mg/kg IM (1:1000, max 0.5 mg) q20min x3; or IV infusion 0.1-1 mcg/kg/min
0.5-2 mg/kg IV bolus then 0.5-2 mg/kg/hr infusion for bronchodilation and sedation
Load: 6 mg/kg IV over 20 min; maintenance: 0.5-1 mg/kg/hr; monitor levels

investigations.name: Arterial blood gas
investigations.indication: Assess severity of respiratory failure and CO2 retention

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Rule out pneumothorax, pneumonia, foreign body

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes
investigations.indication: Monitor for hypokalemia from beta-agonist use

investigations.name: Peak flow (if cooperative)
investigations.indication: Objective measure of airflow obstruction and response to treatment

counselling: Your child had a very severe asthma attack that needed intensive hospital treatment
After discharge, take all prescribed medicines regularly especially the preventer inhaler daily
Get an asthma action plan from your doctor and keep it accessible at home and school
Avoid known triggers: dust, smoke, strong perfumes, cold air, and pets if allergic
If the reliever inhaler is not working or your child is struggling to breathe, come to hospital immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist/allergist for all cases; ICU consultation for refractory cases
hospitalisation_criteria: All status asthmaticus requires hospitalization, typically in PICU; admit if not responding to repeated bronchodilators, SpO2 <90%, altered consciousness, or silent chest
notes: Status asthmaticus is refractory to standard bronchodilator therapy and requires ICU admission. Monitor for cardiac arrhythmias with continuous beta-agonist use.
source: GINA 2024, NAEPP EPR-3, CHOP PICU Pathway
snomed_display: Acute severe exacerbation of severe persistent asthma co-occurrent with allergic rhinitis

first_line_drugs.notes: Tidal volume 6-8 mL/kg IBW; PEEP >=5 cmH2O per FiO2 table; Pplat <28 cmH2O
Target SpO2 92-97% (mild-moderate); SpO2 >=88% acceptable in severe PARDS after PEEP optimization
Target negative or neutral fluid balance after initial resuscitation; furosemide 1 mg/kg IV PRN

second_line_drugs.notes: >=12 hr/day for moderate-severe PARDS; improves V/Q matching and oxygenation
5-20 ppm for refractory hypoxemia; discontinue if no response in 30-60 min
Cisatracurium 1-2 mcg/kg/min or vecuronium 1-2 mcg/kg/min for severe PARDS; <=48h
Consider VV-ECMO for refractory hypoxemia despite optimized ventilation; OI >40

investigations.name: Arterial blood gas
investigations.indication: Calculate P/F ratio for PARDS severity classification

counselling: Your child has a serious lung condition called ARDS that needs intensive care treatment
A breathing machine (ventilator) is necessary to help the lungs heal
Recovery takes time - lungs need days to weeks to recover
The medical team will keep you informed about your child's progress daily
After recovery, some children may need follow-up lung function tests
referral_criteria: Manage in PICU; pediatric intensivist leads care; consult pulmonology and infectious disease as needed
hospitalisation_criteria: All PARDS cases require PICU admission for mechanical ventilation, close monitoring, and potentially ECMO for refractory cases
