notes: No specific pharmacotherapy for PARDS; management is supportive with lung-protective ventilation. Non-adherence to PALICC recommendations is associated with higher mortality.
guideline_changes: PALICC-2 replaced PALICC-1 (2015) with updated severity stratification and ventilation targets

diagnosis_name: Lung abscess and necrosis of lung

first_line_drugs.notes: 30-40 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 2.7 g/day); covers S. aureus and anaerobes
50-100 mg/kg/day IV once daily (max 4 g); combined with clindamycin for broad coverage
200-300 mg/kg/day (ampicillin) IV divided q6h (max 12 g/day); alternative empiric

second_line_drugs.notes: 300 mg/kg/day (piperacillin) IV divided q6-8h (max 16 g/day) for polymicrobial
40-60 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 4 g/day) if MRSA confirmed; trough 15-20
For large (>4 cm) peripheral abscess not responding to 7-10 days of antibiotics
10 mg/kg IV/PO q8h (max 600 mg/dose); for MRSA if vancomycin not tolerated

investigations.name: CT chest with contrast
investigations.indication: Confirm abscess, assess size and guide intervention

counselling: Your child has a serious lung infection with abscess formation that needs prolonged antibiotics
IV antibiotics will be given for 2-4 weeks followed by oral antibiotics to complete 4-6 weeks total
Your child will need to stay in hospital for several weeks
Regular CT scans will monitor if the abscess is shrinking
Most children recover completely but complete the full antibiotic course to prevent relapse
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist and infectious disease; interventional radiology or surgery if abscess >4 cm not responding to 7-10 days of antibiotics
hospitalisation_criteria: All lung abscesses require hospitalization for prolonged IV antibiotics; ICU admission if sepsis or respiratory failure
notes: Prolonged IV antibiotics (2-4 weeks) followed by oral step-down (total 4-6 weeks) is standard. Surgical drainage is rarely needed if antibiotics started early.

first_line_drugs.notes: 200-300 mg/kg/day (ampicillin) IV divided q6h (max 12 g amp/day); empiric first-line
Ceftriaxone 50-100 mg/kg/day IV daily + clindamycin 30-40 mg/kg/day IV q6-8h
10-14 Fr tube; alteplase 0.1 mg/kg (max 6 mg) q12h x4-6 doses with 1h dwell time

second_line_drugs.notes: For multiloculated empyema or failure of chest tube + fibrinolytics after 48-72h
40-60 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 4 g/day) if MRSA suspected; trough 15-20 mcg/mL
60 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 6 g/day) for polymicrobial or resistant infection

investigations.name: Chest ultrasound
investigations.indication: Characterize effusion (simple vs loculated) and guide drainage

investigations.name: CT chest with contrast
investigations.indication: Assess for loculations, lung parenchyma, and guide intervention

investigations.name: Pleural fluid analysis (cell count, pH, glucose, LDH, culture)

counselling: Your child has pus collection around the lung which needs long-term antibiotic treatment
A chest tube may be placed to drain the fluid - this is necessary for recovery
IV antibiotics will be given for 1-2 weeks followed by oral antibiotics for 2-4 more weeks
Your child may need to stay in hospital for 1-2 weeks or longer
Follow-up chest X-ray will be done to ensure the infection has cleared completely
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric surgeon/thoracic surgeon for chest tube placement and VATS if needed; infectious disease for antibiotic guidance
hospitalisation_criteria: All empyema cases require hospitalization for IV antibiotics and drainage; surgical referral for multiloculated collections
notes: Total antibiotic duration (IV + oral) is typically 3-4 weeks. Intrapleural fibrinolytics with chest tube drainage are superior to drainage alone.

first_line_drugs.notes: 18-20G needle; send fluid for cell count, glucose, LDH, pH, Gram stain, culture
Ceftriaxone 50-100 mg/kg/day IV daily + clindamycin 30-40 mg/kg/day IV q6-8h
10-14 Fr pigtail catheter for complicated effusion (pH <7.0, glucose <40, LDH >1000, pus)

second_line_drugs.notes: 40,000 IU in 40 mL NS (>=10 kg) BID x3 days via chest tube; dwell 4h then drain
Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery for multiloculated effusion or fibrinolytic failure
Furosemide 1-2 mg/kg IV/PO q6-12h for transudative effusion (e.g., nephrotic, CHF)

investigations.name: Serum albumin and LDH
investigations.indication: Compare with pleural fluid for Light criteria

counselling: There is fluid collection around your child's lung that needs to be investigated and treated
A needle may be used to take a sample of the fluid to find the cause
If the fluid is infected, antibiotics and a drainage tube may be needed
Some effusions resolve with treating the underlying cause without needing drainage
Regular follow-up and repeat imaging will ensure the fluid is clearing
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist for all significant effusions; surgeon if drainage needed; nephrology/cardiology if transudative
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for symptomatic effusion causing respiratory distress, complicated parapneumonic effusion, or need for thoracentesis/chest tube
notes: Determine if effusion is transudative or exudative using Light criteria to guide management. Parapneumonic effusion requires antibiotics and may need drainage if complicated.

first_line_drugs.notes: 100% O2 via NRB mask for small (<20%) stable primary spontaneous PTX; monitor q4-6h
14-16G needle at 4th ICS mid-axillary line (preferred in peds); for tension PTX or large PTX
10-14 Fr pigtail catheter; connect to water seal or -20 cmH2O suction; 4th-5th ICS MAL

second_line_drugs.notes: 16-18G over-needle catheter with 3-way stopcock; for stable patients with large 1st episode PSP
One-way flutter valve attached to pigtail catheter for ambulatory management of stable PTX
For recurrent PTX (>=2 episodes); bleb resection + mechanical or chemical pleurodesis

investigations.name: CT chest
investigations.indication: If recurrent PTX or to plan surgical intervention

investigations.name: ABG
investigations.indication: If respiratory distress or large PTX

counselling: Your child has air leaking from the lung into the space around it
A small pneumothorax may heal on its own with rest and oxygen; larger ones need a chest tube
Your child should avoid vigorous exercise and air travel until fully healed
If sudden chest pain or breathlessness occurs again, come to hospital immediately
Recurrence is possible; discuss with doctor about preventive surgery if it happens again
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric surgeon/thoracic surgeon for chest tube placement; VATS referral for recurrent pneumothorax
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all symptomatic pneumothorax; immediate intervention for tension pneumothorax; observation for small (<20%) asymptomatic primary spontaneous PTX
notes: Tension pneumothorax requires immediate needle decompression followed by chest tube placement. The 4th ICS at the anterior axillary line is the preferred site in pediatric patients.

first_line_drugs.notes: Titrate to SpO2 92-97%; via nasal cannula, face mask, or high-flow nasal cannula (HFNC)
CPAP 5-10 cmH2O or BiPAP via nasal/face mask; trial before intubation if able
Tidal volume 6-8 mL/kg IBW; PEEP per ARDSNet table; plateau pressure <28 cmH2O

second_line_drugs.notes: Flow rate 2 L/kg/min (max ~60 L/min); FiO2 titrated to SpO2 target
Calfactant 3 mL/kg or beractant 4 mL/kg via ETT for severe ARDS in young children
12-16 hr/day in mechanically ventilated patients with severe hypoxemia (P/F <150)
Start 20 ppm; titrate down; for refractory hypoxemia; assess response at 30 min

investigations.name: Arterial blood gas
investigations.indication: Confirm type of respiratory failure (hypoxemic vs hypercapnic)

counselling: Your child is having difficulty breathing and needs oxygen and close monitoring in the hospital
The medical team may need to use a breathing machine if your child is not improving
This is a serious condition but most children recover well with proper treatment
After discharge, follow-up with the doctor to ensure lungs have recovered fully
If your child was on a ventilator, some cough and tiredness is normal for a few weeks after
referral_criteria: Manage in PICU; consult pediatric pulmonologist and intensivist for all cases
hospitalisation_criteria: All acute respiratory failure requires hospitalization; PICU admission for need for mechanical ventilation, severe hypoxemia (P/F <200), or hemodynamic instability
notes: Treat underlying cause aggressively. Lung-protective ventilation with low tidal volumes is the cornerstone of management in pediatric acute respiratory failure.
guideline_changes: PALICC-2 (2023) updated ventilation and oxygenation targets for pediatric respiratory failure

first_line_drugs.notes: Neb: 0.15 mg/kg (min 2.5 mg) q20min x3 doses; MDI: 4-8 puffs via spacer q20min
Neb: 250 mcg (<20 kg) or 500 mcg (>=20 kg) q20min x3 doses with albuterol
1-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) for 3-5 days for moderate-severe episodes

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.6 mg/kg PO/IM x1 dose (max 16 mg); may repeat x1 in 24-48h as alternative to pred
0.01 mg/kg IM (1:1000, max 0.5 mg) q15-20min x3 for severe bronchospasm
25-50 mg/kg IV over 20 min (max 2 g) for refractory bronchospasm

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Rule out pneumonia, foreign body, or pneumothorax

counselling: Bronchospasm means the airways in the lungs have tightened making it hard to breathe
Give the inhaler medicine with a spacer as shown by the doctor - this is the most effective way
If your child is not improving after 3 puffs of the blue inhaler, come to the hospital immediately
Keep the rescue inhaler available at all times including at school
Identify and avoid triggers like dust, smoke, cold air, and strong smells
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if recurrent bronchospasm suggesting asthma, poor response to standard bronchodilators, or diagnostic uncertainty
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe bronchospasm not responding to initial bronchodilator therapy, oxygen saturation <92% despite treatment, or respiratory distress
notes: Inhaled SABA via MDI with spacer is preferred over nebulizer in cooperative children. Assess response after each treatment and escalate as needed.

first_line_drugs.notes: Professional application q3-6mo; high-risk children q3mo per AAP Bright Futures
Rice-grain amount <3yr, pea-size 3-6yr, brush 2x/day
Topical application to cavitated lesions biannually, isolate field 1-3min
Apply to pit/fissure or cavitated lesion as interim therapeutic restoration

second_line_drugs.notes: Professional tray application q6mo for moderate-high risk, 4min application
Apply to high-risk surfaces q3mo as adjunct to fluoride
0.25mg/d age 6mo-3yr, 0.5mg/d 3-6yr, 1mg/d 6-16yr if water <0.3ppm F

investigations.name: Dental examination with caries risk assessment
Dental radiographs (bitewings)
investigations.indication: Detect interproximal caries and assess bone levels

counselling: Brush teeth twice daily with fluoride toothpaste; supervise brushing until age 8
Limit sugary drinks and snacks between meals; avoid bottle feeding at bedtime
Fluoride varnish applied by dentist every 3-6 months helps prevent new cavities
First dental visit should be by age 1 year or when first tooth appears
Silver diamine fluoride (SDF) can arrest cavities without drilling in young children
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric dentist for early childhood caries, caries in child <3 years, need for treatment under GA, or special healthcare needs
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if dental abscess with facial cellulitis, Ludwig angina (floor of mouth infection), or need for extensive dental rehabilitation under GA
notes: Caries risk assessment drives treatment intensity; high-risk children need q3mo fluoride varnish and SDF for active lesions. Restorative treatment under GA may be needed for extensive ECC in young children.
guideline_changes: SDF 38% now recommended biannually for arresting cavitated lesions (AAPD 2023 update)

diagnosis_name: Diseases of pulp and periapical tissues

first_line_drugs.notes: Pulpotomy agent: apply 2-3mm layer over pulp stumps, cover with GIC/composite
Pulpotomy agent: mix and apply over pulp stumps, sets in 12min, restore same visit
5-10mg/kg PO q6-8h PRN pain, max 40mg/kg/day
25-50mg/kg/day PO divided q8h x7-10d for periapical abscess, max 500mg/dose

second_line_drugs.notes: Pulpotomy hemostatic agent: apply 15sec to pulp stumps, rinse, restore
Direct/indirect pulp cap: apply thin layer over near/micro-exposure, restore
Pulpectomy obturation material for primary teeth, fill canals to apex
10-25mg/kg/day PO divided q8h x7d for PCN-allergic patients, max 450mg/dose

investigations.name: Dental radiograph (periapical)
investigations.indication: Assess extent of caries and periapical pathology

investigations.name: Pulp vitality testing
investigations.indication: Determine if pulp is vital or necrotic

counselling: If your child has a toothache, give paracetamol/ibuprofen and see the dentist urgently
Do not apply aspirin or home remedies directly on the gum - this can burn tissue
Tooth infection (abscess) with face swelling needs immediate dental treatment
Baby tooth infections must be treated to protect the permanent teeth growing underneath
Regular dental checkups every 6 months can catch problems before they become painful
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric dentist for pulp therapy (pulpotomy/pulpectomy), periapical abscess requiring drainage, or management under sedation/GA
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if facial cellulitis from dental abscess, spreading infection (Ludwig angina, orbital cellulitis), fever with trismus, or airway compromise
notes: Pulpotomy with MTA/Biodentine is preferred for vital primary teeth with reversible pulpitis. Pulpectomy indicated for irreversible pulpitis or necrosis; extraction considered if root resorption >1/3.
guideline_changes: MTA/Biodentine now preferred over formocresol for pulpotomy based on high-certainty evidence

first_line_drugs.notes: Rinse 15mL for 30sec bid x2wk; children >6yr who can spit; after professional cleaning
Mechanical debridement of plaque/calculus, polish, q6mo or as needed
Brush 2x/day with soft-bristled brush, supervised in children <8yr

second_line_drugs.notes: 7.5mg/kg PO q8h x7d for necrotizing gingivitis, max 250mg/dose
25-50mg/kg/day PO divided q8h x7d for aggressive periodontitis with systemic Abx
Dilute to 1.5%, rinse 30sec bid as adjunct for acute NUG
2mg/kg/day PO x14-21d as adjunct for aggressive periodontitis, max 100mg/day

investigations.name: Dental examination and probing
investigations.indication: Assess pocket depth and attachment loss

investigations.name: Dental radiographs (panoramic)
investigations.indication: Assess alveolar bone level in periodontitis

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: If necrotizing gingivitis or suspected underlying hematologic condition

counselling: Brush teeth twice daily with soft brush using correct technique; use interdental brush/floss
Bleeding gums during brushing is a sign of gingivitis - do not stop brushing, it will improve
Professional dental cleaning removes hardened plaque (tarite) that brushing cannot
Eat crunchy fruits and vegetables which naturally help clean teeth
Chlorhexidine mouthwash is for short-term use (2 weeks) only; it can stain teeth with prolonged use
referral_criteria: Refer to periodontist if aggressive periodontitis with bone loss, necrotizing gingivitis not responding to treatment, or gingival overgrowth (drug-induced)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if necrotizing gingivitis with systemic toxicity (fever, dehydration), spreading infection, or underlying immunodeficiency presenting with severe periodontal disease
notes: Plaque-induced gingivitis is reversible with oral hygiene improvement; CHX rinse is adjunctive, not a substitute. Necrotizing gingivitis requires debridement plus systemic antibiotics if febrile.

first_line_drugs.notes: Magic mouthwash (diphenhydramine + Maalox +/- lidocaine) swish-spit q4-6h for pain
15 mg/kg PO q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day) for pain and fever
10 mg/kg PO q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day) for pain and inflammation; age >6mo

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/dose PO 4x/day (max 800 mg) x7 days for herpetic gingivostomatitis
15 mL swish-spit BID x10 days; prevent secondary infection of ulcers
Apply to ulcers TID-QID after meals; for recurrent aphthous stomatitis
1-5 mL PO swish-spit QID x7-14 days for oral candidiasis

investigations.name: Clinical examination
investigations.indication: Usually sufficient — diagnose HSV, HFM, aphthous clinically

investigations.name: HSV PCR or DFA from lesion
investigations.indication: If herpetic gingivostomatitis suspected and immunocompromised

investigations.name: CBC with differential
investigations.indication: Rule out neutropenia if recurrent ulcers

counselling: Your child has mouth sores — this is usually from a virus and heals in 7-10 days
Give cold/soft foods: ice cream, yogurt, cold milk — avoid spicy, salty, or acidic foods
Keep the child hydrated — use a syringe or cup if the child refuses to drink due to pain
Give pain medicine (paracetamol/ibuprofen) 30 minutes before meals so the child can eat
The child is contagious — wash hands frequently and keep separate utensils for a few days
referral_criteria: ENT/oral surgery if large ulcers not healing >3 weeks. Dermatology for recurrent aphthous stomatitis. Immunology if recurrent severe oral infections (immunodeficiency screening). Oncology if non-healing ulcer suspicious for malignancy
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe dehydration from inability to drink. Herpetic gingivostomatitis with refusal of all oral intake. Oral candidiasis with systemic spread (immunocompromised). Airway compromise from severe oral/pharyngeal swelling
notes: Identify etiology: viral (HSV, enterovirus), aphthous, fungal, or trauma-related. Maintain hydration; soft/bland diet. Herpetic stomatitis most common in children 1-5 years.

diagnosis_name: Other diseases of lip and oral mucosa

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply thin layer to ulcer tid-qid after meals and at bedtime until healed
10-15mg/kg PO q4-6h PRN pain, max 75mg/kg/day or 4g/day
Equal parts diphenhydramine/Maalox/viscous lidocaine; swish 5mL and spit q6h PRN

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply to ulcer bid-tid for severe/recurrent aphthous ulcers, use for ≤2wk
Rinse 5mL for 2min then spit, bid-qid for widespread oral ulcers
Swish 5-10mL qid as mucosal protectant for oral mucositis
5-10mg/kg PO q6-8h PRN pain/inflammation, max 40mg/kg/day

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Screen for iron, B12, folate deficiency in recurrent aphthae

investigations.name: Serum iron, ferritin, B12, folate
investigations.indication: Nutritional deficiencies causing recurrent oral ulcers

investigations.name: Anti-tTG IgA
investigations.indication: Screen for celiac disease in recurrent aphthous ulcers

investigations.name: Biopsy of oral lesion
investigations.indication: If non-healing ulcer >3 weeks or atypical appearance

counselling: Most mouth ulcers heal in 7-14 days without specific treatment
Avoid spicy, salty, and acidic foods which irritate ulcers
Apply prescribed mouth paste directly on the ulcer after meals and at bedtime
Rinse with salt water (half teaspoon salt in 1 glass warm water) 3-4 times daily
If ulcers recur frequently, keep a food diary - some foods (citrus, tomato) may trigger them
referral_criteria: Refer to oral medicine specialist if recurrent aphthous ulcers (>3 episodes/year), ulcers lasting >3 weeks, suspected Behcet disease, or mucosal lesion suggestive of malignancy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe oral ulceration preventing oral intake (dehydration risk), suspected Behcet disease with systemic involvement, or oral manifestation of systemic disease
notes: Most aphthous ulcers are self-limiting within 7-14 days; topical steroids reduce pain and healing time but not recurrence. Rule out systemic causes (Behcet, IBD, celiac) if recurrent or severe.
snomed_display: Other diseases of conjunctiva due to viruses and chlamydiae NEC

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided QD-BID (max 40 mg/day) x8-12 weeks
1 mg/kg/day PO QD (max 40 mg); FDA-approved for pediatric erosive esophagitis
88-440 mcg swallowed BID (age-based); for eosinophilic esophagitis

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 mg PO BID (mixed with sucralose); for eosinophilic esophagitis
SC q2-4wk weight-based dosing; for EoE refractory to PPI/steroids, age >=1yr
2-food or 6-food empiric elimination diet for EoE; dietitian-guided

investigations.name: Upper GI endoscopy with biopsy
investigations.indication: Diagnose cause — reflux esophagitis vs EoE (>15 eos/HPF)

counselling: Your child has inflammation of the food pipe (esophagus) — medicine will help it heal
Give the PPI (omeprazole/esomeprazole) 30 minutes before breakfast on an empty stomach
If food allergy is suspected, an elimination diet may be tried under doctor's guidance
Avoid spicy, acidic, and fried foods that may worsen symptoms
An endoscopy (camera test) may be needed to check the cause — it is safe in children
referral_criteria: Pediatric gastroenterology for endoscopy and biopsy. Allergy/immunology if eosinophilic esophagitis diagnosed. Dietitian for elimination diet guidance. Surgery for refractory strictures
hospitalisation_criteria: Dysphagia with food impaction (emergency endoscopy needed). Severe dehydration from inability to swallow. GI bleeding. Esophageal stricture requiring dilation
notes: Distinguish reflux esophagitis (PPI-responsive) from eosinophilic esophagitis (may need topical steroids or biologics). EGD with biopsy required for EoE diagnosis.
guideline_changes: PPI-nonresponsive EoE no longer a diagnostic category; dupilumab approved for EoE age 1+ (2022)

first_line_drugs.notes: Keep baby head elevated ~30 degrees, hold upright 15-20 min after feeds, avoid immediate lying after feeding; first-line treatment
Small frequent feeds, avoid overfeeding, proper burping after feeds; reduces reflux episodes
1-2 mg/kg/day OD PO for 2-4 weeks trial, only if symptomatic GERD; not for simple GER
Thickened formula or rice cereal if bottle-fed; reduces regurgitation

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.2-0.3 mg/kg/dose TDS PO, short course, use cautiously in select cases

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Check for anemia if chronic GERD

investigations.name: Ultrasound abdomen
investigations.indication: Rule out pyloric stenosis or structural causes in persistent vomiting

investigations.name: Liver function tests
investigations.indication: If persistent vomiting to rule out hepatobiliary causes

counselling: Keep baby's head elevated after feeds and hold upright for 15-20 minutes
Give small frequent feeds and avoid overfeeding
Burp baby properly after every feed
Most babies outgrow reflux by 12-18 months - this is usually normal
Do not lay baby flat immediately after feeding
Return if baby has poor weight gain, persistent vomiting, blood in vomit, or choking episodes
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Poor weight gain or failure to thrive
Persistent vomiting or blood in vomit
Severe irritability or refusal to feed
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric gastroenterologist if poor weight gain despite treatment, persistent vomiting beyond 18 months, hematemesis, suspected aspiration, recurrent pneumonia, or Sandifer syndrome
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe GERD with failure to thrive, aspiration pneumonia, apparent life-threatening event (ALTE/BRUE), hematemesis, or severe dehydration from persistent vomiting
notes: Most infant GER is physiological and self-limiting, resolving by 12-18 months. Non-pharmacological measures are first-line: keep head elevated 30 degrees, hold upright 15-20 min after feeds, small frequent feeds, proper burping, avoid overfeeding. Thickened feeds (rice cereal) for bottle-fed infants reduce regurgitation. PPI only for symptomatic GERD with poor weight gain or complications. Do not medicate normal thriving babies with mild regurgitation.
source: ESPGHAN/NASPGHAN 2018 Pediatric GERD Clinical Practice Guidelines, IAP GI Protocol
guideline_changes: NASPGHAN/ESPGHAN 2018 GERD CPG (most recent): 4-8 weeks PPI trial for typical GERD symptoms only after dietary modifications fail in infants; avoid acid suppression for extraesophageal symptoms (cough, wheezing); no new 2024-2025 GERD guideline revision published.

first_line_drugs.notes: Upright positioning post-feed; smaller/frequent feeds; thickened feeds in infants
1 mg/kg/day PO QD-BID (max 20 mg BID) x4-8 weeks; if lifestyle changes fail
0.7-1 mg/kg/day PO QD (max 40 mg); for erosive GERD

second_line_drugs.notes: Famotidine 0.5 mg/kg PO BID (max 20 mg BID); H2RA if PPI not tolerated
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO QD (max 30 mg); alternative PPI
Laparoscopic antireflux surgery for refractory GERD with complications

investigations.name: Trial of PPI therapy (4-8 weeks)
investigations.indication: Empiric approach — symptom response supports diagnosis

investigations.name: Upper GI endoscopy with biopsy
investigations.indication: If red flags or failed PPI trial — assess esophagitis, rule out EoE

investigations.name: pH-impedance monitoring (24h)
investigations.indication: Gold standard for quantifying reflux — before surgery or if diagnosis unclear

investigations.name: Upper GI barium study
investigations.indication: Rule out anatomic abnormalities (malrotation, hiatal hernia) — not for diagnosing GERD

counselling: Your child has acid reflux — this is very common in infants and often resolves by 12-18 months
Keep the baby upright for 20-30 minutes after feeding — avoid lying flat immediately
Give smaller, more frequent feeds rather than large feeds
For older children: avoid eating 2-3 hours before bedtime, reduce fatty and spicy foods
Do not use acid-blocking medicine in infants who are spitting up but growing well — this is normal
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI if: red flags (failure to thrive, GI bleeding, respiratory complications), failed 4-8 week PPI trial, or suspected complications. Surgery referral for severe refractory GERD or complications
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe failure to thrive from GERD. Aspiration pneumonia. Apparent life-threatening event (ALTE/BRUE) from reflux. Esophageal stricture. Severe esophagitis with bleeding
notes: Avoid acid suppression in infants with extraesophageal symptoms only. PPI trial of 4-8 weeks recommended for typical GERD symptoms before further workup.

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO QD-BID (max 40 mg/day) x4-8 weeks for ulcer healing
50 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 1 g/dose) x14 days as part of triple therapy
15 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 500 mg/dose) x14 days; susceptibility-guided

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg/kg/day PO QD (max 40 mg) x8 weeks; alternative PPI
0.5 mg/kg PO BID (max 20 mg BID) if PPI contraindicated/not tolerated
40-80 mg/kg/day PO divided QID (max 1 g/dose); cytoprotective adjunct

investigations.name: Upper GI endoscopy with biopsy
investigations.indication: Visualize ulcer, H. pylori testing, rule out malignancy in older children

investigations.name: H. pylori testing (biopsy CLO test, stool antigen, or UBT)
investigations.indication: All peptic ulcers must be tested for H. pylori

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Assess for anemia from blood loss

investigations.indication: Iron deficiency from chronic blood loss

counselling: Your child has an ulcer in the stomach or intestine — acid-reducing medicine will help heal it
If H. pylori bacteria is the cause, a 14-day antibiotic course is needed — do not skip doses
Avoid giving painkillers (ibuprofen, aspirin) without doctor's advice — they can worsen ulcers
Feed the child regular balanced meals — no need for bland diet unless it helps with symptoms
Follow up for a confirmation test to make sure the bacteria is completely gone
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI for endoscopy and H. pylori management. Surgery for perforation or uncontrolled GI bleeding. Hematology if severe anemia from chronic blood loss
hospitalisation_criteria: Upper GI bleeding (hematemesis, melena, hemodynamic instability). Perforation (acute abdomen with free air). Severe anemia (Hb <7 g/dL). Intractable vomiting
notes: Test for H. pylori in all pediatric peptic ulcers; eradication confirmed 4-8 weeks post-treatment. NSAID-related ulcers require NSAID cessation and PPI therapy.
guideline_changes: 2024 H. pylori guidelines emphasize 14-day susceptibility-guided triple therapy

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 40 mg/day) x4-8 weeks for acid suppression
50 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 1 g/dose) x14 days; part of H. pylori triple therapy
15 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 500 mg/dose) x14 days; if susceptible

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 500 mg/dose) x14 days; if clarithromycin-resistant
Quadruple therapy component; age >12yr, 262 mg PO QID x14 days
40-80 mg/kg/day PO divided QID (max 1 g/dose); mucosal protectant

investigations.indication: H. pylori detection, histology, gastritis grading

investigations.name: H. pylori stool antigen or urea breath test
investigations.indication: Non-invasive H. pylori testing; confirm eradication 4 weeks post-treatment
