investigations.indication: Anemia from chronic GI blood loss

investigations.name: Serum iron, ferritin
investigations.indication: Iron deficiency from occult blood loss

counselling: Your child has stomach inflammation — acid-reducing medicine (PPI) will help heal it
If H. pylori bacteria is found, a combination of antibiotics for 14 days is needed
Complete the full course of antibiotics — stopping early can cause the bacteria to become resistant
Avoid giving the child spicy foods, fizzy drinks, and painkillers (NSAIDs) without doctor's advice
A repeat test after treatment is done to confirm the bacteria is gone
referral_criteria: Pediatric gastroenterology for endoscopy if red flags present (GI bleeding, weight loss, anemia). Infectious disease for resistant H. pylori. Surgery for perforated ulcer
hospitalisation_criteria: GI bleeding (hematemesis, melena). Perforation (acute abdomen). Severe dehydration from persistent vomiting. Severe anemia requiring transfusion
notes: H. pylori testing is indicated for ulcers/erosions. Susceptibility testing is mandatory per 2024 guidelines before initiating eradication therapy. Non-H. pylori gastritis managed with PPI alone.
guideline_changes: 2024 update mandates susceptibility-guided therapy; high-dose PPI + amoxicillin-based triple therapy x14 days

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.15 mg/kg/dose BD-TDS PO, short duration 2-3 days, for vomiting control
1 mg/kg/day OD PO before meals x 7-10 days, first-line acid suppression
Small frequent sips PO, especially if vomiting or mild diarrhoea
1 mg/kg/day OD PO before meals x 7-10 days, alternative PPI
OD PO x 3-5 days, optional probiotic for gut recovery
Age-appropriate dose TDS PO, short-term, only if significant abdominal pain

second_line_drugs.notes: 2-4 mg/kg/dose BD PO x 7-10 days, if PPI not available/tolerated

investigations.name: Stool routine and microscopy
investigations.indication: Associated loose stools or suspected infection

investigations.name: Liver function test
investigations.indication: Persistent vomiting to rule out hepatic cause

counselling: Give small frequent meals instead of large meals
Preferred foods: curd, buttermilk, khichdi, rice, banana, coconut water
Avoid spicy food, fried food, and junk food
Keep child well hydrated with ORS and fluids
If persistent vomiting, blood in vomit, severe abdominal pain, or dehydration - come to hospital immediately
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Persistent vomiting or blood in vomit
Severe abdominal pain not relieved by medication
Signs of dehydration such as dry mouth or no urine
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric gastroenterologist if persistent vomiting >5 days despite treatment, recurrent episodes, suspected H. pylori, or upper GI bleeding
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if persistent vomiting with dehydration, hematemesis, severe abdominal pain with guarding, or inability to tolerate oral intake
notes: Ondansetron for vomiting control. PPI (Omeprazole) is first-line acid suppression, preferred over H2 blockers. Maintain hydration. Avoid unnecessary antibiotics. Small frequent meals.
guideline_changes: ESPGHAN/NASPGHAN 2024: updated H. pylori guidelines with bismuth-based quadruple therapy for resistant strains; PPIs remain first-line acid suppression in children; PPI deprescription emphasized after 4-8 wk courses; ACG 2024 highlights rising clarithromycin resistance.

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg/dose (pip) IV q8h (max 4.5 g/dose) preop and for complicated cases
Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IV q24h (max 2g) + Metronidazole 10 mg/kg IV q8h (max 500 mg)
Laparoscopic appendectomy; standard of care for confirmed appendicitis

second_line_drugs.notes: IV amoxicillin-clavulanate then PO x7-10 days for uncomplicated; 58-75% 1yr success
20 mg/kg IV q8h (max 1 g) for severe allergy or resistant organisms
15 mg/kg IV q12h (max 1 g/day) for complicated cases with penicillin allergy

investigations.name: CRP
investigations.indication: Elevated in complicated appendicitis — helps differentiate

investigations.name: CT abdomen (if US inconclusive)
investigations.indication: Higher sensitivity — use low-dose protocol in children

counselling: Your child has appendicitis — surgery (removal of the appendix) is usually the best treatment
The operation is done through small holes in the belly (laparoscopic) — recovery is faster
After surgery, the child can usually eat within 24 hours and go home in 1-2 days
Watch for fever, increasing belly pain, or vomiting after discharge — come back immediately
Appendicitis is not preventable — there is nothing you did wrong
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery mandatory. Interventional radiology for percutaneous drainage of appendiceal abscess. PICU if perforated with sepsis
hospitalisation_criteria: All acute appendicitis requires hospitalisation for surgery or antibiotic-first management. Peritonitis. Appendiceal abscess or phlegmon. Perforation with sepsis
notes: Uncomplicated appendicitis may be managed non-operatively with antibiotics in select cases, but surgery remains standard. Complicated appendicitis with abscess may benefit from initial antibiotics and interval appendectomy.
guideline_changes: 2024 pathways increasingly support non-operative antibiotic-first for uncomplicated cases

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg/dose (pip) IV q8h (max 4.5 g/dose) perioperative
Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IV q24h (max 2g) + Metronidazole 10 mg/kg IV q8h (max 500 mg)
Laparoscopic preferred; open if needed. Postop abx based on intraop findings

second_line_drugs.notes: 45 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 875 mg/dose) for step-down oral therapy
Cipro 10 mg/kg PO BID + Metro 10 mg/kg PO TID for oral step-down if pen-allergic

investigations.name: Abdominal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Initial imaging of choice in children — appendix visualization

counselling: We suspect appendicitis but need more tests to be sure — imaging will help confirm
Until confirmed, the child should not eat or drink (nil by mouth) in case surgery is needed
If appendicitis is confirmed, surgery will be recommended
After surgery, recovery is usually quick — 2-3 days in most cases
Come back immediately if pain worsens, child develops high fever, or starts vomiting
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery for evaluation and possible appendectomy. Interventional radiology if abscess needs drainage
hospitalisation_criteria: All suspected appendicitis for observation, imaging, and possible surgery. Peritonitis on examination. Hemodynamic instability. Need for NPO status and IV fluids
notes: If unspecified, obtain imaging (US or CT) to classify. Gangrenous/perforated appendicitis requires 7-10 days of antibiotics; uncomplicated needs only perioperative doses.

first_line_drugs.notes: High ligation of processus vaginalis; day surgery; inguinal incision; gold standard technique
Equivalent outcomes to open; allows contralateral ring evaluation; shorter bilateral OR time
Incarcerated hernia: attempt gentle reduction then semi-urgent repair within 24-48 hrs

second_line_drugs.notes: Case-by-case decision; laparoscopic visualization of contralateral ring preferred over blind exploration
Sedation + Trendelenburg for incarcerated hernia; schedule repair within 2-5 days after reduction

counselling: Inguinal hernia in children always needs surgery as it will not close on its own
Surgery is safe and usually done as day care; your child goes home the same day
If the lump becomes hard, painful, and does not go back in, rush to hospital immediately as it could be stuck
After surgery, avoid rough play for 1-2 weeks; normal activities can resume gradually
Slight swelling around the wound is normal; fever or redness around the wound needs medical attention
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric surgeon at diagnosis for elective repair; urgent referral for irreducible hernia
hospitalisation_criteria: Day surgery for elective repair; admit for incarcerated hernia (urgent reduction and repair within 24-48h), or if premature infant needing observation post-anesthesia
notes: Pediatric inguinal hernias do not close spontaneously and always require surgical repair. Premature infants have higher incidence and should be repaired before NICU discharge or shortly after.
guideline_changes: AAP 2023 guideline: laparoscopic approach equivalent to open; allows contralateral ring evaluation

investigations.name: Color Doppler
investigations.indication: Assess testicular perfusion in suspected incarceration

counselling: Hernias do not resolve spontaneously and require surgery — हर्निया अपने आप ठीक नहीं होता, ऑपरेशन ज़रूरी है
Hydroceles often resolve without treatment — हाइड्रोसील अक्सर बिना इलाज के ठीक हो जाता है
Seek urgent care if swelling becomes painful or irreducible — अगर सूजन दर्दनाक हो या अंदर न जाए तो तुरंत डॉक्टर को दिखाएं
Surgery is safe with excellent outcomes — ऑपरेशन सुरक्षित है और परिणाम बहुत अच्छे होते हैं
Monitor swelling size and behavior — सूजन के आकार और बदलाव पर नज़र रखें
warning_signs: Irreducible swelling (does not go back in) — सूजन जो अंदर न जाए
Sudden increase in size of swelling — सूजन का अचानक बढ़ जाना
Tender, tense or red swelling — दर्दनाक, तनी हुई या लाल सूजन
Vomiting or abdominal distension — उल्टी या पेट फूलना
Refusal to feed or excessive crying — दूध न पीना या बहुत रोना
expected_course: Inguinal hernia: requires surgical repair (herniotomy) within 1-2 weeks of diagnosis. In preterm infants, surgery may be done before discharge. Non-communicating hydrocele: usually resolves by 12-18 months. Communicating hydrocele: may need surgery if persistent beyond 12 months or increasing in size.
key_clinical_points: Hernia: varies with crying, reducible, transillumination negative
Hydrocele: constant size, not reducible, transillumination positive
Incarcerated hernia is a surgical emergency - irreducible, tender, vomiting
Risk of incarceration is highest in first year - early surgical referral for hernia
Preterm infants have higher risk of inguinal hernia
Bilateral inguinal hernia is more common in preterm babies
Always examine contralateral side - bilateral hernias occur in 10-15% of neonates

severity_assessment.mild: Small reducible hernia or non-communicating hydrocele - elective surgical referral for hernia, observation for hydrocele
severity_assessment.moderate: Large hernia, communicating hydrocele increasing in size - early surgical referral, close monitoring
severity_assessment.severe: Incarcerated or strangulated hernia - emergency surgical management, immediate hospital referral

monitoring_parameters.frequency: At each visit; parents to monitor daily
At initial assessment and if character changes
Daily by parents; at each visit
At each visit to rule out testicular torsion

first_line_drugs.notes: Observe until age 4-5 yrs; 95% of defects <1cm close spontaneously; no strapping or coins
Suture repair (Mayo technique) if persistent after age 5 or symptomatic; day surgery; infraumbilical incision

second_line_drugs.notes: Immediate repair for strangulated hernia; resuscitate, broad-spectrum antibiotics, emergent OR
May consider repair before age 5 for large defects >2cm, proboscoid hernias, or symptomatic cases

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis
investigations.indication: No investigations needed; diagnosis is clinical

counselling: Umbilical hernia is very common in babies and almost always closes by itself by age 4-5 years
Do NOT tape coins or apply any binding on the hernia - this does not help and can cause skin problems
If the hernia becomes hard, painful, or changes color, rush to hospital immediately
Surgery is only needed if it has not closed by age 5 or if it is very large
Avoid lifting heavy objects for 2 weeks after surgery if done
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric surgeon if hernia persists after age 4-5 years, defect >2 cm, symptomatic, or proboscoid hernia
hospitalisation_criteria: Day surgery for elective repair; emergency admission for strangulated hernia (very rare)
notes: Umbilical hernias are extremely common in infants, especially in African American and preterm infants. Avoid referral to surgery before age 4-5 unless symptomatic or incarcerated.
source: AAFP/AAP Choosing Wisely, ACS Pediatric Surgery Guidelines
guideline_changes: Choosing Wisely recommends deferring surgical referral until age 4-5 years for asymptomatic cases

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 ml/kg IV bolus over 30-60 min for initial rehydration; maintenance per Holliday-Segar formula
0.1-0.15 mg/kg/dose IV q8h for vomiting
10-15 mg/kg/dose IV q6h for fever
1 sachet BD PO, reduces duration of diarrhea
Start early when tolerated, small frequent sips
20 mg OD PO for 14 days (<6 months: 10 mg OD), start with oral transition

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-75 mg/kg/day IV OD/BD, only if dysentery/sepsis/toxic child
15 mg/kg/day IV OD, add-on if severe sepsis suspected
7.5-10 mg/kg/dose IV TDS, only if amoebic dysentery suspected

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes
investigations.indication: If severe dehydration or prolonged diarrhea

investigations.name: Renal function tests
investigations.indication: Rule out acute kidney injury in severe dehydration

counselling: Continue breastfeeding and complementary foods during illness
Give ORS sip by sip after every loose stool
Continue zinc for full 14 days even after diarrhea stops
Offer curd, khichdi, banana, coconut water as tolerated
Return immediately if child becomes lethargic, stops urinating, or has blood in stool
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Signs of severe dehydration such as sunken eyes or no tears
Electrolyte imbalance symptoms such as muscle cramps or weakness
No urine output for more than 6 hours
Signs of shock such as cold hands or weak pulse
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric gastroenterologist if persistent diarrhea >14 days, recurrent severe episodes, suspected malabsorption, or failure to thrive
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe dehydration, unable to tolerate oral fluids, persistent vomiting, toxic appearance, age <2 months, decreased urine output, or electrolyte imbalance
notes: IV fluids are the cornerstone. Maintenance fluid per Holliday-Segar: 100 ml/kg first 10 kg + 50 ml/kg next 10 kg. Avoid routine triple antibiotics unless clearly indicated. Early transition to ORS. Continue feeding as early as possible (curd, khichdi, banana, coconut water).
source: IAP 2024 Diarrhea Guidelines, WHO Diarrheal Disease Management, NASPGHAN 2024 AGE Clinical Pathway
guideline_changes: NASPGHAN 2024 AGE update: Lactobacillus not routinely recommended (two large RCTs showed no benefit); ondansetron preferred antiemetic for children >=6 months; early oral rehydration emphasized over IV where possible; Saccharomyces boulardii retains moderate evidence for reducing diarrhea duration.

first_line_drugs.notes: Pneumatic reduction under fluoroscopy; 83-97% success rate. Max pressure 120 mmHg
NS 20 mL/kg bolus; correct dehydration before reduction attempt

second_line_drugs.notes: US-guided saline enema reduction; 73-92% success. No radiation exposure
Operative manual reduction or resection if enema fails, peritonitis, or perforation
0.5 mg/kg IV single dose (max 10 mg); may reduce recurrence rate (adjunctive)

investigations.name: Abdominal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Target sign (doughnut sign) — sensitivity >95% for ileocolic intussusception

investigations.name: Abdominal X-ray
investigations.indication: Obstruction pattern, free air if perforation

counselling: Your child has a condition where part of the intestine slides into itself like a telescope — this is called intussusception
An air enema (non-surgical procedure) can fix this in most cases — success rate is very high
The child may cry during the procedure but it avoids surgery in most cases
There is a 5-10% chance of it happening again — come back immediately if symptoms return (pain, bloody stool, vomiting)
Monitor the child closely for 24 hours after the procedure — watch for recurrence of symptoms
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery if: enema reduction fails, peritonitis, perforation, recurrent intussusception (>3 episodes — evaluate for lead point), or age >2 years (higher chance of pathological lead point)
hospitalisation_criteria: All intussusception cases require hospitalisation for reduction and 24-hour observation. Peritonitis or perforation require emergency surgery. Recurrent episodes require admission for surgical evaluation
notes: Air enema is first-line for ileocolic intussusception without peritonitis. Recurrence rate is 5-10%; lead points more common in children >2 years requiring surgical evaluation.
guideline_changes: 2024 meta-analysis confirms air enema superiority over hydrostatic reduction in success rates

first_line_drugs.notes: Insert NG tube for suction; strict NPO. Fluid resuscitate with NS/LR 20 mL/kg boluses
NS or LR 20 mL/kg bolus; correct electrolytes. Maintenance 1.5x calculated rate
Emergency laparotomy/laparoscopy for strangulation, volvulus, or failed conservative Rx

second_line_drugs.notes: Therapeutic contrast enema for meconium ileus or adhesive partial obstruction
100 mg/kg/dose IV q8h (max 4.5 g) if peritonitis or perforation suspected

investigations.name: Abdominal X-ray (erect and supine)
investigations.indication: Air-fluid levels, dilated loops, free air if perforation

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes
investigations.indication: Dehydration and electrolyte imbalance from vomiting/third-spacing

investigations.name: Upper GI contrast study
investigations.indication: Malrotation with volvulus — upper GI series is diagnostic

counselling: Your child has a bowel blockage — this is serious and needs hospital treatment
The child must not eat or drink until the blockage is cleared
A tube through the nose (NG tube) will help remove trapped air and fluid from the stomach
Surgery may be needed if the blockage does not clear with non-surgical treatment
Come back immediately after discharge if the child develops vomiting, abdominal distension, or stops passing stool
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery mandatory for all intestinal obstructions. Emergency surgery for: volvulus, strangulation, perforation, or failed conservative management
hospitalisation_criteria: All intestinal obstructions require hospitalisation. Signs: vomiting (especially bilious in children), abdominal distension, absent bowel sounds, abdominal pain. Emergency for complete obstruction or peritonitis
notes: Etiology must be urgently identified (volvulus, hernia, adhesions, intussusception). Midgut volvulus requires emergent surgery without delay for imaging.

first_line_drugs.notes: Disimpaction 1-1.5 g/kg/day x3-6 days. Maintenance 0.4-0.8 g/kg/day

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-3 mL/kg/day divided q12h, alternative osmotic laxative

investigations.name: Abdominal X-ray
investigations.indication: Assess fecal loading if uncertain clinical picture

investigations.name: Thyroid function tests
investigations.indication: If associated growth failure or other symptoms of hypothyroidism

investigations.name: Barium enema or anorectal manometry
investigations.indication: If Hirschsprung disease suspected (onset at birth, failure to thrive)

counselling: Give PEG laxative daily as prescribed - it is safe for long-term use and not habit-forming
Include more fruits, vegetables, and whole grains in diet; ensure adequate water intake
Establish a regular toilet routine - sit on toilet for 5-10 minutes after meals
Do not punish or scold the child for soiling; it is not intentional
Treatment takes 3-6 months; do not stop laxative suddenly when stools improve
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric gastroenterologist if no response to 3 months of PEG therapy, suspected Hirschsprung disease (delayed meconium passage, abdominal distension), or associated failure to thrive
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe fecal impaction not responding to oral disimpaction, signs of intestinal obstruction, or severe abdominal pain with vomiting
notes: PEG is best-evidenced agent. Dietary fibre and adequate fluid are essential adjuncts.

first_line_drugs.notes: Stool softeners, high-fiber diet, sitz baths after each BM; topical glyceryl trinitrate 0.2% ointment BID x6-8 wks
Simple fistulotomy for persistent fistula-in-ano; sphincter-sparing in children; heals by secondary intention
For perianal abscess: bedside or OR I&D under GA; pack wound loosely; sitz baths post-op

second_line_drugs.notes: Applied BID to anal margin for chronic fissure unresponsive to GTN; fewer headaches than GTN
Injection into internal anal sphincter for refractory chronic fissure; used off-label in older children
Complete excision of fistula tract for complex or recurrent fistulae; rarely needed in children

investigations.name: Examination under anesthesia
investigations.indication: If complex perianal disease or suspected Crohn

investigations.name: MRI pelvis
investigations.indication: If fistula-in-ano or suspected Crohn perianal disease

counselling: Anal fissures are common in children and usually heal with stool softeners and proper diet
Give high-fiber foods (fruits, vegetables, whole grains) and plenty of water to prevent hard stools
Apply the prescribed ointment gently around the anal area as directed
Sitz baths (sitting in warm water for 10-15 minutes) after each bowel movement help healing
Do not delay bowel movements - encourage the child to go when they feel the urge
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric surgeon if fissure not healing after 6-8 weeks of conservative treatment, recurrent fissures, or if fistula present
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for perianal abscess requiring I&D under anesthesia, or if Crohn disease suspected with complex perianal disease
notes: Most pediatric perianal fistulae are superficial and may resolve spontaneously without sphincter involvement. Exclude Crohn disease in any child with recurrent or complex perianal disease.
guideline_changes: 2025 systematic review supports initial surgical intervention for recurrent pediatric perianal fistula

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 ml/kg/day PO div BD, after meals or at bedtime, first-line stool softener
Apply locally before passing stool, 2-3 times/day for pain relief
5-10 ml PO at bedtime for 5-7 days, stool bulking and smooth passage
Warm water with light pink KMnO4 solution, 10-15 minutes 2-3 times/day, promotes healing and reduces pain
SOS for acute constipation, short-term use only
1 teaspoon (3-5 g) in 1 glass water at bedtime OD for 5-7 days, fiber supplement for stool consistency

investigations.name: Stool routine and microscopy
investigations.indication: Rule out occult blood, parasites, or infection if recurrent

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Assess for anemia if significant or recurrent bleeding

investigations.name: Stool occult blood test
investigations.indication: Confirm GI bleeding if history of blood in stool

counselling: Give lactulose regularly to keep stool soft - this is the most important treatment
Sitz bath in warm water for 10-15 minutes 2-3 times daily helps healing
Apply lignocaine gel before passing stool to reduce pain
Increase fiber in diet - papaya, banana, green vegetables, adequate water
Avoid junk food and excess milk which worsen constipation
Return if persistent bleeding, severe pain not relieved, or no improvement in 5-7 days
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Persistent or increasing bleeding during stools
Severe pain not relieved by medication
No improvement in symptoms after 5 to 7 days
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric surgeon if chronic fissure not healing after 4-6 weeks of conservative treatment, recurrent fissures, or suspected underlying condition (Crohn disease, anorectal anomaly)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if significant rectal bleeding, suspected perianal abscess, or need for examination under anesthesia
notes: Treatment principles: soften stool, relieve pain, heal fissure, prevent recurrence. Sitz bath with warm water + potassium permanganate (light pink solution) for 10-15 min 2-3 times/day is essential. High fiber diet: papaya, banana, vegetables. Adequate water intake. Avoid junk food and excess milk.
source: IAP Pediatric Surgery Guidelines, ASCRS Anal Fissure Guidelines 2023, ESPGHAN Constipation Guidelines
guideline_changes: ASCRS 2023: Topical calcium channel blockers (diltiazem) recommended as first-line pharmacotherapy for chronic fissures with better side-effect profile than GTN; conservative management (stool softeners, fiber, sitz baths) remains first-choice for acute fissures in children; IAP Pediatric Surgery 2024: PEG-based laxatives preferred over lactulose for chronic constipation-associated fissures

first_line_drugs.notes: Gentle digital reduction with lubrication; apply sugar/salt to edematous mucosa before reduction
0.4-0.8 g/kg/day PO QD (max 17 g) to prevent straining
Screen for cystic fibrosis, treat constipation, parasites, malnutrition

second_line_drugs.notes: Submucosal injection of 30% saline or 5% phenol above dentate line; outpatient procedure
For recurrent prolapse after failed conservative Rx; age >4yr or chronic predisposition
Anal encirclement suture for recurrent prolapse in select patients

investigations.name: Sweat chloride test
investigations.indication: Rule out cystic fibrosis — MANDATORY in all children with rectal prolapse

investigations.name: Stool examination for parasites
investigations.indication: Parasitic infections (Trichuris, Ascaris) cause prolapse in endemic areas

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Anemia from chronic disease or parasites

counselling: Your child's rectum is coming out — this usually gets better on its own with treatment of the underlying cause
Keep the child's stools soft with stool softeners — hard stools and straining worsen the problem
If the rectum comes out, gently push it back with a lubricated finger — do not panic
Apply sugar or salt to the swollen tissue to reduce swelling before pushing back if needed
All children with rectal prolapse must be tested for cystic fibrosis — ask your doctor about the sweat test
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery if: recurrent prolapse despite conservative management, incarceration, or need for surgical fixation. CF team if sweat test positive. GI for underlying cause workup
hospitalisation_criteria: Incarcerated prolapse that cannot be manually reduced. Strangulated prolapse (ischemic tissue). Post-surgical care after rectopexy. Newly diagnosed cystic fibrosis
notes: Self-limiting in 90% of children <3 years with conservative management. All children with rectal prolapse should be tested for cystic fibrosis (sweat chloride test).

first_line_drugs.notes: 150 mg/kg IV over 1h, then 50 mg/kg over 4h, then 100 mg/kg over 16h for APAP toxicity
Continuous infusion to maintain glucose >70 mg/dL; 6-8 mg/kg/min glucose infusion rate
2-10 mg IV single dose for coagulopathy; repeat if INR remains elevated

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg IV q8h x14-21 days if HSV hepatitis suspected (neonates especially)
2 mg/kg/day PO/IV (max 60 mg) for autoimmune hepatitis-related ALF
10-15 mL/kg IV for active bleeding with coagulopathy; avoid routine use
Emergency listing for ALF meeting King's College or PELD criteria

investigations.name: PT/INR (serial q6-12h)
investigations.indication: Key prognostic marker — rising INR indicates worsening

investigations.name: Viral hepatitis panel (HAV IgM, HBsAg, HCV RNA)

counselling: Your child's liver is failing and needs emergency treatment — this is life-threatening
The child may need transfer to a liver transplant center if the liver does not recover
Multiple medicines and close monitoring are needed — the child will be in the ICU
Please share all information about medicines taken, illnesses, or toxin exposure
The medical team will keep you informed at every step — liver transplant may be discussed
referral_criteria: Pediatric hepatology and liver transplant center URGENTLY. PICU mandatory. Nephrology if hepatorenal syndrome. Neurosurgery if intracranial hypertension from hepatic encephalopathy
hospitalisation_criteria: All pediatric acute liver failure (PALF) requires PICU admission. Criteria: coagulopathy (INR >=1.5 with encephalopathy or INR >=2.0 without encephalopathy) in acute liver disease without pre-existing chronic liver disease
notes: Transfer to pediatric liver transplant center urgently. NAC beneficial for APAP-induced ALF; role in non-APAP PALF remains uncertain. Treat identifiable causes (HSV, Wilson, AIH).
guideline_changes: 2024 ISPGHAN consensus on pediatric ALF management; NAC role debated for non-APAP PALF

first_line_drugs.notes: Etiology-specific: antivirals for HBV/HCV, chelation for Wilson, UDCA for cholestasis
10-20 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for cholestatic liver diseases
High-calorie diet 120-150% RDA; MCT-enriched formula; fat-soluble vitamin supplementation

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-3 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 100 mg) for ascites management
1-2 mg/kg/day PO/IV (max 6 mg/kg/day) for ascites; combine with spironolactone
Definitive treatment for decompensated cirrhosis or liver failure

investigations.name: Liver function tests (comprehensive)
investigations.indication: AST, ALT, bilirubin, albumin, GGT — monitor disease progression

investigations.name: Liver ultrasound with Doppler
investigations.indication: Liver size, nodularity, portal flow, ascites

investigations.name: Upper GI endoscopy
investigations.indication: Variceal screening — start at diagnosis of cirrhosis

counselling: Your child has liver scarring (cirrhosis) — lifelong monitoring and treatment of the cause is needed
Feed the child high-calorie nutritious food — liver disease increases nutritional needs by 20-50%
Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) supplements must be taken as prescribed
Come back urgently if: child vomits blood, has black stools, develops a swollen belly, or becomes confused
Regular ultrasound and blood tests every 3-6 months are needed to monitor the liver
referral_criteria: Pediatric hepatology mandatory. Liver transplant evaluation early for decompensated cirrhosis. GI for variceal screening/banding. Nutrition/dietitian. Genetics if inherited liver disease
hospitalisation_criteria: Variceal bleeding (hematemesis/melena). Ascites not responding to diuretics. Hepatic encephalopathy. Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. Hepatorenal syndrome. Acute decompensation
notes: Management is etiology-directed. Monitor for complications: variceal bleeding, ascites, hepatorenal syndrome. Liver transplant evaluation should be early for decompensation.
guideline_changes: Resmetirom (THR-beta agonist) approved 2024 for MASH in adults; pediatric trials pending

first_line_drugs.notes: 2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) x2-4wk, then taper over 6-8wk to 2.5-5 mg/day maintenance
1-2.5 mg/kg/day PO QD (added after initial steroid response); steroid-sparing agent

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 1 g BID) if azathioprine intolerant/refractory
3-5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; bridge therapy for severe AIH or steroid-refractory
0.05-0.1 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; target trough 3-5 ng/mL for refractory AIH

investigations.name: Liver function tests
investigations.indication: AST, ALT markedly elevated; bilirubin, albumin for severity

investigations.name: Liver biopsy
investigations.indication: Interface hepatitis — confirms diagnosis and grades severity

counselling: Your child has autoimmune liver disease — the body's immune system is attacking the liver
Steroid medicine and an immunosuppressant will be needed, possibly for life
Do not stop the medicines without asking the doctor — disease flares are very common when stopped
Regular blood tests every 2-4 weeks initially, then every 3 months, are needed to monitor response
Report immediately if the child becomes yellow (jaundiced), has dark urine, or develops a swollen belly
referral_criteria: Pediatric hepatology mandatory. Liver transplant evaluation if steroid-refractory or decompensated. Immunology for atypical cases. Genetics if overlap with other autoimmune diseases
hospitalisation_criteria: New diagnosis with significant coagulopathy or jaundice. Acute liver failure from autoimmune hepatitis. Disease flare with hepatic decompensation. Initiation of immunosuppression requiring close monitoring
notes: Autoimmune hepatitis (AIH) is the most common inflammatory liver disease requiring treatment. Relapse rate 60-80% after withdrawal; many patients need lifelong immunosuppression.
guideline_changes: EASL 2025 updated AIH guidelines; mycophenolate increasingly used as steroid-sparing agent

first_line_drugs.notes: Ceftriaxone 50-75 mg/kg IV q24h (max 2g) + Metronidazole 30 mg/kg/day IV divided TID
US-guided needle aspiration (<5cm) or catheter drainage (>5cm)

second_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg/dose (pip) IV q8h (max 4.5 g) for polymicrobial pyogenic abscess
35-50 mg/kg/day PO/IV divided TID x7-10 days for amoebic liver abscess
20 mg/kg IV q8h (max 1 g) for resistant organisms or treatment failure
Open drainage for ruptured abscess, peritonitis, or failed percutaneous approach

investigations.name: Abdominal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Identify abscess location, size, and guide drainage

investigations.name: Amoebic serology (IHA/ELISA)
investigations.indication: Differentiate amoebic from pyogenic abscess — common in India

counselling: Your child has an infection inside the liver (abscess) — antibiotics and possibly drainage are needed
IV antibiotics will be given for several days, followed by oral antibiotics for weeks
If the abscess is large, a needle will be used to drain the pus under ultrasound guidance
Complete the full course of antibiotics (usually 2-6 weeks) even after feeling better
Follow-up ultrasound is needed to confirm the abscess has resolved
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery/interventional radiology for drainage (abscess >5 cm or not responding to antibiotics alone). GI if underlying liver disease. Immunology if recurrent liver abscesses
hospitalisation_criteria: All liver abscesses require hospitalisation for IV antibiotics. Drainage needed for: abscess >5 cm, no clinical improvement in 48-72h of antibiotics, or risk of rupture. Sepsis or hemodynamic instability
notes: Pyogenic abscesses require both antibiotics and drainage; amoebic abscesses often resolve with metronidazole alone. Antibiotic duration 2-6 weeks depending on response.
source: Pediatric Surgery 2023, Johns Hopkins ABX Guide 2024

first_line_drugs.notes: Structured diet + exercise program; target 7-10% weight loss if overweight/obese
Reduce sugar-sweetened beverages, processed foods; increase fiber and whole foods
400-800 IU/day PO for biopsy-proven NASH in children without diabetes

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for cholestatic liver disorders
500 mg PO BID (age >=10yr) for NAFLD with insulin resistance; limited evidence

investigations.name: ALT (serial q3-6 months)
investigations.indication: Primary monitoring marker — >2x ULN warrants further evaluation

investigations.name: Fasting glucose and HbA1c
investigations.indication: Screen for insulin resistance and diabetes

investigations.name: Liver biopsy
investigations.indication: If persistent elevation despite lifestyle changes — stage fibrosis/NASH

counselling: Your child has fatty liver disease (NAFLD/MASLD) — this is caused by excess weight and poor diet
The most important treatment is lifestyle change: healthier food and more physical activity
Cut down sugar-sweetened drinks (cola, juice, packaged drinks) — switch to water and buttermilk
60 minutes of active play/exercise every day is the best medicine for this condition
Regular blood tests every 6 months to check liver enzymes and follow-up with the doctor
referral_criteria: Pediatric hepatology/GI if ALT persistently elevated despite 6 months of lifestyle changes. Liver biopsy if NASH suspected. Dietitian for structured meal planning. Endocrinology if insulin resistance/prediabetes
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Consider if: acute liver failure from NASH, liver biopsy under sedation, severe metabolic complications (diabetes, hypertension crisis)
notes: NAFLD/MASLD is the most common pediatric chronic liver disease. Screen overweight children with ALT; liver biopsy for indeterminate cases. Lifestyle modification is primary treatment.
guideline_changes: NAFLD renamed MASLD/MASH; resmetirom approved 2024 for adult MASH; pediatric guidance forthcoming

first_line_drugs.notes: For esophageal varices with bleeding or high-risk stigmata; repeat q2-4wk until obliteration
1-2 mcg/kg IV bolus then 1-2 mcg/kg/h infusion (max 50 mcg/h) for acute variceal bleed
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID; titrate to 25% HR reduction. Limited pediatric evidence

second_line_drugs.notes: Endoscopic injection for variceal bleeding if banding not feasible (younger children)
1-3 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 100 mg) for ascites
Transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt for refractory bleeding/ascites
Surgical bypass for extrahepatic portal vein obstruction in eligible children

investigations.name: Upper GI endoscopy
investigations.indication: Variceal grading and banding — first screening at diagnosis

investigations.name: Liver ultrasound with Doppler
investigations.indication: Portal vein flow direction, spleen size, ascites

investigations.name: CT/MR angiography
investigations.indication: Map portal venous anatomy for surgical planning

counselling: Your child has high blood pressure in the liver blood vessels — this can cause the veins in the food pipe to swell and bleed
An endoscopy (camera test) is needed to check for swollen veins and may need treatment (banding)
If the child vomits blood or has black tarry stools — rush to the hospital immediately
Salt restriction may be needed if the child has a swollen belly (fluid accumulation)
Regular follow-up every 3-6 months with endoscopy is essential to prevent bleeding
referral_criteria: Pediatric hepatology/GI mandatory. GI endoscopy for variceal surveillance and banding. Interventional radiology for TIPS. Pediatric surgery for shunt procedures (Meso-Rex). Liver transplant if decompensated cirrhosis
hospitalisation_criteria: Acute variceal bleeding (hematemesis/melena) — emergency. New onset ascites. Hepatic encephalopathy. Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. Scheduled endoscopic band ligation
notes: Beta-blockers for primary prophylaxis lack robust pediatric evidence; band ligation preferred for secondary prophylaxis. Evaluate for liver transplant if decompensated cirrhosis.
guideline_changes: Baveno VII criteria for non-invasive assessment of portal hypertension; beta-blockers debated in children

first_line_drugs.notes: 1.5-2x maintenance with D5 0.9% NS; bolus 20 mL/kg if hemodynamically unstable
15 mg/kg PO/IV q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day); first-line analgesia
0.5 mg/kg IV q6h (max 30 mg/dose) x5 days for pain; NSAID adjunct
Begin PO/NG feeds within 24-48h if tolerated; low-fat diet not required

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.2 mg/kg IV q2-4h (max 10 mg) for severe pain unresponsive to non-opioids
20 mg/kg IV q8h (max 1 g) ONLY for infected necrosis; prophylactic abx NOT recommended
For biliary pancreatitis with choledocholithiasis or ductal pathology

investigations.name: Serum lipase
investigations.indication: Primary diagnostic test — >3x upper limit of normal confirms pancreatitis

investigations.name: Serum amylase
investigations.indication: Supportive — may be normal in 15-20% of cases

investigations.name: Liver function tests
investigations.indication: Screen for biliary cause (elevated bilirubin, ALP)

counselling: Your child has inflammation of the pancreas — rest, IV fluids, and pain management are the main treatment
The child can start eating within 24-48 hours when tolerated — no need for prolonged fasting
Low-fat diet is NOT mandatory in mild cases — a regular diet is fine when tolerated
Report immediately if pain returns, the child develops fever, or the belly becomes more swollen
Follow up to check for recurrent pancreatitis — some children need long-term monitoring
referral_criteria: Pediatric gastroenterology for all cases. Surgery/IR if infected pancreatic necrosis or pseudocyst. ERCP for biliary pancreatitis with choledocholithiasis. Genetics if recurrent pancreatitis (PRSS1, SPINK1, CFTR)
hospitalisation_criteria: All acute pancreatitis should be hospitalised for IV fluids and pain management. Severe pancreatitis (organ failure, necrosis on CT). Intractable pain or vomiting. Unable to tolerate oral intake
notes: Aggressive IV hydration and early enteral feeding are cornerstones of treatment. Prophylactic antibiotics not indicated. Opioids are safe and should not be withheld for pain.
guideline_changes: 2024 BSPGHAN guidance published; liberal IV fluids associated with improved outcomes in pediatric AP

first_line_drugs.notes: 500-2500 lipase units/kg/meal PO; 250-500 LU/kg/snack. Enteric-coated capsules
Vitamins A, D, E, K at 1-2x RDA PO daily; monitor levels q6-12 months

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg/kg/day PO QD to enhance PERT efficacy by reducing gastric acid inactivation
Medium-chain triglycerides 1-2 mL/kg/day PO for improved fat absorption
ERCP for chronic pancreatitis with duct stones, strictures, or pseudocysts

investigations.name: Fecal elastase
investigations.indication: Primary test for exocrine insufficiency — <200 mcg/g is diagnostic

investigations.name: 72-hour fecal fat (coefficient of fat absorption)
investigations.indication: Gold standard for steatorrhea — <93% absorption in children >6 months

investigations.name: Sweat chloride test
investigations.indication: Rule out cystic fibrosis as underlying cause

counselling: Your child's pancreas is not producing enough enzymes to digest food properly
Pancreatic enzyme capsules must be taken WITH every meal and snack — never after the meal
Do not crush or chew the enzyme capsules — swallow whole or sprinkle contents on soft acidic food
Extra fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are needed because the body cannot absorb them well
Watch for greasy, foul-smelling stools — this means the enzyme dose may need to be increased
referral_criteria: Pediatric gastroenterology mandatory. CF team if cystic fibrosis is the cause. Endocrinology if CF-related diabetes develops. Dietitian for nutritional optimization. Surgery/ERCP for chronic pancreatitis with duct pathology
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe malnutrition from exocrine insufficiency. Acute pancreatitis flare. Distal intestinal obstruction syndrome (CF patients). ERCP for duct intervention
notes: PERT is the mainstay for exocrine pancreatic insufficiency. Do not exceed 10,000 LU/kg/day to avoid fibrosing colonopathy. Monitor growth and nutritional status regularly.
guideline_changes: 2024 European guidelines on EPI diagnosis and PERT dosing published (UEG/EPC/ESPGHAN)

first_line_drugs.notes: Lifelong elimination of wheat, barley, rye. Dietitian-guided. Labeled GF oats permitted
Iron, folate, calcium, vitamin D, zinc as needed based on deficiency labs

second_line_drugs.notes: Short-term lactose avoidance if secondary lactase deficiency present at diagnosis
1-2 mg/kg/day PO x2-4wk for celiac crisis with severe malabsorption (rare)

investigations.name: Total serum IgA
investigations.indication: Rule out IgA deficiency (false negative tTG-IgA)

investigations.name: Upper GI endoscopy with duodenal biopsy
investigations.indication: Villous atrophy — Marsh classification confirms diagnosis

investigations.name: CBC, iron, ferritin, folate, vitamin D, calcium
investigations.indication: Nutritional deficiency screening — common at diagnosis

investigations.name: HLA DQ2/DQ8 typing
investigations.indication: Negative result rules out celiac disease (high NPV)

counselling: Your child has celiac disease — a lifelong strict gluten-free diet is the only treatment
Avoid wheat (roti, bread, pasta), barley, and rye completely — even tiny amounts cause damage
Rice, corn, jowar, bajra, and certified gluten-free oats are safe
Read food labels carefully — gluten is hidden in many processed foods, sauces, and medicines
Join a celiac support group — they can help with diet planning and recipes for Indian meals
referral_criteria: Pediatric gastroenterology for diagnosis confirmation. Dietitian specializing in celiac disease MANDATORY. Endocrinology if associated thyroid disease. Dermatology for dermatitis herpetiformis. Re-evaluate non-responders for refractory celiac or wrong diagnosis
hospitalisation_criteria: Celiac crisis (severe diarrhea, dehydration, metabolic derangement — rare). Severe anemia requiring transfusion. Failure to thrive not responding to GFD. Suspected refractory celiac disease
notes: GFD is the only proven treatment. Monitor tTG-IgA for adherence. Follow-up with dietitian and serology at 3-6 month intervals initially, then annually.
guideline_changes: 2024 ESPGHAN position paper on GFD implementation; oats allowed if labeled gluten-free

first_line_drugs.notes: High-calorie diet 120-150% RDA; oral supplements or enteral nutrition as needed
Iron, calcium, zinc, magnesium, fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) based on labs
Gluten-free diet for celiac, PERT for pancreatic insufficiency, antibiotics for SIBO

second_line_drugs.notes: TPN for severe malabsorption/intestinal failure; individualized caloric and micronutrient goals
0.05-0.1 mg/kg/dose PO (max 2 mg/dose) for symptomatic diarrhea; age >2yr only
240 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for bile acid malabsorption

investigations.name: tTG-IgA
investigations.indication: Screen for celiac disease — most common cause in India

investigations.name: Stool for reducing substances and pH
CBC, iron, ferritin, vitamin D, calcium, zinc
Upper GI endoscopy with biopsy
investigations.indication: If celiac serology positive or etiology unclear

counselling: Your child's body is not absorbing food properly — we need to find and treat the cause
Extra calories, vitamins, and minerals may be needed to help the child grow
Follow the dietitian's meal plan — the child may need special formulas or supplements
Weight and height will be monitored closely — bring the child for regular follow-ups
Some causes of malabsorption need lifelong dietary changes — the doctor will explain based on the diagnosis
referral_criteria: Pediatric gastroenterology mandatory for diagnostic workup. Dietitian for nutritional rehabilitation. Allergist if food allergy causing malabsorption. Genetics if inherited cause (CF, congenital enteropathy)
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe failure to thrive (weight loss >10%). Severe dehydration. Malnutrition requiring parenteral nutrition. Electrolyte imbalance. Diagnostic workup requiring endoscopy
notes: Thorough workup essential to identify cause (celiac, IBD, EPI, SIBO, cow milk protein allergy). Prompt referral to pediatric GI for growth failure associated with malabsorption.

first_line_drugs.notes: NS 20 mL/kg bolus; crossmatch blood. Transfuse pRBC 10-15 mL/kg if Hb <7 or unstable
<20kg: 10 mg IV daily; 20-55kg: 20 mg IV daily; >55kg: 40 mg IV daily; for upper GI bleed
1-2 mcg/kg IV bolus then 1-2 mcg/kg/h infusion (max 50 mcg/h) for variceal hemorrhage

second_line_drugs.notes: EGD within 12h for variceal bleed or 24h for non-variceal; band ligation or injection
15-25 mg/kg IV q6-8h (max 1 g/dose) as adjunct for severe hemorrhage
40-80 mg/kg/day PO divided QID (max 1 g/dose) for mucosal protection

investigations.name: Meckel scan (Tc-99m pertechnetate)
investigations.indication: Young children with painless rectal bleeding — Meckel diverticulum

counselling: Your child is bleeding from the stomach or intestines — this needs urgent hospital treatment
The child may need blood transfusion if a lot of blood has been lost
A camera test (endoscopy) will be done to find the source of bleeding and treat it
Do not give any painkillers (especially ibuprofen/aspirin) without doctor's advice
After discharge, follow up for repeat blood tests to make sure bleeding has stopped
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI urgently for endoscopy. Surgery if massive hemorrhage not controlled endoscopically. Interventional radiology for angiographic embolization. Hematology if coagulopathy
hospitalisation_criteria: All significant GI bleeding requires hospitalisation. Hemodynamic instability. Hemoglobin drop >2 g/dL. Active hematemesis or melena. Need for endoscopic intervention or blood transfusion
notes: Stabilize hemodynamics first. Upper GI bleed (hematemesis, melena) requires IV PPI and early endoscopy. Lower GI bleed: consider Meckel scan in young children, colonoscopy in older children.

first_line_drugs.notes: TID x5-7 days, first-line for localised non-bullous impetigo

second_line_drugs.notes: 25-50 mg/kg/day divided q6-8h x7 days, extensive or bullous impetigo
8-10 mg/kg/day TMP component q12h x7 days, if MRSA suspected

investigations.name: Wound swab culture and sensitivity
investigations.indication: Recurrent impetigo or failure of first-line treatment

investigations.name: Urinalysis
investigations.indication: If GAS impetigo - screen for post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis 2-3 weeks later

counselling: Keep affected area clean; wash gently with soap and water before applying ointment
Child should not share towels, clothes, or bedding to prevent spread to siblings
Keep fingernails short and discourage scratching of the sores
Child can return to school after 24 hours of antibiotic treatment if lesions are covered
Complete the full course of antibiotics; do not stop early even if skin looks better
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if recurrent impetigo despite adequate treatment, suspected MRSA (culture-guided), or deep tissue involvement (ecthyma)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if extensive bullous impetigo with systemic toxicity, suspected staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome, or post-streptococcal complications (glomerulonephritis signs)
notes: Systemic antibiotics for extensive disease, bullous impetigo, or suspected MRSA.

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mg/kg/day PO divided tid (max 1.5g/dose); outpatient, non-purulent
30-40 mg/kg/day PO divided tid (max 1.8g/day); MRSA suspected or PCN allergy

second_line_drugs.notes: 8-12 mg/kg/day TMP PO divided bid (max 320 mg TMP bid); MRSA, purulent
100 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 6g/day); moderate-severe, inpatient
2-4 mg/kg/day PO divided bid (max 200 mg/day); age >=8yr, MRSA

counselling: Give antibiotics exactly as prescribed — complete the full course
Mark the border of the redness with a pen and watch if it spreads beyond the mark
Keep the affected area elevated to reduce swelling
Apply warm compresses for comfort if advised by the doctor
Return if redness spreads rapidly, fever develops, or streaking appears — this needs urgent review
referral_criteria: Refer to surgeon if abscess formation needing incision and drainage, or if necrotizing fasciitis suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if rapid spread, systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia), facial cellulitis, periorbital/orbital cellulitis, or failed outpatient antibiotics
notes: Mark and monitor erythema borders. Elevate affected extremity. Hospitalize if systemic toxicity, rapid spread, or failed outpatient therapy.

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply liberally 2-3 times daily, long-term use even after improvement, cornerstone of therapy
Apply thin layer once daily for 5-7 days acute flare, then taper to 2 times/week maintenance, avoid prolonged use on face
Apply once daily at night for scalp/extensive lesions, 1-2 weeks then taper
Natural moisturization, coconut oil in summer, aloe vera-based lotion in winter, safe and soothing
Age-appropriate dose PO for 5-7 days, for pruritus and sleep disturbance
Age-appropriate dose PO for 5-7 days, alternative antihistamine for itching

investigations.name: CBC with absolute eosinophil count
investigations.indication: Allergy workup, elevated eosinophils suggest atopy

counselling: Apply moisturizer generously 2-3 times daily - this is the most important treatment
Apply moisturizer within 3 minutes of bathing to lock in moisture
Use steroid cream only during flare-ups as directed, do not use continuously
Use lukewarm water for bath and mild soap-free cleanser only
Avoid woolen clothes, harsh soaps, dust, and suspected food allergens
This is a chronic condition with relapses - regular skin care prevents flare-ups
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Oozing or pus from skin lesions suggesting infection
Severe redness or swelling of affected skin
No response to treatment after one week
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if no improvement with topical steroids after 2 weeks, severe widespread disease, suspected secondary infection requiring systemic treatment, or need for immunomodulators (tacrolimus/pimecrolimus)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if erythroderma, severe secondary bacterial infection (oozing, fever), eczema herpeticum, or severe disease not responding to outpatient management
notes: Treatment principles: restore skin barrier, reduce inflammation, control itching, prevent relapse. Moisturizer is cornerstone. Steroid for flare only - avoid overuse. Lukewarm water bath, mild soap-free cleanser, apply moisturizer within 3 minutes after bath. Avoid harsh soaps, woolen clothes, dust/allergens.
source: AAP Atopic Dermatitis Clinical Report 2025, AAD Guidelines 2024, AAAAI/ACAAI Joint Task Force 2023, NICE
guideline_changes: AAP 2025: Updated clinical report on skin-directed management - daily bathing with lukewarm water and gentle cleanser, moisturizer within minutes of bath; proactive intermittent topical anti-inflammatory maintenance to prevent flares; AAD 2024: Updated treatment guidelines with expanded role of topical calcineurin inhibitors and JAK inhibitors for moderate-severe disease; AAAAI 2023: Dupilumab recommended for moderate-severe AD in children >=6 months

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to scalp, lather, leave 5 min then rinse; daily for 2 weeks then 1-2x/week
Apply to affected area BID for 7-14 days; for erythema in skin folds
Apply after bathing to restore epidermal barrier; use fragrance-free products

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply to affected area BID for 2-4 weeks or shampoo 2x/week; antifungal alternative
Apply to scalp, leave 5-10 min, rinse; 2-3x/week for 2-4 weeks
Apply to scalp, lather, rinse; 2-3x/week; mild OTC antifungal option
Apply BID to facial SD; steroid-sparing for children >=2 yr

investigations.name: Skin biopsy
investigations.indication: If atypical presentation or suspected Langerhans cell histiocytosis

counselling: Cradle cap in babies is very common and harmless - it usually resolves by 6-12 months
Apply mineral oil or coconut oil to scalp, leave 15 minutes, then gently comb off scales
Use antifungal shampoo 2-3 times per week on scalp; leave on for 5 minutes before rinsing
Mild steroid cream can be used on red, inflamed areas for 1-2 weeks only
Condition may recur; maintenance antifungal shampoo once weekly can help prevent flares
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if severe seborrheic dermatitis not responding to antifungal + topical steroid, diagnostic uncertainty (consider psoriasis, Langerhans cell histiocytosis in infants)
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not needed for typical SD; consider if erythrodermic presentation or suspected Leiner disease in infants
notes: Infantile seborrheic dermatitis (cradle cap) is usually self-limiting and responds to gentle scale removal with mineral oil. Recurrence is common; maintenance therapy may be needed.
guideline_changes: Tapinarof 1% and ruxolitinib 1.5% cream in pipeline for adolescent facial SD

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply thick layer to affected area with every diaper change; first-line barrier protection
Apply to affected area 3-4x daily after diaper change for 7-14 days if candidal
Apply generously at each diaper change as barrier; use with frequent diaper changes

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply BID for 3-7 days for moderate inflammation; do not use under occlusion long-term
Apply to affected area BID for 7-14 days; for candidal diaper dermatitis
Apply to affected area BID-TID for 7-14 days; alternative antifungal for candidal rash

investigations.name: KOH preparation
investigations.indication: If candidal superinfection suspected (satellite lesions, >3 days)

counselling: Change diaper frequently - at least every 2-3 hours and immediately after soiling
Apply thick layer of zinc oxide cream as barrier at every diaper change
Allow diaper-free time for 10-15 minutes several times a day for air exposure
Use plain water or mild wipes for cleaning; avoid scented wipes
If rash has satellite lesions (small red dots around main rash), it may be fungal - tell doctor
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if not responding to standard treatment in 7 days, suspected underlying condition (psoriasis, Langerhans cell histiocytosis), or recurrent severe episodes
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not typically needed; consider if extensive erosive dermatitis with secondary infection or failure to thrive
notes: Frequent diaper changes and air exposure are the cornerstone of management. If rash persists >3 days despite barrier care, consider candidal superinfection.

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply thin layer to affected area BID for 10-14 days; use lowest potency effective
Apply to affected area BID for 10-14 days; avoid face/intertriginous areas
Apply liberally after bathing and PRN; use fragrance-free products (e.g. CeraVe)

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO for 7 days then taper over 7 days; for severe/widespread disease
Apply BID to affected area for up to 3 weeks; for face/eyelids in children >=2 yr
2 mg/kg/day PO divided Q6-8h (max 25 mg/dose) for pruritus relief

investigations.name: Patch testing
investigations.indication: Identify contact allergen in recurrent cases

counselling: Identify and strictly avoid the substance causing the allergic reaction
Apply steroid cream as directed - thin layer only; do not use on face for more than 1 week
Use fragrance-free soap, detergent, and moisturisers
Keep child nails short to prevent scratching and secondary infection
If rash recurs, patch testing by a dermatologist can identify the exact allergen
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist/allergist if recurrent contact dermatitis, allergen identification needed (patch testing), or not responding to topical treatment
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed; admit if severe widespread reaction with systemic symptoms, secondary bacterial infection requiring IV antibiotics, or erythroderma
notes: Identify and avoid the offending allergen via patch testing if recurrent. Pre-treatment with barrier cream or topical steroid may help if adhesive/dressing exposure is unavoidable.
guideline_changes: AAP 2025 clinical report emphasizes daily bathing with fragrance-free cleansers

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply liberally BID and after hand washing; restore epidermal barrier (e.g. CeraVe, Cetaphil)
Apply to affected area BID for 10-14 days; low potency suitable for children
Apply BID for 10-14 days; for moderate lesions on trunk/extremities

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO for 7 days then taper; for severe widespread ICD only
Apply BID for up to 3 weeks; steroid-sparing for face/flexures in children >=2 yr
2 mg/kg/day PO divided Q6-8h (max 25 mg/dose) for pruritus

investigations.name: Patch testing
investigations.indication: To differentiate from allergic contact dermatitis if diagnosis uncertain

investigations.name: Skin biopsy
investigations.indication: If diagnosis uncertain after clinical assessment and patch testing

counselling: Identify and remove the irritant causing the rash - this is the key to healing
Apply barrier cream (zinc oxide or petroleum jelly) before exposure to known irritants
Use fragrance-free soap and moisturisers; avoid harsh detergents
Moisturise skin regularly - at least twice daily and after hand washing
Cotton gloves at night can prevent scratching during sleep
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if chronic ICD not responding to irritant avoidance and topical treatment, or occupational exposure in adolescents
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed; admit if severe widespread ICD with secondary infection or significant skin barrier breakdown
notes: Identify and remove the causative irritant. Barrier creams (zinc oxide, dimethicone) can prevent recurrence in occupational or habitual exposure.

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to affected area BID for 10-14 days; low potency for pediatric use
Apply liberally BID and after bathing; restore skin barrier
6-23 mo: 2.5 mg PO daily; 2-5 yr: 5 mg daily; >=6 yr: 10 mg daily for itch

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply BID for 10-14 days; for moderate dermatitis on trunk/extremities
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO for 7 days then taper over 7 days; severe cases only
Apply BID for face/intertriginous areas; steroid-sparing for children >=2 yr

investigations.name: Patch testing
investigations.indication: Identify specific contact allergen if recurrent

investigations.name: Skin biopsy
investigations.indication: If diagnosis uncertain; differentiate from psoriasis, dermatophytosis

counselling: Try to identify what is causing the rash - common culprits are detergent, nickel jewellery, plants
Apply moisturiser regularly and steroid cream on inflamed areas only
Use fragrance-free products for skin care and laundry
Avoid scratching; keep nails short and apply cool compresses for itch relief
If rash keeps coming back, allergy testing (patch test) can identify the specific cause
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist/allergist for patch testing if recurrent or chronic contact dermatitis, or diagnosis uncertain
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed; consider if severe generalised reaction or secondary infection requiring IV treatment
notes: Attempt to classify as allergic vs irritant via history and patch testing. Avoid irritants; use mild cleansers and fragrance-free products.

first_line_drugs.notes: 6-23 mo: 2.5 mg PO daily; 2-5 yr: 2.5-5 mg daily; >=6 yr: 5-10 mg daily
Apply thin layer to affected area BID for 7-14 days; for symptomatic skin lesions
Apply liberally after bathing and PRN to restore skin barrier

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) for 3-5 days for severe systemic reactions
1.25 mg/kg/dose PO Q6h (max 50 mg/dose); useful for acute pruritus/urticaria
2 mg/kg/day PO divided Q6-8h (max 25 mg/dose); for persistent itch

investigations.name: Skin prick testing
investigations.indication: If food allergy suspected; done by allergist

counselling: Stop the suspected food or medicine immediately - this is the most important step
Keep a food diary to help identify which food is causing the skin reaction
Give antihistamine as prescribed for itching; apply moisturiser for skin dryness
If swelling of lips, tongue, or breathing difficulty occurs, use epinephrine and go to hospital
See an allergist for proper allergy testing if food allergy is suspected
referral_criteria: Refer to allergist if food allergy suspected for IgE testing and oral food challenge; refer to dermatologist if drug reaction with skin involvement
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe drug reaction (SJS/TEN), anaphylaxis, widespread blistering, mucosal involvement, or systemic symptoms (fever, organ involvement)
notes: Discontinue offending ingested substance (food, drug) immediately. Referral to allergist for IgE testing if food allergy suspected.

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to lichenified plaques BID for 2-4 weeks; may use under occlusion overnight
2 mg/kg/day PO divided Q6-8h (max 25 mg/dose); break itch-scratch cycle at bedtime
Apply liberally TID and after bathing to reduce dryness-triggered pruritus

5-10 mg/kg/day PO divided TID; for neuropathic itch component; off-label in children

investigations.name: Skin biopsy
investigations.indication: If diagnosis uncertain; shows epidermal thickening and dermal fibrosis

investigations.name: Complete blood count with eosinophils
investigations.indication: Exclude underlying atopic or parasitic conditions

counselling: The main goal is to break the itch-scratch cycle - keep nails very short
Apply thick moisturiser and steroid cream as directed; cover area with bandage at night
Hydroxyzine at bedtime helps reduce scratching during sleep
Stress and anxiety can worsen the condition - relaxation techniques may help
Treatment takes weeks to months; be patient and consistent with cream application
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if not responding to potent topical steroids, need for systemic therapy, or suspicion of underlying neuropathic itch
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed; consider if severe secondary infection or need for inpatient behavioural intervention
notes: No FDA-approved therapy exists for LSC; breaking the itch-scratch cycle is the primary goal. Behavioral intervention and covering affected areas at night can help in children.
guideline_changes: Dupilumab and nemolizumab emerging for refractory prurigo nodularis

first_line_drugs.notes: 6-23 mo: 2.5 mg PO daily; 2-5 yr: 2.5-5 mg daily; >=6 yr: 5-10 mg daily
2 mg/kg/day PO divided Q6-8h (max 25 mg/dose); sedating, useful for nocturnal itch
Apply liberally after bathing to reduce xerosis-related pruritus

second_line_drugs.notes: 2-11 yr: 30 mg PO BID; >=12 yr: 60 mg BID or 180 mg daily; non-sedating
2-5 yr: 5 mg PO daily; >=6 yr: 10 mg daily; non-sedating option
1.25 mg/kg/dose PO Q6h (max 50 mg/dose); for acute severe itch; avoid in <2 yr

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Screen for eosinophilia, iron deficiency, lymphoma

investigations.name: Skin scraping for scabies

counselling: Moisturise skin liberally; dry skin is the most common cause of itching in children
Use lukewarm baths and mild soap; avoid hot water which worsens itching
Give antihistamine at bedtime if itching disturbs sleep
Keep fingernails short; cotton gloves at night can prevent scratching
If itching persists despite moisturising, see doctor for further investigation
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if chronic pruritus >6 weeks not responding to antihistamines and moisturisers, or underlying systemic cause suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed; consider if severe excoriations with secondary infection, or pruritus as part of systemic illness (liver, kidney, lymphoma)
notes: Investigate underlying cause (xerosis, scabies, systemic disease) before chronic antihistamine use. Up-dose second-gen antihistamines up to 4x before switching agents.
guideline_changes: Up-dosing of second-gen antihistamines (2-4x) now recommended before escalation

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to affected area BID for 7-14 days; first-line for mild unspecified dermatitis
Apply liberally BID-TID and after bathing; fragrance-free ceramide-based preferred
6-23 mo: 2.5 mg PO daily; 2-5 yr: 5 mg daily; >=6 yr: 10 mg daily for pruritus

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply BID for 14 days; for moderate dermatitis on trunk/extremities
Apply BID for face/eyelids/flexures; for children >=2 yr; steroid-sparing
1 mg/kg/day PO for 5-7 days then taper; for severe flares only

investigations.name: Skin biopsy
investigations.indication: If diagnosis uncertain or chronic unresponsive dermatitis

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Eosinophilia may suggest allergic or parasitic cause

counselling: Apply moisturiser generously twice daily and after bathing
Use mild steroid cream only on the red, itchy areas - not on normal skin
Use fragrance-free soap and detergent; dress in soft cotton clothing
If rash does not improve in 2 weeks, come back for further evaluation
Keep a diary of potential triggers (food, detergent, stress) to identify the cause
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if persistent dermatitis not responding to topical treatment, diagnostic uncertainty, or need for patch testing/skin biopsy
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed; admit if erythroderma, severe secondary infection, or suspected systemic cause requiring inpatient workup
notes: Attempt to identify specific etiology before treating empirically. If persistent, consider patch testing, skin biopsy, or dermatology referral.
guideline_changes: AAP 2025 update on skin-directed management for atopic/unspecified dermatitis

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to plaques daily-BID; for <30% BSA; can combine with topical steroid; >=12 yr
Apply to plaques BID for 2-4 weeks; medium-high potency for trunk/extremities
Apply BID to face/flexural psoriasis for 2-4 weeks; lower potency for sensitive areas

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.2-0.7 mg/kg PO/SC once weekly (max 25 mg/wk); for moderate-severe; monitor CBC/LFTs
6-17 yr: 35-70 kg 20 mg PO BID; >70 kg 30 mg BID; titrate over 5 days
>=6 yr: <60 kg 0.75 mg/kg SC wk 0,4 then Q12W; 60-100 kg 45 mg; >100 kg 90 mg

investigations.name: Skin biopsy

investigations.name: Throat swab culture
investigations.indication: If guttate psoriasis triggered by streptococcal infection

investigations.name: Liver and renal function tests
investigations.indication: Baseline before starting methotrexate or systemic therapy

counselling: Psoriasis is a chronic condition with flare-ups and remissions - it is manageable but not curable
Apply steroid cream or calcipotriene as directed; do not use strong steroids on face or groin
Keep skin well moisturised - this reduces scaling and itch
Stress and infections (especially strep throat) can trigger flares
Emotional support is important; psoriasis is not contagious - educate school and friends
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist for moderate-severe psoriasis (>10% BSA), psoriatic arthritis symptoms, failure of topical therapy, or need for systemic/biologic therapy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if erythrodermic or pustular psoriasis, psoriasis with severe secondary infection, or metabolic derangement from extensive disease
notes: Topical therapy is first-line for mild-moderate psoriasis; screen for psoriatic arthritis, metabolic syndrome, and psychosocial impact. Biologics require pre-treatment TB and hepatitis screening.
guideline_changes: Apremilast approved for pediatric psoriasis ages 6-17 in Aug 2024

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to affected area TID-QID PRN for itch relief; lukewarm oatmeal baths may help
2-5 yr: 2.5-5 mg PO daily; >=6 yr: 5-10 mg daily; for symptomatic pruritus
Apply to itchy lesions BID for 1-2 weeks; for moderate pruritus

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/dose PO QID (max 800 mg/dose) x 7 days; some evidence for faster resolution
3x/week for 2-4 weeks; for severe/widespread pruritic cases; accelerates clearance
25-40 mg/kg/day PO divided QID for 14 days; limited evidence for faster resolution

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis (usually sufficient)

investigations.name: RPR/VDRL

investigations.name: KOH preparation
investigations.indication: If fungal infection (tinea) needs to be excluded

counselling: Pityriasis rosea is harmless and heals on its own in 6-8 weeks
It is not contagious - child can attend school normally
Avoid hot baths and vigorous exercise which may make the rash worse
Use calamine lotion or mild steroid cream for itch relief
The rash follows a Christmas tree pattern on the back - this is characteristic and not worrying
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if atypical presentation (no herald patch, face involvement), persisting >12 weeks, or diagnostic uncertainty (rule out secondary syphilis in adolescents)
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not needed for pityriasis rosea
notes: Pityriasis rosea is self-limited (4-8 weeks); treatment is symptomatic. Avoid hot water and strenuous exercise which may exacerbate the rash.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.25 mg/kg/dose PO daily; can updose to 2-4x standard dose if uncontrolled
2-5yr: 5mg PO daily; >=6yr: 10mg daily; updose to 2x if needed
6-11yr: 30mg PO BID; >=12yr: 180mg PO daily; updose up to 4x if needed

second_line_drugs.notes: 150-300mg SC q4wks for chronic spontaneous urticaria; age >=12yr
6mo-5yr: 4mg PO daily; 6-14yr: 5mg; add-on to antihistamine
3-5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; for refractory CSU, short-term use
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO short course 3-7 days for acute flares only

investigations.name: CBC with differential
investigations.indication: Screen for eosinophilia or other abnormalities

investigations.name: ESR and CRP
investigations.indication: Screen for underlying inflammatory condition in chronic urticaria

investigations.name: Thyroid function and anti-thyroid antibodies
investigations.indication: Autoimmune thyroid disease associated with chronic urticaria

counselling: Hives (urticaria) are itchy raised welts that can appear anywhere and usually resolve within 24 hours per lesion
Antihistamine medicine (cetirizine or loratadine) should be taken daily, not just when itchy
If hives last more than 6 weeks, it is called chronic urticaria and may need longer treatment
Cool compresses and loose cotton clothing help reduce itching
If hives come with swelling of lips/tongue or difficulty breathing, use epinephrine and go to hospital
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric allergist/dermatologist for chronic urticaria (>6 weeks), angioedema, or if standard antihistamines at 4x dose are ineffective
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if associated angioedema with airway compromise, anaphylaxis, or systemic vasculitis
notes: Updose second-gen antihistamines up to 4x before adding biologics. Avoid first-generation antihistamines per international guidelines.
guideline_changes: 2024 global update recommends same algorithm for children as adults with weight-based dosing

first_line_drugs.notes: 5-10 mg/kg/dose PO Q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); for pain and inflammation in EM minor
1.25 mg/kg/dose PO Q6h (max 50 mg/dose); for pruritus; can also use as oral rinse
Apply to skin lesions BID for 7-14 days; for symptomatic relief of EM minor

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/dose PO QID (max 400 mg/dose) for 5 days; if HSV-triggered EM confirmed
>=12 yr: 500 mg PO BID x 5 days; for HSV-associated recurrent EM; suppressive dosing
Apply to oral lesions Q3h PRN (max 4 doses/day); for painful oral mucosal involvement

investigations.name: Skin biopsy
investigations.indication: If diagnosis uncertain; interface dermatitis with lymphocytic infiltrate

investigations.name: Mycoplasma IgM
investigations.indication: If preceded by respiratory symptoms; Mycoplasma-induced rash

counselling: Erythema multiforme usually heals on its own in 1-4 weeks
Give pain medicine as needed; soft cool foods if mouth sores are present
Avoid hot or spicy foods that may irritate mouth ulcers
If triggered by cold sores (herpes), antiviral medicine can prevent recurrence
Watch for spreading rash, blistering of skin, or difficulty eating/drinking - see doctor urgently
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if recurrent EM (>3 episodes/year), EM major with mucosal involvement, suspected SJS (>1 mucosal site, extensive skin detachment), or need for HSV suppressive therapy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if EM major with severe oral involvement preventing eating, suspected SJS/TEN (detachment >10% BSA), dehydration, or systemic toxicity
notes: EM is usually self-limited (1-4 weeks); supportive care is the mainstay. If HSV-associated and recurrent, consider long-term antiviral suppression.

first_line_drugs.notes: 5-10 mg/kg/dose PO Q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); first-line NSAID for pain/inflammation
5-7 mg/kg/dose PO Q12h (max 500 mg/dose); alternative NSAID for older children
Bed rest with leg elevation; compressive bandaging; reduces inflammation and pain

0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO for 1-2 weeks then taper; only after infection excluded
0.5 mg PO daily-BID (children >4 yr); for recurrent EN; off-label in pediatrics

investigations.name: Stool examination
investigations.indication: If GI symptoms present; screen for IBD

counselling: Erythema nodosum usually resolves on its own in 2-6 weeks
Rest legs as much as possible; elevate legs when sitting
Take ibuprofen regularly with food for pain and inflammation
The bumps may leave bruise-like marks that fade gradually - this is normal
It is important to find the underlying cause - complete all prescribed blood tests
referral_criteria: Refer to rheumatologist or dermatologist if recurrent EN, suspected underlying systemic disease (IBD, sarcoidosis, TB), or not responding to NSAIDs
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed; admit if unable to walk due to pain, suspected underlying serious condition (TB, lymphoma), or systemic toxicity
notes: EN is usually self-limited (2-6 weeks); investigate underlying cause (streptococcal infection, IBD, sarcoidosis, TB). Treat the underlying condition when identified.

first_line_drugs.notes: 6-23 mo: 2.5 mg PO daily; 2-5 yr: 5 mg daily; >=6 yr: 10 mg daily; for itch/urticaria
Apply to affected area BID for 7-14 days; for localized erythematous rash
Apply liberally BID-TID; soothe and protect inflamed skin

1.25 mg/kg/dose PO Q6h (max 50 mg/dose); for acute symptomatic relief
2 mg/kg/day PO divided Q6-8h (max 25 mg/dose); for persistent pruritus

investigations.name: Skin biopsy
investigations.indication: If diagnosis uncertain or chronic erythema

counselling: Most erythematous rashes in children are self-limiting and resolve within 1-2 weeks
Apply moisturiser and mild steroid cream as directed for itch and redness
If caused by a medicine, stop the medicine and inform your doctor
Take photos of the rash progression to show the doctor at follow-up
Seek urgent care if rash spreads rapidly, blisters form, or child develops fever
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if diagnosis uncertain, rash persisting >2 weeks, suspected drug reaction, or mucosal involvement
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if suspected severe drug eruption (SJS/TEN), widespread blistering, mucosal involvement, or systemic toxicity
notes: Identify and discontinue causative drug or irritant if toxic erythema is suspected. Most cases resolve spontaneously once the trigger is removed.

Apply pea-sized amount to face at bedtime; first-line retinoid; OTC available
Apply to affected area daily; combination preferred over antibiotic monotherapy

investigations.indication: If suspected hormonal cause (hirsutism, irregular periods) in adolescent females

counselling: Wash face gently twice daily with mild cleanser; do not scrub or pick at pimples
Apply benzoyl peroxide and retinoid at bedtime on dry skin; start every other day and increase
Skin may get slightly dry and irritated initially - this improves with regular moisturiser use
Results take 6-8 weeks to show; continue treatment patiently
Isotretinoin (if prescribed) requires strict pregnancy prevention in females and regular blood tests

hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed; consider if acne fulminans (explosive onset with fever, joint pain, ulcerative acne)
notes: Multimodal topical therapy is first-line per AAD 2024; always pair topical antibiotics with benzoyl peroxide to prevent resistance. Isotretinoin requires pregnancy prevention (iPLEDGE).
guideline_changes: AAD 2024 now conditionally recommends sarecycline; emphasizes multimodal topical therapy

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to affected follicles TID for 7-10 days; for superficial bacterial folliculitis
Apply to affected area BID for 7-14 days; for localized bacterial folliculitis
Apply for 15-20 min TID-QID to promote drainage; supportive measure for mild cases

Children >8 yr: 2.2 mg/kg/dose PO BID (max 100 mg/dose) for 7-10 days
Apply BID for 2-4 weeks; for Malassezia (pityrosporum) folliculitis
