investigations.indication: If recurrent bacterial skin infections (screen for diabetes)

counselling: Keep the affected area clean and dry; wash with mild antibacterial soap
Do not squeeze or pick at the bumps as this can spread infection
Wear loose-fitting cotton clothing to reduce friction and sweating
Use separate towels from other family members until infection clears
If bumps keep coming back, see doctor for culture to check if MRSA is the cause
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if recurrent folliculitis despite treatment, suspected pityrosporum folliculitis, or deep folliculitis/furuncles needing drainage
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed; consider if carbuncle/large abscess requiring surgical drainage, systemic toxicity, or immunocompromised child
notes: Most mild folliculitis resolves spontaneously with hygiene measures. Culture swab if MRSA suspected; TMP-SMX or clindamycin may be needed.

first_line_drugs.notes: Move child to cool environment; remove excess clothing; allow skin to air dry
Apply to affected area 3-4x daily (avoid face/genitals); for children >2 yr; soothes itch
Bathe every 2-3 hours without soap; allow air drying; reduces skin temperature

Apply thin layer to affected area to prevent ductal obstruction in recurrent miliaria

counselling: Move child to a cool, well-ventilated room - this is the most important treatment
Dress in loose, light cotton clothing; avoid overdressing the baby
Keep room temperature comfortable; use fan or air conditioner if available
Bathe with lukewarm water 2-3 times daily without soap; pat dry gently
Calamine lotion can be applied for comfort; no other medicines are usually needed
referral_criteria: Referral usually not needed; refer to dermatologist if miliaria profunda (deep, persistent variant) or recurrent severe episodes
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not needed for miliaria; consider only if secondary bacterial infection (miliaria pustulosa) with systemic symptoms
notes: Miliaria resolves in 2-3 days with cooling alone; pharmacotherapy rarely needed. Ensure lightweight cotton clothing and adequate ventilation in warm environments.

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to depigmented areas BID for >=6 months; first-line for facial vitiligo; >=2 yr
Apply to affected area BID; first-line alternative TCI for vitiligo; >=2 yr
Apply to depigmented patches daily for 3-6 months; medium-potency TCS for trunk/limbs

2-3x/week for 6-12 months; combine with topical TCI/TCS for enhanced repigmentation
Apply to hyperpigmented area BID for 8-12 weeks; for post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation
Apply BID for 3-6 months; for melasma/PIH; well tolerated in children

investigations.name: CBC with differential

investigations.indication: Enhances depigmented patches for diagnosis and extent mapping

investigations.indication: Autoimmune thyroid screening in vitiligo patients

counselling: Vitiligo treatment takes 6-12 months to show results - be patient and consistent
Apply topical cream as directed; combine with sun exposure (10-15 min) for better results
Use sunscreen on depigmented areas to prevent sunburn and protect from further damage
Vitiligo is not contagious and does not cause physical harm
Emotional and psychological support for the child is very important; counselling may help
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist for rapidly spreading vitiligo, facial/acral involvement, or consideration for phototherapy/systemic therapy; refer to ophthalmologist for associated eye involvement
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not needed for pigmentation disorders
notes: Early aggressive treatment of vitiligo in children (<12 yr onset) yields better outcomes. Topical calcineurin inhibitors are preferred over steroids for facial lesions due to lower atrophy risk.
guideline_changes: Ruxolitinib 1.5% cream (Opzelura) approved for vitiligo >=12 yr in 2022

first_line_drugs.notes: FMF: <5yr 0.5mg/day, 5-10yr 1mg/day, >10yr 1.5mg/day PO; PFAPA: same dosing
PFAPA: 1-2 mg/kg PO single dose at flare onset; aborts episode rapidly
1-2 mg/kg/day SC (max 100mg); for colchicine-resistant FMF or CAPS; age >=8mo

second_line_drugs.notes: 2-4 mg/kg SC q4-8wks (max 300mg); for CAPS age >=2yr and refractory FMF
Loading: 4.4 mg/kg SC then 2.2 mg/kg SC weekly (max 320mg); CAPS age >=2yr
Surgical; definitive for PFAPA if medical therapy fails; high cure rate

investigations.name: CBC, ESR, CRP, SAA during fever
investigations.indication: Markedly elevated during attacks; SAA for FMF monitoring

investigations.name: 24-hour urine protein
investigations.indication: Screen for AA amyloidosis in FMF

investigations.name: Ferritin
investigations.indication: Screen for MAS if very high during flare

counselling: Periodic fever syndromes cause regular episodes of high fever lasting a few days each time
Colchicine must be taken daily to prevent attacks and protect the kidneys (in FMF)
For PFAPA, a single dose of steroid can stop a fever episode within hours
Keep a fever diary documenting frequency, duration, and associated symptoms
Genetic testing may help confirm the exact type of periodic fever syndrome
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric rheumatologist for all suspected autoinflammatory syndromes; genetics for confirmation
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe disease flares with serositis, very high inflammatory markers, suspected MAS, or diagnostic workup requiring serial sampling
notes: Colchicine is lifelong for FMF to prevent amyloidosis. IL-1 blockade is highly effective for CAPS and colchicine-resistant autoinflammatory syndromes.

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/m2/wk PO (preferred) or SC, max 25mg/wk; with folic acid 1mg/day
5-10 mg/kg/dose PO BID, max 500mg BID; for mild oligo-JIA
20mg SC q2wks (<30kg) or 40mg SC q2wks (>=30kg); first-line biologic
1 mg/kg intra-articular (max 40mg large joint, 20mg small); for oligoarthritis

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.8 mg/kg SC weekly (max 50mg) or 0.4 mg/kg SC 2x/wk
sJIA: 8-12 mg/kg IV q2wks (<30kg) or 8mg/kg q2wks (>=30kg)
10 mg/kg IV q4wks (max 1g) for polyarticular JIA
6 mg/kg IV load then 3 mg/kg q3days; for MAS in sJIA refractory to steroids

investigations.name: RF and anti-CCP antibodies
investigations.indication: Classify JIA subtype and predict erosive disease

counselling: Juvenile arthritis is a condition where your child's immune system causes joint inflammation
Medicines are needed for a long time (years) to control the disease and prevent joint damage
Regular exercise and physiotherapy are very important to keep joints flexible and muscles strong
Eye checkups every 3-6 months are essential as arthritis can silently affect the eyes (uveitis)
Your child can and should attend school and participate in activities with appropriate support
referral_criteria: All JIA cases should be managed by pediatric rheumatologist; ophthalmology screening for uveitis; orthopedics for joint damage
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for macrophage activation syndrome (MAS), severe systemic JIA flare with serositis, or for IV biologic infusions if no outpatient infusion center
notes: Early DMARD/biologic initiation improves inactive disease rates significantly (83% if biologic started within 6mo). Screen all sJIA patients for lung disease.
guideline_changes: Draft 2025 ACR update: prefer oral MTX, earlier biologics, less NSAID/steroid reliance; emapalumab approved 2025 for MAS

first_line_drugs.notes: 5-10 mg/kg/dose PO BID (max 500mg BID) for symptomatic relief
10 mg/kg/dose PO q6-8h (max 40mg/kg/day or 2400mg/day)
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO short course for moderate inflammation, taper over 2-4 wks

second_line_drugs.notes: 30-50 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 2g/day); for reactive arthritis
10-15 mg/m2/wk PO/SC if persistent >3mo; with folic acid supplementation
1 mg/kg per joint (max 40mg large, 20mg small) for monoarticular disease

investigations.name: Joint aspiration (synovial fluid analysis)
investigations.indication: Rule out septic arthritis if acute monoarthritis with fever

counselling: Your child's joint pain and swelling need investigation to find the exact cause
Anti-inflammatory medicines (NSAIDs) will help with pain but the underlying cause must be treated
If the joint is hot, red, and very painful, infection must be ruled out urgently
Rest the affected joint but encourage gentle movement once pain improves
Most reactive arthritis in children resolves completely within a few months
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric rheumatologist if arthritis persists >6 weeks; urgent orthopedic referral if septic arthritis suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if septic arthritis suspected (hot, swollen joint with fever), for joint aspiration, or if unable to bear weight
notes: Rule out septic arthritis before immunosuppressive therapy. Most reactive arthritis in children resolves within 3-12 months with NSAIDs alone.

first_line_drugs.notes: 2 g/kg single infusion over 10-12h, within first 10 days of fever onset
30-50 mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses during acute phase, then 3-5 mg/kg/day once afebrile

second_line_drugs.notes: 5 mg/kg single dose, for IVIG-resistant Kawasaki disease
For high-risk IVIG-resistant cases per Kobayashi/Egami scoring

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: At diagnosis, 2 weeks and 6 weeks; assess coronary arteries

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Thrombocytosis, leukocytosis, anaemia in Kawasaki disease

investigations.name: CRP and ESR
investigations.indication: Inflammatory markers to guide treatment response

investigations.name: Liver function tests
investigations.indication: Hepatic involvement and albumin level (low albumin = IVIG resistance risk)

investigations.name: Urinalysis
investigations.indication: Sterile pyuria common in Kawasaki disease

counselling: This is a serious condition affecting blood vessels of the heart - treatment must start urgently
IVIG infusion may cause fever and headache; this is expected and managed in hospital
Give aspirin as prescribed - this prevents blood clots in heart vessels
Avoid live vaccines (MMR, varicella) for 11 months after IVIG treatment
Regular follow-up echocardiograms at 2 weeks and 6 weeks are essential to check heart arteries
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist immediately on diagnosis for echocardiography and co-management; ongoing follow-up for coronary artery monitoring
hospitalisation_criteria: All children with suspected Kawasaki disease should be hospitalised for IVIG infusion; emergent admission if coronary artery aneurysm, IVIG-resistant fever, shock/hemodynamic instability (Kawasaki shock syndrome)
notes: Echocardiogram at diagnosis, 2 weeks and 6 weeks. Coronary artery aneurysm is the key complication.

first_line_drugs.notes: 5 mg/kg/day PO (max 400mg/day); recommended for ALL SLE patients long-term
0.5-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60mg); taper rapidly once controlled; minimize cumulative dose
600 mg/m2/dose PO BID (max 1.5g BID); preferred for lupus nephritis induction
10 mg/kg IV q4wks or 200mg SC weekly; age >=5yr; add-on to standard therapy

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-3 mg/kg/day PO for maintenance after nephritis induction; check TPMT first
500-1000 mg/m2 IV monthly x6 for severe nephritis or CNS lupus
375 mg/m2 IV weekly x4 or 750 mg/m2 x2 doses; for refractory disease
23.7mg PO BID (adult dose); approved for lupus nephritis; limited peds data

investigations.name: ANA and anti-dsDNA antibodies
investigations.indication: Confirm diagnosis and monitor disease activity

investigations.name: Renal biopsy
investigations.indication: If proteinuria or active urine sediment (nephritis)

counselling: Lupus is a chronic autoimmune condition that needs lifelong treatment and monitoring
Hydroxychloroquine is a very important medicine that should be continued long-term
Strict sun protection is essential: sunscreen daily, avoid midday sun, wear protective clothing
Report any new symptoms: face rash, joint pain, mouth sores, swelling, blood in urine
Regular blood and urine tests are needed to detect organ involvement early
referral_criteria: All pediatric SLE should be managed by pediatric rheumatologist; nephrology for lupus nephritis; hematology for severe cytopenias
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for lupus nephritis flare, severe cytopenias, CNS lupus, serositis, pulmonary hemorrhage, or MAS
notes: Hydroxychloroquine is cornerstone therapy for all pediatric SLE. Monitor growth velocity and pubertal development given cumulative steroid exposure risk.
guideline_changes: ACR 2025: uniform HCQ use, limit glucocorticoid duration, early biologic/DMARD introduction

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60mg) or IV methylprednisolone 30mg/kg (max 1g) x3 days
15-20 mg/m2/wk PO or SC (max 40mg/wk) with folic acid 1mg/day
2 g/kg IV divided over 2-5 days, monthly for moderate-severe disease

second_line_drugs.notes: 375 mg/m2 IV weekly x4 or 750 mg/m2 (max 1g) x2 doses 2wks apart
600 mg/m2/dose PO BID (max 1.5g BID) for steroid-sparing
10 mg/kg IV q4wks (max 1g); for refractory JDM
5mg PO BID or weight-based; emerging for refractory cases

investigations.name: CK, LDH, AST, aldolase
investigations.indication: Muscle enzyme levels to assess disease activity

investigations.name: MRI muscles (thighs)
investigations.indication: Assess muscle inflammation pattern and guide biopsy

investigations.name: Myositis-specific antibodies (MSA panel)
investigations.indication: Classify subtype and predict organ involvement

counselling: Dermatomyositis is an autoimmune condition where the immune system attacks muscles and skin
Medicines to suppress the immune system are needed for months to years - do not stop without doctor advice
Strict sun protection is essential: sunscreen SPF 50+, full sleeves, hat, and avoid midday sun
Regular gentle exercise and physiotherapy help maintain muscle strength
Blood tests are needed every few weeks to monitor disease activity and medicine side effects
referral_criteria: All cases should be managed by pediatric rheumatologist; dermatology co-management for skin disease; pulmonology if ILD suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe muscle weakness (unable to sit/stand), dysphagia, respiratory muscle weakness, pulmonary involvement, or for IV pulse steroid therapy
notes: Combine corticosteroids with MTX from onset to improve outcomes and reduce steroid dependence. Sun protection and physical therapy are essential adjuncts.
guideline_changes: JAK inhibitors emerging for refractory JDM; treat-to-target recommendations published 2025

diagnosis_name: Other systemic involvement of connective tissue

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg/dose PO q6-8h (max 40mg/kg/day); up to 1/3 of MCTD controlled with NSAIDs
5 mg/kg/day PO (max 400mg/day); for arthritis, skin, and constitutional symptoms
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO for moderate disease; taper as tolerated over weeks

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/m2/wk PO/SC for arthritis or myositis component; with folic acid
600 mg/m2/dose PO BID (max 1.5g BID) for organ-threatening features
0.25-0.5 mg/kg/dose PO TID (max 3mg/kg/day) for Raynaud phenomenon
1-3 mg/kg/day PO daily for steroid-sparing maintenance

counselling: Mixed connective tissue disease affects multiple body systems and needs long-term treatment
Take all medicines regularly as prescribed; do not stop without doctor advice
Cold weather can cause fingers to turn white/blue (Raynaud phenomenon) - keep hands warm with gloves
Regular blood tests and checkups are needed to monitor for organ involvement
Report any new symptoms like breathlessness, chest pain, or skin changes to your doctor
referral_criteria: All cases should be managed by pediatric rheumatologist; pulmonology for ILD or pulmonary hypertension screening; cardiology if cardiac involvement
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for organ-threatening disease (renal crisis, pulmonary hypertension, severe myositis), or disease flares not responding to outpatient therapy
notes: Treatment is tailored to the dominant clinical phenotype (lupus-like, scleroderma-like, myositis-like). Monitor for pulmonary hypertension and ILD as major complications.
snomed_display: [X]Other systemic atrophy affecting the central nervous system in neoplastic disease classified elsewhere

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60mg) x4-6wks then slow taper; or IV pulse 30mg/kg (max 1g) x3d
15-20 mg/m2/wk SC (max 40mg/wk) with folic acid 1mg/day; start at disease onset
2 g/kg IV divided over 2-5 days monthly for moderate-severe or refractory disease

second_line_drugs.notes: 600 mg/m2/dose PO BID (max 1.5g BID) as steroid-sparing agent
375 mg/m2 IV weekly x4; for refractory myositis with autoantibodies
1-3 mg/kg/day PO daily for maintenance; check TPMT before starting
5mg PO BID (or weight-based); emerging for refractory inflammatory myopathy

investigations.name: CK, LDH, AST, aldolase
investigations.indication: Muscle enzyme levels to confirm myositis and track activity

investigations.name: MRI muscles
investigations.indication: Assess inflammation pattern and guide biopsy site

investigations.name: EMG/NCS
investigations.indication: Confirm myopathic pattern and exclude neuropathy

counselling: Myositis means inflammation of muscles; treatment needs to start early and continue for a long time
Steroid medicine is essential initially - do not stop without doctor advice as the disease can flare
Regular blood tests are needed to monitor muscle enzymes and medicine side effects
Gentle physiotherapy helps maintain strength; avoid very intense exercise during active disease
Sun protection is important if skin rash is present (dermatomyositis)
referral_criteria: All inflammatory myositis should be managed by pediatric rheumatologist; pulmonology if respiratory muscle weakness or ILD
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe weakness (unable to stand/sit), dysphagia, respiratory muscle involvement, or for IV pulse steroid therapy
notes: Combine steroids with methotrexate from onset to achieve steroid-free remission. Steroid taper is slow (1-2 years) to prevent relapse; monitor CK and muscle strength.
guideline_changes: JAK inhibitors emerging as option for refractory inflammatory myositis

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg/dose PO q6-8h (max 40mg/kg/day or 2400mg/day); age >=3mo
15 mg/kg/dose PO q4-6h (max 75mg/kg/day or 4g/day); if NSAIDs contraindicated
5-7 mg/kg/dose PO BID (max 500mg BID); for older children >=2yr

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg/kg/day PO divided TID (max 150mg/day); age >=1yr
Stretching, gentle strengthening, heat/ice; non-pharmacologic first-line adjunct

investigations.name: CBC, ESR, CRP
investigations.indication: Assess for underlying inflammation or infection

investigations.name: Urinalysis
investigations.indication: Check for myoglobinuria if rhabdomyolysis suspected

counselling: Muscle pain in children usually gets better with rest and pain medicine within a few days
Give ibuprofen or paracetamol as directed for pain relief
If muscle pain follows a viral illness, it is usually benign and self-limiting
Encourage gentle stretching and movement once pain improves
Return if pain is severe, not improving in a week, or if weakness, rash, or dark urine develops
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric rheumatologist if myalgia persists >2 weeks, associated with weakness or elevated CK, or suspected inflammatory myopathy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe myalgia with elevated CK suggesting rhabdomyolysis, dark/cola-colored urine, or suspected inflammatory myositis with systemic features
notes: Investigate underlying cause before chronic NSAID use; limit OTC NSAID to 10 days without physician review. NSAIDs reduce opioid requirements significantly in pediatric pain.

first_line_drugs.notes: 150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 6g/day) for 3-4 weeks
40 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 2.7g/day); use if MRSA prevalence >=10%
150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 12g/day); low MRSA areas

second_line_drugs.notes: 60 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 4g/day); for confirmed MRSA
100-150 mg/kg/day PO divided q8h (max 4g/day); oral switch

investigations.name: MRI of affected bone
investigations.indication: Gold standard for extent of infection

counselling: Your child has a bone infection that needs several weeks of antibiotics to clear completely
Do not stop antibiotics early even if the child feels better — incomplete treatment causes relapse
Keep the affected limb rested and elevated initially
Return if fever returns, swelling worsens, or the child refuses to use the limb
Follow-up blood tests (CRP) will be done to confirm the infection is clearing
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric orthopedic surgeon if abscess needs drainage, if septic arthritis coexists, or if chronic osteomyelitis with sequestrum
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for IV antibiotics (initial 3-5 days minimum), surgical drainage if abscess present, sepsis, or inability to tolerate oral antibiotics
notes: S. aureus is most common pathogen; Kingella kingae predominates in children <3yr. Transition to oral therapy guided by clinical response, typically after 3-5 days IV.
guideline_changes: 4-month shorter regimens now considered for non-severe pediatric cases

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg IV/PO q6-12h for edema and fluid overload; avoid high doses if oliguric
0.06-0.3 mg/kg/day PO once daily for hypertension; max 5-10 mg/day based on age
Restrict fluid to insensible losses + urine output during acute phase; low-salt diet

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.25-0.5 mg/kg PO q6-8h for hypertensive urgency; short-acting for acute BP control
250-500 mg PO BID-TID for 10 days if active strep infection; eradication only, does not alter GN course
IV pulse 30 mg/kg (max 1g) x3 days for rapidly progressive/crescentic disease only

investigations.name: Urinalysis with microscopy
investigations.indication: Dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts confirm glomerulonephritis

investigations.name: Serum complement C3 and C4
investigations.indication: Low C3 with normal C4 typical of APSGN

counselling: Child's kidneys are inflamed, usually after a throat or skin infection
Restrict salt and water intake during the swelling phase
Blood pressure medicine and water pills will help control swelling and BP
This condition usually heals completely in 2-4 weeks
Follow up with urine and blood tests; protein in urine should disappear within months
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist if rapidly progressive course, persistent low complement >8 weeks, or nephrotic-range proteinuria
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if hypertensive emergency, significant fluid overload, oliguria, or rapidly deteriorating renal function
notes: APSGN is self-limiting; supportive care with diuretics and antihypertensives is mainstay. Immunosuppression reserved for crescentic disease on biopsy.
guideline_changes: Updated CDC clinical guidelines for post-streptococcal GN published

first_line_drugs.notes: IV pulse 30 mg/kg/day (max 1 g) for 3 consecutive days; then oral pred 1-2 mg/kg/day taper
IV pulse 500-750 mg/m2 monthly x6 or PO 2 mg/kg/day x8-12 wk; for induction

second_line_drugs.notes: 375 mg/m2 IV weekly x4 for ANCA-associated RPGN; non-inferior to cyclophosphamide
5-7 exchanges over 7-14 days for anti-GBM disease or severe ANCA vasculitis with DAH
1200 mg/m2/day PO divided BID for maintenance after induction; alternative to azathioprine
1-2 mg/kg/day PO for maintenance therapy after induction remission achieved

investigations.name: Renal biopsy
investigations.indication: Urgent; crescentic GN classification guides therapy

investigations.name: Serum creatinine (serial)
investigations.indication: Monitor rate of renal function decline

counselling: Child has rapidly worsening kidney disease that needs urgent treatment
Strong medicines to suppress the immune system are needed to stop kidney damage
Kidney biopsy (taking a small piece of kidney with a needle) is needed to guide treatment
Treatment requires close monitoring with frequent blood tests
With early and aggressive treatment, kidney function can be preserved in many cases
referral_criteria: Urgent referral to pediatric nephrologist; renal biopsy within 48 hours
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require admission for urgent renal biopsy, pulse immunosuppression, and monitoring
notes: Urgent renal biopsy is essential to guide therapy. Aggressive induction immunosuppression has markedly improved outcomes; dialysis-dependent AKI predicts poor prognosis.
guideline_changes: Rituximab increasingly used in pediatric ANCA-associated RPGN

first_line_drugs.notes: Serial urinalysis q3-6mo; monitor BP, proteinuria, serum creatinine; reassurance if isolated
Enalapril 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO if proteinuria >0.5 g/day develops; renoprotective

second_line_drugs.notes: Losartan 0.7-1.4 mg/kg/day PO if ACEi not tolerated; for proteinuric hematuria
Adjunctive therapy for IgA nephropathy with proteinuria >1 g/day; per KDIGO GN guidelines

investigations.name: Urine calcium/creatinine ratio
investigations.indication: Hypercalciuria is common cause of isolated hematuria

investigations.name: Serum creatinine, C3, C4
investigations.indication: Assess renal function and complement levels

investigations.name: Family urine screening
investigations.indication: Thin basement membrane disease and Alport syndrome

counselling: Blood in urine is being investigated; most causes in children are not dangerous
Regular check-ups with urine tests every 3-6 months are needed
If protein starts appearing in urine or blood pressure rises, medicine may be needed
Vigorous exercise may temporarily worsen visible blood in urine; this is usually harmless
Report any swelling of face/legs, tea-colored urine, or high BP immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist if proteinuria develops, hypertension, family history of renal disease, or hearing loss (Alport syndrome)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if gross hematuria with clots causing retention, or associated nephritic syndrome features
notes: Isolated microscopic hematuria without proteinuria has good prognosis. Refer to nephrology if proteinuria, hypertension, or family history of renal disease.

first_line_drugs.notes: 2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) daily x4 wk, then 1.5 mg/kg (max 50 mg) alt-day x4 wk
Albumin 1 g/kg IV over 2-4h with furosemide 1 mg/kg IV for severe edema/anasarca

second_line_drugs.notes: 2 mg/kg/day PO for 8-12 wk for frequent relapsers; cumulative max 168 mg/kg
4-6 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; for steroid-dependent or steroid-resistant NS; monitor trough levels
1200 mg/m2/day PO divided BID (max 2 g/day); for SDNS/FRNS; continue >=12 months
375 mg/m2 IV x1-2 doses; for SDNS refractory to standard steroid-sparing agents

investigations.name: Complement C3, C4
investigations.indication: Normal in MCNS; low suggests other glomerulopathy

counselling: Child's kidneys are leaking protein into urine causing body swelling
Steroid medicine (prednisolone) is the main treatment; follow the dose schedule carefully
Check urine with dipstick daily at home; record results in a diary
Restrict salt in diet during active disease; no need to restrict protein
Relapses are common (60-70% of children relapse); come back if swelling returns or urine shows protein
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist if steroid-resistant (no remission by 4 weeks), frequent relapses (>2/6 months), or steroid-dependent
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe edema/anasarca, respiratory distress from pleural effusion, peritonitis, or thrombosis
notes: 85-90% of children achieve remission with steroids. Steroid-sparing agents selected based on toxicity profile, adherence, and relapse pattern.
guideline_changes: KDIGO 2025 first dedicated guideline for nephrotic syndrome in children; rituximab now recommended

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg IV/PO q6-12h for edema and volume overload; monitor electrolytes
0.06-0.3 mg/kg/day PO once daily for hypertension; max 5-10 mg/day
Fluid and sodium restriction; monitor renal function, electrolytes, complement levels

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO for persistent proteinuria or hypertension; renoprotective
1-2 mg/kg/day PO if biopsy shows crescentic or proliferative GN requiring immunosuppression

investigations.name: Urinalysis with microscopy
investigations.indication: RBC casts, dysmorphic RBCs confirm glomerular disease

investigations.name: Renal biopsy
investigations.indication: If atypical features or not improving as expected

counselling: Child's kidneys are inflamed; the exact cause is being investigated
Restrict salt and water during the swelling phase
Blood pressure and kidney function will be monitored closely
Additional tests including kidney biopsy may be needed to find the exact cause
Most cases in children respond well to treatment once the cause is identified
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist for full evaluation; renal biopsy if not typical APSGN
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if hypertensive emergency, significant fluid overload, or rapidly deteriorating renal function
notes: Establish specific diagnosis via serology and biopsy when possible. Treatment is primarily supportive unless biopsy reveals disease requiring immunosuppression.

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-75 mg/kg IV/IM daily (max 2 g) for initial empiric therapy; switch to oral when afebrile x24-48h
8 mg/kg PO daily (max 400 mg) for outpatient oral therapy; 10-14 day total course
25-45 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for 10-14 days; oral alternative for outpatient treatment

second_line_drugs.notes: Ampicillin 100 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h + gentamicin 5-7.5 mg/kg/day IV; for neonates/young infants
10-20 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 750 mg/dose); reserve for resistant organisms in older children

investigations.name: CBC, CRP
investigations.indication: Elevated WBC and CRP support pyelonephritis

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Rule out obstruction, abscess; assess kidney size

investigations.name: VCUG
investigations.indication: After first febrile UTI in child <2 years per AAP

investigations.name: DMSA scan
investigations.indication: Detect renal scarring 4-6 months after acute infection

counselling: Child has a kidney infection with fever that needs antibiotic treatment for 10-14 days
Complete the full antibiotic course even if child feels better
Ensure child drinks plenty of fluids
If child has recurrent infections, further tests will be done to check for kidney problems
Toilet training and regular voiding habits are important to prevent future infections
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist/urologist for first febrile UTI in child <2 years; recurrent pyelonephritis; or abnormal imaging
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if age <2 months, toxic-appearing, unable to tolerate oral medication, or immunocompromised
notes: Oral therapy is equivalent to IV for non-toxic children >2 months. Obtain urine culture before antibiotics; VCUG if febrile UTI in child <2 years.
guideline_changes: AAP 2024 Redbook supports shorter 5-10 day courses for uncomplicated cases

diagnosis_name: Tubulo-interstitial nephritis, not specified as acute or chronic

first_line_drugs.notes: Identify and discontinue causative drug (NSAIDs, antibiotics, PPIs); treat underlying infection if present
1 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) for 4-6 wk then gradual taper; for drug-induced or immune-mediated ATIN

second_line_drugs.notes: 600-1200 mg/m2/day PO divided BID for steroid-refractory TIN; emerging evidence
BP control, fluid/electrolyte management; monitor renal function for recovery; low-salt diet

investigations.name: Renal biopsy
investigations.indication: If diagnosis uncertain or not improving with drug withdrawal

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Assess kidney size; enlarged in acute TIN

counselling: Child's kidney is inflamed, most likely from a medicine; the causative medicine must be stopped
Kidney function usually recovers once the medicine is stopped
Steroid medicine may be given to help the kidney heal faster
Blood tests will be repeated regularly to ensure kidney function is improving
Always inform doctors about this drug reaction to avoid the medicine in future
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist if renal function not recovering after stopping offending agent, or renal biopsy needed
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if AKI, significant electrolyte disturbance, or need for renal biopsy
notes: Removing the offending agent is most important step; most cases recover spontaneously. Steroids may accelerate recovery but evidence from RCTs is limited.
guideline_changes: Role of mycophenolate mofetil emerging for steroid-refractory TIN

first_line_drugs.notes: TMP-SMX 2 mg/kg PO daily or nitrofurantoin 1-2 mg/kg PO daily for VUR grade III-V or post-UTI
Treat bladder-bowel dysfunction with timed voiding, constipation management before surgical intervention

second_line_drugs.notes: Deflux (dextranomer/HA) subureteral injection for VUR grade III-IV; 70-80% success per injection
Open or robotic-assisted surgical correction for high-grade VUR refractory to conservative management
0.1-0.2 mg/kg PO BID-TID if associated overactive bladder; treat BBD concomitantly

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Assess for hydronephrosis and renal size

counselling: Child's urine flows backward from the bladder to the kidneys which can cause infections
Daily low-dose antibiotic prevents urinary infections while waiting for reflux to resolve
Treat constipation and ensure regular toilet habits as these affect reflux
Many children outgrow reflux by age 5-6 years; surgery is only for severe cases
Bring child for urine test if there is fever without obvious source
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist for high-grade VUR (IV-V), recurrent febrile UTIs despite prophylaxis, or renal scarring
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for febrile UTI, post-surgical management, or acute pyelonephritis
notes: Risk-based approach: low-grade VUR often resolves spontaneously. Treat BBD before considering surgery; antibiotic prophylaxis for recurrent febrile UTIs.
guideline_changes: 2024 EAU/ESPU VUR chapter updated with risk-based management approach

first_line_drugs.notes: Serial renal US q3-6mo; ~50% resolve spontaneously; SFU grade 1-2 typically managed conservatively
Amoxicillin 10-15 mg/kg PO daily or TMP-SMX 2 mg/kg PO daily for high-grade HN to prevent UTI

second_line_drugs.notes: Open or laparoscopic repair for UPJ obstruction with declining function or SFU grade 3-4
Diuretic renography to assess drainage and differential renal function; guides surgical decision

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound (serial)
investigations.indication: Monitor APD and SFU grade q3-6 months

investigations.name: MAG3 diuretic renogram
investigations.indication: Assess drainage and differential renal function for SFU 3-4

investigations.name: VCUG
investigations.indication: If UTI occurs to rule out vesicoureteral reflux

investigations.name: Urine culture
investigations.indication: If febrile illness to detect UTI early

counselling: Baby's kidney is swollen with urine because of slow drainage; this was found on ultrasound
Many cases improve on their own without surgery; regular ultrasound monitoring is needed
Antibiotic medicine may be given daily to prevent urinary infections
Surgery is only needed if the kidney function is getting worse
Keep all follow-up ultrasound appointments every 3-6 months
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist if SFU grade 3-4, declining function on renogram, or febrile UTI
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for febrile UTI with hydronephrosis or postoperatively after pyeloplasty
notes: About half of hydronephrosis cases resolve spontaneously. Surgery indicated for febrile UTIs, deteriorating hydronephrosis, or declining renal function on renogram.

first_line_drugs.notes: Assess volume status; restrict fluids to insensible losses + urine output if oliguric; avoid fluid overload
1-2 mg/kg IV bolus or 0.1-0.4 mg/kg/h infusion; convert oliguric to non-oliguric AKI if euvolemic
Calcium gluconate 100 mg/kg IV, insulin-glucose, kayexalate for hyperkalemia; bicarb for acidosis

second_line_drugs.notes: Continuous renal replacement therapy for fluid overload >10%, refractory electrolyte/acid-base derangement
Preferred RRT modality in small children; for AKI refractory to conservative measures
0.2 mg/kg IV once for tumor lysis syndrome-associated AKI; prevents uric acid nephropathy

investigations.name: Serum creatinine (serial)
investigations.indication: Monitor trend; staging per pRIFLE criteria

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes (K, Na, Ca, PO4)
investigations.indication: Hyperkalemia is life-threatening; frequent monitoring needed

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Assess kidney size and rule out obstruction

counselling: Child's kidneys have suddenly stopped working properly; this needs urgent treatment
Fluid intake and urine output will be carefully measured
Blood tests will be checked frequently; some may show high potassium which needs immediate treatment
Dialysis (blood cleaning machine) may be needed if kidneys do not recover quickly
Most children recover kidney function if the cause is treated early
referral_criteria: Immediate referral to pediatric nephrologist; ICU if hemodynamically unstable or needing dialysis
hospitalisation_criteria: All AKI cases require admission; ICU for dialysis, hyperkalemia, fluid overload >10%, or metabolic acidosis
notes: Identify and treat underlying cause promptly. Avoid nephrotoxins; adjust all renally-cleared drug doses; fluid overload >10% associated with poor outcomes.
guideline_changes: Pediatric-specific AKI biomarkers (NGAL, KIM-1) increasingly used for early detection

diagnosis_name: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) with Anuric / Oligoanuric Regimen

first_line_drugs.notes: Use as maintenance fluid for insensible water loss in anuric AKI. Daily fluid = 400 mL/m²/day or about 40% of total daily fluid requirement in infants.
Replace previous 24-hour urine output mL for mL in addition to insensible loss calculation.
Add to replacement fluid only if hypokalemia is present and serum potassium is monitored.

second_line_drugs.notes: May be considered only in selected fluid-overloaded patients with residual renal responsiveness; not routine in established anuric AKI.
Indicated if fluid restriction fails or if refractory hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, uremic symptoms, or fluid overload develops.

investigations.name: Serum Electrolytes (Na, K, Cl, HCO3)
investigations.indication: Assess hyperkalemia, hyponatremia, acidosis, and fluid prescription safety

investigations.name: Urine Output Charting
investigations.indication: Needed for fluid replacement calculation and AKI severity monitoring

investigations.name: Body Weight
investigations.indication: Daily fluid balance and fluid overload assessment

investigations.name: ABG / VBG
investigations.indication: If metabolic acidosis or respiratory compromise suspected

investigations.name: Ultrasound KUB
investigations.indication: Evaluate cause of AKI and exclude obstruction

counselling: In anuric AKI, fluid intake must be carefully restricted to avoid overload. — पेशाब बंद होने पर पानी और तरल पदार्थ सीमित रखना बहुत जरूरी है।
Daily fluid is calculated from insensible loss plus measured urine output. — रोज़ाना का पानी शरीर की ज़रूरत और पेशाब की मात्रा से तय होता है।
Do not add potassium unless blood potassium is low and the doctor advises it. — जब तक डॉक्टर न कहें, पोटैशियम न दें।
Strict intake-output charting and daily weight are essential. — पानी का हिसाब और रोज़ वजन लेना बहुत जरूरी है।
Dialysis may be needed if kidney function does not improve or complications appear. — अगर किडनी ठीक नहीं होती तो डायलिसिस की जरूरत पड़ सकती है।
warning_signs: No urine or very low urine output
Palpitations or weakness due to hyperkalemia
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric nephrologist / intensivist if persistent anuria, rising creatinine, refractory electrolyte imbalance, severe acidosis, hypertension, or fluid overload.
hospitalisation_criteria: All children with anuric AKI need admission for strict monitoring, IV fluid regulation, and serial biochemical assessment.
notes: Daily fluid requirement in anuric AKI = insensible water loss + previous 24-hour urine output. Insensible water loss may be estimated as 400 mL/m²/day. BSA formula: ([4 x weight in kg] + 7) / (90 + weight in kg). Add KCl only if hypokalemia is present.
source: Provided pediatric anuric regimen in AKI protocol
expected_course: Fluid prescription is revised every 24 hours based on urine output, weight, and labs. Improvement depends on underlying cause of AKI; some children may progress to dialysis if complications develop.
key_clinical_points: In anuric AKI, fluid overload prevention is the main goal.
Use insensible loss plus urine output, not full maintenance, for daily fluid prescription.
Potassium should not be routinely added unless hypokalemia is documented.

severity_assessment.mild: Reduced urine output with stable electrolytes and no fluid overload.
severity_assessment.moderate: Oliguria/anuria with rising creatinine or mild electrolyte / fluid imbalance.
severity_assessment.severe: Persistent anuria, fluid overload, severe acidosis, hyperkalemia, hypertension, or uremic symptoms.

monitoring_parameters.frequency: 12-24 hourly or more often if unstable

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO daily; ACEi for proteinuria and BP control; target BP <50th percentile
50 U/kg SC/IV 3x/wk for CKD anemia; target Hb 11-12 g/dL; supplement iron first
Phosphate binder; dose titrated to normalize serum phosphorus; give with meals

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.05 mg/kg/day SC for CKD-related growth failure; per KDOQI; continue until target height or transplant
Vitamin D 1000-2000 IU/day PO; calcitriol 0.01-0.05 mcg/kg/day if secondary hyperparathyroidism
Non-calcium phosphate binder; 400-800 mg PO TID with meals if calcium-based binders insufficient
0.45 mcg/kg SC/IV q2-4wk; longer-acting ESA alternative to erythropoietin

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Anemia of CKD; guide EPO therapy

counselling: Child's kidneys are not working fully; this is a long-term condition needing regular care
Blood pressure medicine (ACE inhibitor) protects the remaining kidney function
Follow the dietary advice: reduce salt, ensure adequate protein (not too much or too little)
Growth may be slow; growth hormone injection may help
Keep all follow-up appointments; blood and urine tests are needed every 3-6 months
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist at diagnosis; dietitian, and transplant team for CKD stage 4-5
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for hyperkalemia, severe fluid overload, uremic symptoms, or dialysis initiation
notes: Multidisciplinary CKD clinic follow-up essential. Preterm and SGA infants at increased CKD risk; early RAS blockade for proteinuria slows progression.
guideline_changes: KDIGO 2024 major update with dedicated pediatric chapter including growth and development focus

first_line_drugs.notes: Urgent workup: renal US, electrolytes, urinalysis, serology; classify as AKI vs CKD to guide therapy
1-2 mg/kg IV/PO for fluid overload; dose adjust based on renal function response
Correct hyperkalemia, acidosis, hyperphosphatemia; restrict potassium and phosphorus intake

second_line_drugs.notes: PD or HD if conservative measures fail; PD preferred in young children; preemptive transplant evaluation
0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO if CKD with proteinuria/HTN confirmed; avoid in AKI with hemodynamic instability

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Kidney size differentiates AKI (normal/enlarged) from CKD (small)

investigations.name: Urinalysis
investigations.indication: Casts, protein, and blood guide etiology

counselling: Child's kidneys are not working well; the doctor is investigating whether this is sudden or long-standing
Blood tests and urine tests will help determine the cause and best treatment
Fluid and diet may need to be restricted temporarily
If kidney function does not improve, dialysis or transplant may be discussed
Regular follow-up with the kidney specialist is essential
referral_criteria: Urgent referral to pediatric nephrologist for workup and classification
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for electrolyte derangements, fluid overload, uremic symptoms, or need for dialysis
notes: Priority is distinguishing AKI from CKD via renal US (kidney size) and history. Multidisciplinary team approach with nephrology, dietitian, and social work.

first_line_drugs.notes: IV NS bolus then maintenance fluids; ketorolac 0.5 mg/kg IV (max 30 mg) for acute colic
Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy for stones <2 cm; preferred first-line for most pediatric stones

second_line_drugs.notes: Flexible URS with laser lithotripsy for ureteral or lower pole stones; holmium laser preferred
0.4 mg PO daily (off-label) for medical expulsive therapy of distal ureteral stones 5-10 mm
1-2 mEq/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for hypocitraturia prevention; adjust per urine citrate
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO for hypercalciuria prevention; monitor electrolytes

investigations.name: Non-contrast CT abdomen (low-dose)
investigations.indication: Gold standard for stone detection; size and location

investigations.name: Renal and bladder ultrasound
investigations.indication: First-line in children; avoid radiation when possible

investigations.name: 24-hour urine metabolic profile
investigations.indication: Calcium, oxalate, citrate, uric acid, cystine

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes, calcium, uric acid, creatinine

counselling: Child has a kidney or ureter stone causing pain
Increase water intake to at least 2 liters per day to prevent new stones
Pain medicine will be given; most small stones pass on their own
Stone should be collected and sent for analysis to find the cause
Diet changes (less salt, less animal protein) help prevent recurrence
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist if stone >5mm, obstruction, infection, or not passing spontaneously
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if infected obstructed stone (surgical emergency), intractable pain, or AKI
notes: Metabolic evaluation with stone analysis is mandatory in all pediatric stone formers. Tailor prevention to stone composition; dietary modifications are first-line prevention.
guideline_changes: AUA 2025 updated surgical stones guideline with pediatric-specific sections

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.2 mg/kg PO BID-TID; anticholinergic for detrusor overactivity; max 5 mg TID
CIC 4-6x daily from birth; cornerstone of neurogenic bladder management
0.05-0.1 mg/kg PO BID; antimuscarinic alternative if oxybutynin not tolerated

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.4-0.8 mg/kg PO daily; beta-3 agonist for refractory detrusor overactivity
6-12 units/kg intradetrusor injection; max 200 units; for refractory cases
Surgical bladder augmentation for patients refractory to medical therapy

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Screen for hydronephrosis and renal damage

investigations.name: Urine culture
investigations.indication: Baseline and if symptomatic UTI suspected

counselling: Child's bladder does not empty properly due to nerve problems; regular catheterization is needed
Clean intermittent catheterization (CIC) is the most important treatment; learn the proper technique
Medicine will help the bladder relax and hold more urine
Regular kidney ultrasounds are needed to protect kidneys from damage
Proper bowel management is also important; constipation worsens bladder problems
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist and nephrologist at diagnosis; urodynamics needed
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for febrile UTI, obstructive uropathy, or acute kidney injury
notes: Proactive CIC and antimuscarinics from early life preserve upper tract function. Urodynamic studies guide therapy; monitor renal function and bladder compliance regularly.
guideline_changes: Beta-3 agonists (mirabegron) now approved for pediatric use

first_line_drugs.notes: Timed voiding, bladder training, pelvic floor exercises; first-line for all pediatric bladder dysfunction
0.1-0.2 mg/kg PO BID-TID; anticholinergic for overactive bladder; max 5 mg TID

second_line_drugs.notes: 25-50 mg PO daily (>5 yr); beta-3 agonist for refractory OAB
Intradetrusor injection 50-200 units for refractory detrusor overactivity
0.2-0.6 mg PO at bedtime for associated nocturnal enuresis; restrict fluids

investigations.name: Voiding diary
investigations.indication: Record frequency, volume, and incontinence episodes

counselling: Child has bladder problems that can be improved with regular toilet habits
Timed voiding (going to the toilet every 2-3 hours) is the most important step
Treat constipation as it makes bladder problems worse
Medicine helps the bladder relax; give it regularly as prescribed
Be patient; improvement takes 3-6 months of consistent effort
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist if not responding to behavioral therapy and antimuscarinics after 3 months
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for febrile UTI or acute urinary retention
notes: Behavioral modifications are first-line before pharmacotherapy. Screen for bladder-bowel dysfunction as combined management improves outcomes.
guideline_changes: FDA approval of beta-3 agonists for pediatric bladder dysfunction

diagnosis_name: Urinary tract infection, site not specified

first_line_drugs.notes: 25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided TID-QID for 7-10 days; first-line for uncomplicated lower UTI
25-45 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for 7-10 days; alternative oral first-line
8 mg/kg PO daily (max 400 mg) for 7-10 days; once-daily dosing improves adherence

second_line_drugs.notes: 6-12 mg TMP/kg/day PO divided BID for 7-10 days; avoid if local resistance >20%
5-7 mg/kg/day PO divided QID for 7 days; for lower UTI only; not for pyelonephritis
50-75 mg/kg IV/IM daily (max 2 g) for toxic-appearing children or infants <2 months

investigations.name: Urine culture (clean catch or catheter)
investigations.indication: Gold standard for diagnosis; must be collected before antibiotics

investigations.name: VCUG
investigations.indication: Recurrent febrile UTI or abnormal renal US

counselling: Child has a urine infection that needs antibiotic treatment
Complete the full antibiotic course; do not stop early even if symptoms improve
Encourage child to drink plenty of water throughout the day
Toilet habits are important: do not hold urine, empty bladder completely, wipe front to back
Treat constipation as it contributes to urinary infections
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist/urologist for first febrile UTI <2 years, recurrent UTIs (>3/year), or abnormal imaging
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if age <2 months, toxic-appearing, unable to tolerate oral medication, or complicated UTI with obstruction
notes: Obtain urine culture before starting empiric antibiotics. Screen toilet-trained children for bladder-bowel dysfunction; shorter courses may suffice for afebrile UTI.
guideline_changes: AAP 2024 supports shorter antibiotic courses (5-10 days) for uncomplicated UTI

first_line_drugs.notes: Explain to parents: common condition, improves with age, not child's fault; mandatory first step
Fluid management (avoid excess after 6-7 PM), timed voiding before bed, bladder training (hold urine for increasing intervals), positive reinforcement with bladder diary
Most effective long-term therapy, use for 3-4 months if available
0.2 mg (200 mcg) PO at bedtime, can increase to 0.4 mg if needed; duration 4-12 weeks then taper; restrict fluids after dose

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.9-1.5 mg/kg/dose PO at bedtime (max 50 mg for <12y, 75 mg for >=12y); use only if desmopressin fails; monitor for cardiac side effects; avoid in overdose-risk families
0.2-0.3 mg/kg/dose BD-TDS PO (max 5 mg TDS); useful when overactive bladder component present; may combine with desmopressin for refractory cases
1-2 mg PO at bedtime; alternative anticholinergic if oxybutynin not tolerated; fewer side effects than oxybutynin

investigations.name: Urine routine and microscopy
investigations.indication: Rule out UTI in all cases

investigations.name: Ultrasound KUB
investigations.indication: Suspected structural abnormality or secondary enuresis

investigations.name: Blood sugar
investigations.indication: Rule out diabetes if polyuria or secondary enuresis

counselling: Bedwetting is common and not the child's fault - never punish
Restrict fluids after 6-7 PM, encourage more fluids during daytime
Child should pass urine before bedtime every night
Reward dry nights with a star chart - positive reinforcement works
Most children outgrow bedwetting naturally; improvement expected in 4-8 weeks
If on desmopressin, restrict fluids after taking the dose to avoid low sodium
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Daytime incontinence in addition to bedwetting
Weak urine stream or straining to urinate
Secondary enuresis after being dry for more than 6 months
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist/urologist if daytime incontinence, weak urinary stream, recurrent UTI, secondary enuresis with red flags, or no response to 3 months of treatment
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not typically required for primary enuresis. Admit only if severe hyponatremia on desmopressin or if secondary enuresis requires workup for underlying neurological/renal cause
notes: Behavioral therapy is first-line: fluid restriction after 6-7 PM, timed voiding before bed, wake child once at night, bladder training with increasing daytime holding intervals, positive reinforcement with reward calendar. Enuresis alarm is the most effective long-term therapy (use 3-4 months). Medication only if moderate-severe or significant social impact. Monitor for hyponatremia on desmopressin. Never punish the child.
source: ICCS 2020 Standardization Document, IAP Enuresis Protocol, J Pediatr Urol 2024
guideline_changes: ICCS 2020 updated standardization: active therapy from age 6 years; enuresis alarm remains most effective long-term treatment (3-4 months); combination desmopressin + imipramine shown more effective than desmopressin + oxybutynin for refractory cases (2024 study). No major 2024-2025 ICCS guideline revision.

first_line_drugs.notes: Watch and wait for noncommunicating hydrocele in infants; most resolve by 12-24 months of age
Inguinal approach hydrocelectomy if persists >12-24 mo, communicating type, or symptomatic

second_line_drugs.notes: Laparoscopic extraperitoneal closure; safe and effective in children; shorter recovery
10 mg/kg PO q6h post-op for pain control; acetaminophen 15 mg/kg q6h alternative; avoid narcotics in infants

investigations.name: Clinical examination with transillumination
investigations.indication: Transillumination positive for hydrocele; confirms fluid

investigations.name: Scrotal ultrasound
investigations.indication: If diagnosis uncertain; differentiate from inguinal hernia or testicular pathology

counselling: Baby has fluid around the testicle causing swelling; this is usually harmless
In infants, most hydroceles resolve on their own by 12-24 months
Surgery is only needed if the swelling persists beyond 1-2 years or increases in size
Surgery is a minor procedure; baby usually goes home the same day
If swelling suddenly becomes painful or red, come to hospital immediately (may be hernia)
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric surgeon if hydrocele persists beyond 12-24 months, communicating type, or rapidly enlarging
hospitalisation_criteria: Day-care surgery; overnight admission rarely needed unless complications
notes: Noncommunicating hydroceles in infants usually resolve spontaneously. Spermatoceles are benign, asymptomatic, and rarely require intervention in children.
guideline_changes: Laparoscopic hydrocele repair increasingly adopted as safe alternative

first_line_drugs.notes: Scrotal exploration and detorsion ASAP; >90% salvage if within 6h; do not delay for imaging
Fix both testes to dartos fascia at time of surgery; prevents recurrence and contralateral torsion

second_line_drugs.notes: Outward rotation (open book) at bedside as temporizing measure; does not replace surgery
Removal of non-viable testis if necrotic on exploration; consider prosthesis discussion later

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis (no delay for imaging)
investigations.indication: Surgery should not be delayed for ultrasound if clinical suspicion is high

investigations.name: Scrotal ultrasound with Doppler
investigations.indication: Only if diagnosis uncertain and will not delay surgery; absent blood flow

counselling: Child's testicle has twisted, cutting off blood supply; this is a surgical emergency
Surgery must happen within 6 hours for the best chance of saving the testicle
Both testicles will be fixed during surgery to prevent future torsion
After surgery, some swelling and bruising is normal; ice packs and pain medicine help
If the testicle could not be saved, discuss prosthesis options when child is older
referral_criteria: IMMEDIATE surgical referral; do not delay for imaging when clinical suspicion is high
hospitalisation_criteria: Emergency admission for immediate scrotal exploration; no delay for imaging
notes: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency; salvage rate drops rapidly after 6 hours. Clinical diagnosis should not be delayed by imaging when suspicion is high.

first_line_drugs.notes: Bed rest, scrotal elevation, ice packs, ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO q6-8h or acetaminophen 15 mg/kg q6h
Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM x1 + doxycycline 2 mg/kg PO BID x10 days; for sexually active adolescents only

second_line_drugs.notes: 4-6 mg TMP/kg/day PO divided BID for 10-14 days if UTI-associated epididymitis with positive culture
25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided QID for 10-14 days for culture-positive bacterial epididymitis

investigations.name: Scrotal ultrasound with Doppler
investigations.indication: Differentiate from testicular torsion; assess blood flow

investigations.name: VCUG
investigations.indication: Consider if UTI-associated; rule out structural abnormality

counselling: Child has swelling and pain in the testicle area; this is usually due to infection
Rest, scrotal support, and pain medicine will help
In prepubertal boys, antibiotics are only needed if urine test shows infection
It is important to rule out testicular torsion (twisted testicle) which is an emergency
Complete the antibiotic course if prescribed; follow up if not improving in 3-5 days
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist to evaluate for anatomic abnormalities; emergency referral if torsion cannot be ruled out
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if testicular torsion cannot be excluded clinically or if systemically unwell
notes: In prepubertal boys, antibiotics reserved for those with pyuria or positive urine culture. Refer to urology to evaluate for anatomic abnormalities.
guideline_changes: Evidence supports withholding antibiotics in prepubertal boys without pyuria

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to foreskin tip BID with gentle retraction for 4-8 wk; ~65% success rate
Apply to foreskin BID for 4-8 wk; alternative topical steroid with good efficacy

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply to foreskin BID for 4-6 wk; potent steroid for resistant physiologic phimosis
Dorsal slit or limited preputioplasty for failed steroid therapy; foreskin-sparing alternative
Definitive surgical option for pathologic phimosis, BXO, recurrent UTIs, or failed steroid therapy

investigations.name: Urinalysis
investigations.indication: Screen for UTI if history of recurrent infections

counselling: Tight foreskin is normal in young boys and usually loosens by age 3-4 years
Steroid cream applied to the foreskin tip twice daily for 4-8 weeks works in most cases
Gently retract the foreskin as far as it goes without force while applying cream
Surgery (circumcision) is only needed if cream does not work or skin disease (BXO) is present
Good hygiene with gentle retraction during bathing is important once foreskin is retractable
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist if steroid cream fails after 2 courses or pathologic phimosis suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for paraphimosis (emergency) or circumcision under general anesthesia
notes: Topical steroids are first-line; treatment can be repeated if phimosis recurs. BXO is an absolute indication for circumcision at any age.

first_line_drugs.notes: Warm water soaks 2-3x daily; gentle retraction and hygiene; supportive care for mild balanitis
Apply to foreskin BID for 4-6 wk; for associated phimosis in children >3 yr

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply topically BID for 7-14 days if candidal balanitis suspected
Apply topically TID for 5-7 days for suspected bacterial infection; avoid neosporin in children
Cephalexin 25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided TID-QID for 7 days if refractory or strep-positive

investigations.name: Blood glucose
investigations.indication: Screen for diabetes in recurrent balanitis in older children

counselling: Child has an infection/inflammation of the foreskin/penis tip
Warm water soaks 2-3 times daily will help reduce swelling and pain
Do not forcefully retract the foreskin; gentle cleaning is sufficient
Apply the prescribed cream/ointment as directed
If symptoms return frequently, the doctor may recommend steroid cream or circumcision
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist if recurrent episodes or suspected balanitis xerotica obliterans
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if unable to pass urine (acute retention), spreading cellulitis, or systemic signs of infection
notes: Most cases resolve with hygiene and topical therapy. Refer to urology if recurrent or refractory to exclude balanitis xerotica obliterans.

first_line_drugs.notes: BG at 2h, then q3-6h for 24h; target >45 mg/dL after 4h of life
Breastfeed within 1h; supplement with expressed milk or formula if needed
Skin-to-skin or servo-controlled warmer; SGA infants are hypothermia-prone

second_line_drugs.notes: D10W 2 mL/kg bolus then GIR 4-6 mg/kg/min if BG persistently <45
Venous Hct at 4-6h; PET if symptomatic with Hct >65%

investigations.name: TORCH screening
investigations.indication: If intrauterine infection suspected as cause of SGA

counselling: Baby is smaller than expected; frequent feeding is very important to prevent low sugar
Keep baby warm with skin-to-skin contact and proper wrapping
Blood sugar and temperature will be checked frequently in the first 1-2 days
Baby may take some time to catch up in growth; regular follow-up with weight checks is important
Breastfeed on demand, at least every 2-3 hours
referral_criteria: Refer to neonatologist if refractory hypoglycemia, polycythemia requiring PET, or poor growth
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if symptomatic hypoglycemia, weight loss >10%, hypothermia, or polycythemia with Hct >65% symptomatic
notes: SGA neonates at risk for hypoglycemia, polycythemia, and hypothermia. Monitor feeding adequacy and weight gain closely.
snomed_display: Symmetrical small for gestational age foetus

first_line_drugs.notes: Check blood glucose within 2h of birth, then q3-6h for 24h; feed early
Initiate breastfeeding within 1h of birth; supplement if BG <45 mg/dL
Skin-to-skin contact; servo-controlled warmer; target axillary 36.5-37.4C

second_line_drugs.notes: D10W 2 mL/kg bolus then 4-6 mg/kg/min GIR if BG <25 mg/dL or symptomatic
Check venous Hct at 4-6h; PET if Hct >65% with symptoms

investigations.name: Blood glucose monitoring
investigations.indication: High risk for hypoglycemia; check at 2h then q3-6h x 24h

investigations.name: Venous hematocrit
investigations.indication: Screen for polycythemia at 4-6 hours of life

investigations.name: Serum calcium
investigations.indication: Risk of hypocalcemia in SGA/IUGR infants

investigations.name: Thyroid function test
investigations.indication: Screen if intrauterine infection or syndromic features suspected

counselling: Baby is smaller than expected for their age in the womb; close monitoring is needed
Feed baby frequently - every 2-3 hours - to prevent low blood sugar
Keep baby warm with skin-to-skin contact and warm clothing
Blood sugar will be checked frequently in the first 24-48 hours
Regular weight checks and follow-up are important to ensure baby is catching up in growth
referral_criteria: Refer to neonatologist if persistent hypoglycemia, poor growth, or feeding difficulties
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if symptomatic hypoglycemia, weight loss >10%, hypothermia, or polycythemia requiring intervention
notes: High risk for hypoglycemia, polycythemia, hypothermia, and feeding intolerance. Monitor closely for first 24-48 hours.

first_line_drugs.notes: Nasal CPAP 5-8 cmH2O from delivery room; avoid intubation if possible
Loading 20 mg/kg IV/PO, maintenance 5-10 mg/kg/day for apnea of prematurity
Poractant alfa 200 mg/kg if FiO2 >0.3 on CPAP; early rescue preferred

second_line_drugs.notes: Early parenteral nutrition; protein 2-3 g/kg/day, lipids 1-3 g/kg/day from Day 0-1
2-4 mg/kg/day from 2-6 weeks of age; continue through first year
400 IU/day once on full enteral feeds; for bone health

investigations.name: Cranial ultrasound
investigations.indication: IVH screening at day 3-7 and at 36 weeks PMA

investigations.name: ROP screening
investigations.indication: From 31 weeks PMA or 4 weeks postnatal age

investigations.name: Hearing screen (ABR)
investigations.indication: Before discharge; risk factor for sensorineural hearing loss

counselling: Baby is born early and needs extra support for breathing, feeding, and warmth
Mother's milk is the best medicine for premature babies; express milk every 2-3 hours
Kangaroo mother care should be done daily; it helps baby grow and bond
Baby will need vitamin D and iron supplements; continue as advised after discharge
Use corrected age (not birth age) for developmental milestones; follow up regularly with pediatrician
referral_criteria: Refer to NICU at birth; ROP screening, audiology, and developmental follow-up after discharge
hospitalisation_criteria: All preterm infants require monitoring; admit to NICU if <34 weeks GA or with respiratory distress
notes: Delayed cord clamping >60 seconds recommended. Minimize handling, maintain thermoregulation, and promote human milk feeding.
guideline_changes: LISA increasingly adopted; delayed cord clamping (>60s) now standard

first_line_drugs.notes: Start Day 0: amino acids 2-3 g/kg/day, lipids Day 1 1-3 g/kg/day, D10W
Mother's own milk 10-20 mL/kg/day from Day 1; advance 20-30 mL/kg/day
Loading 20 mg/kg, maintenance 5-10 mg/kg/day for apnea prevention

second_line_drugs.notes: Add at 100 mL/kg/day enteral volume; human milk-based preferred for VLBW
250-400 IU/kg SC 3x/week with iron; limited benefit, ROP risk debated
2-4 mg/kg/day elemental iron from 2 weeks of age

investigations.name: Cranial ultrasound (serial)
investigations.indication: IVH screening at day 3, 7, 14, and 36 weeks PMA

counselling: Baby is very small and will need NICU care for several weeks
Mother's breastmilk is the most important medicine; express milk every 2-3 hours around the clock
Blood transfusions may be needed as small babies become anemic; this is expected
Baby will need supplements (iron, vitamin D, calcium) after discharge
Developmental follow-up and eye screening are essential; attend all appointments
referral_criteria: Born at tertiary NICU; refer for ROP, audiology, and neurodevelopmental follow-up
hospitalisation_criteria: All VLBW infants require NICU admission until stable feeding, thermoregulation, and breathing (typically 34-36 weeks PMA)
notes: Prioritize human milk to reduce NEC. Minimize blood draws, use delayed cord clamping, and target restrictive transfusion thresholds.
guideline_changes: Human milk-based fortifier shown to reduce NEC vs bovine-based in VLBW

first_line_drugs.notes: Continuous skin-to-skin contact; improves thermoregulation and BF
Mother's own milk preferred; start trophic feeds 10-20 mL/kg/day
Maintain 36.5-37.4C; use warmer/incubator for infants <1500g

second_line_drugs.notes: Start if enteral feeds not tolerated; amino acids 2-3 g/kg/day from Day 0
2-4 mg/kg/day elemental iron from 2-6 weeks of age

investigations.name: Thyroid screening
investigations.indication: Newborn thyroid screen if not done

investigations.name: Hearing screen (OAE)
investigations.indication: Before discharge; risk factor for hearing loss

counselling: Baby is small and needs extra care with warmth and feeding
Kangaroo mother care (skin-to-skin) is the best way to keep baby warm and help them grow
Breastfeed frequently; mother's milk is the best nutrition for low birth weight babies
Baby will need iron and vitamin D supplements from 2 weeks of age
Regular weight checks are important; follow up every week until weight gain is satisfactory
referral_criteria: Refer to NICU if birth weight <1500g, unable to maintain temperature, or feeding intolerance
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if BW <1800g, unable to maintain temperature >36.5C, or feeding difficulties
notes: WHO recommends immediate KMC for all LBW infants regardless of stability. Monitor glucose, temperature, and weight gain closely.
guideline_changes: WHO 2022 updated guidelines emphasizing kangaroo mother care immediately after birth

first_line_drugs.notes: Poractant alfa 200 mg/kg via LISA or ETT within 2h of birth for RDS
Loading 20 mg/kg IV/PO, then 5-10 mg/kg/day maintenance for apnea prevention
Start CPAP 6-8 cmH2O from birth; avoid intubation if possible
Betamethasone 12 mg IM x2 doses 24h apart to mother before delivery

second_line_drugs.notes: Start amino acids Day 0 at 2-3 g/kg/day; lipids Day 1 at 1-3 g/kg/day
0.1 mg/kg IV q24h x3 for IVH prevention in <28 wk; debated

investigations.name: Cranial ultrasound
investigations.indication: Screen for IVH at day 3-7, then weekly

investigations.name: ROP screening
investigations.indication: Retinopathy of prematurity screening from 31 weeks PMA

investigations.name: Hearing screen (OAE/ABR)
investigations.indication: Before discharge; high risk for hearing loss

counselling: Baby is born very early and needs special care in NICU for many weeks
Mother's breastmilk is extremely important; express milk every 2-3 hours
Kangaroo care (skin-to-skin) is beneficial and parents should do it daily
Baby may have breathing, feeding, and growth challenges that will improve with time
Follow-up with developmental specialist is essential after discharge; use corrected age for milestones
referral_criteria: Deliver at tertiary NICU with capacity for extreme preterm care; refer for ROP screening, audiology, and developmental follow-up
hospitalisation_criteria: All extremely preterm infants require NICU admission until at least 34-36 weeks corrected gestational age
notes: Active resuscitation now considered from 22 weeks at proactive centers. Thermoregulation with polyethylene wrap critical at delivery.
guideline_changes: LISA (less invasive surfactant) emerging as preferred delivery method for surfactant

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 ml (10 drops) once daily PO, contains Vitamins A, B-complex, C, D. Continue till 1 year of age
2-3 mg/kg/day elemental iron PO once daily, start 2-4 weeks after birth, continue till 6-12 months corrected age
400 IU/day PO once daily, continue at least 1 year
50-75 mg/kg/day elemental calcium PO once daily, for bone mineralization and prevention of osteopenia of prematurity
1-5 ml/day divided with feeds PO, for increased caloric intake and weight gain
5-6 drops SOS PO, only if symptomatic infantile colic. Avoid routine use
Pediasure or preterm formula if poor weight gain, 1/7 scoop per extra feed. Prefer breastfeeding first

investigations.name: ROP Screening
investigations.indication: Retinopathy of prematurity screening as per schedule

investigations.name: Weight monitoring
investigations.indication: Weekly weight check for growth assessment

counselling: Continue breastfeeding as first choice, feed every 2-3 hours
Give all supplements regularly as prescribed - do not skip
Target weight gain is 15-20 g/kg/day
Report immediately if poor feeding or poor weight gain
Report immediately if lethargy or repeated vomiting
Report immediately if seizures or abnormal movements
Report immediately if breathing difficulty
Follow-up every 2 weeks initially, then monthly
Keep all immunization appointments on schedule (use chronological age)
Normal development is possible with proper follow-up
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Poor feeding or poor weight gain
referral_criteria: Refer to neonatologist if poor weight gain despite adequate feeding, developmental delay, suspected ROP (retinopathy of prematurity), or recurrent infections
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if failure to thrive, severe anemia, apnea episodes, suspected sepsis, or respiratory distress
notes: Goals: adequate weight gain (15-20 g/kg/day), prevent nutritional deficiencies (iron, calcium, Vitamin D), monitor neurodevelopment, prevent anemia/rickets/infections. Exclusive breastfeeding till 6 months corrected age, feeds every 2-3 hours. Fortified feeds if poor weight gain. Use CORRECTED age for growth/development, CHRONOLOGICAL age for vaccinations. Catch-up growth expected by 1-2 years.
source: IAP 2024 Neonatal Guidelines, WHO 2022 Preterm Care Recommendations, AAP 2023
guideline_changes: IAP 2025: New consensus on developmentally supportive follow-up with color-coded risk stratification and validated screening tools; WHO 2022: 11 new recommendations for preterm/LBW care including home visits by trained health workers; AAP 2023: Primary care framework for neurodevelopmental monitoring of preterm infants

first_line_drugs.notes: Screen BG at 2h of birth; q3-6h for 24h if at risk for hypoglycemia
Initiate breastfeeding within 1h; monitor for adequate intake
Observe for meconium aspiration, polycythemia, hypoglycemia signs

second_line_drugs.notes: D10W infusion if symptomatic hypoglycemia; GIR 4-6 mg/kg/min
Venous Hct at 4-6h of life; treat if >65% with symptoms

investigations.name: Blood glucose
investigations.indication: Screen for hypoglycemia at 2h then q3-6h x24h

investigations.name: Venous hematocrit
investigations.indication: Screen for polycythemia at 4-6h of life

investigations.name: Serum bilirubin
investigations.indication: Monitor for jaundice if clinically indicated

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: If respiratory distress; rule out meconium aspiration

counselling: Baby was born after the due date; this requires monitoring for a few days
Feed baby early and frequently to prevent low blood sugar
Watch for difficulty breathing, yellow skin color, or poor feeding
Dry, peeling skin is normal for post-term babies and will improve on its own
Follow up within 3-5 days of discharge for weight and jaundice check
referral_criteria: Refer to neonatologist if meconium aspiration, persistent hypoglycemia, or polycythemia
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if respiratory distress, meconium aspiration, symptomatic polycythemia, or persistent hypoglycemia
notes: Post-term neonates at higher risk for meconium aspiration, hypoglycemia, and polycythemia. Close monitoring first 24-48h.

first_line_drugs.notes: Screen BG at 2h of age; if <25 mg/dL refeed and recheck in 1h
Breastfeed or formula within 1h of birth; q2-3h feeds for first 24h

second_line_drugs.notes: D10W 2 mL/kg bolus then GIR 4-6 mg/kg/min for persistent hypoglycemia
0.5 mL/kg buccal for mild asymptomatic hypoglycemia; massage into mucosa

investigations.name: Blood glucose monitoring
investigations.indication: High risk for hypoglycemia; screen at 2h then q3-6h x12h

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: If IDM; screen for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy

counselling: Baby is larger than expected; blood sugar will be checked frequently in first 12-24 hours
Feed baby within the first hour of birth and then every 2-3 hours
If mother has diabetes, baby needs closer monitoring
Watch for excessive sleepiness, tremors, or poor feeding and report to doctor
Baby may have bruising or swelling from birth which will resolve on its own
referral_criteria: Refer to neonatologist if persistent hypoglycemia requiring IV dextrose or birth injuries suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if symptomatic hypoglycemia, birth injuries (clavicle fracture, brachial plexus injury), or BG persistently <45 mg/dL
notes: LGA infants of diabetic mothers have highest hypoglycemia risk. Screen glucose for 12h; monitor for birth injuries from macrosomia.

first_line_drugs.notes: Monitor size, watch for jaundice; resolves spontaneously in 2 weeks to 3 months
Serial TSB if jaundice develops; phototherapy per AAP thresholds if indicated

second_line_drugs.notes: If hyperbilirubinemia develops from hematoma reabsorption; per hour-specific nomogram
Monitor hemoglobin if large cephalohematoma; transfuse only if symptomatic anemia

investigations.name: Serum bilirubin (serial)
investigations.indication: Monitor for hyperbilirubinemia from hematoma reabsorption

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Large hematomas may cause drop in hemoglobin

investigations.name: Skull X-ray
investigations.indication: Only if underlying skull fracture suspected

counselling: Baby has a blood collection under the scalp bone from birth; this is common and not dangerous
Do NOT squeeze or try to drain the swelling; it will absorb on its own in weeks to months
The swelling may feel hard at the edges as it heals; this is normal
Watch for increasing yellowness of skin (jaundice) and bring baby for bilirubin check if concerned
No special care is needed; handle baby normally
referral_criteria: Refer if rapidly enlarging, associated skull fracture, or coagulopathy suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if associated significant jaundice requiring phototherapy or symptomatic anemia from large hematoma
notes: Do NOT aspirate cephalohematoma — risk of infection. Resolves spontaneously. Calcified rim may persist for months but is benign.

counselling: Most birth injuries are mild and heal without long-term problems — ज़्यादातर जन्म-चोटें हल्की होती हैं और बिना किसी लंबी समस्या के ठीक हो जाती हैं
Minor scalp swelling or bruising often settles on its own — सिर की हल्की सूजन या नील अपने आप ठीक हो जाती है
Handle the baby gently — बच्चे को नरमी से पकड़ें
Do not pull or lift forcefully under the arms — बच्चे को बाज़ुओं के नीचे से ज़ोर से न उठाएं
Follow-up is important if there is decreased limb movement or persistent swelling — अगर हाथ-पैर की हलचल कम हो या सूजन बनी रहे तो जांच ज़रूरी है
Seek urgent care if baby becomes pale, lethargic, poorly feeding, or swelling increases — अगर बच्चा पीला, सुस्त, कम दूध पीने वाला हो जाए या सूजन बढ़ जाए तो तुरंत डॉक्टर को दिखाएं
warning_signs: Expanding or tense swelling on scalp — सिर पर बढ़ती या तनी हुई सूजन
Baby not moving one arm or leg — बच्चे का एक हाथ या पैर न हिलाना
Facial asymmetry during crying — रोते समय चेहरे का एक तरफ़ लटकना
Pale or blue color — बच्चे का पीला या नीला पड़ जाना
Seizures or excessive irritability — दौरे या बहुत ज़्यादा चिड़चिड़ापन
Poor feeding or vomiting — ठीक से दूध न पीना या उल्टी
referral_criteria: Suspected subgaleal hemorrhage
Shock or poor perfusion
Persistent seizures
Organ injury suspected
Depressed skull fracture
Persistent brachial plexus weakness
Major fracture requiring splinting/orthopedic care
expected_course: Caput succedaneum resolves within 48-72 hours. Cephalhematoma may take 2-8 weeks to resolve and may calcify. Clavicle fractures heal within 2-3 weeks with callus formation.
