Erb palsy recovers in 80-90% of cases within 3-6 months. Facial nerve palsy usually resolves within 2-3 weeks. Subgaleal hemorrhage is a medical emergency requiring immediate NICU care.
key_clinical_points: Caput succedaneum crosses suture lines; cephalhematoma does not
Cephalhematoma may cause significant jaundice requiring phototherapy
Clavicle fracture: Moro reflex asymmetry is the key clinical sign
Erb palsy: waiter tip posture (arm adducted, internally rotated, wrist flexed)
Subgaleal hemorrhage can cause hypovolemic shock - monitor head circumference serially
Always check for associated injuries when one birth injury is found
Document mode of delivery and risk factors carefully

severity_assessment.mild: Caput succedaneum, small bruises, small cephalhematoma - observation and reassurance
severity_assessment.moderate: Clavicle fracture, Erb palsy, facial nerve palsy, large cephalhematoma - active monitoring, immobilization, physiotherapy referral
severity_assessment.severe: Subgaleal hemorrhage, skull fracture, organ injury, persistent neurological deficit - emergency stabilization, NICU admission, surgical referral

monitoring_parameters.frequency: Daily for first 3 days if cephalhematoma or subgaleal hemorrhage
If cephalhematoma - check at 48-72 hours and as clinically indicated
At each assessment for brachial plexus or fracture cases
Continuously if subgaleal hemorrhage; otherwise at each visit

first_line_drugs.notes: 21-30% FiO2 for term, increase to 100% if no response; initiate within 60 sec
3:1 ratio with PPV; 90 compressions + 30 breaths/min if HR <60 despite PPV
0.01-0.03 mg/kg IV (1:10,000) via UVC if HR <60 after 60s of CC + PPV

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.05-0.1 mg/kg via ETT if IV access delayed; less reliable absorption
NS or O-neg blood 10 mL/kg IV over 5-10 min if hypovolemia suspected

investigations.name: Blood gas (cord or early arterial)
investigations.indication: pH <7.0 and base deficit >12 indicate significant asphyxia

investigations.name: aEEG monitoring
investigations.indication: Continuous seizure monitoring; guides cooling decision

counselling: Baby did not breathe well at birth and needed help; this can affect the brain
Baby will be closely monitored in the NICU for at least a few days
If brain injury is moderate-severe, cooling therapy may be offered within 6 hours of birth
Long-term follow-up with developmental specialist is very important
Most babies with mild birth asphyxia recover completely; outcome depends on severity
referral_criteria: Refer to tertiary NICU with cooling capability if moderate-severe encephalopathy; neurology follow-up
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require NICU admission; cooling facility needed for moderate-severe HIE
notes: Effective ventilation is the single most important step. Evaluate for therapeutic hypothermia if moderate-severe encephalopathy develops.
guideline_changes: NRP 2025 updates: establish alternate airway before compressions; epinephrine after 60s of CC

first_line_drugs.notes: Poractant alfa 200 mg/kg via thin catheter on CPAP; within 2h of birth preferred
Start 6-8 cmH2O immediately after birth; primary respiratory support
Betamethasone 12 mg IM x2 to mother 24h apart before preterm delivery

second_line_drugs.notes: Intubate-Surfactant-Extubate technique if LISA not available
Volume-targeted; TV 4-6 mL/kg if intubation required; minimize duration
20 mg/kg loading then 5-10 mg/kg/day; facilitates extubation and prevents apnea

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Ground glass appearance with air bronchograms confirms RDS

investigations.name: Blood gas (arterial/capillary)
investigations.indication: Assess oxygenation and guide FiO2/surfactant decision

counselling: Baby's lungs lack a substance called surfactant needed for breathing; this is common in premature babies
Surfactant medicine will be given directly into the lungs through a thin tube
CPAP or ventilator support will help baby breathe until lungs mature
Antenatal steroid injection (if given before birth) has already helped lung development
Most babies improve significantly after surfactant treatment and gradually need less breathing support
referral_criteria: Deliver at center with NICU and surfactant availability; refer for antenatal steroids before preterm delivery
hospitalisation_criteria: All RDS cases require NICU admission until weaned off respiratory support
notes: LISA reduces need for mechanical ventilation and BPD compared to INSURE. Poractant alfa 200 mg/kg superior to lower doses and beractant.
guideline_changes: LISA (less invasive surfactant administration) recommended over INSURE where expertise available

first_line_drugs.notes: FiO2 to maintain SpO2 92-97%; usually <40% FiO2 sufficient
5-7 cmH2O nasal CPAP if moderate respiratory distress; aids fluid clearance
D10W + electrolytes at maintenance; NPO until RR <60-70 to reduce aspiration risk

second_line_drugs.notes: 2-8 L/min heated humidified; alternative to CPAP for mild TTN
OG/NG feeds if RR 60-80 and unable to safely PO feed

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Perihilar streaking, fluid in fissures; hyperinflation

investigations.name: CBC, CRP
investigations.indication: Rule out pneumonia if not improving

counselling: Baby is breathing fast because of fluid left in the lungs from birth; this is temporary
This condition resolves on its own within 1-3 days; no specific medicine is needed
Baby may need oxygen support and IV fluids until breathing normalizes
Once breathing rate comes down, breastfeeding can start
This is common after cesarean delivery and is not dangerous
referral_criteria: Refer to NICU if requiring >40% FiO2 or not improving within 72 hours
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if tachypnea (RR >60) with increased work of breathing or requiring supplemental oxygen
notes: TTN is self-limiting; resolves within 24-72h. No proven pharmacotherapy (diuretics, salbutamol, steroids not recommended). Rule out other causes.

first_line_drugs.notes: Nasal CPAP 5-8 cmH2O from birth; first-line respiratory support for preterm
Titrate to SpO2 90-95% (preterm) or 92-97% (term); avoid hyperoxia
Poractant alfa 200 mg/kg if FiO2 >0.3 on CPAP for RDS; via LISA if available

second_line_drugs.notes: Intubate if FiO2 >0.5-0.6 on CPAP, recurrent apnea, or respiratory failure
1-8 L/min for mild-moderate respiratory distress; heated humidified

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Identify cause: RDS, TTN, pneumonia, pneumothorax, CDH

investigations.name: Pre and post-ductal SpO2
investigations.indication: Screen for cyanotic heart disease or PPHN

counselling: Baby is having difficulty breathing which is common in newborns, especially premature babies
Oxygen or CPAP (gentle air pressure through the nose) will help baby breathe
If needed, surfactant medicine can be given directly into the lungs
Most cases of breathing difficulty improve within 2-3 days
Baby will remain in NICU until breathing comfortably on room air
referral_criteria: Refer to NICU; tertiary center if requiring advanced respiratory support
hospitalisation_criteria: All neonates with respiratory distress require admission for monitoring and support
notes: Identify underlying cause: RDS, TTN, pneumonia, pneumothorax, or CDH. Chest X-ray and blood gas guide management. Most TTN resolves by 72h.
snomed_display: Respiratory distress in early neonatal period

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mg/kg IV q8-12h; covers GBS, Listeria, and common pathogens
5 mg/kg IV q24-48h based on GA; synergistic with ampicillin for gram-negatives
Supplemental O2, CPAP, or mechanical ventilation based on severity

second_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg IV q8-12h; alternative to gentamicin if renal concerns or CSF coverage needed
Consider if concurrent RDS; poractant alfa 200 mg/kg via ETT

investigations.name: CBC with differential
investigations.indication: WBC count, neutrophil ratio for infection

investigations.name: Blood gas
investigations.indication: Assess respiratory status and oxygen needs

counselling: Baby has a lung infection that started before or during birth
IV antibiotics will be given for 7-10 days; full course is important
Baby may need oxygen or breathing support during treatment
Blood cultures will guide the specific antibiotic choice
Baby will recover fully in most cases; follow up if breathing problems persist after discharge
referral_criteria: Refer to NICU if requiring mechanical ventilation or not responding to first-line antibiotics
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require admission for IV antibiotics; NICU if respiratory support needed
notes: Treat for 7-10 days; shorter courses (<=5 days) may suffice for culture-negative cases. Obtain blood culture before antibiotics.

first_line_drugs.notes: Supplemental O2, CPAP, or MV; PEEP 6-8 cmH2O; rate 40-60; Ti 0.5-0.6s
100-200 mg/kg via ETT for severe MAS with high FiO2 requirement; may reduce ECMO need
20 ppm for PPHN complicating MAS (OI >15-25); wean as oxygenation improves

second_line_drugs.notes: High-frequency oscillatory ventilation for refractory hypoxemia or air trapping
Ampicillin + gentamicin empirically; MAS mimics pneumonia and culture often sent
For severe refractory respiratory failure; last resort after maximal medical therapy

investigations.name: Blood gas (arterial)
investigations.indication: Assess oxygenation and calculate oxygenation index

investigations.name: Blood culture
investigations.indication: MAS mimics pneumonia; rule out infection

counselling: Baby breathed in meconium (first stool) during birth, causing lung irritation
Baby needs oxygen and breathing support; most cases resolve in 3-5 days
In severe cases, special gas (nitric oxide) may be used to help the lungs
Baby will be in NICU until breathing normalizes
Full recovery is expected in most cases; follow up if coughing or fast breathing continues
referral_criteria: Refer to tertiary NICU if requiring iNO, HFOV, or consideration for ECMO
hospitalisation_criteria: All MAS cases require admission; NICU if supplemental O2 needed
notes: Supportive care is the mainstay; no specific therapy. Do not routinely suction non-vigorous infants. Most resolve in 3-5 days.
guideline_changes: Routine endotracheal suctioning of non-vigorous meconium-stained infants no longer recommended (since 2015 NRP)

first_line_drugs.notes: 23-25G needle or 18-24G angiocath; 2nd-3rd ICS MCL; for tension pneumothorax emergently
Pigtail catheter (6-8 Fr) or trocar; connect to underwater seal -10 to -20 cmH2O suction

second_line_drugs.notes: For small asymptomatic pneumothorax; high-flow or 100% O2 may hasten reabsorption
Transition to HFOV if pneumothorax recurs on conventional ventilation

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Confirm pneumothorax; assess size and tension

counselling: Air has leaked from baby's lung into the chest space, causing breathing difficulty
Needle or tube drainage may be needed to remove the trapped air
Baby will be on continuous monitoring and may need oxygen support
Most cases resolve within a few days after drainage
In rare cases, the air leak may recur; doctor will monitor closely
referral_criteria: Refer to NICU; pediatric surgeon if recurrent or complicated pneumothorax
hospitalisation_criteria: All symptomatic pneumothorax require NICU admission; tension pneumothorax is a life-threatening emergency
notes: Tension pneumothorax is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate needle decompression. Transillumination is rapid bedside diagnostic tool.

diagnosis_name: Pulmonary hemorrhage originating in perinatal period

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.5 mL of 1:10,000 via ETT for vasoconstriction; may repeat x1
Raise PEEP to 8-10 cmH2O or switch to HFOV to tamponade bleeding
10-15 mL/kg to replace blood loss; maintain Hb >12 during acute phase

second_line_drugs.notes: 100-200 mg/kg after bleeding stops; restores surfactant inactivated by blood/protein
10-15 mL/kg FFP for coagulopathy; vitamin K 1 mg IV if not already given
50-100 mcg/kg IV for refractory hemorrhage; case reports only

counselling: Baby has bleeding from the lungs which is a serious emergency
Baby will need a breathing tube and ventilator to support breathing
Blood transfusion may be needed to replace lost blood
This is more common in very premature and sick babies
Treatment is aggressive but many babies recover; doctor will keep you updated
referral_criteria: Immediate NICU with ventilator support; may need tertiary transfer
hospitalisation_criteria: Life-threatening emergency requiring immediate NICU admission and mechanical ventilation
notes: Life-threatening emergency. Increase MAP to tamponade bleeding, correct coagulopathy, and transfuse aggressively. Surfactant after bleeding stops.

first_line_drugs.notes: Loading 20 mg/kg IV/PO, maintenance 5-10 mg/kg/day; may increase to 10 if persistent
Nasal CPAP 5-7 cmH2O to splint airway and reduce apnea frequency

second_line_drugs.notes: Nasal intermittent PPV; may be more effective than CPAP for severe apnea
0.5-2 mg/kg/h IV infusion; for caffeine-refractory apnea; limited use

investigations.name: CBC, CRP
investigations.indication: Rule out sepsis as trigger for new-onset apnea

investigations.name: Cranial ultrasound
investigations.indication: Rule out IVH or CNS cause in preterm

investigations.name: EEG
investigations.indication: If seizures suspected as cause of apnea events

counselling: Baby stops breathing for short periods; this is common in premature babies
Caffeine medicine will help baby remember to breathe; it is safe
Baby will be on a monitor that alerts nurses when breathing pauses occur
Baby can go home when there have been no apnea episodes for 5-7 days
If baby turns blue or stops breathing at home, stimulate gently and call emergency
referral_criteria: Refer to NICU; investigate for underlying causes if apnea is new-onset in a previously well neonate
hospitalisation_criteria: All significant apnea episodes require hospital monitoring until event-free for 5-7 days
notes: Caffeine citrate (CAP trial) reduces apnea, BPD, and improves neurodevelopment. Rule out sepsis, anemia, and metabolic causes for new-onset apnea.

first_line_drugs.notes: Start 20 ppm when OI >15-25; wean by 5 ppm increments; avoid abrupt stop
Maintain preductal SpO2 >95%; target PaO2 60-80 mmHg; avoid hyperoxia
Optimize lung recruitment; target pH 7.35-7.45; avoid overventilation

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg PO q6h or 0.4 mg/kg IV over 3h; for iNO-refractory PPHN
0.33-0.99 mcg/kg/min IV; inodilator for PPHN with cardiac dysfunction
1-20 ng/kg/min IV; prostacyclin analog for refractory PPHN
For severe refractory PPHN (OI >40) failing maximal medical therapy

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Confirm PPHN; estimate PA pressure; rule out structural heart disease

investigations.name: Blood gas (arterial)
investigations.indication: Calculate oxygenation index to guide therapy

counselling: Baby's lung blood vessels are not opening properly, making it hard to get oxygen
Special gas (nitric oxide) breathed through a tube helps open these blood vessels
This is a serious condition requiring intensive care
Most babies improve within 5-7 days with treatment
Follow-up after discharge for lung and heart health is important
referral_criteria: Refer to tertiary NICU with iNO capability; refer for ECMO if not responding to maximal therapy
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require NICU admission with mechanical ventilation and hemodynamic monitoring
notes: iNO is the only FDA-approved pulmonary vasodilator for neonatal PPHN. 30% are iNO non-responders; sildenafil is best alternative.
guideline_changes: Sildenafil IV formulation and milrinone increasingly used for iNO-refractory PPHN

first_line_drugs.notes: Multidisciplinary: cardiology, ophthalmology, audiology, neurology assessments
Repair of PDA or other CHD when indicated; timing based on cardiac status

second_line_drugs.notes: Early audiology referral; hearing aids or cochlear implant for SNHL
Ophthalmology referral; lens aspiration if visually significant cataract

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Screen for PDA, pulmonary stenosis, and other CHD

investigations.name: CBC with platelet count
investigations.indication: Thrombocytopenia and blueberry muffin rash evaluation

counselling: Baby was infected with rubella virus before birth; there is no specific antiviral treatment
Baby may have heart, eye, and hearing problems that need specialist care
Baby can spread the virus for several months; strict hand hygiene and isolation from pregnant women
Regular follow-up with multiple specialists (heart, eye, ear) is essential
Early hearing aids or cochlear implant can help if hearing loss is found
referral_criteria: Immediate referral to cardiology, ophthalmology, and audiology; genetics consultation
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for stabilization, cardiac evaluation, and multidisciplinary assessment at birth
notes: No specific antiviral treatment. Infants shed virus for months; isolate and enforce hand hygiene. Screen glucose and thyroid function.

first_line_drugs.notes: 16 mg/kg PO q12h for 6 months; start within first month of life
6 mg/kg IV q12h; for life-threatening disease, switch to oral when stable

second_line_drugs.notes: For asymptomatic cCMV or mild symptoms; serial hearing tests recommended

investigations.name: CMV PCR (urine/saliva)
investigations.indication: Confirm diagnosis within first 3 weeks of life

investigations.name: Auditory brainstem response (ABR)
investigations.indication: Baseline hearing assessment; repeat q6 months

counselling: Baby has an infection (CMV virus) caught from mother during pregnancy
Antiviral medicine will be given for 6 months to protect baby's hearing
Regular blood tests are needed to monitor for side effects of medication
Hearing tests will be done every 6 months as hearing loss can develop later
Good hand hygiene is important; CMV spreads through body fluids
referral_criteria: Refer to infectious disease specialist, audiology, and ophthalmology at diagnosis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for IV ganciclovir if severe/life-threatening disease; outpatient oral valganciclovir for stable cases
notes: Monitor CBC weekly (neutropenia common side effect). Treatment improves hearing outcomes if started within 1 month of life.
guideline_changes: 6 months valganciclovir now standard for symptomatic cCMV; oral preferred over IV for non-critical

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 mL/kg IV over 15-30 min; repeat up to 40-60 mL/kg total if responsive
0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/min IV continuous infusion for fluid-refractory shock
Empiric antibiotics within 1h of recognition; meningitis dosing if suspected

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-20 mcg/kg/min IV; alternative first-line vasopressor to epinephrine
1-2 mg/kg IV q8h for catecholamine-resistant shock; stress dose steroids
0.05-0.5 mcg/kg/min IV for warm shock with low SVR

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Assess cardiac function and guide vasopressor choice

counselling: Baby is critically ill with a severe blood infection causing shock; intensive care is needed
Multiple medicines through IV are needed to support blood pressure and fight infection
Baby may need a breathing machine and close monitoring of heart and blood pressure
Blood transfusions and other treatments may be needed
Recovery depends on how quickly treatment started; the NICU team is doing everything possible
referral_criteria: Immediate transfer to tertiary NICU with vasopressor and ventilator capability
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require immediate NICU/PICU admission; this is a life-threatening emergency
notes: Start vasopressors if still shocked after 40-60 mL/kg fluid. Monitor lactate, UOP, and perfusion. Hydrocortisone for refractory shock.
guideline_changes: Epinephrine now favored equally with dopamine as first-line vasopressor per recent RCTs

first_line_drugs.notes: 2 mg/kg/day PO x2 days, then 1 mg/kg/day x2-6 mo, then 1 mg/kg 3x/wk to 12 mo
100 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h for 12 months
10 mg PO 3x/week throughout pyrimethamine therapy; protects bone marrow

second_line_drugs.notes: Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day if CSF protein >1 g/dL or severe chorioretinitis

investigations.name: CBC with differential (weekly)
investigations.indication: Monitor for pyrimethamine-induced bone marrow suppression

counselling: Baby has a parasitic infection caught from mother during pregnancy; treatment is for 12 months
Give all three medicines as prescribed; do not stop without doctor's advice
Blood tests every 1-2 weeks are essential to check for medicine side effects on blood cells
Eye checkups are needed every 3-6 months as eye problems can appear later
The infection is not contagious from baby to others
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric infectious disease specialist, ophthalmology, and neurology
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe symptomatic disease (seizures, hydrocephalus) or CNS involvement
notes: Treat for 12 months minimum. Monitor CBC weekly while on daily pyrimethamine due to bone marrow suppression.

first_line_drugs.notes: 200-300 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h for meningitis dosing; 3 weeks minimum
5 mg/kg IV q24h (term) or q36-48h (preterm); monitor troughs <2 mcg/mL
200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h; better CSF penetration than gentamicin

second_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg IV q8-12h for suspected MRSA or late-onset; target trough 10-15
40 mg/kg IV q8h for resistant gram-negative meningitis

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis
investigations.indication: Cell count, glucose, protein, gram stain, culture

investigations.name: CBC with differential
investigations.indication: WBC count and markers of infection

investigations.name: CRP
investigations.indication: Inflammatory marker for monitoring treatment response

counselling: Baby has a serious brain infection that needs strong IV antibiotics for at least 3 weeks
Baby will stay in NICU during the entire treatment
Repeat spinal tap (lumbar puncture) is needed to check if infection is clearing
Some babies may develop complications like hydrocephalus; head size will be monitored
Long-term follow-up for hearing and development is essential after discharge
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurology and infectious disease; neurosurgery if hydrocephalus develops
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require NICU admission for minimum 21 days of IV antibiotics
notes: Treat minimum 21 days or 14 days after CSF sterilization, whichever is longer. Repeat LP at 48-72h to document sterilization.

second_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg IV q8-12h if MRSA suspected; adjust based on culture results
5-7.5 mg/kg IV q8h if anaerobic or necrotizing fasciitis concern
Rarely needed; only for necrotizing fasciitis or abscess formation

investigations.name: CBC and CRP if not improving

counselling: Keep umbilical cord clean and dry. — नाल को साफ़ और सूखा रखें।
Avoid tight clothing over cord. — नाल पर कसे कपड़े न पहनाएं।
Do not apply oils, powders or traditional substances. — तेल, पाउडर या घरेलू चीज़ें न लगाएं।
Watch for warning signs: redness, swelling, foul smell, pus, fever. — लालिमा, सूजन, बदबू, पीप या बुखार पर ध्यान दें।
Normal cord separation occurs in 7–14 days. — नाल आमतौर पर 7-14 दिन में गिर जाती है।
Seek medical help if discharge or redness increases. — रिसाव या लालिमा बढ़े तो तुरंत डॉक्टर को दिखाएं।
warning_signs: Purulent or foul-smelling umbilical discharge — नाल से पीप या बदबू आना
Spreading redness beyond 1 cm from stump — नाल से 1 सेमी से ज़्यादा लालिमा फैलना
Fever or hypothermia — बुखार या शरीर ठंडा पड़ना
Lethargy or poor feeding — सुस्ती या दूध न पीना
Skin discoloration around umbilicus — नाभि के आस-पास त्वचा का रंग बदलना
expected_course: Localized mild omphalitis with topical treatment usually resolves within 5-7 days. Moderate to severe omphalitis requires hospitalization with IV antibiotics (Ampicillin + Gentamicin) for 7-10 days. Complications like necrotizing fasciitis, peritonitis, or systemic sepsis are life-threatening and require urgent surgical intervention. Mortality is 7-15% even with treatment in severe cases.
key_clinical_points: Omphalitis is a neonatal emergency that can progress rapidly to sepsis
Redness spreading >0.5-1 cm from stump base is concerning
Most common organisms: Staph aureus, Group A Streptococcus, Gram-negative bacilli
Home delivery with unsterile cord care is the biggest risk factor in India
4% chlorhexidine application at birth reduces omphalitis risk (WHO recommendation)
Delayed cord separation (>3 weeks) with discharge may indicate underlying immune deficiency

severity_assessment.mild: Minimal periumbilical erythema (<0.5 cm), no systemic signs, baby feeding well — topical mupirocin, dry cord care, close follow-up
severity_assessment.moderate: Erythema 0.5-2 cm, mild induration, no systemic signs — oral antibiotics, daily review, consider admission
severity_assessment.severe: Spreading cellulitis >2 cm, systemic signs (fever, lethargy, poor feeding), pus — immediate admission, IV Ampicillin + Gentamicin, surgical consult

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.5% ointment prophylaxis to both eyes within 1h of birth
25-50 mg/kg IM/IV x1 dose (max 125 mg) for gonococcal conjunctivitis
50 mg/kg/day PO divided q6h x14 days for chlamydial conjunctivitis

second_line_drugs.notes: Frequent saline eye lavage for gonococcal conjunctivitis as adjunct
20 mg/kg/day PO x3 days for chlamydial; if erythromycin fails

investigations.name: Conjunctival swab for gram stain and culture
investigations.indication: Identify organism; gram-negative diplococci for gonococcal

counselling: Baby has an eye infection that needs treatment; clean eyes gently with warm water
Complete the full course of antibiotic medicine even if the eye looks better
If pus increases, eye becomes very swollen, or baby seems unwell, come back immediately
Gonococcal eye infection is serious and needs injection medicine
Good hand hygiene before and after touching baby's eyes is essential
referral_criteria: Refer to ophthalmology if gonococcal conjunctivitis, corneal involvement, or not improving with treatment
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for gonococcal ophthalmia (medical emergency); systemic antibiotics required
notes: Gonococcal ophthalmia is a medical emergency requiring systemic antibiotics. Topical treatment alone insufficient for chlamydial infection.

diagnosis_name: Intracranial nontraumatic hemorrhage of fetus and newborn

first_line_drugs.notes: Maintain hemodynamic stability, correct coagulopathy, minimize handling
Head US at Day 3-7, repeat at 14d and 36 wk PMA; monitor for hydrocephalus

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mL/kg for symptomatic anemia from hemorrhage; maintain Hb >10 in acute phase
Neurosurgery consult for progressive post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus
25 mg/kg/day PO for temporizing slowly progressive hydrocephalus; limited evidence

investigations.name: Cranial ultrasound (serial)
investigations.indication: Grade IVH; repeat at day 3, 7, 14, and 36 weeks PMA

investigations.name: Coagulation profile
investigations.indication: Rule out coagulopathy contributing to hemorrhage

investigations.name: Brain MRI
investigations.indication: At term-equivalent age for detailed assessment

counselling: Baby has bleeding inside the brain; this is more common in premature babies
Handle baby gently; minimize disturbances and loud noises
Head size will be measured regularly to check for fluid buildup (hydrocephalus)
Mild bleeding (grade 1-2) usually resolves on its own
Regular follow-up with brain scans and developmental assessments is important after discharge
referral_criteria: Refer to neurosurgery if progressive hydrocephalus; neurology for developmental follow-up
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require NICU monitoring; neurosurgery consultation for grade III-IV IVH
notes: Prevention key: antenatal steroids, delayed cord clamping, minimal handling. Most grade I-II IVH resolve; grade III-IV carry higher neurodevelopmental risk.
snomed_display: Intracranial nontraumatic haemorrhage of foetus and newborn

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg IV/SC for active bleeding; 0.3-0.5 mg/kg if <1500g birth weight
0.5-1 mg IM at birth for all neonates; single dose prevents VKDB

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mL/kg IV for moderate-severe active bleeding with coagulopathy
2 mg PO at birth, 2-4 wks, and 6-8 wks if parents decline IM; less reliable

investigations.name: PT/INR
investigations.indication: Markedly prolonged in VKDB; normalizes after vitamin K

investigations.name: Cranial ultrasound
investigations.indication: If intracranial hemorrhage suspected (irritability, bulging fontanelle)

counselling: Baby has a bleeding problem due to low vitamin K; injection will correct this
Vitamin K injection at birth prevents this condition; it is safe and strongly recommended
If baby is actively bleeding, treatment with vitamin K and blood products will be given
Watch for unusual bruising, blood in stool, or excessive bleeding from umbilical cord
Follow up as advised; most babies recover completely with treatment
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric hematologist if bleeding is severe or does not respond to vitamin K
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all active bleeding cases; ICU admission for intracranial hemorrhage
notes: IM vitamin K at birth is the standard of care for VKDB prevention. Late VKDB (2-12 weeks) occurs with missed or oral-only prophylaxis.
snomed_display: Haemorrhagic disease of the newborn due to factor II deficiency

diagnosis_name: Blood in Stool in a Neonate
counselling: Small streaks of blood are often benign (anal fissure or allergy) — खून की हल्की लकीरें अक्सर सामान्य होती हैं (गुदा में दरार या एलर्जी)
Reassure if baby is active and feeding well — अगर बच्चा सक्रिय है और अच्छे से दूध पी रहा है तो चिंता न करें
Avoid frequent rectal temperature checks — बार-बार गुदा से तापमान न लें
Continue breastfeeding unless advised otherwise — जब तक डॉक्टर न कहे, माँ का दूध पिलाना जारी रखें
Watch for warning signs: fever, distension, vomiting, poor feeding — खतरे के संकेत देखें: बुखार, पेट फूलना, उल्टी, कम दूध पीना
Seek urgent care if symptoms worsen — अगर लक्षण बिगड़ें तो तुरंत डॉक्टर को दिखाएं
warning_signs: Large amount of blood in stool — पखाने में ज़्यादा खून आना
Abdominal distension or hardness — पेट का फूलना या सख्त होना
Bilious (green) vomiting — हरे रंग की उल्टी
Fever or hypothermia — बुखार या शरीर ठंडा पड़ना
Baby becoming lethargic or pale — बच्चे का सुस्त या पीला पड़ जाना
Refusal to feed — दूध न पीना
Black tarry stools — काला चिपचिपा पखाना
referral_criteria: Suspected NEC
Sepsis or shock
Persistent or large bleeding
Abdominal distension or obstruction
Failure to thrive
Coagulopathy suspected
expected_course: Anal fissure: resolves within a few days with gentle care. CMPA-related blood in stool: improves within 2-4 weeks of maternal dairy elimination. Swallowed maternal blood: self-limiting, resolves in 1-2 days. NEC or sepsis: requires hospitalization and may take weeks to recover.
key_clinical_points: APT test differentiates swallowed maternal blood from neonatal GI bleeding
Anal fissure is the most common cause in well-appearing neonates
CMPA should be suspected if blood with mucus in breastfed baby
NEC is more common in preterm infants — triad of abdominal distension, bloody stools, pneumatosis intestinalis
Always check coagulation profile if bleeding disorder suspected
Vitamin K administration status must be verified
Hemodynamic stability assessment is the first priority

severity_assessment.mild: Streaks of blood, well baby, feeding normally, no distension — observation, maternal dairy trial if CMPA suspected
severity_assessment.moderate: Mucoid bloody stools, feeding adequately but with mild discomfort — investigations (stool culture, fecal calprotectin), close follow-up
severity_assessment.severe: Large blood volume, distension, vomiting, lethargy, shock signs — NPO, IV fluids, antibiotics, NICU admission, surgical consult

monitoring_parameters.frequency: If significant bleeding — at presentation and repeat in 12-24 hours
Daily if admitted, at follow-up if outpatient

first_line_drugs.notes: Irradiance >=30 mcW/cm2/nm; start when TSB at or above threshold for GA/age
0.5-1 g/kg IV over 2h for isoimmune hemolytic disease if TSB approaching exchange level

second_line_drugs.notes: Double-volume (160 mL/kg); when TSB exceeds exchange threshold despite phototherapy
D10W + electrolytes at 1.5x maintenance as supplement to feeds during phototherapy

investigations.name: CBC with reticulocyte count
investigations.indication: Assess severity of hemolysis and anemia

investigations.name: Blood group and Rh (mother and baby)
G6PD screen
investigations.indication: Rule out enzyme deficiency contributing to hemolysis

counselling: Baby's blood cells are being destroyed by mother's antibodies; phototherapy (blue light) helps
Breastfeeding should continue during phototherapy; it does not need to be stopped
Baby's eyes will be covered during light therapy to protect them
Bilirubin levels will be checked frequently with blood tests
If light therapy is not enough, blood exchange may be needed; doctor will explain if required
referral_criteria: Refer to neonatologist if TSB approaching exchange transfusion level, rapidly rising bilirubin (>0.5 mg/dL/h), or hemoglobin dropping
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for phototherapy if TSB at or above treatment threshold; NICU for exchange transfusion or IVIG
notes: Rh and ABO incompatibility most common causes. Check DAT, reticulocyte count, and serial bilirubin. IVIG may reduce need for exchange.
guideline_changes: AAP 2022 raised phototherapy thresholds; IVIG role more selective
snomed_display: Hemolytic disease due to isoimmunization NOS

counselling: Not all jaundice is benign after 2 weeks of age. — 2 हफ्ते बाद भी पीलिया सामान्य नहीं माना जाना चाहिए।
Pale stools and dark urine are danger signs and need urgent evaluation. — सफ़ेद पोटी और गहरे रंग का पेशाब खतरे के निशान हैं, तुरंत जांच कराएं।
Early surgery in biliary atresia can preserve native liver function. — पित्त नली की बीमारी में जल्दी ऑपरेशन से लीवर बचाया जा सकता है।
Good nutrition and vitamin supplementation are important. — अच्छा पोषण और विटामिन की खुराक ज़रूरी है।
Regular follow-up and monitoring are essential. — नियमित जांच बहुत ज़रूरी है।
warning_signs: Pale or white stools — सफ़ेद या मिट्टी जैसी पोटी
Dark yellow or brown urine — गहरे पीले या भूरे रंग का पेशाब
Bleeding from any site — कहीं से भी खून आना
Poor feeding or weight loss — दूध न पीना या वज़न कम होना
expected_course: Neonatal cholestasis requires urgent evaluation. Biliary atresia must be ruled out within the first 45-60 days of life for best surgical outcomes (Kasai procedure). Metabolic and infectious causes may respond to specific treatment. Without timely intervention, progressive liver damage and cirrhosis can occur.
key_clinical_points: Any jaundice persisting beyond 14 days in a term infant must be fractionated (total + direct bilirubin).
Direct bilirubin >1 mg/dL (if total <5) or >20% of total (if total >5) defines cholestasis.
Stool color card is a simple, effective screening tool for parents.
High GGT (>300 IU/L) with hepatomegaly is suggestive of biliary atresia.
Kasai portoenterostomy should ideally be performed before 45-60 days of age.
Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (A, D, E, K) is essential in cholestasis.

severity_assessment.mild: Persistent jaundice with normal direct bilirubin, normal stool color, thriving baby — monitor and recheck.
severity_assessment.moderate: Elevated direct bilirubin, slightly abnormal LFTs, normal stool color — complete workup urgently.
severity_assessment.severe: Pale/acholic stools, high GGT, hepatomegaly, coagulopathy, failure to thrive — urgent specialist referral, suspect biliary atresia.

monitoring_parameters.frequency: Weekly until resolved or diagnosis confirmed
Daily by parents using stool color card

second_line_drugs.notes: Double-volume 160 mL/kg; if TSB exceeds exchange threshold despite phototherapy
0.5-1 g/kg IV over 2h for isoimmune hemolytic disease approaching exchange level

counselling: Mild jaundice is common but timing and progression are important — हल्का पीलापन आम है लेकिन समय और बढ़ोतरी पर ध्यान दें
Ensure adequate feeding (8–12 feeds/day) — दिन में 8-12 बार दूध पिलाएं
Return if jaundice worsens or feeding decreases — पीलापन बढ़े या दूध कम पिए तो तुरंत आएं
Educate about danger signs — खतरे के लक्षण पहचानें: सुस्ती, तेज़ रोना, पीठ तनना, बुखार
warning_signs: Jaundice within first 24 hours of life — जन्म के 24 घंटे में पीलापन (हमेशा खतरनाक)
Poor feeding or lethargy — दूध न पीना या सुस्ती
High-pitched cry — तेज़ पतली आवाज़ में रोना
Fever or pallor — बुखार या पीलापन
Rapidly worsening jaundice — तेज़ी से बढ़ता पीलापन
Dark urine or pale stools — गहरे रंग का पेशाब या सफ़ेद मल
guideline_changes: AAP 2022 raised phototherapy thresholds by ~2 mg/dL for healthy term infants
expected_course: Physiological jaundice typically appears after 24 hours, peaks at day 3-5 in term neonates and day 5-7 in preterms, and resolves by 2 weeks. Breastfeeding jaundice may persist longer but is benign if baby is thriving. Pathological jaundice (onset <24h, rapid rise, hemolysis) requires urgent investigation and phototherapy. Kernicterus is preventable with timely intervention.
key_clinical_points: Jaundice <24 hours is ALWAYS pathological — investigate immediately
Bhutani nomogram guides phototherapy decisions based on age in hours and TSB
Ensure adequate breastfeeding (8-12 feeds/day) to promote bilirubin excretion
Check blood group and Coombs test if hemolysis suspected
G6PD deficiency is common in India and can cause severe hemolytic jaundice
TSB >20 mg/dL in term neonate is dangerous and requires urgent phototherapy

severity_assessment.mild: Jaundice limited to face and chest, good feeding, TSB in low-risk zone on nomogram — reassurance, close follow-up in 24-48h
severity_assessment.moderate: Jaundice extending to abdomen, TSB in intermediate zone, risk factors present — repeat TSB, close monitoring, consider phototherapy
severity_assessment.severe: Jaundice to palms/soles, TSB in high-risk zone, signs of encephalopathy — urgent admission, intensive phototherapy, consider exchange transfusion

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 mL/kg over 2-4h; use restrictive thresholds (Hb 7-10 based on support)
2-4 mg/kg/day elemental iron from 2-6 weeks in preterm infants

second_line_drugs.notes: 250-400 IU/kg SC 3x/week; modest reduction in late transfusions; ROP concern
60-120 seconds at birth; reduces anemia and transfusion need in preterms

investigations.name: CBC with reticulocyte count
investigations.indication: Severity of anemia and bone marrow response

counselling: Baby has low blood count and may need a blood transfusion
Blood tests (heel pricks) are needed frequently; each test takes only a small amount
Baby will receive iron supplements to help build blood
Premature babies commonly need transfusions; this is expected and safe
Breast milk and iron-rich supplements will help after discharge
referral_criteria: Refer to hematologist if anemia is persistent, unusual, or not responding to standard treatment
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if symptomatic anemia (poor feeding, tachycardia, apnea) or hemoglobin below transfusion threshold
notes: Minimize phlebotomy losses. Restrictive transfusion thresholds are safe per ETTNO/TOP trials. Delayed cord clamping is standard.
guideline_changes: Restrictive transfusion thresholds (Hb 7-10 g/dL based on age/support) validated by ETTNO/TOP trials

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mL/kg; threshold <25,000/mcL (restrictive) per PlaNeT-2 trial
Antibiotics for sepsis, maternal ITP workup; most cases self-limiting

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 g/kg IV prior to platelet transfusion for alloimmune thrombocytopenia (NAIT)
10-15 mL/kg at <50,000/mcL only for active bleeding or pre-procedure

investigations.name: Maternal platelet count and anti-platelet antibodies
investigations.indication: Evaluate for NAIT or maternal ITP

counselling: Baby has low platelet count which means blood clotting is reduced
Blood tests will be repeated frequently to monitor the platelet count
Platelet transfusion may be needed if count is very low or baby is bleeding
Most cases in newborns resolve on their own within 1-2 weeks
Handle baby gently; avoid unnecessary procedures to reduce bleeding risk
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric hematologist if thrombocytopenia persists >2 weeks or suspected NAIT
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if platelets <50,000/mcL with bleeding or <25,000/mcL regardless of symptoms
notes: Restrictive threshold (25K) is safer than liberal (50K) per PlaNeT-2 trial. For NAIT, use HPA-matched or washed maternal platelets.
guideline_changes: PlaNeT-2 trial established restrictive platelet transfusion threshold of 25,000/mcL
snomed_display: Transient neonatal thrombocytopenia due to isoimmunization

first_line_drugs.notes: Increase fluid intake; NS bolus 10-20 mL/kg for mild symptomatic polycythemia
PET with NS; Vol = (Hct observed - 50)/Hct observed x 90 mL/kg x wt(kg)

second_line_drugs.notes: Monitor Hct q6-12h; many cases resolve with hydration alone

investigations.name: Venous hematocrit
investigations.indication: Confirm diagnosis; must be venous not capillary

counselling: Baby's blood is thicker than normal which can cause problems with blood flow
Extra fluids and close monitoring will be given
If the blood is very thick or baby has symptoms, a partial blood exchange may be needed
This condition usually resolves within a few days with treatment
Follow up for a blood count check after discharge as advised
referral_criteria: Refer to neonatologist if symptomatic polycythemia requiring partial exchange transfusion
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if venous Hct >70% (asymptomatic) or >65% with symptoms (jitteriness, poor feeding, cyanosis)
notes: PET for Hct >70% asymptomatic or >65% symptomatic. Use venous (not capillary) Hct for decision-making. Long-term benefit of PET unproven.
snomed_display: Neonatal polycythemia due to intra-uterine growth retardation

first_line_drugs.notes: D10W 2 mL/kg (200 mg/kg) IV push for BG <25 or symptomatic; then infusion
D10W at GIR 4-6 mg/kg/min; increase by 2 mg/kg/min q30min to max 12-15
Breastfeed or formula within 1h of birth for at-risk infants; q2-3h

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5 mL/kg buccal massage for mild asymptomatic hypoglycemia; recheck in 30 min
0.02-0.03 mg/kg IM/IV (max 1 mg) for refractory hypoglycemia; temporary bridge
5-15 mg/kg/day PO divided q8h for persistent hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia

investigations.name: Blood glucose (serial)
investigations.indication: Bedside and lab glucose monitoring q30min-1h during treatment

investigations.name: Insulin level (critical sample)
investigations.indication: Collect during hypoglycemia episode to evaluate for hyperinsulinism

investigations.name: Cortisol and growth hormone
investigations.indication: Collect during hypoglycemia if persistent; rule out hormonal causes

investigations.name: Urine ketones
investigations.indication: Absence of ketones during hypoglycemia suggests hyperinsulinism

investigations.name: Blood gas
investigations.indication: Metabolic acidosis if associated organic acidemia

counselling: Baby has low blood sugar which needs to be corrected quickly
Feed baby frequently - every 2-3 hours - breastmilk is preferred
Blood sugar will be checked frequently by heel prick; this is necessary
If blood sugar stays low despite feeding, IV sugar water (dextrose) will be given
Watch for shakiness, poor feeding, or excessive sleepiness at home after discharge
referral_criteria: Refer to endocrinologist if hypoglycemia persists beyond 48-72 hours or requires GIR >12 mg/kg/min
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if BG <25 mg/dL, symptomatic, or requiring IV dextrose infusion
notes: Screen at-risk groups: IDM, SGA, LGA, preterm. Target BG >45 mg/dL after 4h of life. Max peripheral dextrose D12.5%; higher needs central line.
guideline_changes: Dextrose gel 40% increasingly adopted as first intervention for mild asymptomatic hypoglycemia

diagnosis_name: Transitory neonatal disorders of calcium and magnesium metabolism

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mL/kg (10-20 mg/kg elemental Ca) IV slow push over 10-30 min with cardiac monitoring
50-75 mg/kg/day elemental Ca IV continuous or 80 mg/kg/day PO divided q6h

second_line_drugs.notes: 25-50 mg/kg IV/IM q8-12h for hypomagnesemia; monitor for bradycardia
0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/day PO for persistent hypocalcemia from hypoparathyroidism

investigations.name: Serum calcium (total and ionized)
investigations.indication: Confirm hypocalcemia; ionized Ca more accurate

investigations.name: PTH level
investigations.indication: Low in hypoparathyroidism, high in vitamin D deficiency

counselling: Baby has low calcium or magnesium in the blood causing jitteriness or seizures
Calcium medicine will be given through IV slowly with heart monitoring
Oral calcium supplement will continue after IV treatment
Breastmilk or low-phosphorus formula helps maintain calcium levels
Follow up for repeat blood tests to ensure levels remain normal
referral_criteria: Refer to endocrinologist if hypocalcemia persists >1 week or hypoparathyroidism suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if symptomatic (seizures, tetany) or ionized calcium <4 mg/dL
notes: IV calcium must be given slowly under cardiac monitoring to prevent bradycardia. Correct hypomagnesemia first as it impairs Ca correction.

first_line_drugs.notes: NS 10-20 mL/kg over 15-30 min for shock; repeat as needed up to 40-60 mL/kg
D10W + 0.45% NaCl at maintenance rate; adjust Na+ by <=10-12 mEq/L/day
Assess latch, frequency, and adequacy; supplement if weight loss >10%

second_line_drugs.notes: Small frequent breastmilk/formula feeds if mild and tolerated orally
Check Na, K, BUN, Cr q8-12h during correction; slow correction for hypernatremia

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes (Na, K)
investigations.indication: Hypernatremic dehydration common in breastfed neonates

counselling: Baby is dehydrated, usually from inadequate feeding; fluids will be given through IV
Breastfeeding support will be provided to improve feeding technique
Monitor baby's urine output - at least 6-8 wet diapers per day means adequate feeding
Weight will be checked daily; expect steady weight gain after rehydration
If weight loss >10% from birth weight, bring baby to hospital immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to neonatologist if hypernatremic dehydration (Na >150), seizures, or AKI
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if moderate-severe dehydration, weight loss >10%, hypernatremic, or hemodynamically unstable
notes: Hypernatremic dehydration common in breastfed neonates with poor intake. Correct sodium slowly (<=10-12 mEq/L/day) to prevent cerebral edema.

first_line_drugs.notes: Large-bore NGT to continuous or intermittent suction; strict NPO
NS 10-20 mL/kg bolus; then maintenance + deficit replacement
Laparotomy for mechanical obstruction; urgency depends on etiology

second_line_drugs.notes: Gastrografin enema; diagnostic and therapeutic for meconium ileus
Mucolytic via NGT or enema for meconium ileus; 2-4% solution
Broad-spectrum: ampicillin + gentamicin if perforation suspected

investigations.name: Abdominal X-ray (erect and supine)
investigations.indication: Air-fluid levels, dilated bowel loops, absence of distal gas

investigations.name: Contrast enema
investigations.indication: Diagnostic and potentially therapeutic for meconium ileus

counselling: Baby has a blockage in the intestine; the green vomit is a warning sign that needs urgent treatment
Baby cannot be fed by mouth until the blockage is resolved
Surgery may be needed depending on the cause of the blockage
A tube will be placed through the nose to drain the stomach and reduce pressure
After treatment, feeds will be started slowly once the intestine recovers
referral_criteria: Immediate pediatric surgery referral for all neonatal intestinal obstruction
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require emergency admission; bilious vomiting in a neonate is obstruction until proven otherwise
notes: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is obstruction until proven otherwise. Obtain upright abdominal XR and surgical consult urgently.

first_line_drugs.notes: Ampicillin 50 mg/kg IV q8-12h + Gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV q24-48h
15 mg/kg IV load, then 7.5 mg/kg IV q12h for anaerobic coverage
Bowel rest 7-14 days; NGT decompression; TPN for nutrition

second_line_drugs.notes: Laparotomy or peritoneal drain for perforation (pneumoperitoneum)
15 mg/kg IV q8-12h if MRSA or late-onset concern; adjust per culture
Lactobacillus/Bifidobacterium for VLBW NEC prevention

investigations.name: Abdominal X-ray (serial)
investigations.indication: Pneumatosis intestinalis, portal venous gas, pneumoperitoneum

counselling: Baby has a serious bowel infection that needs strong antibiotics and bowel rest
Baby will not be fed by mouth for 7-14 days; nutrition will be given through IV
Mother should continue to express and store breastmilk as it will be used when feeds restart
Surgery may be needed if the bowel becomes perforated (develops a hole)
Recovery takes time; baby will be closely monitored with X-rays and blood tests
referral_criteria: Immediate pediatric surgical consultation for all NEC cases; transfer to center with pediatric surgery if not available
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require NICU admission; Bell stage II and above require intensive monitoring
notes: Stage with Bell criteria. Pneumoperitoneum is absolute surgical indication. Human milk feeding significantly reduces NEC incidence.
guideline_changes: Probiotics increasingly recommended for NEC prevention in VLBW infants; lactoferrin under study
snomed_display: Necrotizing enterocolitis of newborn stage 2B Bell's classification

diagnosis_name: Disturbance of temperature regulation of newborn

first_line_drugs.notes: Servo-controlled to maintain axillary temp 36.5-37.4C; for hypothermia rewarming
Kangaroo mother care for stable infants; effective thermoregulation method
Apply at birth for preterms <32 wk without drying; reduces heat loss

second_line_drugs.notes: Warmed NS/D10W for severely hypothermic infants; slow rewarming 0.5C/h
Obtain cultures and start empiric antibiotics if hypothermia unexplained

investigations.name: Blood culture and CBC
investigations.indication: Sepsis screening if temperature instability is unexplained

investigations.name: Blood gas
investigations.indication: Assess for metabolic acidosis in severe hypothermia

investigations.name: Thyroid function test
investigations.indication: Rule out congenital hypothyroidism if persistent hypothermia

counselling: Baby is unable to maintain normal body temperature; needs help staying warm
Kangaroo mother care (skin-to-skin) is one of the best ways to warm baby
Keep baby well wrapped; avoid bathing until temperature is stable
Hypothermia (low temperature) can be a sign of infection; doctor will investigate
At home, ensure room temperature is warm and baby is well dressed, especially at night
referral_criteria: Refer to NICU if severe hypothermia (<32C), not responding to rewarming, or suspected sepsis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if temperature <36C despite rewarming measures or if associated with other danger signs
notes: Hypothermia (<36.5C) increases mortality in neonates. Rewarm slowly at 0.5C/h. Unexplained temperature instability warrants sepsis workup.

counselling: Neonatal fever is serious and requires admission — नवजात शिशु में बुखार गंभीर है और भर्ती होना ज़रूरी है।
Early treatment improves outcome — जल्दी इलाज शुरू करने से बच्चा जल्दी ठीक होता है।
Avoid overheating or over-wrapping — बच्चे को ज़्यादा कपड़े या गर्मी में न रखें।
Watch for danger signs — खतरे के संकेतों पर ध्यान रखें।
warning_signs: Poor feeding or refusal to feed
expected_course: Depends on underlying cause. Bacterial infections require 7-14 days of antibiotics. Viral infections may resolve in 3-5 days. HSV requires 14-21 days of acyclovir. Early sepsis workup and empirical antibiotics are critical as delays worsen prognosis significantly.
key_clinical_points: All febrile neonates (<28 days) must be admitted for full sepsis workup.
Send all cultures BEFORE starting antibiotics.
HSV must be considered in any febrile neonate, especially with vesicular rash, seizures, or liver involvement.
Even a well-appearing febrile neonate has significant risk of serious bacterial infection.
Hypothermia in a neonate is as concerning as fever.

severity_assessment.mild: Low-grade fever with well-appearing neonate, normal feeding, and no risk factors -- still requires full workup and admission.
severity_assessment.moderate: Fever with reduced feeding, mild irritability, or maternal risk factors (PROM, GBS).
severity_assessment.severe: High fever with lethargy, seizures, respiratory distress, poor perfusion, bulging fontanelle, or hemodynamic instability.

counselling: Most newborn rashes are harmless and self-resolving — ज़्यादातर नवजात के दाने हानिरहित होते हैं और अपने आप ठीक हो जाते हैं
No need for creams, antibiotics, or medications — किसी क्रीम, एंटीबायोटिक या दवा की ज़रूरत नहीं है
Avoid talcum powder and scented products — टैल्कम पाउडर और खुशबूदार चीज़ों से बचें
Maintain hygiene with plain lukewarm water bathing — गुनगुने पानी से साफ़ सफ़ाई रखें
Rash will gradually resolve over days to weeks — दाने धीरे-धीरे कुछ दिनों से हफ़्तों में ठीक हो जाएंगे
Seek medical care if red flag symptoms appear — अगर खतरे के संकेत दिखें तो तुरंत डॉक्टर को दिखाएं
warning_signs: Fever with rash — दाने के साथ बुखार
Blisters or pus-filled bumps — छाले या मवाद वाले दाने
Rash spreading rapidly — दाने तेज़ी से फैलना
Baby not feeding well — बच्चे का ठीक से दूध न पीना
Baby becoming lethargic or irritable — बच्चे का सुस्त या चिड़चिड़ा हो जाना
Purple or dark red spots on skin — त्वचा पर बैंगनी या गहरे लाल धब्बे
expected_course: Most benign neonatal rashes appear within the first 1–2 weeks of life and resolve spontaneously within days to weeks without any treatment. Erythema toxicum typically appears on day 1–3 and resolves by 5–14 days. Milia resolve within 4 weeks. Neonatal acne may take 1–3 months to clear.
key_clinical_points: Erythema toxicum is the most common benign neonatal rash — seen in up to 50% of term neonates
Milia are tiny white papules on the nose and face — do not squeeze
Neonatal acne is hormonal and self-limiting
Transient neonatal pustular melanosis is more common in dark-skinned neonates
Cutis marmorata is a normal vasomotor response to cold
Key differentiator: benign rashes occur in well, afebrile neonates with good feeding
Investigate only if systemic signs (fever, poor feeding, hepatosplenomegaly) are present
Avoid unnecessary topical steroids or antibiotics

severity_assessment.mild: Localized rash, well baby, feeding normally, afebrile — observation and reassurance only
severity_assessment.moderate: Widespread rash but baby otherwise well — close observation, consider dermatology review if persistent beyond 2 weeks
severity_assessment.severe: Rash with fever, vesicles/bullae, poor feeding, lethargy, or systemic signs — investigate for infection (CBC, CRP, blood culture, skin swab), consider NICU admission

monitoring_parameters.frequency: Daily by parents, at each OPD visit
If any new rash or systemic concern

diagnosis_name: Breast Masses, Vaginal Bleeding and Other Genital Anomalies in Neonates
counselling: These findings are common and usually hormonal / physiological — ये लक्षण आम हैं और आमतौर पर हार्मोन के कारण होते हैं
Breast swelling or milky discharge often resolves spontaneously — स्तन की सूजन या दूध जैसा रिसाव अपने आप ठीक हो जाता है
Do not squeeze or massage the breasts — स्तनों को दबाएं या मालिश न करें
First-week vaginal bleeding is usually self-limiting — पहले हफ्ते में योनि से खून आना आमतौर पर अपने आप रुक जाता है
Maintain gentle hygiene only; no internal cleaning — सिर्फ़ हल्की सफ़ाई रखें; अंदर की सफ़ाई न करें

Seek review if bleeding persists, discharge is foul-smelling, breast becomes red/tender, or genitalia appear unclear — अगर खून बंद न हो, बदबूदार रिसाव हो, स्तन लाल/दर्दनाक हो, या जननांग स्पष्ट न दिखें तो डॉक्टर को दिखाएं
warning_signs: Persistent vaginal bleeding beyond first week — पहले हफ्ते के बाद भी योनि से खून आना
Breast redness, warmth or pus — स्तन पर लालिमा, गर्मी या मवाद
Fever or poor feeding — बुखार या कम दूध पीना
Inguinal swelling that does not reduce — जांघ की सूजन जो कम न हो
Signs of salt loss: vomiting, poor weight gain, dehydration — नमक की कमी के संकेत: उल्टी, वज़न न बढ़ना, पानी की कमी
referral_criteria: Ambiguous genitalia
Persistent vaginal bleeding
Breast abscess / mastitis
Hard or non-reducible inguinal / labial mass
Suspected imperforate hymen / cloacal anomaly
Salt-loss symptoms or suspicion of CAH
expected_course: Neonatal breast enlargement resolves within 2-4 weeks as maternal hormones clear. Witch milk (galactorrhea) may persist for up to 2 months. First-week vaginal bleeding (estrogen withdrawal) resolves within 3-5 days. Mucoid vaginal discharge may persist for 2-4 weeks. All are self-limiting and benign.
key_clinical_points: Neonatal breast enlargement is due to transplacental maternal estrogen — never squeeze
Squeezing breasts can cause mastitis/abscess — educate caregivers strongly
Vaginal bleeding in first week is estrogen withdrawal — benign if small amount
Always examine for ambiguous genitalia — rule out CAH (check 17-OHP, electrolytes)
Imperforate hymen presents as bulging introitus — needs surgical referral
Breast abscess requires antibiotics and possibly drainage
Salt-losing CAH is a neonatal emergency — vomiting, dehydration, hyperkalemia

severity_assessment.mild: Bilateral breast swelling, clear/milky nipple discharge, small amount vaginal bleeding in first week — reassurance only
severity_assessment.moderate: Unilateral tender breast, persistent bleeding, prominent clitoris — investigation (CBC, 17-OHP, electrolytes), close follow-up
severity_assessment.severe: Breast abscess, ambiguous genitalia, salt loss symptoms, hard inguinal mass — urgent evaluation, specialist referral, possible NICU admission

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg IV loading over 10-15 min; additional 10 mg/kg if seizures persist (max 40)
Check and correct glucose, calcium, magnesium, sodium before/with anticonvulsants

second_line_drugs.notes: 40-60 mg/kg IV loading; maintenance 10 mg/kg IV q8h; fewer sedation side effects
20 mg/kg IV PE loading; second-line per ILAE if phenobarbital fails
0.05-0.15 mg/kg IV bolus then 0.1-0.4 mg/kg/h infusion for refractory seizures

investigations.name: Blood glucose
investigations.indication: Hypoglycemia is most common treatable cause

investigations.name: EEG or aEEG
investigations.indication: Confirm electrical seizure activity; guide treatment

counselling: Baby is having seizures which need immediate treatment to protect the brain
Seizures in newborns can look different from older children - subtle movements, lip smacking, or cycling movements
Blood tests will identify if low sugar, calcium, or infection is causing the seizures
Medicine (phenobarbital) will be given to stop and prevent seizures
Follow-up with a brain specialist (neurologist) and EEG testing is important
referral_criteria: Refer to neonatologist/neurologist; tertiary NICU for continuous EEG monitoring
hospitalisation_criteria: All neonatal seizures require NICU admission for monitoring and treatment
notes: Phenobarbital is first-line regardless of etiology (ILAE 2023). Confirm seizures with aEEG/EEG when possible. Treat underlying cause concurrently.
guideline_changes: ILAE 2023 guidelines confirm phenobarbital as first-line; levetiracetam has lower efficacy (28% vs 80%)

first_line_drugs.notes: Cool to 33.5-34.5C within 6h of birth; maintain x72h then rewarm 0.5C/h
Correct acidosis, maintain euglycemia, avoid hyperthermia, seizure monitoring

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg IV loading for seizures; additional 10 mg/kg if seizures persist
1000 IU/kg IV q24h x5d; neuroprotective adjunct under study; not yet standard
Cooling at 6-24h may provide small benefit if missed <6h window

investigations.name: Blood gas
investigations.indication: pH, lactate, base deficit for severity assessment

counselling: Baby's brain was injured due to lack of oxygen at birth; cooling therapy protects the brain
Baby will be cooled for 72 hours then slowly rewarmed; this is a proven treatment
Baby may have seizures which will be treated with medicines
Brain MRI at 4-7 days will help understand how much the brain was affected
Long-term developmental follow-up is essential; early intervention can greatly help
referral_criteria: Refer to tertiary NICU with therapeutic hypothermia capability within 6 hours of birth; neurology and developmental follow-up
hospitalisation_criteria: All moderate-severe HIE require NICU admission; therapeutic hypothermia must start within 6 hours
notes: Eligible: >=36 wk GA with moderate-severe HIE. Requires continuous neuromonitoring (aEEG) and MRI at 4-7 days. Mild HIE not cooled outside trials.
guideline_changes: AAP 2026 clinical report: hypothermia may benefit if started 6-24h post-birth; mild HIE still not indicated outside trials

counselling: Spitting up is common and improves by 4–6 months
Avoid overfeeding and home remedies
Maintain proper feeding position and burping
Return immediately if green vomiting, fever, poor feeding, or weight loss
Monitor baby's growth and activity
warning_signs: Bilious (green/yellow) vomiting — हरी या पीली उल्टी
Projectile vomiting — तेज़ ज़ोर से उल्टी आना
Blood in vomit — उल्टी में खून आना
Failure to pass meconium within 24 hours — 24 घंटे में पहला मल न आना
Lethargy or poor feeding — सुस्ती या दूध न पीना
Dehydration (dry mouth, sunken eyes, no tears) — पानी की कमी
Fever or hypothermia — बुखार या शरीर का ठंडा पड़ना
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric surgeon if bilious vomiting, abdominal distension, or suspected intestinal obstruction. Refer to pediatric GI if persistent non-bilious vomiting not responding to conservative management.
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if bilious vomiting, projectile vomiting with dehydration, suspected surgical cause, sepsis, or failure to thrive.
notes: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is malrotation with volvulus until proven otherwise. Requires urgent upper GI contrast study.
source: NNF Guidelines 2024, Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics 22e
expected_course: Most functional vomiting (GER, overfeeding) resolves within days with positional and feeding modifications. Bilious or projectile vomiting requires urgent evaluation. Pyloric stenosis presents at 3-6 weeks with progressive projectile vomiting.
key_clinical_points: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is a surgical emergency until proven otherwise
Always check for abdominal distension and bowel sounds
Assess hydration status and weight change
Pyloric stenosis: non-bilious projectile vomiting at 3-6 weeks, olive-shaped mass
Sepsis can present as vomiting with lethargy in neonates

severity_assessment.mild: Occasional spit-up after feeds, gaining weight, no distress
severity_assessment.moderate: Frequent vomiting, some weight loss, mild dehydration, irritability
severity_assessment.severe: Bilious/projectile vomiting, significant dehydration, abdominal distension, lethargy

first_line_drugs.notes: OG/NG tube feeding for preterm <32-34 wks unable to coordinate suck-swallow
Add HMF when volume reaches 100 mL/kg/day in preterm infants
D10W + electrolytes; TPN if enteral feeds not tolerated >48-72h

second_line_drugs.notes: Feeding therapy and oral stimulation program for dysphagia
24 kcal/oz preterm formula if mother's milk unavailable

investigations.name: Blood glucose
investigations.indication: Rule out hypoglycemia contributing to poor feeding

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes
investigations.indication: Assess hydration status and electrolyte balance

investigations.name: Thyroid function test
investigations.indication: Rule out congenital hypothyroidism causing poor feeding

investigations.name: Modified barium swallow
investigations.indication: Evaluate swallowing mechanism if aspiration suspected

counselling: Premature babies often cannot suck and swallow well; this improves with time
Mother's own milk is the best food; keep expressing milk regularly even if baby is tube-fed
Baby will gradually move from tube feeds to breast/bottle as they grow stronger
Kangaroo care (skin-to-skin) helps baby learn to feed
Weight gain will be monitored closely; do not worry if it is slow initially
referral_criteria: Refer to feeding therapist/OT if poor feeding persists beyond expected corrected age; refer to gastroenterology if GERD or anatomical cause suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if weight loss >10% birth weight, signs of dehydration, or inability to tolerate any oral feeds
notes: Prioritize mother's own milk; human milk reduces NEC risk. Advance feeds by 10-30 mL/kg/day as tolerated.

diagnosis_name: Neonatal withdrawal symptoms from maternal drug use

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.04-0.08 mg/kg PO q3-4h; titrate by 0.04 mg/kg per Finnegan score; wean 10-20%/day
Eat-Sleep-Console model: swaddling, low-stimulation, rooming-in, breastfeeding

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.05-0.1 mg/kg PO q6h; longer acting; may reduce treatment duration
0.5-1 mcg/kg PO q4-6h as adjunct for refractory symptoms
Loading 20 mg/kg, maintenance 5 mg/kg/day; for polysubstance or non-opioid withdrawal

investigations.name: Meconium drug screen
investigations.indication: Detects exposure over longer window than urine

investigations.name: Electrolytes
investigations.indication: Dehydration from poor feeding and excessive losses

counselling: Baby is showing withdrawal symptoms from medicines/substances mother took during pregnancy
Keeping baby with mother (rooming-in) and breastfeeding help reduce withdrawal symptoms
Swaddling, quiet environment, and gentle handling comfort the baby
Medicine may be needed if baby cannot eat or sleep well despite comfort measures
This is not the baby's fault; the goal is to make baby comfortable while symptoms resolve
referral_criteria: Refer to neonatologist if high Finnegan scores requiring pharmacotherapy; social work involvement for family support
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for observation minimum 3-5 days; longer if pharmacotherapy needed (ESC model)
notes: Eat-Sleep-Console approach reduces pharmacotherapy use by 50%. Prioritize rooming-in and breastfeeding (if not contraindicated).
guideline_changes: Eat-Sleep-Console (ESC) approach replacing Finnegan scoring at many centers; reduces pharmacotherapy need

first_line_drugs.notes: NS 10-20 mL/kg IV over 15-30 min via UVC; repeat up to 40-60 mL/kg total
0.01-0.03 mg/kg IV (0.1-0.3 mL/kg of 1:10,000) via UVC; repeat q3-5 min
Effective PPV is the most critical step; initiate within 60 seconds of birth

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-20 mcg/kg/min IV infusion for fluid-refractory hypotension
O-negative 10-20 mL/kg for hemorrhagic shock; uncrossmatched if emergent
1-2 mg/kg IV for vasopressor-resistant shock; adrenal insufficiency

investigations.name: Blood gas (arterial)
investigations.indication: Lactate, pH, and base deficit to assess shock severity

investigations.name: CBC with differential
investigations.indication: Hemoglobin for hemorrhagic shock; WBC for sepsis

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Evaluate cardiac function and etiology of shock

counselling: Baby was very sick at birth and needed emergency resuscitation
Baby will be monitored closely in the NICU for several days
Multiple medicines and fluids through IV are needed to support blood pressure and circulation
Cause of the shock will be investigated and treated specifically
Recovery depends on the underlying cause; doctor will update you regularly
referral_criteria: Immediate NICU/PICU care; cardiology if cardiogenic shock suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require immediate NICU admission; life-threatening emergency
notes: Identify shock etiology: hypovolemic, cardiogenic, or distributive. Effective ventilation is the single most important intervention in neonatal resuscitation.
snomed_display: Fetal and/or neonatal disorder of respiratory system

first_line_drugs.notes: Early intervention PT/OT/Speech starting from infancy; individualized goals
10-30 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for associated seizures; titrate over 2-4 wks
Ongoing developmental support; fine motor and adaptive skill training

second_line_drugs.notes: 3-5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for seizures in infants
Surgical placement if oral feeding unsafe; for severe cases with dysphagia

investigations.name: TORCH screening
investigations.indication: Rule out congenital infection (CMV, Zika, toxoplasmosis)

counselling: Child's head is smaller than normal which may affect development
Early intervention (physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy) is the most important treatment
There is no medicine to grow the head, but therapy helps the child reach their best potential
Seizures may develop; if child has jerking movements or staring spells, report immediately
Join a support group for families of children with developmental conditions; many resources are available
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist and genetics at diagnosis; early intervention program
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for seizure control, feeding difficulties requiring tube placement, or acute illness in compromised child
notes: No treatment reverses microcephaly; management is supportive with early intervention services. Seizure control and nutritional support are key priorities.
guideline_changes: CDC/AAP updated developmental milestone surveillance tools in 2022

first_line_drugs.notes: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt with programmable valve; antibiotic-impregnated catheter recommended (Level I)
Endoscopic third ventriculostomy; preferred in obstructive hydrocephalus >6 months age

second_line_drugs.notes: ETV with choroid plexus cauterization; lower shunt dependency rates in selected patients
25-100 mg/kg/day PO divided TID; temporary CSF reduction; NOT recommended for post-hemorrhagic

investigations.name: MRI brain with CSF flow study
investigations.indication: Determine type and severity of hydrocephalus

counselling: Watch for signs of increased head pressure: vomiting, excessive sleepiness, bulging fontanelle, sunset eyes
Keep regular follow-up appointments for head circumference measurement
If a shunt is placed, learn the signs of shunt blockage: headache, vomiting, drowsiness
Avoid rough play or contact sports that could damage the shunt
Development may be delayed; early intervention therapy helps your child reach their potential
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurosurgeon at diagnosis for surgical planning; ophthalmology for papilledema screening
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for acute raised ICP symptoms, shunt malfunction or infection, pre-operative preparation for VP shunt or ETV
notes: Antibiotic-impregnated shunt tubing reduces infection risk (Level I evidence). ETV success depends on age and etiology; higher failure rates in premature and very young infants.
guideline_changes: 2024 narrative review supports increasing ETV+CPC use over VP shunt in select patients

first_line_drugs.notes: Individualized surgical approach based on malformation type; e.g. Chiari decompression with duraplasty
10-30 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for associated seizures
Early intervention PT/OT/Speech to maximize cognitive and motor function

second_line_drugs.notes: For associated hydrocephalus; programmable valve preferred
15-30 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for seizures; monitor LFTs

investigations.name: Brain MRI
investigations.indication: Detailed structural assessment of the malformation

investigations.name: Genetic testing
investigations.indication: Identify underlying genetic cause; guide counseling

counselling: Child has a brain structure abnormality present from birth
Management depends on the specific type; the neurologist will explain the treatment plan
Early intervention therapies are essential to maximize development
Some children may need surgery (shunt for fluid, decompression)
Long-term follow-up with multiple specialists is needed; keep all appointments
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurology and neurosurgery at diagnosis; genetics for syndromic evaluation
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for raised intracranial pressure, hydrocephalus, intractable seizures, or surgical intervention
notes: Management is highly individualized based on specific malformation and severity. Multidisciplinary team including neurosurgery, neurology, genetics, and rehabilitation is essential.

first_line_drugs.notes: Jatene procedure; anatomic repair switching aorta and PA with coronary reimplantation; within first 2 weeks of life
0.01-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV continuous; maintains ductal patency for systemic-pulmonary mixing pre-surgery
Rashkind procedure; emergent catheter-based creation of ASD to improve mixing; for severe cyanosis

second_line_drugs.notes: Atrial switch operation; historical alternative now reserved for late-presenting or complex anatomy cases
For d-TGA with VSD and PS; LV-to-aorta baffle + RV-to-PA conduit

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Confirm diagnosis, assess coronary pattern, and associated lesions

counselling: Your baby needs surgery within the first 2 weeks of life - this is urgent but has excellent outcomes
The baby will be in NICU/PICU before and after surgery
After arterial switch operation, most children lead completely normal active lives
Lifelong cardiology follow-up is required to monitor coronary arteries and heart valve function
Ensure all routine vaccinations are given on schedule after recovery
referral_criteria: Emergent referral to pediatric cardiac surgery centre at prenatal or postnatal diagnosis; lifelong cardiology follow-up
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require immediate NICU admission; PGE1 to maintain ductal patency; Rashkind septostomy if severely cyanotic; arterial switch surgery within 2 weeks
notes: Arterial switch must be performed within 2-4 weeks before LV deconditions in low-pressure pulmonary circuit. Lifelong follow-up required for coronary and neo-aortic root complications.
source: AHA/ACC CHD Guidelines, ACC 2025 ACHD Guideline

first_line_drugs.notes: Small restrictive VSDs; many close spontaneously in first year; serial echo q6-12mo monitoring
For moderate-large VSDs with CHF, Qp:Qs >2, or PA hypertension; via sternotomy with CPB
1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; first-line diuretic for CHF symptoms from large L-to-R shunt

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q8h; afterload reduction for symptomatic CHF management
1-3 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; potassium-sparing diuretic adjunct for CHF
Amplatzer muscular VSD occluder for large muscular VSDs; percutaneous or hybrid approach
5-10 mcg/kg/day PO divided q12h; for symptomatic CHF with large shunt pre-surgery

investigations.name: Echocardiography with Doppler
investigations.indication: Confirm diagnosis, assess shunt size, and PA pressure

investigations.name: ECG
investigations.indication: LV hypertrophy (moderate VSD) or biventricular hypertrophy (large VSD)

investigations.name: Cardiac catheterisation
investigations.indication: Assess PVR before closure if pulmonary hypertension suspected

counselling: Many small VSDs close on their own during the first year of life
Watch for fast breathing during feeds, poor weight gain, or excessive sweating - report to doctor
If surgery is needed, it has excellent outcomes; most children recover fully
Extra calories may be needed as heart has to work harder - your doctor will guide feeding
Regular echo follow-up is important to track the size of the hole
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist for all VSDs; refer to cardiac surgery if large VSD with heart failure, Qp:Qs >2, or rising PA pressure
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if heart failure (poor feeding, tachypnea, hepatomegaly), planned surgical or device closure, or signs of pulmonary hypertension
notes: Large VSDs with elevated PA pressure must be closed early to prevent irreversible pulmonary vascular obstructive disease. Perimembranous and outlet VSDs require surgical closure.
source: AHA/ACC 2024 CHD Management, ACC VSD Algorithm
guideline_changes: Transcatheter VSD closure devices gaining wider use for muscular defects

first_line_drugs.notes: Small ASDs (<5mm) in children <2yr; many close spontaneously; serial echo monitoring q6-12mo
Percutaneous Amplatzer or GORE occluder for secundum ASD ≥5mm with RV volume overload; via femoral vein
Open repair via sternotomy with CPB for large, sinus venosus, or primum ASDs not amenable to device

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; for symptomatic volume overload pre-intervention
5-10 mcg/kg/day PO divided q12h; for RV failure symptoms if present pre-operatively

investigations.name: Echocardiography with Doppler
investigations.indication: Confirm diagnosis, assess shunt size and RV dilation

investigations.name: ECG
investigations.indication: Right axis deviation, rSR pattern in V1

investigations.name: Cardiac catheterisation
investigations.indication: Pre-device closure to assess PVR if pulmonary hypertension suspected

counselling: Many small ASDs close on their own in the first 2 years - regular echo monitoring is needed
The child can usually participate in normal physical activities unless doctor advises otherwise
Watch for symptoms like easy tiredness, frequent respiratory infections, or poor weight gain
If device closure or surgery is needed, it has excellent outcomes with low risk
Keep all routine vaccinations up to date including annual influenza vaccine
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist for all detected ASDs; refer to cardiac surgery/interventional cardiology if significant shunt (Qp:Qs >1.5) with RV dilation
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if heart failure symptoms, planned device closure or surgical repair, paradoxical embolism, or arrhythmia (atrial fibrillation/flutter)
notes: Closure is recommended if significant L-to-R shunt (Qp:Qs >1.5) with RV dilation. Device closure has low morbidity and avoids sternotomy for eligible secundum defects.
guideline_changes: Transcatheter device closure increasingly preferred over surgery for secundum ASDs

first_line_drugs.notes: VSD patch closure + RVOT reconstruction; typically performed at 3-6 months of age

investigations.name: Cardiac catheterisation

counselling: Your child has a combination of 4 heart defects that will need surgery, usually around 3-6 months
Blue spells (tet spells) may happen - hold baby in knee-chest position and go to hospital immediately
Ensure adequate hydration and avoid prolonged crying which can trigger spells
Surgery has excellent outcomes; most children lead normal lives after repair
Lifelong cardiology follow-up is required even after successful repair
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiac surgeon at diagnosis for surgical planning; urgently if symptomatic cyanosis or hypercyanotic spells
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if hypercyanotic (tet) spell, oxygen saturation <75%, severe cyanosis, planned surgical repair, or need for palliative shunt

first_line_drugs.notes: First-line for moderate-severe valvar PS (gradient >40mmHg); percutaneous via femoral vein; excellent outcomes
Mild PS (gradient <40mmHg) requires no intervention; serial echo q1-2yr to monitor progression
