counselling: Mild pulmonary stenosis usually does not need treatment and is monitored with regular echo
If balloon procedure is needed, it is done through a small tube in the leg vein - very safe
Child can usually participate in normal activities; severe PS may need exercise restriction
Regular cardiology follow-up is needed even after successful balloon valvuloplasty
Watch for increasing tiredness, bluish discolouration, or fainting with exertion

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if critical PS in neonate (PGE1 dependent), planned balloon valvuloplasty, right heart failure, or significant cyanosis
notes: Balloon valvuloplasty is curative in most cases with excellent long-term outcomes. Patients with Noonan or Williams syndromes have lower success rates with balloon and may need surgical repair.

first_line_drugs.notes: Percutaneous; indicated for critical valvar AS in neonates who are ductal-dependent regardless of gradient

investigations.indication: Assess cardiac silhouette and pulmonary vascularity

counselling: Your child has a heart valve problem that needs regular cardiology follow-up
Watch for symptoms like difficulty breathing during feeds, poor weight gain, or bluish skin
Maintain good dental hygiene to prevent heart valve infections (endocarditis)
Ensure all routine vaccinations including influenza are up to date
Some children may need surgery or catheter procedures - your cardiologist will guide timing

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if critical aortic stenosis in neonate (ductal-dependent), acute heart failure, syncope with exertion, or planned catheterisation/surgical intervention

diagnosis_name: Congenital malformation of cardiac chambers and connections, unspecified

counselling: Your child has a heart condition that requires further testing to understand fully
Keep all cardiology appointments and scheduled echocardiograms
Watch for fast breathing, poor feeding, excessive sweating, or bluish discolouration
All routine vaccinations should be given on schedule unless specifically advised otherwise
Lifelong cardiology follow-up is important even after any corrective procedures

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if symptomatic heart failure, cyanosis, arrhythmia, need for diagnostic cardiac catheterisation, or planned surgical/interventional procedure

counselling: PDA is a heart condition where a blood vessel that should close after birth stays open
Medicine (ibuprofen or paracetamol) is tried first to close the PDA
If medicine does not work, a small procedure or surgery may be needed
Watch for fast breathing, poor feeding, or excessive sweating during feeds — these suggest the PDA is significant
Many small PDAs close on their own and may just need monitoring
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist for echocardiography-confirmed hemodynamically significant PDA; interventional cardiology if catheter closure considered
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for hemodynamically significant PDA with heart failure, initiation of pharmacological closure, or transcatheter/surgical closure
notes: Treat only hemodynamically significant PDA; echo-guided assessment essential. Early closure <14 days not shown to improve outcomes per AAP 2025.
guideline_changes: AAP 2025 report: prophylactic treatment no longer recommended; acetaminophen now recognized option

first_line_drugs.notes: Coarctectomy with extended end-to-end anastomosis; primary technique in neonates and infants

After repair, lifelong blood pressure monitoring is essential - check BP in both arms
Regular cardiology follow-up is needed even after successful surgery
Report any headache, nosebleeds, or leg pain with exercise to the cardiologist
Maintain good dental hygiene for endocarditis prevention

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if neonatal critical coarctation (PGE1 needed), heart failure, planned surgical or catheter intervention, or hypertensive emergency

guideline_changes: 2024 STS clinical practice guidelines published for neonatal/infant coarctation management

counselling: Most great vein anomalies are discovered incidentally and do not cause problems
Regular cardiology follow-up is important to monitor for any changes
If your child has a LSVC, it is important to inform doctors before any central line placement or pacemaker
Surgery is only needed if the anomaly is causing symptoms
Keep all routine vaccinations on schedule

counselling: This is a serious heart condition requiring urgent surgery - your baby will be in NICU/PICU
After surgery, regular cardiology follow-up is essential to monitor for vein narrowing
Watch for fast breathing, difficulty feeding, or bluish skin and report immediately
Ensure all vaccinations including RSV prophylaxis are given as recommended
Long-term outcomes are generally good after successful surgical repair
referral_criteria: Emergent referral to pediatric cardiac surgeon at diagnosis; ongoing follow-up with pediatric cardiologist for pulmonary vein stenosis surveillance

counselling: Bilateral choanal atresia is a breathing emergency in newborns — the baby cannot breathe through the nose
An oral airway will be used temporarily until surgery can be done
After surgery, nasal drops and suctioning will be needed to keep the airway open
Watch for noisy breathing, feeding difficulty, or blue spells and report immediately
Follow-up endoscopy will be needed to check for re-narrowing of the nasal passages
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric ENT surgeon; emergency if bilateral atresia in neonate (obligate nasal breather)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all bilateral cases immediately (neonatal emergency); unilateral cases may be managed electively but admit if feeding/breathing difficulty
notes: Bilateral atresia is a neonatal emergency as newborns are obligate nasal breathers. Unilateral cases may be repaired electively.

first_line_drugs.notes: Primary palate repair at 9-12 months; Furlow or von Langenbeck technique; before speech development
Palatal obturator if needed; specialized bottles; upright positioning; dietitian monitoring growth
PE tubes at time of palate repair or earlier for chronic OME; audiometry follow-up q6mo

second_line_drugs.notes: Begin by age 12-18 mo post-repair; target velopharyngeal competence; nasometry assessment at age 3-4
Secondary surgery for velopharyngeal insufficiency if persistent after age 4; improves speech
Mixed dentition stage (age 7-11); iliac crest bone graft to alveolar cleft for canine eruption
For symptomatic oronasal fistulae post-palatoplasty; local flaps or obturator

counselling: Cleft palate repair surgery is done around 9-12 months to help with speech development
Use special feeding bottles and hold baby upright during feeds to prevent milk going into the nose
Ear infections are common; your child will need regular hearing tests
Speech therapy after surgery is very important for clear speech
Multiple specialists will see your child as part of the cleft team over many years
referral_criteria: Refer to multidisciplinary cleft team at birth: plastic surgery, ENT, audiology, speech-language pathology, orthodontics
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for palatoplasty, PE tube insertion, and any secondary surgeries (pharyngeal flap, bone graft)
notes: Timing of palate repair balances speech outcomes (earlier is better) against maxillary growth (later preserves growth). Interdisciplinary cleft team follow-up is required through adolescence.
source: ACPA 2024 Parameters of Care, NEJM 2023 Timing Study
guideline_changes: NEJM 2023 RCT showed 6-month palate repair noninferior to 12-month repair for speech outcomes

first_line_drugs.notes: Millard or Fisher technique at 3-6 months; rule of 10s (10 wks, 10 lb, Hb 10g/dL); under GA
Pre-surgical orthodontic device from 1-2 wks of age; narrows cleft, molds nose; improves surgical result
Specialized bottles (Haberman, Dr. Brown's); lactation consultant; upright feeding position

second_line_drugs.notes: Preliminary procedure at 2-4 wks for wide clefts to approximate lip segments before definitive repair
Nasal revision at age 4-6 or after skeletal maturity; addresses residual nasal asymmetry
Scar revision and vermilion adjustment if needed; typically deferred until school age or later

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Rule out associated cardiac defects if syndromic

counselling: Cleft lip is a common birth defect that is fully correctable with surgery
Surgery to repair the lip is usually done around 3-6 months of age
Use specialized feeding bottles as shown; your baby can feed well with the right technique
Nasoalveolar molding (NAM) device before surgery gives better results if started early
Your child will need follow-up with the cleft team for several years but the results are excellent
referral_criteria: Refer to multidisciplinary cleft team at birth: plastic surgery, ENT, speech therapy, orthodontics, audiology
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for lip repair surgery (day surgery or 1-2 nights), and for any revision surgeries
notes: Multidisciplinary cleft team management from birth through adulthood is the standard of care. NAM if started within first 2 weeks significantly improves primary surgical outcomes.
source: ACPA 2024 Parameters of Care, WHO Smile Train Guidelines
guideline_changes: 2024 ACPA update emphasizes nasoalveolar molding (NAM) pre-surgical orthodontics

first_line_drugs.notes: Lip repair at 3-6 mo, palate repair at 9-12 mo; NAM from birth if available; PE tubes at palatoplasty
Custom appliance from 1-2 wks; molds cleft lip, alveolus, and nose pre-surgically; improves symmetry
Specialized bottles; upright positioning; weight monitoring; supplemental NG feeds if growth faltering
ABR at birth; tympanometry regularly; PE tubes for chronic OME; hearing aids if sensorineural loss

second_line_drugs.notes: At mixed dentition (age 7-11); iliac crest autograft; enables canine eruption and stabilizes arch
Pharyngeal flap or sphincter pharyngoplasty for VPI; after age 4 if speech therapy insufficient
Le Fort I advancement at skeletal maturity (age 16-18) for midface hypoplasia; orthodontics pre/post
Final nasal reconstruction after skeletal maturity; septorhinoplasty for residual deformity

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: If associated syndrome suspected (Pierre Robin, velocardiofacial)

investigations.name: Genetic evaluation
investigations.indication: If dysmorphic features suggest a syndrome

counselling: Your baby has both cleft lip and palate which will need staged surgeries over several years
Lip repair is done around 3-6 months and palate repair around 9-12 months
Feeding support with special bottles is needed from birth - your cleft nurse will teach you
Your child will need speech therapy, dental care, and hearing checks throughout childhood
With proper care, children with cleft lip and palate grow up to live completely normal lives
referral_criteria: Refer to multidisciplinary cleft team at birth; ENT, audiology, speech therapy, orthodontics, and psychosocial support
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for staged surgical repairs (lip, palate, bone graft, secondary procedures), PE tube insertion, and any revision surgeries
notes: Combined cleft lip and palate requires the most complex staged surgical protocol with 5+ procedures over 18 years. Multidisciplinary team follow-up from birth through adulthood is mandatory.
source: ACPA 2024 Parameters of Care, WHO/Smile Train Protocol
guideline_changes: 2024 ACPA emphasizes integrated NAM + staged surgical protocol; earlier palate repair gaining evidence

counselling: Tongue tie is a common cause of breastfeeding difficulty in newborns — जीभ का बंधन (टंग टाई) स्तनपान में कठिनाई का आम कारण है
Mild tongue tie without feeding issues may only need observation — हल्के टंग टाई में सिर्फ़ निगरानी काफ़ी हो सकती है
Frenotomy is generally safe, quick, and effective when indicated — फ्रेनोटॉमी सुरक्षित, जल्दी और असरदार प्रक्रिया है
Most babies feed better soon after release procedure — अधिकतर बच्चे प्रक्रिया के बाद बेहतर दूध पीते हैं
Simple anterior tongue tie usually does not require general anesthesia — साधारण टंग टाई में बेहोशी की ज़रूरत नहीं होती
Seek review if feeding remains poor or bleeding occurs after procedure — अगर दूध पीने में सुधार न हो या खून आए तो दोबारा दिखाएं
warning_signs: Persistent poor latch despite correct positioning — सही स्थिति में भी दूध सही से न पकड़ पाना
Inadequate weight gain — वज़न पर्याप्त न बढ़ना
Maternal nipple trauma or bleeding — माँ के निप्पल में दर्द या खून
Heart-shaped or notched tongue tip — जीभ का दिल जैसा आकार
Clicking sounds during feeds — दूध पीते समय क्लिक की आवाज़
expected_course: Mild anterior tongue tie without feeding difficulty may not require intervention. If frenotomy is performed, immediate improvement in latch is often seen. Post-procedure, babies can breastfeed right away. Posterior tongue tie (Type 3-4) may require ENT or specialist referral. Speech issues may become apparent later if untreated.
key_clinical_points: Always assess feeding function, not just appearance of frenulum
Coryllos classification helps guide management decisions
Type 1-2 (anterior) often amenable to office frenotomy
Type 3-4 (posterior/submucosal) may need specialist assessment
Maternal lactation support is essential alongside surgical intervention
Re-attachment of frenulum can occur; tongue exercises may help prevent this

severity_assessment.mild: Short frenulum visible but baby feeding well, gaining weight, no maternal nipple pain — observe and reassure
severity_assessment.moderate: Feeding difficulty with poor latch, slow feeds, maternal pain — consider frenotomy, optimize positioning
severity_assessment.severe: Failure to thrive, significant maternal nipple trauma, posterior thick tie — urgent frenotomy or specialist referral

first_line_drugs.notes: Primary anastomosis via thoracotomy if gap allows; staged if long-gap EA
Continuous low suction of upper pouch to prevent aspiration
NPO with TPN support until surgical repair and feeding tolerance

second_line_drugs.notes: G-tube for feeding if delayed primary repair (long-gap EA)
Serial traction sutures for long-gap EA to achieve primary anastomosis

counselling: Esophageal atresia means the food pipe is not connected — surgery is needed to repair it
A Replogle tube in the baby's throat continuously suctions saliva to prevent choking
The baby will be fed through IV or a tube initially; oral feeding begins after surgical repair heals
After repair, the baby may have reflux — positioning and medications help manage this
Long-term follow-up with a pediatric surgeon and GI specialist is needed for years

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit immediately at birth; NICU care with Replogle tube, NPO, IV fluids, and surgical planning

Baby cannot be fed by mouth until after surgical repair

counselling: Pyloric stenosis means the stomach outlet is too narrow — surgery (pyloromyotomy) is the cure
Surgery is safe with excellent outcomes — feeding resumes within hours after surgery
Before surgery, IV fluids will correct the baby's dehydration and electrolyte imbalance
After surgery, start with small frequent feeds and gradually increase
Watch for vomiting after discharge — if persistent projectile vomiting returns, contact the surgeon

Stabilize with IV fluids and TPN preoperatively and postoperatively

second_line_drugs.notes: Alternative bypass if duodeno-duodenostomy not feasible

counselling: Duodenal atresia means the baby's intestine is blocked — surgery is the only treatment
The baby will have a nasogastric tube to drain the stomach before surgery
After surgery, feeding starts slowly through a tube, then orally as the baby tolerates
Watch for vomiting, abdominal distension, or feeding intolerance after surgery and report
Screen for Down syndrome (30% association) and cardiac anomalies is recommended

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit immediately after birth for surgical planning; NPO with NGT decompression and IV fluids/TPN
notes: Classic double-bubble sign on X-ray. Screen for Down syndrome (30% association) and cardiac anomalies.

first_line_drugs.notes: Resection of atretic segment with primary anastomosis; taper if needed

Breastmilk can be started slowly once the doctor gives permission

Follow up regularly to monitor growth and nutrition after discharge

hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require NICU admission for surgical management

investigations.indication: Confirm level and type of atresia

counselling: Surgery is necessary and cannot be delayed; the blocked portion of intestine must be removed
Baby will be on IV nutrition (drip feeding) until the intestine heals and milk feeds can restart
Recovery takes 2-4 weeks; baby will stay in NICU during this time
Watch for vomiting, abdominal distension, or green-colored vomit after discharge and report immediately
Long-term growth and feeding are usually normal after successful surgery
referral_criteria: Immediate referral to pediatric surgeon at birth upon diagnosis of intestinal atresia
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require hospitalization; this is a surgical emergency requiring NICU admission
notes: Multiple atresias possible; preserve bowel length to prevent short bowel syndrome. Screen for cystic fibrosis if meconium ileus coexists.

diagnosis_name: Congenital absence, atresia and stenosis of anus with fistula

first_line_drugs.notes: PSARP or primary anoplasty based on fistula type and severity
Diverting colostomy for complex malformations; definitive repair at 3-6 months

second_line_drugs.notes: Preop stabilization with IV fluids, NPO, NGT decompression
Perioperative broad-spectrum antibiotics; ampicillin + gentamicin

investigations.name: Invertogram/cross-table lateral X-ray
investigations.indication: Determine level of atresia (high vs low)

counselling: Your baby has an anorectal malformation — surgery is needed to create a normal anal opening
A colostomy (temporary stoma) may be needed first, with definitive surgery at 3-6 months
Learning to care for the colostomy will be taught before discharge
After definitive repair, regular anal dilatations may be needed for several months
Long-term follow-up for bowel function is important — constipation and soiling may need management
referral_criteria: Refer immediately to pediatric surgeon at birth; screen for VACTERL association (vertebral, cardiac, renal, limb anomalies)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit at birth for surgical evaluation and management; emergency colostomy if high malformation without perineal fistula
notes: Perineal fistula can undergo primary repair without colostomy. Screen for VACTERL association with echo, renal US, spine imaging.
snomed_display: Congenital occlusion of anus with fistula

first_line_drugs.notes: Soave, Swenson, or Duhamel; single-stage preferred. Laparoscopic or transanal approach
20 mL/kg NS via rectal catheter BID-TID preoperatively to decompress

second_line_drugs.notes: Staged approach for severe enterocolitis or total colonic aganglionosis
30 mg/kg/day IV/PO divided TID x7-14 days for Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis
Intrasphincteric injection for postop obstructive symptoms; 1-4 units/kg per session

investigations.name: Rectal suction biopsy
investigations.indication: Gold standard — absence of ganglion cells confirms diagnosis

investigations.name: Anorectal manometry
investigations.indication: Absent rectoanal inhibitory reflex supports diagnosis

investigations.name: Abdominal X-ray
investigations.indication: Dilated proximal bowel, assess for enterocolitis

counselling: Your child has Hirschsprung disease — a section of the bowel lacks nerves and cannot push stool through
Surgery is needed to remove the abnormal part and connect the healthy bowel
Rectal irrigations (washing) will be done daily until surgery to prevent dangerous bowel swelling
After surgery, the child may have loose or frequent stools initially — this usually improves over months
Come back urgently if the child develops: fever with distended belly (enterocolitis), vomiting, or bloody stools
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery mandatory for definitive surgical management. GI for post-surgical bowel management. Genetics counselling (RET gene testing in family). Neonatology if diagnosed in newborn period
hospitalisation_criteria: New diagnosis for stabilization and rectal irrigations. Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis (HAEC) — medical emergency. Pre-operative preparation. Post-operative care
notes: Definitive treatment is surgical. Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis (HAEC) is a life-threatening complication requiring urgent rectal irrigations and IV antibiotics.
guideline_changes: 2023 NASPGHAN guidance on postsurgical defecation issues; trend toward single-stage pull-through

second_line_drugs.notes: 250-1500 IU IM 2x/wk for 4 wk; limited role; not recommended by AUA for unilateral UDT
Nasal spray 1.2 mg/day for 4 wk; limited efficacy; not widely recommended

counselling: Condition is common in newborn males — नवजात लड़कों में यह आम समस्या है
Most cases resolve by 6 months — अधिकतर 6 महीने तक अपने आप ठीक हो जाती है
Surgery is simple and safe if required — ज़रूरत हो तो ऑपरेशन सरल और सुरक्षित है
Early surgery improves fertility — जल्दी ऑपरेशन से प्रजनन क्षमता बेहतर रहती है
Cancer risk is low but higher than normal — कैंसर का खतरा कम है पर सामान्य से ज़्यादा
Regular follow-up till puberty is essential — यौवन तक नियमित जांच ज़रूरी है
warning_signs: Bilateral non-palpable testes — दोनों तरफ़ अंडकोष न मिलना
Empty scrotum with ambiguous genitalia — खाली अंडकोष थैली और अस्पष्ट जननांग
Associated inguinal hernia — साथ में हर्निया होना
Ascending testis (previously normal) — पहले सामान्य था पर अब ऊपर चला गया
Testis not descended by 6 months — 6 महीने तक अंडकोष नीचे न आना
guideline_changes: AUA 2025 guideline update reinforces early orchidopexy by 12 months
expected_course: Spontaneous descent occurs in most cases by 6 months of age, especially in term infants. If testis has not descended by 6 months, it is unlikely to descend spontaneously. Orchidopexy is recommended between 6-18 months for best fertility and malignancy outcomes. Retractile testis needs annual monitoring as it may become ascending.
key_clinical_points: Examine in warm room with calm, relaxed infant for accurate assessment
Two-handed technique: one hand from iliac crest along canal, other hand palpates scrotum
Differentiate retractile (can be brought down and stays) from true UDT (springs back)
Bilateral non-palpable testes require urgent karyotype and hormonal workup to exclude DSD
No role of routine hormonal therapy (hCG) — surgery is the definitive treatment
Orchidopexy by 18 months optimizes fertility and reduces malignancy risk

severity_assessment.mild: Unilateral palpable UDT in inguinal canal, term infant <6 months — observe, follow-up at 6 months
severity_assessment.moderate: Persistent unilateral UDT at 6 months, palpable — refer for orchidopexy (6-18 months)
severity_assessment.severe: Bilateral non-palpable testes or UDT with ambiguous genitalia — urgent pediatric surgery and endocrine evaluation

monitoring_parameters.frequency: Every 3 months until surgery or descent

first_line_drugs.notes: Urethroplasty at 6-18 months of age; technique based on severity (TIP, Mathieu, or staged)
Topical DHT cream or IM testosterone 2 mg/kg q4wk x2-3 doses preop for small glans/proximal hypospadias

second_line_drugs.notes: 250-1000 IU IM 1-2x/wk for 5-6 wk preop; stimulates local testosterone for penile growth
Bupivacaine 0.25% 1 mL/kg caudal block periop for analgesia; reduces opioid need

investigations.name: Karyotype
investigations.indication: Only if severe hypospadias with undescended testes (DSD evaluation)

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Screen for associated urinary tract anomalies in severe cases

counselling: Child's urinary opening is not at the normal position; surgery will correct this
Surgery is usually done between 6-18 months of age when the baby is healthy
Do NOT get the child circumcised before surgery; the foreskin may be used for repair
After surgery, a catheter (tube) will drain urine for a few days
Follow-up visits are important to check for complications like narrowing of the new opening
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist by 6 months of age for surgical planning
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for day-care or short-stay surgery; overnight observation for proximal hypospadias repair
notes: Optimal surgical age is 6-18 months. Long-term follow-up is essential to detect stricture, voiding dysfunction, or recurrent curvature.
guideline_changes: 2024 EAU/ESPU Part II update on hypospadias surgical outcomes and follow-up

diagnosis_name: Renal agenesis and other reduction defects of kidney

first_line_drugs.notes: Yearly BP, urine albumin-creatinine ratio; serial renal US to confirm compensatory hypertrophy
Enalapril 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO if hypertension or proteinuria develops; target BP <75th percentile

second_line_drugs.notes: Losartan 0.7-1.4 mg/kg/day PO once daily if ACEi not tolerated; renoprotective
Moderate protein intake, adequate hydration, avoid nephrotoxins; no routine sports restriction per AAP

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Confirm solitary kidney; assess compensatory hypertrophy

investigations.name: Blood pressure (ambulatory)
investigations.indication: Annual ABPM for early hypertension detection

investigations.name: VCUG
investigations.indication: Screen for VUR in contralateral kidney if UTI history

counselling: Child has only one kidney (or a very small kidney); most people live normal lives with one kidney
Regular blood pressure checks and urine tests are important to protect the remaining kidney
Encourage adequate water intake; avoid dehydration
Contact sports with risk of abdominal injury should be discussed with the doctor
Medicine may be needed if blood pressure rises or protein appears in urine
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist for long-term monitoring; genetics if bilateral or syndromic
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for hypertensive emergency, acute kidney injury, or severe UTI
notes: Solitary kidney predisposes to hypertension and proteinuria over time. Ambulatory BP monitoring preferred; multidisciplinary follow-up for complex CAKUT.
guideline_changes: 2022 Italian consensus on congenital solitary kidney management published

first_line_drugs.notes: Target BP <50th-75th percentile; ACEi (enalapril 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO) or ARB preferred
Serial renal US, eGFR, urine albumin-creatinine ratio q6-12mo; Mayo Imaging Classification for risk

second_line_drugs.notes: V2-receptor antagonist; slows cyst growth in ADPKD; pediatric trials ongoing; adult dose 45-90 mg/d
Shown to slow total kidney volume growth in children with ADPKD; dose per age-based statin protocols
Emerging cyst-modifying agent; IMPEDE-PKD trial ongoing; not yet standard pediatric use

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Monitor cyst burden and kidney size

counselling: Child has cysts (fluid-filled sacs) in the kidneys; this is a lifelong condition
Blood pressure control is the most important treatment to protect kidneys
Regular check-ups with blood tests and ultrasound are essential
Adequate water intake is important; encourage child to drink plenty of fluids
Genetic counseling is recommended as this condition can be inherited
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist at diagnosis; genetics counseling for family
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for hypertensive emergency, acute kidney injury, or infected cyst
notes: BP control with RAS blockade is cornerstone of renoprotection. Novel disease-modifying agents (tolvaptan, metformin) are under active investigation in pediatric ADPKD.
guideline_changes: KDIGO 2025 first guideline on ADPKD; tolvaptan and metformin trials ongoing in children

diagnosis_name: Congenital obstructive defects of renal pelvis and congenital malformations of ureter

first_line_drugs.notes: Serial renal US q3-6mo for mild-moderate hydronephrosis; majority resolve spontaneously
Open or laparoscopic surgical repair of UPJ obstruction if worsening function or SFU grade 3-4

second_line_drugs.notes: Amoxicillin 10-15 mg/kg PO daily or TMP-SMX 2 mg/kg PO daily for high-grade HN
Robotic-assisted laparoscopic approach; emerging in older children with UPJ obstruction
Surgical correction for obstructive megaureter or ureteral malposition if symptomatic

investigations.name: MAG3 diuretic renogram
investigations.indication: Assess drainage and differential renal function

investigations.name: Serum creatinine
investigations.indication: Monitor renal function in bilateral disease

counselling: Baby has a blockage or narrowing in the kidney drainage system found on ultrasound
Many cases resolve on their own with time; regular ultrasound monitoring is needed
Surgery is only needed if the kidney function is declining or infections occur
Antibiotic prophylaxis may be given to prevent urinary infections
Keep all follow-up appointments for ultrasound scans every 3-6 months
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist at diagnosis; nephrology if renal function declining
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for febrile UTI, worsening hydronephrosis with declining function, or surgical intervention
notes: Many cases resolve spontaneously; surgery reserved for declining renal function or recurrent infections. Early diagnosis via prenatal US enables timely monitoring.
guideline_changes: Robotic-assisted laparoscopic pyeloplasty gaining wider pediatric use

investigations.name: VCUG (Voiding Cystourethrogram)
investigations.indication: Bilateral dilation, ureteral dilation, abnormal bladder, or UTI

counselling: Most cases resolve without surgery — ज़्यादातर मामले बिना ऑपरेशन के ठीक हो जाते हैं
Regular follow-up and scans are essential — नियमित जांच और अल्ट्रासाउंड ज़रूरी है
Watch for signs of UTI such as fever, irritability, or poor feeding — पेशाब के संक्रमण के संकेत देखें जैसे बुखार, चिड़चिड़ापन, या कम दूध पीना
Serial imaging helps guide treatment decisions — बार-बार की जाने वाली तस्वीरें इलाज का फैसला करने में मदद करती हैं
Early detection prevents kidney damage — जल्दी पता लगने से गुर्दे की क्षति रुकती है
warning_signs: Fever or irritability suggesting UTI — बुखार या चिड़चिड़ापन जो पेशाब के संक्रमण का संकेत हो
Poor feeding or vomiting — कम दूध पीना या उल्टी
Palpable abdominal mass — पेट में गांठ महसूस होना
Poor urine stream or dribbling — पेशाब की कमज़ोर धार या टपकना
Failure to thrive — वज़न और विकास न बढ़ना
Decreased urine output — पेशाब कम आना
expected_course: Mild hydronephrosis (Grade 1-2): resolves spontaneously in 50-70% of cases by 12-18 months. Moderate hydronephrosis (Grade 3): requires close monitoring; may need surgery if worsening. Severe hydronephrosis (Grade 4): often requires surgical intervention (pyeloplasty). PUJ obstruction is the most common pathological cause. Postnatal ultrasound should be done after day 3.
key_clinical_points: First postnatal ultrasound at 3-7 days of life (not immediately - early scan may be falsely reassuring)
SFU grading system: Grade 1-4 based on pelvic and calyceal dilation
Grade 1-2: serial ultrasound monitoring; no routine antibiotic prophylaxis
Grade 3-4: antibiotic prophylaxis, VCUG, nuclear scan (DTPA/MAG3)
Bilateral hydronephrosis or abnormal bladder: rule out posterior urethral valves
Check serum creatinine at day 3-5 (reflects neonatal renal function, not maternal)
UTI prevention with antibiotic prophylaxis in high-grade cases
Refer to pediatric urologist/nephrologist for Grade 3-4 or bilateral cases

severity_assessment.mild: Grade 1-2 unilateral hydronephrosis, normal renal parenchyma - serial ultrasound at 6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months
severity_assessment.moderate: Grade 3 or bilateral Grade 2, no parenchymal thinning - antibiotic prophylaxis, VCUG, consider nuclear scan, pediatric nephrology referral
severity_assessment.severe: Grade 4, parenchymal thinning, bilateral involvement, abnormal bladder, elevated creatinine - urgent urology referral, full imaging workup, possible surgical intervention

monitoring_parameters.frequency: Day 3-7, then 4-6 weeks, then 3 months, 6 months, 12 months (adjust based on grade)
Day 3-5 for bilateral or high-grade; repeat if abnormal
If fever or UTI symptoms; consider periodic screening in high-grade cases

first_line_drugs.notes: Cystoscopic incision of valves within 48h of birth once metabolically stable; gold standard
Urethral catheter or suprapubic catheter as initial decompression in unstable neonates

second_line_drugs.notes: Cutaneous vesicostomy if infant too small or unstable for primary ablation; temporary diversion
Oxybutynin 0.1-0.2 mg/kg PO BID for secondary bladder dysfunction post-ablation
Clean intermittent catheterization for persistent bladder dysfunction post valve ablation

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Bilateral hydronephrosis, thick bladder wall, dilated posterior urethra

investigations.name: VCUG
investigations.indication: Confirms posterior urethral valves; shows dilated posterior urethra

investigations.name: Serum creatinine (nadir)
investigations.indication: Best predictor of long-term renal outcome; check after drainage

investigations.name: MAG3 renogram
investigations.indication: Assess differential renal function after stabilization

counselling: Baby boy has valve-like flaps blocking urine flow; this needs urgent treatment
A catheter will be placed to drain the bladder immediately
Surgery (endoscopic) to remove the valves will be done once baby is stable
Kidney function needs lifelong monitoring as some damage may already have occurred
Child may need regular catheterization and medicines for bladder function long-term
referral_criteria: Immediate referral to pediatric urologist; nephrology co-management for renal function
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require admission at diagnosis for catheter drainage and stabilization before surgery
notes: Prenatal diagnosis enables early intervention. Multidisciplinary follow-up (urology + nephrology) is essential to monitor bladder and renal function long-term.
guideline_changes: Miniaturized endoscopes (<2mm) now enable valve ablation in neonates >2 kg

first_line_drugs.notes: First-line for DDH diagnosed 0-6 months; 95% success in dysplasia/subluxation, 80% in dislocation
Graf method US at 4-6 wks for risk factors or clinical concern; universal screening under debate
Under GA with arthrogram for failed Pavlik or late diagnosis (6-18 mo); hip spica 12 wks

second_line_drugs.notes: For failed closed reduction or age >18 mo; medial or anterior approach; followed by spica cast
Salter innominate or femoral shortening osteotomy for residual dysplasia in older children
Rhino or Tubingen splint alternative to Pavlik for mild dysplasia; ensures hip in flexion/abduction

investigations.name: Hip ultrasound (Graf method)
investigations.indication: Primary screening and monitoring tool for infants <6 months

investigations.name: Hip X-ray (AP pelvis)
investigations.indication: Assessment after age 4-6 months when ossification visible

investigations.name: MRI hip
investigations.indication: If closed reduction performed to confirm hip position in cast

counselling: DDH means the hip joint is not properly formed; early treatment gives the best results
The Pavlik harness must be worn full-time as directed - do not remove it for bathing unless told
Keep all follow-up ultrasound appointments to check if the hip is developing properly
Double or triple diapering does NOT treat DDH and should not be relied upon
If started early (before 6 months), most babies recover completely with just the harness
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric orthopedic surgeon for all confirmed DDH; urgent referral for dislocated hip
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for closed reduction under anesthesia and spica casting, open reduction surgery, or osteotomy procedures
notes: Early detection and treatment is critical: Pavlik harness before 6 months yields best outcomes. Avascular necrosis of the femoral head is the most serious complication of treatment.
source: AAP 2024, AAOS DDH Quality Measure, BSCOS 2023 Consensus
guideline_changes: 2024 evidence increasingly supports universal ultrasound screening over selective screening

first_line_drugs.notes: Weekly long-leg casts from birth; correct cavus, adductus, varus, then equinus; typically 5-7 casts
Performed after casting if equinus persists (>85% of cases); outpatient under local anesthesia
Boots-and-bar orthosis 23 hrs/day x3 mo, then nighttime until age 5; critical for preventing relapse

second_line_drugs.notes: Daily stretching, taping, continuous passive motion; alternative to casting in select centers
For relapsed clubfoot with dynamic supination; transfer to lateral cuneiform; after age 2.5-3
Extensive surgical release reserved for severe resistant cases; higher complication rate than Ponseti
Gentle serial stretching and reverse-last shoes for non-clubfoot forefoot adduction; resolves in most

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis
investigations.indication: Clubfoot is diagnosed at birth by physical exam

counselling: Clubfoot is very treatable with the Ponseti method of weekly plaster casts starting from birth
Most babies need 5-7 weekly casts followed by a small procedure to lengthen the Achilles tendon
The brace (boots and bar) must be worn 23 hours daily for 3 months then at night until age 5
Brace compliance is the single most important factor in preventing recurrence
Your child will walk and run normally if treatment is completed properly
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric orthopedic surgeon experienced in Ponseti method at birth; re-refer if relapse occurs
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit only for Achilles tenotomy (day procedure) or for major reconstructive surgery if needed for relapsed/resistant clubfoot
notes: The Ponseti method is the worldwide standard for clubfoot with 95% success rate when bracing compliance is maintained. Bracing noncompliance is the leading cause of recurrence.
guideline_changes: 2022 AAP report reinforces Ponseti method as definitive first-line with 95% success rate

diagnosis_name: Congenital musculoskeletal deformities of skull, face, spine and chest

first_line_drugs.notes: Manual stretching, positioning, tummy time from birth; 2024 APTA CPG: start PT within 1-3 months for best outcome
Home stretching program, environmental modifications, positional strategies to prevent plagiocephaly
Custom cranial orthosis for positional plagiocephaly; start 4-6 mo, wear 23 hrs/day for 3-6 months

second_line_drugs.notes: OnabotulinumtoxinA into SCM for refractory CMT; used as adjunct to stretching in older infants
Unipolar or bipolar SCM release for persistent torticollis after >1 yr of PT; improves ROM
TLSO brace for congenital scoliosis monitoring; vacuum bell or Nuss bar for pectus excavatum
Hemivertebra excision or growing rods for progressive congenital scoliosis; individualized timing

investigations.name: Neck ultrasound
investigations.indication: Assess SCM muscle for fibromatosis in torticollis

investigations.name: Cervical spine X-ray
investigations.indication: Rule out skeletal cause of torticollis (hemivertebra)

investigations.name: Spinal X-ray (full spine PA)
investigations.indication: Baseline for congenital scoliosis; serial monitoring

counselling: Congenital torticollis (head tilt) responds very well to physiotherapy started early
Do daily stretching exercises at home as shown by the physiotherapist
Encourage tummy time and turning head to both sides during play
A molding helmet may be needed if the head shape is significantly flattened on one side
Most cases resolve completely with consistent physiotherapy within the first year
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric physiotherapist immediately; orthopedic/neurosurgery referral for congenital scoliosis or if torticollis not improving after 6 months of PT
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for SCM surgical release if torticollis persistent after >1 year of PT, spinal surgery for progressive congenital scoliosis, or pectus repair
notes: Congenital muscular torticollis resolves in >90% with early PT initiated before age 3 months. Congenital vertebral anomalies require serial imaging to monitor curve progression.
guideline_changes: 2024 APTA published first evidence-based CPG for congenital muscular torticollis physical therapy

counselling: Condition is common and usually benign — यह आम है और आमतौर पर गंभीर नहीं है।
Does not affect brain development or intelligence — इससे दिमाग के विकास या बुद्धि पर कोई असर नहीं पड़ता।
Early repositioning gives excellent outcomes — जल्दी करवट बदलने से बहुत अच्छे नतीजे मिलते हैं।
Head shape improves gradually with growth — सिर की शक्ल बढ़ने के साथ धीरे-धीरे ठीक होती है।
Helmet therapy rarely needed and only in selected cases — हेलमेट की ज़रूरत बहुत कम होती है।
Follow advice consistently for best results — सलाह का नियमित रूप से पालन करें।
Increase tummy time during awake hours — जागते समय बच्चे को पेट के बल ज़्यादा लिटाएं।
warning_signs: Cranial ridging or hard ridge along sutures — सिर पर कड़ी लकीर या उभार
Progressive worsening despite repositioning — करवट बदलने के बावजूद बिगड़ना
Abnormal head circumference growth — सिर की गोलाई असामान्य बढ़ना या न बढ़ना
Associated syndromic features — अन्य असामान्य लक्षण दिखना
expected_course: Deformational plagiocephaly improves with repositioning therapy and increased tummy time in the majority of cases. Head shape typically normalizes by 12-18 months as the infant becomes more mobile. Helmet therapy (cranial orthosis) may be considered for persistent moderate-severe cases after 5-6 months of age. Craniosynostosis must always be ruled out.
key_clinical_points: Distinguish deformational plagiocephaly from craniosynostosis — open, non-ridged sutures favor deformational cause.
Check for associated congenital muscular torticollis (present in 20% of cases).
Tummy time is the most effective conservative treatment.
Alternate head position during sleep to reduce pressure on flat side.
Helmet therapy is effective if started between 4-12 months of age when needed.
CT scan is only indicated if craniosynostosis is suspected — avoid unnecessary radiation.

severity_assessment.mild: Slight asymmetry, no ear displacement, open sutures — repositioning and tummy time advice.
severity_assessment.moderate: Obvious asymmetry with ear displacement, forehead prominence — repositioning, physiotherapy if torticollis present, close follow-up.
severity_assessment.severe: Marked asymmetry not improving with repositioning after 2-3 months, or craniosynostosis concern — consider helmet therapy referral or imaging.

first_line_drugs.notes: Optimal age 6 mo-3 yrs; reconstruct collateral ligaments and tendons; preserve best-functioning digit
Office suture ligation within first 3 months for pedunculated postaxial polydactyly; simple and safe

second_line_drugs.notes: Bilhaut-Cloquet procedure for thumb (preaxial) polydactyly; combines two digits into one functional thumb
Defer to age 1+ for complex preaxial polydactyly to allow ossification for accurate anatomic assessment
Surgery at ~1 yr before walking; excise border toe preserving metatarsal alignment; minimize gait impact

investigations.name: X-ray of affected hand/foot
investigations.indication: Assess bony anatomy and joint involvement

counselling: Extra finger or toe is a common birth variation that is corrected with surgery
For a skin-tag type extra finger, simple removal can be done very early
For bony extra digits, surgery is planned around 6-12 months for best results
After surgery, hand therapy may be needed to ensure good function
The cosmetic and functional results are usually excellent
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric hand surgeon for preaxial polydactyly; pediatric orthopedics for postaxial; genetics if other anomalies present
hospitalisation_criteria: Day surgery for excision; observation may be needed for neonatal suture ligation
notes: Surgical timing should be individualized based on polydactyly type, location, and complexity. Earlier surgery (before age 3) yields better postoperative finger shaping and functional recovery.
guideline_changes: Recent evidence favors surgery at 6 mo-3 yrs for better functional and cosmetic outcomes vs. age 4-6

first_line_drugs.notes: Release at 6-24 mo; dorsal flap commissuroplasty with full-thickness skin grafts; staged if adjacent webs
Prioritize thumb-index and ring-small web spaces first (by 6 mo) to prevent growth deviation

second_line_drugs.notes: Local flap designs avoiding skin graft donor site; suitable for simple incomplete syndactyly
Never release both sides of same digit simultaneously; 3+ months between stages to protect vascularity
Bony separation, tendon/nerve reconstruction for osseous syndactyly; higher complication rate
Toe syndactyly often does not require surgery unless functional impairment or shoe-fitting problems

investigations.name: X-ray of affected hand/foot
investigations.indication: Determine if simple (skin) or complex (bony) syndactyly

investigations.name: Genetic evaluation
investigations.indication: If bilateral, multiple limbs, or associated anomalies (Apert, Poland)

counselling: Syndactyly (joined fingers/toes) is a common birth variation corrected with surgery
Surgery is planned between 6-24 months of age for hand syndactyly
If adjacent web spaces are affected, surgery is done in stages to protect blood supply
Hand therapy after surgery helps achieve the best function
Toe syndactyly often does not need surgery unless it causes problems with shoes or walking
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric hand surgeon at birth for hand syndactyly; orthopedics for foot syndactyly; genetics if syndromic
hospitalisation_criteria: Day surgery or 1-night stay for web space release; staged admissions if multiple web spaces involved
notes: Release before age 2 prevents angular deformity in digits of unequal length. Staged surgery is mandatory when adjacent web spaces are involved to protect digit blood supply.
guideline_changes: Graftless syndactyly release techniques gaining adoption, reducing donor site morbidity

first_line_drugs.notes: Fit at sitting age (~6 mo); maintains arm length symmetry; supports bimanual tasks and balance
Introduce at age 2-3 with prehensor; harness-operated; durable for active children
Bimanual training, adaptive techniques, fine motor skills; ongoing with prosthetic transitions

second_line_drugs.notes: Sensor-controlled powered hand; fit from age 2-3; multi-articulated digits available (i-Limb, Michelangelo)
Index finger transfer to thumb position for radial club hand or thumb aplasia; age 6-18 months
Distraction osteogenesis for partial defects (e.g., short forearm); case-by-case basis
Some children function well without prosthesis using adaptive techniques; reassess every 2-3 years

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Screen for associated renal anomalies (radial deficiency)

counselling: Your child has an upper limb difference - many children do remarkably well with or without a prosthesis
A prosthetic hand can be fitted as early as 6 months of age
Occupational therapy helps your child develop skills for daily activities
Let your child choose whether to use a prosthesis as they grow older
Children with upper limb differences often develop excellent compensatory skills
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric orthopedic/hand surgeon, prosthetics team, and occupational therapist at birth
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for surgical reconstruction (pollicization, limb lengthening), or prosthetic fitting under sedation in young children
notes: Many children with unilateral upper limb deficiency adapt remarkably well with or without prostheses. Prosthetic choice should prioritize function and the child's own preferences as they develop.
source: AAPM&R Pediatric Limb Deficiency, JPOSNA Prosthetics Essentials
guideline_changes: Multi-articulated myoelectric prostheses (i-Limb) now available for children with gesture control software

first_line_drugs.notes: Fit when child pulls to stand (~9-12 mo); update annually during growth; multidisciplinary team approach
Gait training, strength, balance; adaptive equipment; ongoing as prosthetic needs change with growth
Limb lengthening (Ilizarov or IM nail) for partial defects; individualized based on defect severity

second_line_drugs.notes: Newer motorized IM nails (PRECICE) for older children; less external hardware; standard procedure now
Ankle disarticulation for severe fibular hemimelia; enables prosthetic fitting with end-bearing stump
For proximal femoral focal deficiency; rotates foot 180 degrees to serve as biological knee joint
AFO or KAFO for mild defects; shoe lifts for leg length discrepancy <2cm

investigations.name: MRI of affected limb
investigations.indication: Assess soft tissue and joint anatomy for surgical planning

counselling: Your child has a limb difference that can be managed very well with modern prosthetics and therapy
Prosthetic fitting starts early (around 9-12 months) when the child begins to stand
Regular prosthetic updates are needed as your child grows
Children with limb differences adapt remarkably well and can participate in sports and activities
Connect with other families through limb difference support groups for shared experience
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric orthopedic surgeon and rehabilitation medicine at birth; prosthetics and orthotics team
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for limb lengthening surgery, amputation/revision surgery, or reconstructive procedures
notes: Decision between limb reconstruction and amputation with prosthetic fitting must involve family and multidisciplinary team. Prosthetic technology advances rapidly, requiring frequent reassessment.
source: AAPM&R Pediatric Limb Deficiency, JPOSNA 2024 Prosthetics Review
guideline_changes: 2024 advances in intramedullary nail lengthening and extramedullary implantable lengthening devices

diagnosis_name: Congenital malformations of skull and face bones

first_line_drugs.notes: Open surgery at 6-12 months for single-suture craniosynostosis; fronto-orbital advancement for coronal
Minimally invasive option for infants <3-4 months; followed by helmet therapy for 6-12 months
IV TXA 10-15 mg/kg bolus then infusion intra-op; reduces blood loss during cranial vault surgery

second_line_drugs.notes: Post-endoscopic or for positional plagiocephaly; custom molding helmet worn 23 hrs/day for 6-12 mo
Le Fort III or monobloc distraction for syndromic craniosynostosis (Apert, Crouzon); age 4-8
For suspected raised ICP; papilledema screening; VP shunt if hydrocephalus develops
Mandibular or midface distraction for severe micrognathia/midface hypoplasia; external devices

investigations.name: Ophthalmology exam (fundoscopy)
investigations.indication: Screen for papilledema indicating raised ICP

investigations.name: MRI brain
investigations.indication: Assess brain if developmental concerns or syndromic

counselling: Craniosynostosis means bones of the skull fused too early; surgery corrects the head shape and protects the brain
The best time for surgery is usually before 6-12 months of age
If your baby had endoscopic surgery, the helmet must be worn 23 hours a day for best results
After surgery, head shape improves significantly over several months
Some complex cases need more than one surgery as your child grows
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurosurgeon and craniofacial surgeon at diagnosis; ophthalmology for raised ICP screening
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for cranial vault surgery, ICU post-operatively, and for any staged craniofacial procedures
notes: Best surgical outcomes for single-suture craniosynostosis occur before 6 months of age. Syndromic cases (Apert, Crouzon, Pfeiffer) require lifelong multidisciplinary craniofacial team care.
guideline_changes: Minimally invasive endoscopic suturectomy gaining wider adoption for early-presenting cases

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 mg/kg/day x3 days q2-4 mo; start in infancy for moderate-severe OI; improves BMD, reduces pain
0.025-0.05 mg/kg q6mo; alternative to pamidronate; fewer infusion days; monitor calcium post-infusion
Gentle handling; lightweight splints/casts; minimize immobilization to prevent further bone loss
Low-impact PT/OT to build muscle strength and mobility; aquatic therapy; avoid contact sports

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 mg/kg/wk oral; less effective than IV bisphosphonates but avoids infusions; for milder types
Fassier-Duval rods for recurrent long bone fractures; grow with child; reduce fracture frequency
Anti-RANKL antibody; emerging use in children unresponsive to bisphosphonates; rebound risk on stopping
Screen for dentinogenesis imperfecta; preventive dental care; crowns for fragile teeth

investigations.name: Genetic testing (COL1A1/COL1A2)
investigations.indication: Confirm OI type and guide prognosis

investigations.name: Skeletal survey X-ray
investigations.indication: Baseline assessment of fractures and deformities

investigations.name: Serum calcium, phosphate, vitamin D, ALP
Audiometry
investigations.indication: Screen for hearing loss (type I OI)

counselling: Osteogenesis imperfecta (brittle bone disease) causes bones to break easily
Handle your child gently but do not restrict all activity - supervised safe play builds strength
Bisphosphonate infusions (given every few months) strengthen bones and reduce fractures
Swimming and water therapy are excellent exercises that build strength without fracture risk
Dental care is important as teeth may also be affected; see a dentist regularly
referral_criteria: Managed by multidisciplinary team: pediatric endocrinologist, orthopedic surgeon, physiotherapist, geneticist, dentist
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for fracture management, bisphosphonate infusions (day admission), intramedullary rod surgery, or spinal surgery
notes: Start bisphosphonate therapy as early as possible since effect on skeleton is greatest during growth. Multidisciplinary care including orthopedics, endocrine, PT, and genetics is essential.
source: OIF Guidelines, Calcified Tissue International 2024 Review
guideline_changes: 2024 meta-analysis confirms IV bisphosphonates improve BMD; comparative trials still needed for optimal regimen

first_line_drugs.notes: Primary closure or GORE-TEX patch via thoracotomy after stabilization
Peak pressure <25 cmH2O, permissive hypercapnia, avoid barotrauma
20 ppm iNO for associated PPHN; wean as oxygenation improves

second_line_drugs.notes: For refractory hypoxemia; 25-30% of CDH neonates require ECMO
Consider if concurrent RDS; not routine for CDH
0.33-0.99 mcg/kg/min IV for pulmonary hypertension support

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Bowel loops in chest, mediastinal shift

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Assess pulmonary hypertension and cardiac function

counselling: CDH is a serious condition where the baby's organs are in the wrong position affecting breathing
Surgery will be done once the baby is stabilized — typically 2-3 days after birth
The baby will be on a ventilator initially — this is expected and necessary
After surgery, the baby may need continued respiratory support for days to weeks
Long-term follow-up for lung function, feeding, growth, and development is essential
referral_criteria: Refer immediately to tertiary NICU with ECMO capability and pediatric surgical team
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all CDH cases to NICU immediately at birth; transfer to surgical NICU for definitive management
notes: Stabilize before surgery; avoid bag-mask ventilation. Repair timing when hemodynamically stable, typically 48-72h post-birth.
guideline_changes: LISA/gentle ventilation strategies increasingly preferred; minimally invasive repair discouraged in neonates

first_line_drugs.notes: Primary fascial closure if defect small (<4 cm) and stable infant
Silo/mesh for giant omphalocele; gradual reduction over days-weeks
IV fluid resuscitation; TPN until enteral feeding established

second_line_drugs.notes: Topical silver sulfadiazine/povidone-iodine; epithelialization for giant defects
Perioperative broad-spectrum coverage; ampicillin + gentamicin

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: 50% have associated cardiac defects; Beckwith-Wiedemann screening

counselling: Baby is born with abdominal organs outside the belly covered by a thin sac; surgery is needed
Large defects may need multiple surgeries over weeks
Baby will be in NICU for several weeks; parents can visit and participate in care
Doctor will check for associated heart or kidney problems
Long-term outcome is usually good once the defect is closed
referral_criteria: Prenatal diagnosis should trigger referral to tertiary center with pediatric surgery and NICU for planned delivery
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require immediate NICU admission at birth
notes: Giant omphalocele (>5 cm) often requires staged approach. Screen for Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome and cardiac anomalies.

first_line_drugs.notes: Preformed spring-loaded silo; bedside placement, gradual reduction over 3-7 days
Direct fascial closure if bowel edema minimal; in OR under GA
Aggressive fluid resuscitation (up to 2-3x maintenance); TPN until feeds

second_line_drugs.notes: Umbilical cord or ward closure techniques; emerging evidence
Broad-spectrum prophylaxis; ampicillin + gentamicin perioperatively

investigations.name: Abdominal X-ray (post-reduction)
investigations.indication: Assess bowel gas pattern and complications

counselling: Gastroschisis means the baby's intestines are outside the body through a hole near the belly button
Surgery will place the intestines back and close the abdomen — this may take several days if done gradually
The baby will need IV nutrition (TPN) until the intestines start working, which can take weeks
Watch for feeding tolerance milestones — this determines when the baby can go home
Long-term outcomes are generally good — most children grow and develop normally
referral_criteria: Refer immediately to neonatal surgical center prenatally or at birth; planned delivery at surgical center is ideal
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit immediately at birth; NICU with aggressive IV fluid resuscitation, bowel protection (warm saline wrap), and surgical planning
notes: Cover exposed bowel with warm saline-soaked gauze and plastic wrap immediately. High fluid losses require aggressive IV resuscitation.
guideline_changes: Bedside spring-loaded silo increasingly preferred over OR primary closure

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to small aplasia cutis lesions BID until healed; mupirocin 2% or bacitracin preferred
Daily gentle cleansing with saline, petroleum jelly, and non-adherent dressing for ACC

second_line_drugs.notes: For accessory skin appendages, dermoid cysts, or large ACC lesions (>4 cm); refer surgery
Apply to scalp BID for localized congenital alopecia; off-label; limited pediatric evidence

investigations.name: MRI (scalp lesions)
investigations.indication: Rule out connection to sagittal sinus in large scalp aplasia cutis

investigations.name: Genetic evaluation
investigations.indication: If suspected syndromic association (e.g., Adams-Oliver syndrome)

counselling: Most congenital skin appendage anomalies are cosmetic and managed conservatively
Keep the area clean and watch for signs of infection (redness, warmth, pus)
For aplasia cutis: keep wound clean and covered; most small defects heal without surgery
Some skin anomalies may be associated with underlying conditions - follow-up blood tests may be needed
Surgical removal of accessory appendages can be done electively when child is older
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric dermatologist/plastic surgeon if large aplasia cutis (>4 cm), aplasia cutis with exposed deep structures, cosmetically significant lesions, or suspected underlying syndrome
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if large scalp aplasia cutis with sagittal sinus exposure, significant bleeding, secondary infection requiring IV antibiotics, or associated systemic anomalies
notes: Most congenital skin appendage malformations are managed conservatively or surgically. Large aplasia cutis defects with exposed dura require urgent neurosurgical consultation.

diagnosis_name: Other congenital malformation syndromes affecting multiple systems

first_line_drugs.notes: CMA, targeted gene panels, or exome sequencing; confirm specific syndrome; family recurrence counseling
Cardiac echo, renal US, ophthalmology, audiology, skeletal survey tailored to specific syndrome
Plot on syndrome-specific growth charts if available; GH evaluation for short stature syndromes (e.g., PWS)
Early intervention PT/OT/speech; behavioral support; educational planning and IEP from age 3

second_line_drugs.notes: Abdominal US q3mo until age 7 for Wilms tumor/hepatoblastoma; AFP levels q3mo until age 4
Somatropin 0.035 mg/kg/day; improves body composition, growth, cognition; exclude OSA before starting
Annual echo for aortic root dilation; beta-blocker (atenolol/losartan) if root Z-score >3
Strict caloric control and supervision for hyperphagia; behavioral strategies; dietitian involvement

investigations.name: Genetic testing (CMA, gene panel, or exome)
Abdominal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Screen for tumors (BWS) or organ anomalies

investigations.name: Skeletal survey
investigations.indication: If skeletal dysplasia or connective tissue disorder suspected

counselling: Your child has a genetic syndrome affecting multiple body systems that needs coordinated care
Accurate genetic diagnosis is the first step to plan proper monitoring and treatment
Multiple specialist appointments will be needed - keep a health folder to track everything
Growth and development may be different but early intervention helps maximize potential
Connect with syndrome-specific support groups for practical advice and emotional support
referral_criteria: Refer to clinical geneticist for diagnosis; multidisciplinary team: cardiology, endocrinology, neurology, developmental pediatrics as indicated by specific syndrome
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit based on syndrome-specific complications: cardiac surgery, tumor surveillance findings, metabolic crises, or acute illness
notes: Q87 encompasses diverse syndromes requiring syndrome-specific management protocols. Accurate genetic diagnosis is the critical first step to guide appropriate surveillance and treatment.
source: ACMG 2023, BWS Consensus 2024, AAP PWS Health Supervision, Marfan/GeneReviews
guideline_changes: 2024 Italian BWS update expanded tumor surveillance; 2024 PWS guidance for transition to adult care published

first_line_drugs.notes: Screen all neonates for congenital heart defects (AVSD in ~40%); surgical repair as indicated
TSH at birth, 6 mo, 12 mo, then annually; treat hypothyroidism with levothyroxine 10-15 mcg/kg/day
PT/OT/speech from infancy; developmental assessment by 12 mo; IEP by age 3
Eye exam by 6 mo then annually; audiology at birth, 6 mo, annually; ABR if needed

second_line_drugs.notes: Cervical spine X-ray if symptomatic; flexion/extension views before sports participation
Polysomnography by age 4 for obstructive sleep apnea; adenotonsillectomy or CPAP if positive
Tissue transglutaminase IgA at age 2 then as indicated; prevalence 5-16% in DS
Screen for ASD, ADHD, anxiety, depression; psychiatric meds used cautiously per AAP 2022 guidance

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Screen for congenital heart defects (within first month)

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Screen for transient myeloproliferative disorder/leukemia in neonates

investigations.name: Audiological assessment
investigations.indication: Screen at birth and every 6 months

counselling: Down syndrome is a genetic condition - your child will need regular checkups with multiple specialists
Early intervention (physiotherapy, speech therapy) from infancy greatly helps development
Heart checkups are essential as many children with Down syndrome have heart defects
Thyroid testing is done every year; hearing and eye tests are also important
Your child can attend school and achieve milestones with appropriate support and encouragement
referral_criteria: Multidisciplinary team from birth: cardiology, ENT, ophthalmology, endocrinology, developmental pediatrics; genetics for family counseling
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for cardiac surgery, acute leukemia, severe infections, airway concerns, or atlantoaxial subluxation with neurological signs
notes: Comprehensive health supervision requires lifelong multisystem screening per AAP schedule. The 2024 collaboration ensures guideline translations are freely available worldwide.
guideline_changes: 2022 AAP update added autism screening (7-19% prevalence), expanded psychiatric medication guidance

first_line_drugs.notes: Full resuscitation per NRP; thermoregulation, respiratory support, NG/G-tube feeding as needed
Echo at birth; diuretics + digoxin for CHF; palliative or corrective cardiac surgery if appropriate
NG tube or gastrostomy for poor feeding; treat GER medically first, fundoplication if refractory

second_line_drugs.notes: Comfort-focused care with family-centered decision-making when prognosis is very poor
Standard antibiotics for pneumonia, UTI, otitis media; these are leading causes of morbidity
Karyotype confirmation; recurrence risk counseling; prenatal diagnosis options for future pregnancies

counselling: Trisomy 13/18 are serious genetic conditions with significant medical challenges
Your medical team will discuss all treatment options with you and support your decisions
Many families find comfort in connecting with support groups for these conditions
Each child is different - some children live longer than expected with active medical care
Palliative care services are available to help manage comfort and quality of life
referral_criteria: Multidisciplinary approach from birth: neonatology, cardiology, genetics, palliative care; surgical specialties as needed based on shared decision-making
hospitalisation_criteria: Most require NICU admission at birth; readmit for acute infections, cardiac decompensation, or surgical interventions based on family-directed goals of care
notes: Median survival is 5-15 days for trisomy 13 and 5-15 days for trisomy 18, but long-term survivors exist with active intervention. Shared decision-making with families is essential.
guideline_changes: AAP 2025 clinical report formally supports active management including resuscitation; no longer comfort-only default

diagnosis_name: Other trisomies and partial trisomies of autosomes

first_line_drugs.notes: Full resuscitation; respiratory support; NG/G-tube feeding; thermoregulation per NRP guidelines
Echo at birth; CHD present in majority; medical management of CHF; surgical repair when appropriate
Karyotype + CMA to define exact trisomy/partial trisomy; guides prognosis and surveillance plan

second_line_drugs.notes: Family-centered palliative approach when prognosis is very poor; shared decision-making essential
Early intervention PT/OT/speech; special education referral; developmental pediatrician follow-up
Prompt treatment of respiratory and urinary infections; influenza and pneumococcal vaccines on schedule

investigations.name: Chromosomal microarray
investigations.indication: Define exact trisomy/partial trisomy and breakpoints

counselling: Your child has an extra piece of chromosome material which affects development
The outlook depends on which chromosome is involved and how much extra material there is
Your medical team will create a personalized monitoring plan for your child
Early intervention services help maximize your child's potential
Genetic counseling is important for understanding the condition and planning
referral_criteria: Refer to clinical geneticist at diagnosis; multidisciplinary team based on specific findings: cardiology, neurology, developmental pediatrics
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit based on specific complications: cardiac surgery, respiratory support, or acute illness management
notes: Prognosis varies widely depending on specific chromosomal abnormality and degree of mosaicism. Shared decision-making with families regarding intensity of intervention is critical.
guideline_changes: AAP 2025 guidance supports active management for trisomies previously considered lethal

first_line_drugs.notes: Somatropin 0.045-0.050 mg/kg/day SC; start from age 2 if growth failure; continue until bone age >=14
Low-dose transdermal estradiol at age 11-12; titrate over 2-3 yrs; add progesterone for cycling
Echo + cardiac MRI at diagnosis; aortic monitoring q3-5 yrs; screen for bicuspid aortic valve, coarctation
Renal US at diagnosis; TSH annually; autoimmune thyroiditis in ~25%; levothyroxine if needed

second_line_drugs.notes: Low-dose oxandrolone 0.03-0.05 mg/kg/day as GH adjunct for severe short stature; monitor virilization
Oocyte/ovarian tissue cryopreservation if ovarian function present; counsel in adolescence
Audiometry every 1-3 yrs; sensorineural and conductive loss common; ENT referral as needed
Screen for nonverbal learning difficulties, social anxiety; neuropsych evaluation recommended

investigations.name: Echocardiography and cardiac MRI
investigations.indication: Baseline cardiac assessment and aortic monitoring

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Screen for horseshoe kidney and other anomalies

investigations.name: Audiometry
investigations.indication: Screen for sensorineural and conductive hearing loss

counselling: Turner syndrome (45,X) affects girls and requires lifelong medical care
Growth hormone injections from early childhood help achieve better adult height
Estrogen treatment at puberty helps develop normal secondary sexual characteristics
Heart monitoring is very important throughout life as aorta problems can occur
Your daughter can live a full, productive life with appropriate medical support
referral_criteria: All cases managed by pediatric endocrinologist; cardiology for aortic surveillance; ENT for hearing; reproductive endocrinology in adolescence
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for cardiac surgery (coarctation repair), acute aortic dissection (rare but emergency), or lymphedema management in neonates
notes: The 2024 international consensus is the most comprehensive TS guideline to date with 87 recommendations. Cardiovascular surveillance is the leading cause of excess mortality and requires lifelong monitoring.
source: International TS Consensus 2024 (Eur J Endocrinol), PES/ESE
guideline_changes: 2024 Aarhus guidelines updated GH dosing, cardiovascular surveillance, and fertility preservation protocols

diagnosis_name: Other sex chromosome abnormalities, female phenotype, not elsewhere classified

first_line_drugs.notes: Full karyotype; CMA for mosaicism detection; includes Triple X (47,XXX) and other variants
Early intervention for speech/language delays; educational accommodations; IEP as needed
Puberty assessment; FSH/LH/estradiol if delayed puberty; bone age annually from age 8

second_line_drugs.notes: Low-dose estradiol if ovarian insufficiency or delayed puberty; start age 11-12, titrate gradually
Screen for anxiety, social difficulties, ADHD; CBT or behavioral therapy as needed
Assess ovarian function; discuss fertility preservation options; refer to reproductive endocrinology

investigations.name: Developmental assessment
investigations.indication: Screen for speech-language and learning delays

investigations.name: FSH, LH, estradiol
investigations.indication: If delayed puberty or ovarian insufficiency suspected

counselling: Your daughter has an extra X chromosome which is usually a mild condition
Most girls with Triple X (47,XXX) have normal appearance and can lead normal lives
Some girls may need help with speech, language, or learning - early support is key
Puberty and fertility are usually normal but should be monitored
Regular developmental checkups help identify and address any concerns early
referral_criteria: Refer to clinical geneticist for confirmation; developmental pediatrician; speech-language pathologist if delays noted; endocrinology if pubertal concerns
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalization rarely needed for the chromosomal condition itself; admit only for associated complications
notes: Triple X syndrome (47,XXX) is the most common condition in this category with generally mild phenotype. Individualized screening and support based on specific karyotype findings is key.
guideline_changes: Expanded use of chromosomal microarray for precise characterization of sex chromosome variants

diagnosis_name: Klinefelter syndrome and other male chromosome abnormalities

first_line_drugs.notes: Begin at puberty (age 11-14) if levels low; IM testosterone enanthate 50mg monthly, titrate upward
Speech-language therapy, educational support; screen for ADHD and learning disabilities by school age
Annual LH/FSH/testosterone from age 10; bone density (DXA) at puberty onset and periodically

second_line_drugs.notes: Alternative to IM injections; daily application; titrate to maintain physiologic levels
Micro-TESE for sperm retrieval in adolescents/young adults; counsel early about fertility options
CBT or counseling for anxiety, depression, social difficulties; multidisciplinary team approach
Reduction mammoplasty if persistent gynecomastia causes distress; typically after puberty

investigations.name: Developmental/neuropsychological assessment
investigations.indication: Identify learning needs and guide intervention

counselling: Klinefelter syndrome is a common chromosome condition (47,XXY) affecting boys
Most boys with Klinefelter have normal intelligence but may need help with speech and learning
Testosterone treatment will be started at puberty to support normal male development
Early speech therapy and educational support make a big difference
Discuss fertility options with your doctor when your child reaches adolescence
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric endocrinologist for pubertal management; speech-language pathologist for early intervention; psychology for behavioral support
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalization rarely needed; admit only for surgical procedures (gynecomastia correction) or acute complications
notes: Early diagnosis (ideally prenatal or infancy) allows proactive developmental and endocrine management. Multidisciplinary care including endocrinology, psychology, and andrology is essential.
source: European Academy of Andrology/ESE 2021, JCEM 2025 Update
guideline_changes: 2025 JCEM review expanded psychological and quality-of-life management recommendations

diagnosis_name: Other chromosome abnormalities, not elsewhere classified

first_line_drugs.notes: Chromosomal microarray + karyotype; consider exome/genome sequencing if CMA non-diagnostic
Early intervention referral; PT/OT/speech as indicated; developmental pediatrician follow-up
Cardiac echo, renal US, ophthalmology, audiology based on phenotype; tailor to specific abnormality

second_line_drugs.notes: Recurrence risk assessment; family karyotyping; prenatal diagnosis options for future pregnancies
Screen for ASD, ADHD, anxiety; therapy and medication as indicated by symptoms
Serial growth parameters; consider GH evaluation if short stature; endocrine referral as needed

counselling: Your child has a chromosome difference that needs ongoing medical monitoring
The specific effects depend on the exact chromosome involved - your doctor will explain
Regular checkups with developmental assessment help identify and address any issues early
Genetic counseling can help you understand recurrence risk for future pregnancies
Connect with support groups for families with similar chromosome conditions
referral_criteria: Refer to clinical geneticist for diagnosis and syndrome-specific surveillance; developmental pediatrician; other specialists based on phenotype
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit based on specific complications: cardiac surgery, acute infections, seizure management, or surgical correction of associated anomalies
notes: Management is symptom-directed as phenotypes vary widely by specific chromosomal abnormality. Regular multidisciplinary follow-up with tailored screening protocols is essential.
guideline_changes: Chromosomal microarray and exome sequencing now standard first-tier diagnostic tests

investigations.name: ECG
investigations.purpose: Supportive test; may be nonspecific in neonates

counselling: Not all murmurs are dangerous; some resolve spontaneously. — सभी दिल की आवाज़ें खतरनाक नहीं होतीं; कुछ अपने आप ठीक हो जाती हैं।
If echocardiography is advised, it is to ensure the baby's heart is normal. — अगर इको टेस्ट बोला गया है तो यह बच्चे के दिल की जांच के लिए है।
Explain warning signs: cyanosis, breathing difficulty, poor feeding. — खतरे के निशान: नीलापन, सांस में तकलीफ, दूध न पीना।
Encourage regular follow-up even after a normal echocardiogram. — सामान्य रिपोर्ट आने पर भी नियमित जांच कराएं।
warning_signs: Blue discoloration of lips or tongue — होंठ या जीभ का नीला पड़ना
Fast or difficulty breathing — तेज़ या मुश्किल सांस लेना
Poor feeding or excessive sweating during feeds — दूध न पीना या दूध पीते समय बहुत पसीना आना
Lethargy or reduced activity — सुस्ती या कम हिलना-डुलना
Cold hands and feet — हाथ-पैर ठंडे पड़ना
expected_course: Innocent murmurs usually resolve within the first few months of life as transitional circulation stabilizes. Pathological murmurs require echocardiography and may need ongoing cardiology follow-up or intervention.
key_clinical_points: Pulse oximetry screening (pre- and post-ductal) is mandatory for all neonates with a murmur.
Femoral pulse assessment is critical to rule out coarctation of aorta.
A grade >=3/6, harsh, diastolic, or pansystolic murmur should be considered pathological until proven otherwise.
Echocardiography is the gold standard investigation.
Do not delay referral if critical congenital heart disease (CCHD) is suspected.

severity_assessment.mild: Soft systolic murmur (grade 1-2/6), asymptomatic, normal saturations and femoral pulses — observe and review.
severity_assessment.moderate: Murmur persisting beyond 48 hours, any associated symptoms, or saturation concern — arrange echocardiography.
severity_assessment.severe: Loud/harsh murmur, cyanosis, desaturation, absent femoral pulses, poor feeding — urgent cardiology referral and consider CCHD.

first_line_drugs.notes: 2-3 drops in each nostril 2-3 times daily, keeps mucosa moist
2-3 drops each nostril TDS for mucosal hydration
Age-appropriate dose OD PO for 2-4 weeks, improves capillary strength
Sit child upright, tilt head slightly forward, pinch soft part of nose for 10 minutes, apply cold compress

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply inside anterior nares BD for 5 days, if crusting/infection present
Apply inside anterior nares BD for 5 days, alternative to mupirocin if crusting/infection

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Recurrent or severe epistaxis to check platelets and anemia

investigations.name: PT/INR
investigations.indication: Recurrent epistaxis to rule out coagulation disorder

counselling: Apply nasal oil drops regularly to keep the nose moist
Teach first aid: sit upright, tilt head forward, pinch soft part of nose for 10 minutes
Keep child's nails short to prevent nose picking
Avoid forceful nose blowing and dry air exposure
Return if bleeding lasts >15-20 minutes, is recurrent, or bleeding from other sites
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Bleeding lasting more than 15 to 20 minutes despite pressure
Bleeding from other sites such as gums or skin bruises
