Pallor or weakness suggesting significant blood loss
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT specialist if recurrent frequent episodes (>1/week), bleeding from posterior nose, suspected bleeding disorder, or epistaxis not controlled by local measures
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if active uncontrollable bleeding >20 minutes despite compression, hemodynamic instability, significant blood loss with pallor/weakness, or suspected posterior epistaxis requiring nasal packing
notes: Most cases are benign anterior bleeds from Kiesselbach plexus due to nose picking or dry mucosa. Moisturization is the cornerstone. Teach first aid: sit upright, tilt head forward, pinch soft nose for 10 min, cold compress. Keep nails short. Avoid nose picking, forceful blowing, dry air.
source: AAO-HNS Clinical Practice Guideline: Nosebleed (2020, AAP-endorsed), IAP ENT Guidelines
guideline_changes: AAO-HNS 2020 CPG (AAP-endorsed): sustained pressure for minimum 5 minutes on lower third of nose; topical tranexamic acid now recommended as superior to other agents for stopping bleeding in first 10 minutes; oxymetazoline preferred topical vasoconstrictor. No major 2024-2025 pediatric-specific updates.

counselling: Many neonates have immature, soft airways causing noise. — कई नवजात शिशुओं की सांस की नली कोमल होती है जिससे आवाज़ आती है।
Laryngomalacia peaks at 6 months and resolves by 12-18 months. — लैरिंगोमलेशिया 6 महीने में सबसे ज़्यादा होता है और 12-18 महीने तक ठीक हो जाता है।
Most cases are self-limiting. — ज़्यादातर मामले अपने आप ठीक हो जाते हैं।
Watch for warning signs like bluish lips, chest indrawing, choking. — नीले होंठ, छाती धंसना, या दम घुटना दिखे तो तुरंत डॉक्टर को दिखाएं।
Feed baby in upright position to reduce noisy breathing. — बच्चे को ऊपर उठाकर (बैठी स्थिति में) दूध पिलाएं।
warning_signs: Blue discoloration of lips — होंठों का नीला पड़ना
Chest retractions or indrawing — छाती का धंसना
Apnea or breathing pauses — सांस रुकना
Choking or aspiration during feeds — दूध पीते समय दम घुटना
Poor weight gain — वज़न न बढ़ना
Stridor at rest or worsening — आराम में भी सांस की आवाज़ या बढ़ना
expected_course: Laryngomalacia (the most common cause) typically presents within the first 2 weeks of life, peaks at 4-8 months, and resolves by 12-18 months in most cases. Symptoms worsen with crying, feeding, and supine position. Severe cases (<10%) may need surgical intervention (supraglottoplasty).
key_clinical_points: Laryngomalacia is the most common cause of noisy breathing in neonates.
Identify the type of sound: stertor (nasal), stridor (laryngeal), wheeze (intrathoracic), grunt (distress).
Grunting is always a red flag indicating respiratory distress.
Flexible laryngoscopy is the gold standard for airway evaluation.
Improvement in prone position supports a diagnosis of laryngomalacia.
Rule out choanal atresia in any neonate with nasal obstruction.

severity_assessment.mild: Intermittent noise during feeds or crying only, good weight gain, normal saturations — reassure and monitor.
severity_assessment.moderate: Persistent stridor, mild feeding difficulty, borderline weight gain — ENT referral for laryngoscopy.
severity_assessment.severe: Stridor at rest with cyanosis, apnea, chest retractions, poor feeding, weight loss — admit, oxygen, urgent ENT evaluation.

monitoring_parameters.parameter: Type and severity of noisy breathing

first_line_drugs.notes: MDI: 2-4 puffs via spacer q4-6h PRN; Neb: 0.15 mg/kg (min 2.5 mg) q4-6h PRN
1-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 40 mg) for 3-5 days for acute wheezing exacerbation
Fluticasone 44-88 mcg 2 puffs BID or budesonide neb 0.25-0.5 mg BID for MTW

second_line_drugs.notes: 6 mo-5 yr: 4 mg PO daily; 6-14 yr: 5 mg PO daily; for recurrent viral wheeze
Budesonide 1 mg neb BID or fluticasone 250 mcg BID at onset of viral illness x7-10d
Neb: 250-500 mcg q6-8h as add-on bronchodilator for acute episodes

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: If first episode, atypical presentation, or not responding to treatment

counselling: Wheezing means there is a whistling sound when your child breathes out, caused by narrowed airways
Many young children wheeze only with viral infections and outgrow it by age 5-6
Give the inhaler with spacer and mask as demonstrated; this is more effective than nebulizer
If wheezing happens frequently (more than once a month), a daily preventer medicine may be needed
Keep your home smoke-free and reduce dust exposure to minimize episodes
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if wheezing is recurrent (>3 episodes), persistent between episodes, or not responding to standard treatment
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <92%, severe respiratory distress, poor feeding in infants, or not improving with bronchodilator therapy
notes: Classify as episodic viral wheeze or multiple-trigger wheeze to guide maintenance therapy. Daily ICS is most effective for multiple-trigger wheeze phenotype.
guideline_changes: ERS updated phenotype-based approach distinguishing episodic viral vs multiple-trigger wheeze

first_line_drugs.notes: Immediate BVM with 100% O2; rate 20-30 breaths/min with advanced airway; ensure chest rise
Cuffed ETT preferred; size (age/4)+3.5; RSI with Rocuronium 1 mg/kg + Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV
0.01 mg/kg IV/IO (1:10,000) q3-5min if progresses to pulseless arrest; max 1 mg/dose
0.02 mg/kg IV (min 0.1 mg, max 0.5 mg) for bradycardia-associated respiratory arrest

second_line_drugs.notes: LMA as rescue airway if intubation fails; size per weight-based chart
0.1 mg/kg IV/IM/IN (max 2 mg) if opioid-induced respiratory arrest; may repeat q2-3min
4 mL/kg ET for neonates with respiratory failure from surfactant deficiency

counselling: Your child stopped breathing and needed emergency treatment to restart breathing
The child will need to be monitored in the ICU on a breathing machine for some time
Please share any information about allergies, medicines, or events before this happened
The medical team will explain each step of treatment to you
After recovery, follow-up is essential to prevent recurrence
referral_criteria: PICU mandatory. Pulmonology for underlying respiratory disease. ENT if upper airway obstruction. Neurology if apnea of central origin. Consider ECMO center if refractory
hospitalisation_criteria: All respiratory arrests require PICU admission. Continued intubation and ventilation until underlying cause treated and respiratory drive adequate
notes: Most pediatric cardiac arrests are secondary to respiratory failure. Verify ETT with waveform capnography; aim ETCO2 35-40 mmHg post-intubation.
guideline_changes: 2025 AHA updated pediatric ventilation rate to 20-30/min with advanced airway and recommends cuffed ETT

counselling: Colic peaks at 6-8 weeks and usually resolves by 3-4 months. — कोलिक 6-8 हफ़्ते में सबसे ज़्यादा होता है और 3-4 महीने तक अपने आप ठीक हो जाता है।
The crying is not due to poor parenting, pain or hunger in most cases. — ज़्यादातर मामलों में रोना खराब परवरिश, दर्द या भूख की वजह से नहीं होता।
There are no long-term consequences for baby's health. — बच्चे की सेहत पर इसका कोई लंबे समय का असर नहीं होता।
Parents should practice self-care, share caregiving and seek support. — माता-पिता अपना ख़्याल रखें, बारी-बारी से बच्चे को संभालें और मदद लें।
Never shake the baby. — बच्चे को कभी झटके से न हिलाएं।
warning_signs: Poor weight gain or failure to thrive
expected_course: Infantile colic is self-limiting. Crying episodes typically peak at 6-8 weeks of age and resolve spontaneously by 3-4 months in most infants. No long-term developmental or health consequences.
key_clinical_points: Colic is a diagnosis of exclusion; always rule out red flags first.
Reassurance and parental support are the mainstay of management.
No strong evidence supports any medication; simethicone and probiotics have limited benefit.
Never shake the baby -- educate parents about shaken baby syndrome risk during frustration.

severity_assessment.mild: Brief crying spells, infant consolable with soothing, feeding and weight gain normal.
severity_assessment.moderate: Prolonged crying >3 hours/day, difficult to console, but infant otherwise thriving and no red flags.
severity_assessment.severe: Excessive inconsolable crying with parental exhaustion, or any red flag symptom present requiring further evaluation.

first_line_drugs.notes: Explain to parents: common condition, self-limiting (peaks at 6-8 weeks), resolves by 3-4 months
0.3 ml (as per preparation) TDS PO after feeds, preferred for gas relief and safety
Proper latching technique, burping after every feed, avoid overfeeding

first_line_drugs.drug: Supplements (Multivitamin Drops + Vitamin D3 400 IU + Iron if indicated)
first_line_drugs.notes: As per standard practice; Multivitamin drops + Vitamin D3 400 IU daily + Iron supplementation if indicated

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-7 drops TDS PO (morning, evening, night), short duration only, avoid routine long-term use
Apply 2-3 times/day for oral/gum care, only if associated irritability

investigations.name: Urine routine and microscopy
investigations.indication: Rule out UTI as cause of excessive crying

investigations.name: Stool occult blood
investigations.indication: Suspected cow milk protein allergy if blood-streaked stools

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: If fever present or prolonged crying beyond 4 months to rule out infection

counselling: Colic is very common and not dangerous - it will resolve by 3-4 months
Gentle tummy massage and warm compress provide relief
Ensure proper breastfeeding technique with burping after every feed
Avoid overfeeding the baby
Breastfeeding mothers should avoid excess tea, coffee, spicy and gas-forming foods
Return if baby has poor feeding, vomiting, fever, blood in stool, or poor weight gain
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Poor feeding or refusal to feed
Vomiting especially if bilious or forceful
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric gastroenterologist if colic persists beyond 4 months, failure to thrive, blood in stool, persistent vomiting, or suspected cow's milk protein allergy
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation rarely needed for colic. Admit only if poor feeding with dehydration, failure to thrive, suspected intestinal obstruction, or intussusception
notes: Reassurance is the most important intervention. Infantile colic is self-limiting, peaks at 6-8 weeks, resolves by 3-4 months. Non-pharmacological measures: gentle abdominal massage, warm compress, carrying/rocking, white noise. Ensure proper latching, burping after every feed, avoid overfeeding. Breastfeeding mothers should avoid excess tea/coffee, spicy food, gas-forming foods. Simethicone preferred over dicyclomine for safety.
source: Rome IV Criteria (2016), IAP Infant Care Guidelines, ESPGHAN Probiotic Working Group
guideline_changes: Rome IV criteria (2016) remain current standard; Lactobacillus reuteri DSM 17938 supported by recent meta-analyses as effective probiotic for breastfed colicky infants; no new 2024-2025 Rome criteria revision. Reassurance and parental support remain cornerstone.

first_line_drugs.notes: 6-12 sessions over 3 months; strongest evidence for functional abdominal pain disorders
8-12 sessions; addresses pain catastrophizing, improves coping and functioning
187 mg PO TID (age >8yr) for IBS-related pain; smooth muscle relaxant

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.5 mg/kg PO QHS (max 25 mg); neuromodulator for chronic functional pain
0.25-0.5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID (max 12 mg); for functional dyspepsia/abdominal migraine
10 billion CFU/day PO; modest evidence for functional abdominal pain

investigations.name: Stool calprotectin
investigations.indication: Distinguish organic from functional cause — <50 mcg/g suggests functional

investigations.name: Celiac screen (tTG-IgA + total IgA)
investigations.indication: Celiac disease screening — recommended in all chronic abdominal pain

investigations.name: Urinalysis
investigations.indication: UTI can present as abdominal pain in children

investigations.name: Abdominal ultrasound
investigations.indication: If atypical features or to reassure family

counselling: Chronic belly pain in children is very common and usually not from a serious disease
Stress, anxiety, and school pressure are common triggers — addressing these often helps the most
Gut-directed therapy (hypnotherapy/CBT) has the strongest evidence — consider it as first treatment
Keep a pain diary: when, where, how severe, and what the child ate — bring it to follow-ups
Red flags that need urgent evaluation: weight loss, blood in stool, fever, night-time pain waking the child
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI if red flags present or no improvement after 4-8 weeks. Psychology/hypnotherapy for gut-brain axis therapy. Dietitian if dietary triggers identified. Surgery urgently if acute abdomen suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Acute abdomen (appendicitis, volvulus, obstruction). Significant weight loss with abdominal pain. GI bleeding. Intractable pain requiring IV pain management
notes: Most pediatric chronic abdominal pain is functional (Rome IV criteria). Red flags (weight loss, GI bleeding, fever) require workup. Gut-brain psychotherapy is first-line per 2025 guidelines.
guideline_changes: 2025 ESPGHAN/NASPGHAN IBS/FAP guidelines published; gut-brain psychotherapies have strongest evidence

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg PO/IV single dose (max 4 mg); may repeat q8h. For AGE-related vomiting
5 mL PO q2-5min after vomiting; increase as tolerated to prevent dehydration

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.25-0.5 mg/kg PO/IM/IV q4-6h (max 25 mg); age >2yr. Sedation risk
1.25 mg/kg PO/IV q6h (max 50 mg/dose); for motion sickness or vestibular vomiting
0.1-0.15 mg/kg PO/IV q6-8h (max 10 mg); limited use due to EPS risk
0.15 mg/kg IV q6h (max 10 mg) for CINV or cyclic vomiting prophylaxis

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes
investigations.indication: Metabolic alkalosis (hypokalemic, hypochloremic) from persistent vomiting

investigations.name: Urinalysis
investigations.indication: UTI can present with vomiting in young children

counselling: Your child is vomiting — small frequent sips of ORS are more effective than large amounts
Wait 10 minutes after vomiting, then restart ORS in small sips (5 mL every 2-5 minutes)
Once vomiting stops, gradually return to a normal diet — do not keep the child on liquids only
Watch for: vomiting green/yellow fluid (bile), blood in vomit, or severe belly pain — come back immediately
For young infants (under 2 months), any persistent vomiting needs urgent evaluation
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery urgently if bilious vomiting in infant (rule out malrotation/volvulus). GI for chronic/recurrent vomiting. Neurology if vomiting with headache or neurological signs (raised ICP). Endocrinology for cyclic vomiting syndrome
hospitalisation_criteria: Bilious vomiting in any infant (surgical emergency until proven otherwise). Severe dehydration. Projectile vomiting in infant <3 months (pyloric stenosis). Hematemesis. Altered mental status. Intractable vomiting >24 hours
notes: Ondansetron is first-line for AGE-related vomiting; single dose usually sufficient. Rule out surgical emergencies (pyloric stenosis, malrotation, obstruction) before attributing to benign cause.

first_line_drugs.notes: Directed therapy based on etiology: antibiotics for infection, chemo for malignancy, ERT for storage disease
Monitor CBC, LFTs, coagulation; imaging with ultrasound; avoid contact sports if splenomegaly

second_line_drugs.notes: 60 U/kg IV q2wk for Gaucher disease; reduces hepatosplenomegaly and improves cytopenias
Age-adjusted dosing PO TID for Gaucher type 1 if ERT unavailable; substrate reduction therapy

counselling: Enlarged liver and spleen is a sign of an underlying condition — investigations are needed to find the cause
Avoid contact sports and rough play to prevent splenic injury while the spleen is enlarged
Report fever, increasing abdominal distension, or unusual bleeding immediately
Follow all scheduled investigations and follow-up visits
Treatment depends on the underlying diagnosis — once identified, targeted therapy will be started
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric hematologist/oncologist or gastroenterologist based on suspected etiology; urgent if rapidly progressive or malignancy suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe cytopenias, suspected malignancy, splenic rupture risk, or need for liver/bone marrow biopsy
notes: Hepatosplenomegaly is a sign, not a diagnosis; urgent workup to identify underlying cause. Common etiologies: infections, hemolytic anemias, storage diseases, malignancies.

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mL/kg PO over 3-4h for dehydration; ad libitum for maintenance
10 mg/day PO (<6mo) or 20 mg/day PO (>6mo) x10-14 days per WHO guidelines
Continue breastfeeding; regular diet. Reduce juice/sugary drinks for toddler diarrhea

second_line_drugs.notes: 1.5 mg/kg PO TID x5-7 days; antisecretory for acute watery diarrhea
0.05-0.1 mg/kg PO TID (max 2 mg/dose); chronic diarrhea only, age >2yr
250-750 mg/day PO x5-7 days as adjunct; evidence for duration reduction

investigations.name: Stool culture
investigations.indication: If bloody diarrhea or persistent diarrhea

investigations.name: Stool calprotectin
investigations.indication: If chronic diarrhea — distinguish inflammatory from functional

counselling: Your child has loose stools — ORS and zinc are the most important treatment
Give ORS after every loose stool — it replaces the water and salts lost
Continue feeding normally — stopping food makes diarrhea worse, not better
If diarrhea lasts more than 2 weeks, further investigation is needed — come for follow-up
Reduce fruit juice and sugary drinks — too much juice is a common cause of loose stools in toddlers
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI if diarrhea persists >14 days (persistent) or >4 weeks (chronic). Allergy/immunology if food allergy or immunodeficiency suspected. Dietitian for chronic diarrhea dietary management
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe dehydration. Failed oral rehydration. Bloody diarrhea with systemic toxicity. Infant <3 months with significant diarrhea. Chronic diarrhea with failure to thrive
notes: Investigate persistent (>14 days) or chronic (>4 weeks) diarrhea for underlying cause. Toddler diarrhea is most common chronic cause in ages 1-3yr and is managed with dietary changes.

counselling: Many newborn movements are normal or benign. — नवजात शिशु की कई हलचलें सामान्य होती हैं।
Seizure mimics do not usually cause brain damage. — ये दौरे जैसी हलचलें आमतौर पर दिमाग को नुकसान नहीं पहुँचाती।
Avoid unnecessary panic or medication without confirmation. — बिना पुष्टि के घबराएं नहीं और दवा शुरू न करें।
Observe for red flags such as apnea, cyanosis, feeding difficulty, eye deviation, or persistent rhythmic movements. — सांस रुकना, नीलापन, दूध न पीना, आंखों का टेढ़ा होना जैसे लक्षणों पर ध्यान दें।
Most mimics resolve with time. — ये अधिकतर अपने आप ठीक हो जाती हैं।
warning_signs: Apnea with bradycardia or cyanosis — सांस रुकना, धड़कन धीमी होना या नीलापन
Eye deviation or lip smacking — आंखों का टेढ़ा होना या होंठ चटकाना
Persistent tonic or clonic movements — लगातार अकड़न या झटके
Non-suppressible movements — पकड़ने पर भी न रुकने वाली हलचल
Poor feeding or lethargy — दूध न पीना या सुस्ती
Abnormal neurological examination — असामान्य तंत्रिका जांच
expected_course: Benign neonatal sleep myoclonus resolves spontaneously by 3-6 months. Jitteriness usually resolves once metabolic cause (hypoglycemia, hypocalcemia) is corrected. Hyperekplexia may require long-term medication. Video-EEG helps differentiate true seizures from mimics. Most mimics have an excellent prognosis.
key_clinical_points: Ask parents to record a video of the episode for review
Stimulus-sensitive and suppressible movements favor mimic over seizure
Normal interictal EEG and neurological exam are reassuring
Do NOT start anticonvulsants without confirmed seizure diagnosis
Benign sleep myoclonus occurs ONLY during sleep and stops on arousal
Jitteriness may indicate underlying metabolic derangement requiring correction

severity_assessment.mild: Isolated jitteriness or brief myoclonic jerks during sleep with normal exam — observation and parental reassurance
severity_assessment.moderate: Recurrent episodes with parental anxiety, uncertain diagnosis — video documentation, metabolic screen, neurology follow-up
severity_assessment.severe: Non-suppressible rhythmic movements, eye deviation, apnea, altered tone — urgent EEG and full neonatal seizure workup, consider admission

first_line_drugs.notes: Balance and coordination training; gait rehab; 4-wk inpatient rehab improves ataxia scores
5 mg PO BID for episodic ataxia type 2; reduces attack frequency (Level B AAN)
50 mg PO BID for mixed/Friedreich/SCA ataxias; probably improves ataxia signs

second_line_drugs.notes: 150 mg PO daily for Friedreich ataxia age >=16 yrs; FDA-approved 2023; NRF2 activator
PO for Niemann-Pick type C; FDA-approved 2024; dose per weight-based schedule
2 g/kg IV over 2-5 days for acute post-infectious cerebellar ataxia; for refractory cases
5-10 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for episodic ataxia type 2 if 4-AP not tolerated

investigations.name: MRI brain with posterior fossa views
investigations.indication: Rule out posterior fossa tumor, stroke, demyelination

investigations.name: Urine toxicology screen
investigations.indication: Rule out drug ingestion in acute ataxia

counselling: Ataxia means your child has difficulty with balance and coordination - this has many possible causes
Keep the home environment safe: remove loose rugs, install grab bars, pad sharp furniture corners
Some causes of ataxia are treatable, so completing all recommended tests is very important
If ataxia came on suddenly, bring the child to the hospital immediately
Physical therapy exercises at home help improve balance and should be practiced daily
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist for all new-onset ataxia for etiology workup; urgent referral if acute onset suggesting stroke or posterior fossa lesion
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for acute-onset ataxia (rule out posterior fossa pathology, stroke, intoxication), progressive ataxia with raised ICP signs, or inability to walk/stand safely
notes: Treatment is etiology-specific; identify underlying cause before selecting therapy. New disease-modifying agents for Friedreich ataxia and NPC represent major recent advances.
guideline_changes: FDA approved omaveloxolone (Skyclarys) 2023 for Friedreich ataxia; levacetylleucine (Aqneursa) 2024 for NPC

first_line_drugs.notes: Urinalysis with microscopy, urine Ca/Cr ratio, serum creatinine, C3/C4, renal US; repeat UA x3 to confirm
Reassurance and serial monitoring q3-6mo if isolated microscopic hematuria without proteinuria or HTN

second_line_drugs.notes: Enalapril 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO if concurrent proteinuria >0.5 g/day develops
Treat underlying UTI if identified; cephalexin 25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided TID-QID for 7-10 days

investigations.name: Urinalysis with microscopy (x3)
investigations.indication: Confirm persistence; dysmorphic RBCs suggest glomerular origin

counselling: Blood has been found in child's urine; most causes in children are not serious
Several tests are needed to find the cause; this may take a few visits
If urine is tea-colored, face is puffy, or blood pressure is high, come immediately
Avoid dehydration; encourage child to drink adequate water
Follow up regularly for urine tests until the cause is identified and resolved
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist if proteinuria present, hypertension, low complement, family history of renal disease, or persistent gross hematuria
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if gross hematuria with clots, associated nephritic syndrome, or significant proteinuria
notes: Most isolated hematuria is benign; confirm persistence with 3 samples 2 weeks apart. Refer to nephrology if proteinuria, HTN, decreased C3, or family history of renal disease.

first_line_drugs.notes: For GCS <9: Rocuronium 1 mg/kg + Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV; use cuffed ETT; verify with ETCO2
5 mL/kg IV over 10-20 min if signs of herniation; monitor Na q4-6h, target Na <160
2-5 mL/kg IV if glucose <60 mg/dL; maintain normoglycemia 80-180 mg/dL

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 g/kg IV over 15-20 min for acute herniation; hold if serum osm >320 mOsm/L
20-40 mg/kg IV load (max 1500 mg) for seizure prophylaxis or treatment
20 mg/kg IV q8h if viral encephalitis suspected; pending LP and PCR
0.1 mg/kg IV/IM/IN (max 2 mg) if opioid toxicity suspected; may repeat q2-3min

investigations.name: Complete metabolic panel
investigations.indication: Electrolytes, glucose, renal and liver function

counselling: Your child is critically ill and needs intensive care unit monitoring
The medical team will keep you informed about every step of treatment
The child may need a breathing tube and ventilator support — this is to protect the airway
Please share any information about medicines, chemicals, or recent illnesses
Recovery may take time — the team will support you throughout
referral_criteria: All comatose children: immediate referral to PICU. Neurosurgery if structural lesion on CT. Toxicology consult if poisoning suspected. Metabolic/genetics if inborn error suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: All comatose children (GCS <=8) require PICU admission with continuous monitoring, airway protection, and ICP management as indicated
notes: Secure airway immediately for GCS <9. Order CT head, full metabolic panel, ammonia, toxicology screen, and continuous EEG monitoring in PICU.
source: Brain Trauma Foundation Peds 3rd Ed, PALS/AHA 2025
guideline_changes: 2025 PALS updated to recommend cuffed ETT for all pediatric intubations

diagnosis_name: Altered Sensorium with Suspected Raised Intracranial Pressure

first_line_drugs.notes: Maintain SpO2 >95%, via mask/HFNC/ventilator as required
10–20 ml/kg IV bolus in shock, then maintenance; avoid hypotonic fluids
5 ml/kg IV bolus over 10–15 min, then 0.1–1 ml/kg/hr infusion for 48–72 hrs (monitor Na <155 mEq/L)
1–5 ml/kg IV over 20 min, repeat every 4–6 hr SOS; monitor serum osmolality <320 mOsm
2–4 ml/kg IV bolus if hypoglycemia present or suspected
20–40 mg/kg IV loading, then 10–20 mg/kg/day divided doses

second_line_drugs.notes: 50–100 mg/kg/day IV divided BD (empirical for meningitis/encephalitis)
10 mg/kg IV every 8 hr if HSV encephalitis suspected
20 mg PE/kg IV loading if seizures not controlled

investigations.name: Serum Electrolytes, Urea, Creatinine, LFT, Ammonia
CT/MRI Brain
investigations.indication: Focal deficit, unequal pupils, suspected raised ICP

investigations.name: Lumbar Puncture
investigations.indication: Suspected meningitis after stabilization (GCS >12 or stable)

counselling: Child is critically ill and requires ICU monitoring — बच्चा गंभीर रूप से बीमार है और ICU में निगरानी जरूरी है
Avoid giving anything orally if child is drowsy — अगर बच्चा सुस्त है तो मुंह से कुछ न दें
Strict adherence to medications is important — दवाइयाँ समय पर और नियमित रूप से देना बहुत जरूरी है
Keep child in calm, low-stimulation environment — बच्चे को शांत माहौल में रखें
Immediate reporting if condition worsens — हालत बिगड़ने पर तुरंत डॉक्टर को बताएं
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric intensivist/neurologist if GCS <12, seizures, focal deficits, or suspected raised ICP
hospitalisation_criteria: GCS <12, seizures, hypoxia, shock, raised ICP signs, inability to maintain airway
notes: Always treat ABC first. Assume raised ICP in all altered sensorium. Avoid overzealous hyperventilation and hypotonic fluids.
source: IAP Pediatric Emergency Guidelines, WHO 2023, Pediatric Neurocritical Care protocols
guideline_changes: Hypertonic saline increasingly preferred over mannitol in pediatric raised ICP
expected_course: Stabilization within 24–72 hrs; recovery depends on etiology (days to weeks)
key_clinical_points: ABC stabilization is the first priority
Hypoglycemia must be corrected immediately
Every altered sensorium patient should be treated as raised ICP until proven otherwise

severity_assessment.mild: Drowsy but arousable, normal vitals
severity_assessment.moderate: Confused, decreased response, abnormal vitals
severity_assessment.severe: GCS <8, unresponsive, airway compromise

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/dose PO 6-8 hourly SOS for fever >100°F
As per weight, TDS PO for 3-5 days, add-on for bodyache/high fever

second_line_drugs.notes: 8-10 mg/kg/day div BD PO for 5-7 days, only if persistent fever >3 days or lab evidence of bacterial infection

investigations.name: Blood culture
investigations.indication: Severe or prolonged fever to identify organism

investigations.name: Malaria antigen / Dengue NS1 + IgM
investigations.indication: If endemic area or clinical suspicion

counselling: Give paracetamol only when fever is above 100°F, do not give routinely
Offer frequent fluids - ORS, water, coconut water - to prevent dehydration
Use tepid sponging with lukewarm water for high fever, do not use cold water
If fever persists beyond 2-3 days, bring child for review with reports
Watch for warning signs: rash, bleeding, vomiting, altered sensorium, breathing difficulty - come to hospital immediately
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Persistent fever for more than 3 days
Rash or any bleeding from skin or gums
referral_criteria: Refer if fever persists >5 days without identifiable focus, suspected enteric fever or tropical infection requiring specialist evaluation, or immunocompromised child
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if toxic appearance, severe dehydration, altered sensorium, bleeding manifestations, persistent high-grade fever with hemodynamic instability, or age <3 months with fever
notes: Symptomatic treatment first. Avoid unnecessary antibiotics unless persistent fever >3 days, toxic child, raised CRP/Procalcitonin, or localizing signs. Supportive: adequate fluids (ORS, water, coconut water), light diet, tepid sponging, rest.
source: IAP Fever Guidelines, AIIMS Febrile Child Protocol, NICE Fever in Under 5s
guideline_changes: IAP 2024: Procalcitonin recommended as key biomarker to differentiate bacterial vs viral fever; emphasis on avoiding empirical antibiotics in first 72 hours unless toxic appearance or positive cultures; AIIMS Protocol: Structured fever workup with CBC, CRP, urine R/M, blood culture for fever >3 days; NICE 2024: Age-stratified approach - infants <3 months with fever require urgent evaluation and empiric antibiotics

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO once at headache onset; max 400 mg/dose; 68% response rate in trials
15 mg/kg PO once at headache onset; max 1000 mg/dose; 54% response rate

second_line_drugs.notes: 5 mg PO if <40 kg, 10 mg PO if >=40 kg; for moderate-severe headache
5-20 mg intranasal once; FDA-approved for adolescents >=12 yrs

investigations.name: Brain MRI
investigations.indication: Only if red flags present (progressive, focal signs, early morning vomiting)

investigations.name: Vision screening
investigations.indication: Refractive errors contribute to headaches in children

counselling: Give pain medicine (ibuprofen/paracetamol) early at the start of headache for best effect
Maintain regular sleep schedule, adequate hydration, and regular meals
Limit screen time; take regular breaks from computer/phone
Keep a headache diary noting triggers, duration, and what helped
Stress and anxiety are common triggers in school-going children; address them
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist if headaches are progressive, waking child from sleep, associated with vomiting, vision changes, or focal neurological signs
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for thunderclap headache, signs of raised ICP, meningism, or new focal neurological deficits
notes: Rule out secondary causes with red flag screening before treating as primary headache. NSAIDs are first-line; triptans reserved for moderate-severe or NSAID-refractory cases.
guideline_changes: AAN guideline reaffirmed Oct 2025; IHS global acute migraine recs published 2024

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.2-0.3 mg/kg (max 10mg), preferred pre-hospital non-IV route
0.5 mg/kg (max 20mg), seizure over 5 minutes, pre-hospital

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg IV (max 4mg), hospital management
15-20 mg/kg IV loading, status epilepticus

investigations.name: Blood glucose
investigations.indication: Rule out hypoglycaemia as seizure cause

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes
investigations.indication: If diarrhoea, vomiting, or abnormal fluid intake

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture
investigations.indication: Mandatory if <12 months or meningeal signs present; consider in 12-18 months if unimmunised

investigations.name: EEG
investigations.indication: Not indicated for simple febrile seizure; only if atypical features or developmental concerns

counselling: Febrile seizures look scary but are generally harmless and do not cause brain damage
During a seizure: lay child on side, do not put anything in mouth, time the seizure
If seizure lasts more than 5 minutes, call ambulance or rush to hospital
Paracetamol and ibuprofen do NOT prevent febrile seizures but help with fever comfort
Most children outgrow febrile seizures by age 5-6 years; long-term medicine is usually not needed
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist if complex febrile seizure (focal, >15 minutes, recurrent within 24 hours), seizure in child <6 months or >5 years, or developmental concerns
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if seizure duration >15 minutes (status epilepticus), post-ictal altered consciousness >1 hour, suspected meningitis (neck stiffness, bulging fontanelle), first seizure in infant <12 months, or complex febrile seizure for observation
notes: Antipyretics do NOT prevent recurrence. Long-term anticonvulsants not recommended for simple febrile seizures.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.01-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV continuous infusion; first-line inotrope; SUCRA 85% in 2024 meta-analysis
0.25-0.75 mcg/kg/min IV (load 50 mcg/kg over 15 min optional); reduces afterload + inotropy
1-2 mg/kg IV q6-12h for pulmonary edema/fluid overload; monitor electrolytes

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-20 mcg/kg/min IV continuous infusion; alternative inotrope if epinephrine unavailable
0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/min IV if vasodilatory component present; SUCRA 80% in meta-analysis
0.01-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV for duct-dependent cardiac lesions in neonates
Consider for refractory cardiogenic shock unresponsive to maximal vasoactive support

counselling: Your child's heart is not pumping blood effectively — this is a serious condition needing ICU care
Medicines will be given through IV to help the heart pump better
The child will be on continuous heart monitoring — alarms may sound but the team is watching
Fluid intake will be strictly controlled — please do not give extra fluids without asking
Follow-up with a heart specialist (cardiologist) is essential after discharge
referral_criteria: Cardiology mandatory. ECMO center referral if refractory to maximal vasoactive support. Cardiac surgery if structural heart disease. Consider cardiac transplant evaluation for end-stage cardiomyopathy
hospitalisation_criteria: All cardiogenic shock patients require PICU admission. Signs: hypotension, tachycardia, poor perfusion, hepatomegaly, pulmonary edema, rising lactate
notes: Avoid fluid boluses in cardiogenic shock unless clear evidence of hypovolemia. Milrinone preferred for post-surgical low cardiac output; dopamine no longer recommended first-line.
source: SSC Peds 2020, ACC Cardiogenic Shock 2025, PICU Network Meta-Analysis 2024
guideline_changes: 2024 network meta-analysis ranks epinephrine over dopamine; milrinone preferred for low CO states

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 mL/kg IV/IO bolus over 5-20 min; repeat up to 40-60 mL/kg in first hour; reassess after each
10-20 mL/kg IV if hemorrhagic shock and no response to 40-60 mL/kg crystalloid; use O-neg if urgent
15-20 mg/kg IV (max 1 g) over 10 min within 3h of trauma; then 2 mg/kg/hr for 8h

second_line_drugs.notes: pRBC:FFP:Plt at 1:1:1 ratio for massive hemorrhage; activate when >40 mL/kg blood products predicted
0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV if persistent hypotension after adequate volume; bridge therapy only
0.5 mL/kg IV slow push during massive transfusion to correct citrate-induced hypocalcemia

counselling: Your child has lost a significant amount of body fluid or blood and needs urgent replacement
IV fluids and possibly blood transfusion will be given to stabilize the child
The child will be monitored closely — heart rate, blood pressure, and urine output
Please tell us about any bleeding, vomiting, diarrhea, or injury before coming to hospital
Follow-up after discharge is important to ensure full recovery
referral_criteria: Surgical consultation if hemorrhagic cause. Gastroenterology if GI bleed. Hematology if coagulopathy. Transfer to trauma center if mechanism warrants
hospitalisation_criteria: All children with hypovolemic shock require admission. Signs: tachycardia, prolonged capillary refill >3 sec, altered mental status, hypotension (late sign), decreased urine output
notes: Tachycardia and poor perfusion are early signs; hypotension is a late and ominous finding in children. Delay intubation if possible until volume resuscitated to avoid hemodynamic collapse.
source: ATLS 10th Ed, SSC Peds 2020, Dutch Pediatric Society CPG

first_line_drugs.notes: High-flow oxygen immediately; maintain SpO2 >94%
Fluid bolus 20 mL/kg over 5–10 min; repeat up to 40–60 mL/kg with reassessment; stop if hepatomegaly, rales, or fluid overload
Start within 1 hour of recognition of septic shock; choice based on local protocol
0.05–0.3 mcg/kg/min IV for cold shock (first-line inotrope)
Start at 0.05 mcg/kg/min and titrate upward for warm shock

second_line_drugs.notes: 5–10 mcg/kg/min if epinephrine not available
Used in myocardial dysfunction with low cardiac output
Indicated in catecholamine-resistant shock or suspected adrenal insufficiency
Add in refractory warm shock not responding to norepinephrine

counselling: Septic shock is a life-threatening emergency requiring ICU care — सेप्टिक शॉक एक जानलेवा आपातकालीन स्थिति है जिसमें ICU में इलाज जरूरी है
Immediate treatment with fluids and antibiotics is essential — तुरंत तरल पदार्थ और एंटीबायोटिक से इलाज बहुत जरूरी है
Continuous monitoring is required — लगातार निगरानी जरूरी है
Condition can worsen rapidly if untreated — इलाज न होने पर हालत तेजी से बिगड़ सकती है
Follow-up and prolonged treatment may be required — फॉलो-अप और लंबे समय तक इलाज की जरूरत हो सकती है
warning_signs: Cold extremities or delayed capillary refill
referral_criteria: Immediate referral to PICU/intensivist
hospitalisation_criteria: All septic shock cases require ICU admission
notes: Follow ABC approach. Early fluid resuscitation and antibiotics are lifesaving. Identify cold vs warm shock to guide inotrope selection.
guideline_changes: Early epinephrine preferred over dopamine in pediatric septic shock
expected_course: Improvement depends on early resuscitation; stabilization usually within 24–72 hours if treated promptly
key_clinical_points: Give antibiotics within 1 hour
Fluid bolus 20 mL/kg rapidly
Start inotropes early if fluid refractory

severity_assessment.mild: Compensated shock with normal BP but poor perfusion
severity_assessment.moderate: Hypotension with tachycardia and poor perfusion
severity_assessment.severe: Refractory shock requiring multiple inotropes

first_line_drugs.notes: Watchful waiting 4-6 weeks for low-risk nodes (<2 cm, mobile, no systemic symptoms); most self-resolve
25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided QID x10 days for suspected bacterial lymphadenitis (S. aureus/GAS)

second_line_drugs.notes: 40-45 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID x10 days; broader coverage for lymphadenitis
30-40 mg/kg/day PO divided TID x10 days; if MRSA suspected or penicillin allergy

investigations.name: Mantoux test/IGRA
investigations.indication: Rule out tuberculosis in endemic area

counselling: Swollen lymph nodes are very common in children and usually due to infections
Most enlarged nodes will shrink on their own within 4-6 weeks
Give the full course of antibiotics if prescribed — do not stop early
Return if the node keeps growing, becomes very hard, or your child develops fever, weight loss, or night sweats
No need to worry if the node is small, soft, movable, and the child is otherwise well
referral_criteria: Refer for biopsy if node >2 cm persisting >6 weeks, supraclavicular location, associated systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss, night sweats), or hard/fixed node
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if suspected malignancy, abscess needing drainage, or systemic illness with lymphadenopathy
notes: 90% of children aged 4-8 have palpable nodes. Red flags: supraclavicular location, >2 cm, systemic symptoms, persistent >6 weeks; biopsy if concerning.

first_line_drugs.notes: Multidisciplinary PT/OT/Speech therapy; federally mandated for ages 0-3 yrs
1-3 sessions/wk targeting expressive/receptive language goals
Fine motor, sensory integration, adaptive skills training; 1-3 sessions/wk

second_line_drugs.notes: 3-6 mg/kg/day elemental iron PO if iron-deficiency anemia contributing to delay
Levothyroxine 10-15 mcg/kg/day PO if hypothyroidism identified

investigations.name: Developmental screening (ASQ/DASII)
investigations.indication: Formal developmental assessment to quantify delay

investigations.name: Thyroid function test
investigations.indication: Rule out hypothyroidism as treatable cause

investigations.name: Hearing assessment (OAE/BERA)
investigations.indication: Hearing loss is common treatable cause of speech delay

investigations.name: Vision screening
investigations.indication: Visual impairment contributes to global delay

investigations.name: Genetic testing (microarray)
investigations.indication: If etiology unclear or dysmorphic features

counselling: Child is not reaching milestones at the expected age; early therapy makes a big difference
Start physiotherapy, speech therapy, and occupational therapy as recommended
Practice exercises at home daily as taught by the therapist
Screen hearing and vision as they can contribute to developmental delay
Track milestones using a milestone diary; share progress with the doctor at each visit
referral_criteria: Refer to developmental pediatrician, early intervention program; audiology and ophthalmology screening
hospitalisation_criteria: Admission usually not required unless associated with acute seizures, severe malnutrition, or metabolic crisis
notes: Earlier identification leads to better outcomes; screen at 9, 18, and 30 months per AAP. Treat underlying causes (metabolic, genetic, nutritional) while providing therapy.
source: AAP 2022, CDC Learn the Signs Act Early
guideline_changes: AAP/CDC updated milestone checklists in 2022 raising threshold from 50% to 75%

first_line_drugs.notes: Target 120-150% estimated energy requirement; calorie-dense foods; fortify breast milk/formula
For infants: assess latch, increase feeds; concentrate formula to 24-30 kcal/oz if needed
Registered dietitian evaluation; structured meal schedule; eliminate juice/low-calorie fillers
Daily supplementation; iron 2-6 mg/kg/day if deficient; zinc 1 mg/kg/day if deficient; PO

second_line_drugs.notes: PediaSure 1.0-1.5 kcal/mL; 1-3 servings/day between meals; ages 1+
2 mg PO BID (ages 2-6); 4 mg PO BID-TID (ages 7+); appetite stimulant; off-label
For severe FTT unresponsive to oral; continuous or bolus enteral feeds; multidisciplinary decision

investigations.name: Urinalysis
investigations.indication: Rule out UTI or renal tubular acidosis

investigations.name: Celiac screen (tTG-IgA)
investigations.indication: Rule out celiac disease if chronic

counselling: Increase calorie density of foods — add ghee, cream, or powdered milk to regular meals
Feed every 2-3 hours; do not let the child fill up on juice or water before meals
Maintain a food diary to track what and how much the child actually eats
Growth catch-up takes weeks — do not expect overnight improvement
Bring the child for weight checks every 1-2 weeks initially
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric gastroenterologist if no weight gain after 3 months of nutritional intervention, or if organic cause (celiac, GERD, metabolic) suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe malnutrition (weight <60% of expected), dehydration, hypothermia, hypoglycemia, or signs of neglect/abuse
notes: Inadequate caloric intake is the most common cause. Multidisciplinary approach (pediatrics, nutrition, social work) is optimal; investigate neglect and psychosocial factors.
guideline_changes: UCSF consensus now uses term 'inadequate growth' replacing 'failure to thrive'

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-3 sessions/week; sensory-based feeding therapy (SOS or Sequential Oral Sensory approach); individualized
1-3 sessions/week; oral motor exercises, swallowing assessment/treatment; ASHA-guided
Structured mealtimes; positive reinforcement; graduated food exposure; parent training

second_line_drugs.notes: High-calorie formula or oral supplements to maintain growth while therapy progresses
Specialized seating, adapted utensils, modified textures per OT/SLP assessment
For severe oral aversion or aspiration risk; bridge to oral feeding; multidisciplinary decision
2 mg PO BID (ages 2-6); appetite stimulant for severe food refusal; off-label; short-term

counselling: Do not force-feed — it worsens food aversion in children
Offer new foods alongside familiar favorites, without pressure
Keep mealtimes under 30 minutes and maintain a regular schedule
Eat together as a family; children learn by watching others eat
Small improvements in feeding are meaningful — be patient with therapy progress
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric GI or feeding team if aspiration risk, weight loss despite intervention, or structural/neurological cause identified
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe failure to thrive with dehydration, aspiration pneumonia, or need for nasogastric/gastrostomy tube placement
notes: Multidisciplinary assessment (OT, SLP, dietitian, GI) is essential. Rule out structural, neurological, and GI causes before treating as behavioral.
guideline_changes: 2024 AOTA state-of-science review updated evidence for sensory-based feeding interventions

first_line_drugs.notes: Dietitian-led caloric needs assessment; age-appropriate dietary plan; weekly weight monitoring
High-calorie diet 120-150% of RDA; fortified foods; frequent small meals; adjust to age
Age-appropriate daily multivitamin with iron and zinc; correct specific deficiencies identified on labs

second_line_drugs.notes: PediaSure/Ensure 1-3 servings/day as caloric supplement; 240 kcal per 8 oz; ages 1-13
2 mg PO BID-TID (ages 2-6) or 4 mg PO BID-TID (ages 7+); appetite stimulant; off-label
For severe malnutrition or oral intake failure; continuous or bolus feeds; inpatient or home

investigations.name: ESR/CRP
investigations.indication: Screen for inflammatory or infectious cause

investigations.name: Stool examination
investigations.indication: Rule out parasitic infection or malabsorption

counselling: Offer 5-6 small energy-dense meals per day instead of 3 large meals
Add ghee, butter, or oil to dal and roti to increase calories
Track weight weekly at the same time on the same scale
Do not force-feed — create a calm, distraction-free mealtime environment
Follow up every 2 weeks until weight gain trend is established
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric gastroenterologist if weight loss exceeds 10% of body weight, chronic diarrhea persists, or malignancy/celiac/IBD suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe dehydration, hemodynamic instability, weight loss >10% with inability to tolerate oral intake, or suspected malignancy
notes: Investigate underlying etiology (malignancy, celiac, IBD, eating disorders, psychosocial). Weight loss >5% in children warrants urgent workup.

diagnosis_name: Systemic inflammatory response syndrome of infectious origin with organ failure

first_line_drugs.notes: Ceftriaxone 50-100 mg/kg IV + Vancomycin 15 mg/kg IV within 1h; adjust per culture results
10-20 mL/kg IV bolus; up to 40-60 mL/kg in first hour; monitor for fluid overload signs
0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/min IV for fluid-refractory shock; titrate to MAP appropriate for age

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/min IV for cold shock with low cardiac output phenotype
2 mg/kg IV bolus (max 100 mg) then divided q6h for catecholamine-resistant shock
20-40 mg/kg IV q8h (max 2 g/dose) if ESBL or multidrug-resistant organisms suspected
1 g/kg IV over 12h; consider for toxic shock syndrome or streptococcal sepsis

counselling: Your child has a serious whole-body infection response with organ involvement
Treatment in the ICU with strong antibiotics and supportive care is needed
Multiple IV medicines and close monitoring will be required
The medical team will update you regularly on your child's condition
Follow-up after discharge is essential to ensure complete recovery of all organ functions
referral_criteria: PICU mandatory. Infectious disease for resistant organisms. Subspecialty referral based on organ failure (nephrology for AKI, pulmonology for ARDS, cardiology for myocardial dysfunction)
hospitalisation_criteria: All patients with sepsis with organ failure (Phoenix Score >=2) require PICU admission. Any hemodynamic instability, respiratory failure, or end-organ damage
notes: Phoenix Sepsis Score >=2 now defines pediatric sepsis with organ dysfunction. Prioritize early antibiotics, aggressive but monitored fluid resuscitation, and early vasoactive support.
source: SSC Peds 2020, Phoenix Sepsis Criteria 2024, SCCM PANDEM 2024
guideline_changes: 2024 Phoenix criteria replaced SIRS-based definitions; PANDEM guidelines published for PICU care

diagnosis_name: Stress Hyperglycemia in Critically Ill Child

first_line_drugs.notes: Start 0.02–0.05 units/kg/hr IV infusion; titrate to maintain RBS 80–180 mg/dL
Use for dilution of insulin infusion (e.g., 1 unit/ml concentration); maintain IV fluids as per requirement

second_line_drugs.notes: Add if glucose falls <100 mg/dL or to maintain GIR in metabolic conditions

investigations.name: Blood Gas (ABG)
investigations.indication: To rule out acidosis (DKA vs stress hyperglycemia)

counselling: High blood sugar is common in critically ill children — गंभीर बीमार बच्चों में शुगर बढ़ना आम है
Insulin infusion will be used to control glucose safely — इंसुलिन इन्फ्यूजन से शुगर को सुरक्षित तरीके से नियंत्रित किया जाएगा
Frequent monitoring of sugar levels is required — बार-बार शुगर की जांच जरूरी है
Do not stop feeding unless advised — जब तक डॉक्टर न कहें, खाना-पीना बंद न करें
Condition will be closely monitored in ICU — ICU में हालत की करीबी निगरानी की जाएगी
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric intensivist/endocrinologist if persistent hyperglycemia or suspected DKA
hospitalisation_criteria: All critically ill children with hyperglycemia requiring insulin infusion
notes: Do not reduce glucose infusion rate in metabolic conditions; instead add insulin to prevent catabolism
guideline_changes: Target glucose range relaxed to 80–180 mg/dL to avoid hypoglycemia risk
expected_course: Glucose stabilizes within 24–48 hrs with proper insulin titration
key_clinical_points: Start insulin if RBS >180 mg/dL
Avoid tight glycemic control to prevent hypoglycemia
Continue nutritional support (do not reduce GIR unnecessarily)

severity_assessment.mild: RBS 180–250 mg/dL, no symptoms
severity_assessment.moderate: RBS 250–400 mg/dL, mild dehydration
severity_assessment.severe: RBS >400 mg/dL or associated with acidosis/altered sensorium

monitoring_parameters.frequency: Hourly for first 4 hours, then 2–4 hourly

diagnosis_name: Electrolyte Imbalance with Laboratory Corrections (Corrected Calcium, Sodium, Osmolality)

first_line_drugs.notes: Management depends on primary abnormality (e.g., hypocalcemia, hyponatremia, hyperglycemia, DKA). Use corrected values to guide therapy rather than measured values alone.
Use isotonic fluids initially; adjust based on corrected sodium and osmolality values

second_line_drugs.notes: Calcium, sodium, potassium correction guided by corrected lab values and clinical condition

investigations.name: Serum Calcium
investigations.indication: To calculate corrected calcium in hypoalbuminemia

investigations.name: Serum Sodium
investigations.indication: To calculate corrected sodium in hyperglycemia

counselling: Lab values may need correction based on albumin or glucose levels — जांच के नंबर एल्ब्युमिन या शुगर के अनुसार ठीक किए जा सकते हैं
Treatment decisions are based on corrected values, not raw reports — इलाज सही किए गए नंबरों पर आधारित होता है, सिर्फ रिपोर्ट पर नहीं
Do not panic with abnormal numbers without clinical correlation — असामान्य नंबर देखकर घबराएं नहीं, डॉक्टर से सलाह लें
Regular monitoring is required for accurate treatment — सही इलाज के लिए नियमित जांच जरूरी है
Follow doctor advice strictly for fluid and electrolyte correction — तरल पदार्थ और इलेक्ट्रोलाइट सुधार के लिए डॉक्टर की सलाह का सख्ती से पालन करें
referral_criteria: Refer if severe electrolyte imbalance, altered sensorium, or complex metabolic disturbance
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if symptomatic electrolyte imbalance or abnormal osmolality requiring IV correction
notes: Important formulae: Corrected Calcium = Total Ca + 0.8(4 - albumin). Corrected Na = Measured Na + 1.6[(glucose - 100)/100]. Osmolality = 2Na + glucose/18 + BUN/2.8.
expected_course: Electrolyte correction depends on underlying cause; lab values normalize gradually with treatment
key_clinical_points: Always use corrected calcium in hypoalbuminemia
Correct sodium in hyperglycemia before interpreting hyponatremia
Osmolality helps assess severity of metabolic derangement

severity_assessment.mild: Minor lab abnormalities without symptoms
severity_assessment.moderate: Lab abnormalities with mild clinical symptoms
severity_assessment.severe: Significant lab derangement with neurological or cardiac symptoms

first_line_drugs.notes: First morning urine protein/creatinine ratio; repeat x3 to exclude orthostatic proteinuria; renal function panel
Enalapril 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO for persistent proteinuria >0.5 g/day; uptitrate q4-8 wk to effect

second_line_drugs.notes: Losartan 0.7-1.4 mg/kg/day PO once daily if ACEi not tolerated; antiproteinuric
Sodium restriction <2 g/day enhances ACEi/ARB antiproteinuric effect; dietary counseling

counselling: Protein is leaking into child's urine; this needs investigation to find the cause
First morning urine sample is important for accurate testing
Orthostatic proteinuria (only when standing) is harmless and common in adolescents
Low-salt diet helps reduce proteinuria along with medicine if needed
Regular follow-up urine tests are needed every 1-3 months
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist if persistent non-orthostatic proteinuria >0.5 g/day, associated hematuria, or declining renal function
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if nephrotic-range proteinuria with edema or associated renal dysfunction
notes: Exclude orthostatic proteinuria (benign) with first morning sample. Persistent non-orthostatic proteinuria warrants nephrology referral and consideration of biopsy.

first_line_drugs.notes: 2-5 mL/kg IV over 10-20 min for raised ICP; may infuse 0.1-1 mL/kg/hr continuous
20-40 mg/kg IV load (max 1500 mg) for seizure prophylaxis in severe TBI (GCS <8)
1-2 mcg/kg IV for sedation/analgesia; continuous infusion 1-3 mcg/kg/hr in ventilated patients
For GCS <9: Rocuronium 1 mg/kg + Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV; avoid hypoxia and hypotension

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 g/kg IV over 15-20 min for acute herniation; hold if serum osm >320
5-15 mg/kg IV load then 1-3 mg/kg/hr infusion for refractory ICP; requires EEG monitoring
20 mg/kg IV load (max rate 1 mg/kg/min) if levetiracetam unavailable; monitor ECG

investigations.name: CT head without contrast
investigations.indication: All moderate-severe TBI (GCS <13); apply PECARN rules for mild TBI

counselling: Head injuries in children need close monitoring — watch for excessive sleepiness, repeated vomiting, or seizures
Keep the child's head slightly elevated while resting
Do not give any sedating medicines without doctor's advice
Return immediately if child becomes confused, has a seizure, or clear fluid leaks from nose/ear
Follow-up with neurosurgery is mandatory — do not miss appointments
referral_criteria: GCS <13 or deteriorating GCS; focal neurological deficits; penetrating injury; skull fracture with depression; seizures; CSF leak; need for ICP monitoring or neurosurgical intervention
hospitalisation_criteria: GCS <15 persisting >2 hours; any abnormality on CT head; post-traumatic seizure; coagulopathy; vomiting >3 times; age <1 year with any mechanism of concern
notes: Maintain CPP 40-50 mmHg; avoid hypotension, hypoxia, and hyperthermia. Place ICP monitor for GCS <8 after resuscitation; target PaCO2 35-39 mmHg.
source: Brain Trauma Foundation Peds TBI 3rd Ed, CHOP TBI Pathway 2024
guideline_changes: 2024 WVU guideline updated levetiracetam as preferred seizure prophylaxis over phenytoin

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid IV bolus; repeat up to 3 boluses then consider blood products
10-20 mL/kg IV if hemorrhagic shock unresponsive to 40-60 mL/kg crystalloid; use O-neg if uncrossmatched
15-20 mg/kg IV (max 1 g) over 10 min within 3h of injury; then 2 mg/kg/hr infusion for 8h
1-2 mcg/kg IV q30-60min PRN or intranasal for trauma pain; avoid sedation in unprotected airway

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg IV for RSI or procedural sedation; maintains hemodynamic stability
pRBC:FFP:Platelets in 1:1:1 ratio for life-threatening hemorrhage; activate early
0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV if vasopressor needed after adequate volume resuscitation

investigations.name: Trauma CT (head, chest, abdomen-pelvis)
investigations.indication: Identify internal injuries in significant mechanism

counselling: Your child has multiple injuries and needs careful monitoring in the hospital
Report immediately if the child becomes drowsy, has difficulty breathing, or has increasing pain
Follow all follow-up appointments with the trauma team
Rehabilitation and physiotherapy may be needed after discharge
Keep the child calm and comfortable — emotional support is important during recovery
referral_criteria: Trauma center referral for: hemodynamic instability despite resuscitation, suspected internal organ injury, traumatic brain injury, spinal injury, vascular injury, or need for surgical intervention
hospitalisation_criteria: All polytrauma patients requiring resuscitation; hemodynamic instability; altered mental status; suspected internal bleeding; any injury requiring surgical intervention
notes: Follow ATLS primary survey (ABCDE) adapted for pediatric anatomy. Hypotension is a late sign in children; tachycardia and poor perfusion are early indicators of shock.
source: ATLS 10th Ed, CHOP Trauma Resuscitation Pathway 2024

diagnosis_name: Open wound of unspecified body region

first_line_drugs.notes: Lidocaine 4%-Epinephrine 0.1%-Tetracaine 0.5% gel; apply to wound edges 20-30 min before repair
Local infiltration 1-2 mg/kg (max 4.5 mg/kg); buffer with NaHCO3 (1:10 ratio) to reduce pain
Copious NS irrigation under pressure (18G syringe); minimum 200 mL for contaminated wounds
TdaP 0.5 mL IM if not current; add TIG 250 units IM if dirty wound and incomplete series

second_line_drugs.notes: 2-octyl cyanoacrylate (Dermabond) for clean linear lacerations <5 cm; not for high-tension areas
25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided q6-8h for 5-7 days if high infection risk (bite, crush, contaminated)
25-45 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h for bite wounds; 5-7 day course

investigations.name: X-ray of affected area
investigations.indication: Rule out fracture or retained foreign body

investigations.name: Wound culture
investigations.indication: If signs of infection present, bite wounds

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: If significant blood loss or infection suspected

counselling: Keep the wound clean and dry for 48 hours, then gently clean with soap and water daily
Watch for signs of infection: increasing redness, swelling, pus, or fever — come back immediately
Complete the full course of antibiotics if prescribed
If stitches were placed, come back on the date given for removal or checkup
Ensure your child's tetanus vaccination is up to date
referral_criteria: Wounds involving tendons, nerves, or vessels; wounds requiring specialist repair (facial lacerations near eyes/lips, hand injuries); bite wounds with deep tissue involvement; wounds with foreign body not removable in ED
hospitalisation_criteria: Wound with active hemorrhage not controlled by pressure; associated fracture or vascular compromise; extensive wound requiring operative repair under general anesthesia; wound infection with systemic signs (fever, cellulitis)
notes: Use absorbable sutures (chromic gut/poliglecaprone) in children to avoid traumatic removal. Assess tetanus status, neurovascular integrity, and tendon function before closure.
source: CHOP Laceration Pathway 2024, Texas Children's Wound Care 2024
guideline_changes: 2024 TCH guideline updated to prefer absorbable deep sutures (poliglecaprone 5-0/6-0)

diagnosis_name: Foreign body in eye and adnexa

first_line_drugs.notes: Irrigate affected eye with 500-1000mL NS; evert lids; sweep fornices with moist cotton swab
1-2 drops for anesthesia before examination/removal; single use; do not prescribe for home
Moisten tip, roll gently over superficial conjunctival/corneal FB under slit lamp

second_line_drugs.notes: For embedded corneal FB; under topical anesthesia; by trained provider; ophthalmology referral
Apply 1cm ribbon qid x5-7d post-removal to prevent infection of corneal defect
1 drop qid x5-7d post-removal for corneal abrasion prophylaxis; age ≥1yr
1 drop bid-tid for ciliary spasm/photophobia after corneal FB removal

investigations.name: Slit lamp examination
investigations.indication: Detailed corneal assessment and FB localisation

investigations.name: Fluorescein staining
investigations.indication: Assess corneal abrasion and rule out perforation (Seidel test)

counselling: Do not rub the eye - this can push the object deeper and scratch the cornea
Flush the eye with clean water if a chemical or small particle is involved
Wear protective eyewear during sports and activities with flying debris
After removal, use prescribed antibiotic drops/ointment to prevent infection
If pain or vision problem continues after removal, return to doctor immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to ophthalmologist if embedded corneal FB, metallic FB with rust ring, suspected intraocular FB (penetrating injury), or corneal ulcer developing
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if suspected globe perforation/penetrating injury, intraocular FB, chemical burn, or corneal ulcer from FB
notes: Check visual acuity before any manipulation; fluorescein exam mandatory to assess corneal defect and rule out penetration. Young children may require sedation or OR removal under GA; ophthalmology consultation for embedded or metallic FBs.

first_line_drugs.notes: Under direct otoscopic visualization; first-line for visible non-spherical objects
Body-temp water via syringe; for non-organic, non-battery objects; intact TM required
Frazier suction tip applied to FB surface; for smooth/round objects in cooperative child

second_line_drugs.notes: Blunt probe tip with dried glue applied to FB surface; for round objects; cooperative patient
Operating microscope + microinstruments; for failed ED attempts, deep FB, or uncooperative child
4 drops bid x5-7d post-removal if canal abrasion or infection present

investigations.name: Otoscopy
investigations.indication: Visualize and identify the foreign body

investigations.name: X-ray temporal bone
investigations.indication: If metallic FB or button battery suspected

counselling: Do not try to remove the object at home - this may push it deeper and damage the ear
If it is a button battery in the ear, go to hospital immediately - it is an emergency
Keep the child calm and do not insert water or any liquid into the ear
After removal, some ear discomfort for 1-2 days is normal
Teach children not to insert objects into ears, nose, or mouth
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if failed removal attempt in clinic, deep or posteriorly lodged FB, button battery in ear (emergent), or uncooperative child needing removal under GA
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if button battery in ear canal (emergent removal), suspected TM perforation from FB, or need for removal under GA
notes: Button batteries require emergent removal due to risk of liquefactive necrosis; do NOT irrigate batteries. Limit removal attempts to 1-2 tries in ED; failed attempts increase complication risk and warrant ENT referral.
snomed_display: Foreign body in right external ear

first_line_drugs.notes: Definitive removal under general anesthesia; procedure of choice in pediatric FB aspiration
Infant <1 yr: 5 back blows + 5 chest thrusts; for acute choking with complete obstruction
Child >1 yr: subdiaphragmatic thrusts until object expelled or child becomes unresponsive

second_line_drugs.notes: Diagnostic or therapeutic in select cases; smaller caliber but limited extraction capability
0.5-0.6 mg/kg IV/PO (max 10 mg) pre/post bronchoscopy to reduce airway edema
Amoxicillin-clavulanate 45 mg/kg/day PO BID if secondary infection from prolonged impaction

investigations.name: Chest X-ray (inspiratory and expiratory)
investigations.indication: Look for air trapping, radio-opaque foreign body

investigations.name: CT chest
investigations.indication: If X-ray negative but clinical suspicion high

counselling: Your child had a foreign body in the airway which has been removed
Keep small objects (nuts, coins, button batteries, small toys) away from children under 3 years
Cut food into small pieces for young children - avoid whole grapes, popcorn, raw carrots
Teach older children to chew food properly and not to talk or laugh while eating
If your child suddenly starts coughing, choking, or turns blue, learn basic choking first aid
referral_criteria: Urgent ENT/pediatric surgery referral for bronchoscopic removal; immediate referral for complete airway obstruction
hospitalisation_criteria: All confirmed or suspected airway foreign bodies require hospital admission for bronchoscopy; observe 24h post-removal for airway edema
notes: Complications increase significantly after 24-48 hours of impaction. Urgent rigid bronchoscopy should not be delayed for imaging if clinical suspicion is high.

first_line_drugs.notes: Within 2h for esophageal button battery or sharp object; within 24h for esophageal coin/food bolus
10mL PO q10min (max 6 doses) while awaiting endoscopy for esophageal button battery
10mL PO q10min alternating with honey while awaiting endoscopy for esophageal battery
AP chest/abdomen; confirm location and track progression; q12-24h if in stomach

second_line_drugs.notes: For small blunt objects past esophagus; stool monitoring; repeat XR if not passed in 2wk
GoLYTELY 25mL/kg/h via NGT for multiple magnets or drug packets beyond endoscope reach
0.02-0.03mg/kg IV (max 0.5mg) for esophageal food bolus; smooth muscle relaxant

investigations.name: AP and lateral chest/abdomen X-ray
investigations.indication: Confirm FB location, type, and number

investigations.name: Repeat X-ray at 12-24 hours
investigations.indication: If FB in stomach - confirm progression through GI tract

investigations.name: Metal detector screening
investigations.indication: For metallic objects as radiation-free monitoring

counselling: If a button battery was swallowed and is in the food pipe (esophagus), it is a medical emergency
If a coin or small blunt object has passed into the stomach, most pass naturally in 4-6 days
Check the child stool for the object; report if not passed in 2 weeks
Do not try to make the child vomit - this can cause more damage
Childproof the home: keep small objects, batteries, and magnets away from young children
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric GI/surgeon immediately if esophageal FB, button battery ingestion, multiple magnet ingestion, or sharp object ingestion
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if esophageal FB of any type, button battery anywhere in GI tract, multiple magnets, sharp object, symptomatic FB (pain, drooling, vomiting), or large object unlikely to pass pylorus
notes: Button batteries in esophagus are a surgical emergency (remove <2h); magnets require urgent removal if >1 ingested due to bowel perforation risk. Most blunt objects past the esophagus pass spontaneously within 4-6 days.
guideline_changes: Honey/sucralfate pre-endoscopy for button battery ingestion now recommended (NASPGHAN 2023)

diagnosis_name: Burn and corrosion, unspecified body region

first_line_drugs.notes: 3 mL/kg/%TBSA over 24h; half in first 8h, rest over 16h; add D5 maintenance for <30 kg
0.1 mg/kg IV q2-4h PRN pain; max 0.2 mg/kg/dose; titrate to comfort
Apply topically to burn wound BID after cleansing; avoid on face/near eyes
TdaP 0.5 mL IM if not current; add TIG 250 units IM if incomplete primary series

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg IV for procedural sedation during debridement; 0.5 mg/kg for analgesia
Apply topically to deep burns BID; preferred for ear cartilage burns
0.5-1 g/kg IV if serum albumin <2 g/dL after 24h; adjunct to crystalloid

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Baseline and monitor for burn-related anaemia

investigations.name: Blood gas
investigations.indication: If inhalation injury suspected; monitor carboxyhaemoglobin

investigations.name: Wound swab culture
investigations.indication: If signs of burn wound infection

counselling: Cool the burn immediately with running cool water for 20 minutes - NOT ice or butter
Do not break blisters; cover with clean cling film and go to hospital
Pain management is very important - give paracetamol/ibuprofen regularly
Keep burn dressings clean; follow wound care instructions from the burn team
Watch for signs of infection: increasing redness, swelling, pus, or fever - see doctor immediately
referral_criteria: Transfer to burn centre if >10% TBSA, full-thickness burn, face/hands/feet/perineum/genitalia/joint burns, electrical or chemical burns, circumferential burns, or inhalation injury suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if >10% TBSA (>5% in <2 years), full-thickness burns, face/airway/hands/feet/perineum, electrical or chemical burns, suspected inhalation injury, circumferential burns, or non-accidental injury suspected
notes: Fluid resuscitation required for burns >10-15% TBSA in children. Monitor UOP target 0.5-1 mL/kg/hr; watch for fluid creep.
guideline_changes: Updated pediatric Parkland formula to 3 mL/kg/%TBSA with mandatory dextrose maintenance

diagnosis_name: Burns classified according to extent of body surface involved

first_line_drugs.notes: 3 mL/kg/%TBSA for first 24h; half in first 8h from time of burn; D5LR maintenance for <30 kg
0.1 mg/kg IV q2-4h PRN; consider PCA for older children; titrate to pain score
Apply topically BID to partial/full thickness burns; reassess wound daily

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mcg/kg IV q1-2h PRN or 1-2 mcg/kg intranasal for procedural pain
Emergent surgical procedure for circumferential full-thickness burns with compartment syndrome
0.1 mg/kg PO BID for severe burns >20% TBSA to reduce catabolism; max 20 mg/day

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes, renal and liver function
investigations.indication: Guide ongoing fluid management and detect complications

investigations.name: Albumin and prealbumin
investigations.indication: Nutritional status monitoring in major burns

investigations.name: Wound swab culture
investigations.indication: Monitor for burn wound infection; targeted antibiotics

investigations.name: Urine output monitoring
investigations.indication: Key guide for fluid resuscitation adequacy

counselling: Burns covering a large area are serious and need hospital treatment with IV fluids
The child will be in hospital for wound care, pain management, and monitoring
Nutrition is very important for burn healing - high-protein, high-calorie diet is needed
After healing, use sunscreen on burn scars and massage with moisturiser to reduce scarring
Compression garments and physiotherapy may be needed to prevent scar contracture
referral_criteria: Transfer to dedicated burn centre for all significant paediatric burns; refer to plastic surgery for scar management; refer to physiotherapy/OT for rehabilitation
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all major burns (>10% TBSA, full-thickness, face/hands/feet/perineum); ICU admission if >20% TBSA, inhalation injury, hemodynamic instability, or need for intubation
notes: Transfer to burn center for >10% TBSA, full-thickness, face/hands/feet/perineum, or electrical/chemical burns. Use Lund-Browder chart for accurate TBSA in children.
guideline_changes: 2024 Utah PTN guideline emphasizes early enteral nutrition within 6h for >20% TBSA burns

diagnosis_name: Poisoning by nonopioid analgesics, antipyretics and antirheumatics

first_line_drugs.notes: IV: 150 mg/kg over 1h, then 15 mg/kg/hr continuous; PO: 140 mg/kg load, then 70 mg/kg q4h x17 doses
1 g/kg PO (max 50 g) within 1-4h of acetaminophen ingestion; most effective within 1h
Maintenance fluids; correct dehydration; monitor hepatic and renal function q6-12h

second_line_drugs.notes: PEG 25 mL/kg/hr via NG for sustained-release preparations or massive NSAID ingestion
1-2 mEq/kg IV for salicylate poisoning; target urine pH 7.5-8.0 for enhanced elimination
For severe salicylate poisoning (level >100 mg/dL, renal failure, or refractory acidosis)

investigations.name: Serum acetaminophen level (4h post-ingestion)
investigations.indication: Plot on Rumack-Matthew nomogram for treatment decision

investigations.name: Basic metabolic panel with blood gas
investigations.indication: Acid-base status, renal function, anion gap

counselling: Paracetamol (acetaminophen) overdose can silently damage the liver — timely treatment is crucial
Keep all painkillers locked away and out of children's reach
The child needs blood tests every 6-12 hours to monitor liver function
Complete the full course of the antidote (NAC) even if the child seems well
After discharge, follow up in 1 week for repeat liver function tests
referral_criteria: Hepatology/liver transplant center if ALT >10,000 or INR >2 or signs of liver failure. Toxicology for complex mixed ingestion. Nephrology if salicylate poisoning with renal failure or severe acidosis requiring hemodialysis
hospitalisation_criteria: Acetaminophen level above treatment line on Rumack-Matthew nomogram; any elevation of ALT/AST; salicylate level >40 mg/dL; metabolic acidosis; altered mental status; intentional ingestion in adolescents
notes: Start NAC within 8h of acetaminophen ingestion for best efficacy; use Rumack-Matthew nomogram for risk assessment. For salicylate poisoning, alkalinize urine and monitor serial levels.
guideline_changes: New consensus guidelines released for acetaminophen poisoning management with updated NAC protocols

diagnosis_name: Poisoning by antiepileptic, sedative-hypnotic and antiparkinsonism drugs

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 g/kg PO (max 50 g) if within 1-2h of ingestion; multi-dose 0.5 g/kg q4h for carbamazepine/phenobarbital
Intubate for GCS <9 or loss of protective reflexes; RSI with Rocuronium 1 mg/kg IV
20 mL/kg bolus for hypotension; maintain euvolemia; monitor glucose and electrolytes

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.01 mg/kg IV (max 0.2 mg) over 15 sec for pure BZD overdose; repeat q60sec up to 0.05 mg/kg or 1 mg total
1-2 mEq/kg IV bolus for QRS widening from carbamazepine/TCA; target serum pH 7.45-7.55
For severe phenobarbital toxicity (level >100 mcg/mL) or refractory carbamazepine poisoning
1.5 mL/kg IV bolus then 0.25 mL/kg/min for lipophilic drug toxicity with cardiovascular collapse

investigations.name: Serum drug levels (specific to ingested drug)
investigations.indication: Quantify exposure and guide antidote therapy

counselling: Keep all medicines locked away and out of reach of children — even a small amount can be dangerous
Bring the medicine bottle or packaging to the hospital so doctors know exactly what was taken
The child will be monitored for several hours even if looking well — effects can be delayed
After discharge, child-proof your home and store all medicines safely
Call Poison Helpline (1800-11-6117) immediately if accidental ingestion happens again
referral_criteria: Toxicology consultation for severe poisoning. Nephrology if hemodialysis indicated (phenobarbital level >100 mcg/mL). PICU for intubated patients or hemodynamic instability
hospitalisation_criteria: GCS <12; respiratory depression requiring intervention; hemodynamic instability; QRS widening on ECG; seizures; need for multi-dose activated charcoal or hemodialysis
notes: Avoid flumazenil in mixed overdose or chronic benzodiazepine use (seizure risk). Multi-dose activated charcoal is effective for phenobarbital, carbamazepine, and phenytoin.

diagnosis_name: Poisoning by diuretics and other unspecified drugs, medicaments and biological substances

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 mL/kg IV bolus for dehydration/hypotension; repeat as needed; correct electrolyte abnormalities
1 g/kg PO (max 50 g) if within 1-2h of ingestion and patient is alert with intact airway
KCl 0.5-1 mEq/kg IV over 1h for hypokalemia; MgSO4 25-50 mg/kg IV for hypomagnesemia

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5 mL/kg IV slow push if symptomatic hypocalcemia or hyperkalemia (from K-sparing diuretics)
Norepinephrine 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV for refractory hypotension despite fluid resuscitation

investigations.name: Basic metabolic panel (serial q4-6h)
investigations.indication: Monitor K, Na, Mg, Ca, renal function

counselling: Keep all medicines locked away from children in a high, secure place
Your child may lose a lot of water and salts — we will replace them through IV
Watch for dizziness, muscle cramps, or fast heartbeat even after discharge
Follow up for blood tests to make sure salts (electrolytes) return to normal
Call Poison Helpline (1800-11-6117) for any future accidental medicine intake
referral_criteria: Nephrology if acute kidney injury or persistent electrolyte derangement. Cardiology if arrhythmias from electrolyte imbalance. Toxicology for complex or massive ingestion
hospitalisation_criteria: Symptomatic electrolyte derangement; ECG abnormalities; hemodynamic instability; large ingested amount; persistent vomiting preventing oral replacement
notes: Diuretic toxicity causes electrolyte derangements (hypoK, hypoNa, hypoMg) and dehydration. Monitor ECG for arrhythmias; effects may be delayed 2-4h until diuretic action begins.
source: AACT Position Statements, Poison Center Guidelines

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg IV loading dose, then 10 mg/kg IV q12h until alcohol level <30 mg/dL; safe in pediatrics
2-5 mL/kg IV bolus for hypoglycemia (common in pediatric ethanol ingestion); monitor glucose q1h
20 mL/kg IV bolus for dehydration; maintain euvolemia; correct metabolic acidosis

second_line_drugs.notes: 7.6 mL/kg loading dose IV, then 0.8-1.3 mL/kg/hr infusion; if fomepizole unavailable
For methanol/ethylene glycol with severe acidosis, visual changes, renal failure, or level >50 mg/dL
1-2 mg/kg IV q4-6h (max 50 mg) for methanol poisoning; enhances formate metabolism
Thiamine 100 mg IV + Pyridoxine 50 mg IV for ethylene glycol; cofactors for detoxification

investigations.name: Serum ethanol/methanol/ethylene glycol levels
investigations.indication: Identify toxic alcohol and guide treatment

investigations.name: Blood glucose (serial q1h)
