investigations.indication: Hypoglycemia common in pediatric ethanol ingestion

counselling: Alcohol and similar liquids (sanitizer, antifreeze) are extremely dangerous for children
Your child's blood sugar can drop dangerously — this is being monitored
Store all household chemicals and alcoholic beverages securely away from children
If the child consumed sanitizer or antifreeze, the antidote must be given for the full prescribed duration
Follow up for blood tests after discharge — kidney and eye checks may be needed
referral_criteria: Nephrology if methanol/ethylene glycol poisoning requiring dialysis. Ophthalmology for methanol exposure (visual assessment). Toxicology for all toxic alcohol ingestions. Psychiatry if intentional in adolescents
hospitalisation_criteria: Any toxic alcohol ingestion (methanol, ethylene glycol); ethanol with hypoglycemia, altered mental status, or glucose <60 mg/dL; any patient requiring fomepizole or hemodialysis
notes: Fomepizole blocks alcohol dehydrogenase, preventing toxic metabolite formation. Start before lab confirmation if high suspicion; early treatment may obviate need for hemodialysis.
guideline_changes: Fomepizole now preferred over ethanol infusion as antidote for toxic alcohols in pediatrics

first_line_drugs.notes: Nothing by mouth; assess airway for edema/stridor; intubate early if oropharyngeal burns or stridor
20 mL/kg IV bolus for shock; maintain euvolemia; correct acid-base and electrolyte abnormalities
Omeprazole 1 mg/kg IV q12-24h (max 40 mg) to reduce acid-mediated injury to damaged mucosa

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg IV q2-4h PRN for pain; avoid oral medications until endoscopy completed
Esophagogastroduodenoscopy for all symptomatic patients to grade injury; guides further management
50-100 mg/kg IV if evidence of perforation or mediastinitis; broad-spectrum coverage

investigations.name: Upper GI endoscopy (EGD)
investigations.indication: Grade mucosal injury — within 12-24h of ingestion

investigations.name: Chest X-ray (PA)
investigations.indication: Rule out mediastinal air (perforation) and aspiration

investigations.name: Abdominal X-ray
investigations.indication: Free air under diaphragm if perforation suspected

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Baseline; watch for infection if severe burns

counselling: Your child has swallowed a burning/corrosive substance — do NOT try to make the child vomit
Do not give milk or water to dilute unless the doctor says so
The child will need a camera test (endoscopy) to check for damage inside
Keep all cleaning products, acids, and alkalis locked away from children
Long-term follow-up may be needed to check for narrowing of the food pipe
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery/GI for endoscopy within 24h. ENT if laryngeal involvement. Transfer to burns center if extensive oral/pharyngeal burns. Pediatric surgery if perforation suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: All symptomatic corrosive ingestions (drooling, dysphagia, vomiting, oral burns, stridor). Intentional ingestion in adolescents. Any patient requiring endoscopy or NPO status
notes: Do NOT induce vomiting, give activated charcoal, or attempt neutralization. Endoscopy within 12-24h for symptomatic children; asymptomatic patients may be observed 2-4h and discharged.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.02-0.05 mg/kg IV q5-10min; double dose until secretions dry; may need very large cumulative doses
25-50 mg/kg IV over 30 min (max 1 g); then 10-20 mg/kg/hr infusion; give within 24-36h of exposure
Remove clothing, wash skin with soap and water; activated charcoal 1 g/kg PO if oral ingestion within 1h

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.3 mg/kg IV (max 10 mg) for seizures from organophosphate poisoning; repeat q5-10min PRN
0.01-0.02 mg/kg IV as alternative to atropine; does not cross BBB so won't treat CNS symptoms
4-8 mg/kg IV load then 0.5-1 mg/kg/hr; alternative oxime used in some regions

investigations.name: Serum cholinesterase (RBC and plasma)
investigations.indication: Confirm organophosphate poisoning — low levels diagnostic

investigations.name: ECG
investigations.indication: Cardiac arrhythmia from cholinergic excess or atropine

counselling: Pesticide poisoning is very serious — your child needs continuous monitoring in the hospital
Remove and discard all contaminated clothing; bathe the child thoroughly
Tell the doctor the exact name of the pesticide and how the child was exposed
Store all pesticides in original containers, locked away from children
Watch for muscle twitching, excessive secretions, or breathing difficulty even after treatment
referral_criteria: Toxicology/poison center consultation for all organophosphate poisonings. PICU if respiratory failure or seizures. Neurology for seizure management. Transfer to higher center if ventilator not available
hospitalisation_criteria: All symptomatic organophosphate/carbamate poisoning (SLUDGE symptoms). Any patient requiring atropine. Respiratory distress. Altered mental status. All intentional exposures
notes: Organophosphate poisoning causes cholinergic crisis (SLUDGE/DUMBELS). Titrate atropine to secretion drying, not pupil size; pralidoxime must be given early before aging occurs.
source: WHO Organophosphate Management, CDC ATSDR Guidelines

diagnosis_name: Toxic effect of venom of contact with venomous animals

first_line_drugs.notes: 4-6 vials IV initial dose (same as adult); 8-12 vials for severe; repeat 4-6 vials q6h x3 for control
20 mL/kg IV bolus for hypotension; aggressive fluid resuscitation for anaphylaxis or venom shock
0.01 mg/kg IM (1:1,000) max 0.3 mg for antivenom anaphylaxis; repeat q5-15min PRN

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 vials IV initial dose for pit viper envenomation; longer half-life, fewer recurrent symptoms
10-15 mL/kg IV for severe coagulopathy (fibrinogen <100, platelets <50K) unresponsive to antivenom
1 mg/kg IV (max 50 mg) premedication or for mild antivenom reactions; give with H2 blocker
TdaP 0.5 mL IM if not current; recommended for all envenomation wounds

investigations.name: Type and crossmatch
investigations.indication: Prepare for transfusion if severe coagulopathy

investigations.name: Serial limb circumference measurements
investigations.indication: Monitor progression of envenomation q15-30 min

counselling: Your child was bitten by a venomous animal — antivenom is the most important treatment
Keep the affected limb still and at heart level — do not apply tourniquet or ice
Do not try to suck out venom or apply any local remedies
Watch for swelling, bleeding, difficulty breathing, or allergic reactions after treatment
Follow up in 1-2 weeks as venom effects can recur (recurrent coagulopathy)
referral_criteria: Toxicology/envenomation specialist. Surgical consultation if compartment syndrome suspected. Hematology if severe coagulopathy. Transfer to antivenom-equipped center if antivenom unavailable
hospitalisation_criteria: All children with signs of envenomation (progressive swelling, coagulopathy, neurotoxicity). Antivenom administration. Observation for minimum 8-12h even for dry bites. Anaphylaxis to antivenom
notes: Children receive larger venom load relative to body size and are at higher morbidity risk. Antivenom dose is based on venom amount (not patient weight); serial limb measurements q15-60 min.
source: ACS Snakebite Guidelines, Texas Children's Envenomation Protocol

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.01 mg/kg (0.1 mL/kg of 1:10,000) IV/IO q3-5min during cardiac arrest; max single dose 1 mg
100% FiO2 via bag-mask ventilation; titrate to SpO2 94-99% once ROSC achieved
20 mL/kg IV/IO bolus for volume resuscitation; repeat as needed up to 60 mL/kg

second_line_drugs.notes: Target 33-34°C for 72h in neonatal HIE; initiate within 6h of birth
2-5 mL/kg IV for hypoglycemia (glucose <60 mg/dL); recheck in 15 min
1 mEq/kg IV slow push if pH <7.0 and prolonged arrest; dilute 1:1 in neonates

investigations.name: Blood gas (arterial)
investigations.indication: Assess pH, pCO2, lactate, base deficit

investigations.name: Blood glucose
investigations.indication: Hypoglycaemia common in post-arrest; monitor frequently

investigations.name: EEG
investigations.indication: If seizures suspected or for prognostication in HIE

investigations.name: MRI brain
investigations.indication: Assess hypoxic-ischaemic injury severity; day 3-5 optimal timing

counselling: This is a medical emergency - follow the doctor team instructions carefully
After recovery, watch for any changes in behaviour, memory, or movement
Baby-proof the home: keep plastic bags, cords, and small objects away from children
Learn infant/child CPR - it can save a life in choking or drowning emergencies
Follow up with your pediatrician for developmental monitoring after the event
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist if post-hypoxic seizures, abnormal neurological exam, developmental regression; refer to PICU for ongoing care
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require emergency hospitalisation; NICU/PICU admission for ongoing resuscitation, therapeutic hypothermia (neonates), seizure management, and neurological monitoring
notes: Prioritize airway management and ventilation; most pediatric arrests are respiratory in origin. Avoid hyperventilation; target normocapnia (ETCO2 35-40 mmHg) post-ROSC.
guideline_changes: 2025 AHA updated ventilation rates to 20-30 breaths/min for pediatric advanced airway

first_line_drugs.notes: Airway, breathing, circulation stabilization per ATLS; treat life-threatening injuries first
10 mg/kg PO q6-8h for pain; max 40 mg/kg/day; avoid if abdominal trauma suspected
20 mL/kg IV/IO bolus if signs of shock; repeat up to 60 mL/kg; assess after each bolus

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg IV q2-4h PRN for moderate-severe pain; monitor respiratory status
2-5 mg IV/SC if coagulopathy detected; evaluate for abusive head trauma

investigations.name: Skeletal survey (full)
investigations.indication: All children <2 years with suspected abuse

investigations.name: CT head without contrast
investigations.indication: Suspected abusive head trauma, altered mental status

investigations.name: CBC with differential
investigations.indication: Baseline evaluation, assess for bleeding disorder

investigations.name: Coagulation profile (PT, aPTT, INR)
investigations.indication: Rule out coagulopathy before attributing bruising to abuse

counselling: Your child is safe now and will receive the best care possible
A social worker will help your family through this process
Follow-up appointments are very important for your child's recovery
Please report any new concerns about your child's safety immediately
Counselling services are available for the whole family at no extra cost
referral_criteria: All cases: mandatory referral to child protection services (CPS). Refer to pediatric surgery for fractures/internal injuries; ophthalmology for retinal hemorrhages; neurosurgery for intracranial injury
hospitalisation_criteria: Any suspected abuse requiring medical stabilization; all children <2 years with unexplained injuries; any child with altered mental status, fractures, or internal organ injury
notes: Mandatory CPS reporting required. Perform skeletal survey in all children <2 years with suspected abuse; use TEN-4-FACESp screening for bruising.
guideline_changes: ACS Best Practices for Trauma Center Recognition of Child Abuse effective Jan 2024

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 mL/kg IV bolus; then aggressive fluid resuscitation targeting UOP 1-2 mL/kg/hr to prevent rhabdomyolysis
12-lead ECG on arrival; continuous monitoring for 24h minimum for all high-voltage injuries
0.1 mg/kg IV q2-4h PRN for pain; titrate to comfort; assess for compartment syndrome

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mEq/kg IV bolus then 150 mEq/L infusion to alkalinize urine if myoglobinuria present
0.5-1 g/kg IV if myoglobinuria persists despite fluids; maintain UOP >2 mL/kg/hr
0.1 mg/kg IV for seizures secondary to electrical injury; max 5 mg/dose

investigations.name: 12-lead ECG
investigations.indication: All electrical injuries — assess for arrhythmia

counselling: Keep your child away from open electrical wiring and switchboards
Cover all electrical sockets with child-proof plugs at home
Follow-up ECG monitoring may be needed even after discharge
Watch for delayed symptoms like muscle pain, dark urine, or confusion and come back immediately
Electrical burns may look small outside but be deep inside — all follow-ups are important
referral_criteria: High-voltage injury (>1000V), lightning strike, burns crossing joints, compartment syndrome, cardiac arrhythmias persisting >24 hours, neurological deficits
hospitalisation_criteria: All high-voltage injuries; any ECG abnormality; visible burns; loss of consciousness; dark/cola-colored urine (myoglobinuria); cardiac arrest at scene
notes: High-voltage injuries require C-spine immobilization and rhabdomyolysis monitoring with serial CK/myoglobin. Lightning-strike victims need neuro re-exams and ophthalmology referral.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.01 mg/kg IM anterolateral thigh (1:1000), max 0.5mg; repeat q5-15min PRN
20 mL/kg IV bolus, repeat PRN for hypotension; max 1L/dose
1-2 mg/kg IV/IM/PO q6h, max 50mg/dose; adjunct only, not substitute for epi
1-2 mg/kg IV, max 125mg; for biphasic reaction prevention

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-1 mcg/kg/min IV continuous for refractory anaphylaxis; max 10 mcg/min
20-30 mcg/kg IV bolus, max 1mg; for beta-blocker-associated refractory hypotension
2mg intranasal single spray; for patients >=30kg as alternative to IM epi

investigations.name: Serum tryptase (within 1-4 hours)
investigations.indication: Confirm mast cell degranulation in anaphylaxis

investigations.name: Specific IgE panel
investigations.indication: Identify trigger allergen (after acute episode resolves)

investigations.name: Skin prick testing
investigations.indication: Comprehensive allergen identification (4-6 weeks after episode)

counselling: Anaphylaxis is a life-threatening allergic reaction - your child must always carry emergency epinephrine
Learn how to use the epinephrine auto-injector and practice with the trainer device
Give epinephrine FIRST if severe reaction occurs, then call ambulance - do not delay
Get a medical alert bracelet for your child listing their allergy
Strictly avoid the identified trigger; inform school, relatives, and caretakers about the allergy
referral_criteria: Urgent referral to pediatric allergist for all anaphylaxis cases for trigger identification and management plan
hospitalisation_criteria: All anaphylaxis requires emergency department evaluation; observe minimum 4-6 hours for biphasic reaction; admit if severe, required multiple epinephrine doses, or has asthma
notes: Epinephrine IM is the ONLY first-line treatment; delays increase mortality. Observe 4-6h minimum post-reaction for biphasic anaphylaxis risk.
guideline_changes: Neffy (epinephrine nasal spray) approved Aug 2024 for patients >=30kg

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.25 mg/kg/dose PO daily; 6-23mo: 2.5mg, 2-5yr: 5mg, >=6yr: 10mg daily
2-5yr: 5mg PO daily; >=6yr: 10mg PO daily
1-2 mg/kg/day PO for acute allergic reactions, max 60mg, short course 3-5 days

second_line_drugs.notes: 75-375mg SC q2-4wks based on IgE level and weight; age >=1yr for food allergy
6mo-5yr: 4mg PO daily; 6-14yr: 5mg PO daily; >=15yr: 10mg PO daily
2-11yr: 30mg PO BID; >=12yr: 60mg PO BID or 180mg daily

investigations.name: Skin prick testing
investigations.indication: Gold standard for IgE-mediated allergy identification

counselling: Identify and strictly avoid the allergen that caused the reaction
Always carry the allergy card listing your child's allergens
Second-generation antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine) are preferred over older ones that cause drowsiness
If your child has severe food allergy, read all food labels carefully
Ask your doctor if your child needs to carry an emergency epinephrine auto-injector
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric allergist for skin prick testing, specific IgE testing, and allergen identification; refer urgently if anaphylaxis history
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe allergic reactions with systemic involvement, anaphylaxis, or for supervised oral food challenge
notes: Second-generation antihistamines are first-line; avoid first-gen due to sedation and CNS effects. Identify and eliminate allergen triggers before escalating pharmacotherapy.
guideline_changes: Omalizumab approved Feb 2024 for IgE-mediated food allergy age >=1yr; Neffy nasal epinephrine approved Aug 2024

first_line_drugs.notes: 300/100 mg PO bid for 5 days; EUA age >=12yr and >=40 kg, within 5 days of onset
5 mg/kg IV day 1 (max 200 mg), then 2.5 mg/kg/day (max 100 mg) for 3 days; >=3 kg

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg IV/PO daily (max 6 mg) for up to 10 days; hospitalized on O2/ventilator
2-4 mg PO daily based on age; hospitalized, EUA >=2yr
Weight-based IV single dose; MIS-C or rapid deterioration despite steroids

counselling: Most children recover from COVID-19 with mild symptoms in 5-7 days
Give paracetamol for fever and ensure plenty of fluids
Watch for breathing difficulty, persistent chest pain, or inability to stay awake — seek emergency care
Monitor for MIS-C symptoms 2-6 weeks after infection: high fever, rash, red eyes, abdominal pain
Isolate the child for 5 days from symptom onset; masks for all household contacts
referral_criteria: Refer urgently if MIS-C suspected, oxygen saturation <94%, respiratory distress, or high-risk child (immunocompromised, cardiac disease)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for hypoxia (SpO2 <94%), respiratory distress, MIS-C, severe dehydration, or high-risk comorbidities
notes: Paxlovid is preferred for outpatient high-risk children >=12yr. Remdesivir 3-day outpatient course is second-line; most beneficial within 7 days of symptom onset.
guideline_changes: Remdesivir FDA-approved for children >=28 days and >=3 kg; Paxlovid EUA for >=12yr

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-7 drops TDS PO only if symptomatic colic, short duration only

counselling: Cord will dry and fall off on its own; do not pull it — नाल सूखकर खुद गिर जाएगी; खींचें नहीं
Keep the area clean and dry at all times — जगह को हमेशा साफ़ और सूखा रखें
Fold diaper below the stump to avoid moisture — डायपर को नाल के नीचे मोड़कर रखें
Avoid any home remedies or powders — कोई घरेलू उपचार या पाउडर न लगाएं
Mild discharge or bleeding after fall is normal — नाल गिरने के बाद हल्का रिसाव या खून सामान्य है
Seek medical help if redness, pus, foul smell, fever, or excessive crying occurs — लालिमा, पीप, बदबू, बुखार या ज़्यादा रोना हो तो डॉक्टर को दिखाएं
warning_signs: Redness extending beyond base of stump — नाल के आस-पास लालिमा फैलना
Purulent or foul-smelling discharge — पीप या बदबूदार रिसाव
Fever, lethargy, poor feeding — बुखार, सुस्ती, दूध न पीना
Cord not falling off by 3-4 weeks — 3-4 हफ़्ते में नाल न गिरना
guideline_changes: IAP 2024: Vitamin D 400 IU/day universally recommended from birth; iron supplementation from 6 weeks for LBW/preterm, 6 months for term; emphasis on developmental milestone screening at every well-baby visit using validated tools. WHO 2024 growth standards reaffirmed.
expected_course: The umbilical cord stump dries and separates naturally within 5-15 days. Color changes from white to yellow to brown/black before falling off. Mild serous ooze after separation is normal. Delayed separation beyond 3 weeks may indicate infection or immune deficiency and warrants evaluation.
key_clinical_points: Dry cord care is the standard of care (WHO recommendation)
4% chlorhexidine application recommended only in high-risk/community settings
Traditional practices (oil, turmeric, ghee, ash) increase infection risk
Delayed separation (>3 weeks) may indicate leukocyte adhesion deficiency
Umbilical granuloma (persistent red nodule) treated with silver nitrate cauterization
Omphalitis signs require urgent referral and IV antibiotics

severity_assessment.mild: Normal drying cord, no discharge, no redness — dry cord care, reassurance
severity_assessment.moderate: Minor serous ooze, mild periumbilical redness, delayed separation — close follow-up, chlorhexidine if needed
severity_assessment.severe: Purulent discharge, spreading erythema, systemic signs (fever, lethargy) — urgent referral for omphalitis management

diagnosis_name: Red Flag Signs in the Neonatal OPD
counselling: Teach parents to recognize red flag signs — माता-पिता को खतरे के निशान पहचानना सिखाएं।
Explain urgency of seeking care immediately if warning signs appear — खतरे के निशान दिखें तो तुरंत डॉक्टर के पास जाएं।
Provide printed or digital list of warning signs — खतरे के निशानों की सूची लिखकर या फ़ोन में दें।
Reassure that early visits are encouraged even if unsure — अगर शक हो तो भी बच्चे को जल्दी दिखाना सही है।
Keep hospital emergency numbers saved in phone — अस्पताल के इमरजेंसी नंबर फ़ोन में सेव रखें।
warning_signs: Poor feeding or refusal to feed — दूध न पीना या मना करना
Fever (>=38 degrees C) or hypothermia (<36.5 degrees C) — बुखार या शरीर ठंडा पड़ना
Fast breathing or chest retractions — तेज़ सांस या छाती धंसना
Blue discoloration of lips or body — होंठ या शरीर का नीला पड़ना
Seizures or abnormal movements — दौरे या असामान्य हरकत
Lethargy or excessive sleepiness — सुस्ती या बहुत ज़्यादा सोना
Blood in stools — पोटी में खून
expected_course: Red flag signs in a neonate indicate potential life-threatening conditions requiring immediate evaluation and stabilization. Neonates can deteriorate rapidly. Early recognition and prompt action (ABC stabilization, referral, admission) can be lifesaving.
key_clinical_points: Any fever >=38 degrees C in a neonate is a red flag requiring sepsis evaluation.
Bilious vomiting in a neonate is a surgical emergency until proven otherwise.
Neonates may present with subtle signs — lethargy, poor feeding, or temperature instability may be the only clues.
Use the FLAIR-CHiPS mnemonic: Feeding/Fever, Lethargy, Apnea, Irritability, Respiratory distress, Cyanosis, Hypothermia, Persistent jaundice, Seizures.
Never delay referral or admission if a life-threatening red flag is identified.
Document all red flags thoroughly for continuity of care.

severity_assessment.mild: No red flags present, routine OPD visit — continue routine care, counsel parents on warning signs.
severity_assessment.moderate: Single red flag, baby appears stable — thorough evaluation, investigations, close monitoring, consider admission.
severity_assessment.severe: Multiple red flags, baby appears sick or unstable — immediate ABC stabilization, admission, urgent referral.

monitoring_parameters.frequency: At each visit; continuous if admitted

counselling: Repeat testing ensures accurate diagnosis — दोबारा जांच से सही पहचान होती है।
Repeat does not always indicate disease — दोबारा जांच का मतलब बीमारी नहीं है।
Early detection improves outcomes — जल्दी पहचान से इलाज बेहतर होता है।
Strict follow-up is essential — समय पर जांच कराना बहुत ज़रूरी है।
Keep all reports safely for every visit — सभी रिपोर्ट संभालकर रखें और हर बार लाएं।
warning_signs: Poor feeding or vomiting — दूध न पीना या उल्टी
Lethargy or excessive sleepiness — सुस्ती या बहुत ज़्यादा सोना
Failure to gain weight — वज़न न बढ़ना
Unusual body odor — शरीर से अजीब गंध
expected_course: Repeat screening is required when the initial sample was collected too early, was inadequate, or when the baby has risk factors (prematurity, NICU stay, blood transfusion). Most repeat screens are normal. Abnormal results require urgent confirmatory testing and specialist referral.
key_clinical_points: Sample collected before 24 hours must be repeated at 48-72 hours.
NICU babies need repeat screening at discharge or day 7-14.
Post-transfusion babies require repeat screening after 72 hours.
Preterm infants (<32 weeks) need repeat at term corrected age.
Poor or inadequate samples need immediate recollection.
Failure to follow up abnormal results is the most dangerous gap in screening programs.

counselling: Screening is safe, painless, and quick — जांच सुरक्षित, दर्दरहित और जल्दी होती है।
Failure does not always mean permanent hearing loss — फेल होने का मतलब हमेशा बहरापन नहीं है।
Follow-up is essential — फॉलो-अप ज़रूरी है।
Early detection improves speech and language development — जल्दी पहचान से बोलने और भाषा के विकास में सुधार होता है।
The 1-2-3 rule: Screen by 1 month, diagnose by 2 months, intervene by 3 months — 1-2-3 नियम: 1 महीने तक जांच, 2 महीने तक पहचान, 3 महीने तक इलाज शुरू।
warning_signs: No startle to loud sound by 1 month — 1 महीने तक तेज़ आवाज़ पर न चौंकना
No babbling by 6 months — 6 महीने तक बोलने की कोशिश न करना
No response to name by 9 months — 9 महीने तक नाम पर प्रतिक्रिया न देना
No words by 12 months — 12 महीने तक कोई शब्द न बोलना
Parent concern about hearing — माता-पिता को सुनने में संदेह
expected_course: Most newborns pass the initial hearing screening. Those who fail require rescreening within 2-4 weeks. Persistent failure leads to diagnostic audiology by 2 months of age. With confirmed hearing loss, intervention (hearing aids, cochlear implant evaluation) should begin by 3 months to optimize speech and language outcomes.
key_clinical_points: Universal newborn hearing screening (UNHS) is recommended for all newborns.
OAE screens cochlear function; AABR assesses the auditory nerve pathway.
AABR is preferred for NICU babies (risk of auditory neuropathy).
High-risk babies need periodic monitoring even if initial screen is normal.
Follow the 1-2-3 rule: Screen by 1 month, Diagnose by 2 months, Intervene by 3 months.

counselling: Breastfeeding is a learned skill that improves with time — स्तनपान एक सीखने वाली कला है जो समय के साथ बेहतर होती है
Feed on demand and watch baby cues — बच्चे की ज़रूरत के अनुसार दूध पिलाएं और उसके संकेत देखें
Avoid pacifiers in first 4-6 weeks — पहले 4-6 हफ़्ते चुसनी न दें
Seek help early if pain or poor feeding — अगर दर्द हो या बच्चा ठीक से दूध न पिए तो जल्दी मदद लें
Exclusive breastfeeding recommended for first 6 months — पहले 6 महीने केवल माँ का दूध पिलाएं
warning_signs: Baby not waking for feeds for more than 4 hours — बच्चा 4 घंटे से ज़्यादा दूध के लिए न जागे
Less than 6 wet diapers in 24 hours after day 4 — चौथे दिन के बाद 24 घंटे में 6 से कम गीले डायपर
Poor weight gain or weight loss after day 5 — पांचवें दिन के बाद वज़न न बढ़ना या घटना
Persistent clicking sounds during feeding — दूध पीते समय लगातार क्लिक की आवाज़
Cracked or bleeding nipples — फटे या खून निकलते निप्पल
Baby appears jaundiced or lethargic — बच्चा पीला या सुस्त दिखे
expected_course: Most mothers and babies establish effective breastfeeding by day 3-5 of life. Initial breast engorgement peaks around day 3-4 and resolves within 24-48 hours. Weight loss of up to 7-10% in first 3-5 days is physiological. Birth weight is typically regained by day 10-14. Mature milk comes in by day 3-5.
key_clinical_points: Colostrum (first milk) is adequate for the first 2-3 days — no supplements needed
Proper latch: wide open mouth, chin touching breast, nose free, lower areola in mouth
Feeding frequency: 8-12 times in 24 hours in the first few weeks
Cross-cradle hold is best for learning; football hold for C-section mothers
Signs of adequate intake: audible swallowing, relaxed hands, satisfied baby after feed
Weight loss >10% or no regain by day 14 requires evaluation
Avoid prelacteal feeds (honey, ghutti, formula) — harmful and unnecessary

monitoring_parameters.frequency: Day 3-5, day 10-14, then weekly for first month
At each OPD visit in first 2 weeks

counselling: Mild redness, swelling, and yellow film can be part of normal healing — हल्की लालिमा, सूजन और पीली परत सामान्य ठीक होने का हिस्सा है
Bleeding should stop with gentle pressure — हल्का दबाव देने से खून रुक जाना चाहिए
Seek urgent review if baby does not pass urine, develops fever, or has continuous bleeding — अगर बच्चा पेशाब न करे, बुखार हो, या लगातार खून बहे तो तुरंत डॉक्टर को दिखाएं
Do not apply home remedies, powders, or antiseptics unless advised — बिना डॉक्टर की सलाह के घरेलू उपचार, पाउडर या एंटीसेप्टिक न लगाएं
Apply petroleum jelly to prevent sticking to diaper — डायपर से चिपकने से बचाने के लिए वैसलीन लगाएं
Follow-up if healing appears delayed or abnormal — अगर ठीक होने में देरी लगे या असामान्य लगे तो दोबारा दिखाएं
warning_signs: Continuous bleeding that does not stop with pressure — लगातार खून बहना जो दबाव से न रुके
No urine output for more than 6-8 hours — 6-8 घंटे से ज़्यादा पेशाब न आना
Pus or foul-smelling discharge — मवाद या बदबूदार रिसाव
Excessive swelling or redness — बहुत ज़्यादा सूजन या लालिमा
Baby refusing feeds or becoming lethargic — बच्चे का दूध न पीना या सुस्त हो जाना
expected_course: Normal healing after circumcision takes 7-10 days. A yellow-white film (fibrin) over the wound is normal and should not be removed. Mild swelling resolves within 3-5 days. If Plastibell is used, the ring falls off in 5-10 days. Complete healing may take 2-3 weeks.
key_clinical_points: Absolute contraindications: hypospadias, epispadias, chordee, buried penis
Delay circumcision in preterm, LBW (<2.5 kg), sick, or bleeding disorder babies
Always check for hypospadias before circumcision — foreskin needed for repair
Plastibell technique: ring falls off in 5-10 days; less bleeding
Pain management: sucrose, EMLA cream, or dorsal penile nerve block
Yellow fibrin film is normal healing — not infection
Written informed consent is mandatory

severity_assessment.mild: Normal post-procedure appearance with mild swelling and yellow film — routine care with petroleum jelly
severity_assessment.moderate: Increased redness, delayed ring separation, mild oozing — close observation, possible antibiotics
severity_assessment.severe: Continuous bleeding, no urine output, pus, fever, glans injury — emergency evaluation, possible surgical intervention

monitoring_parameters.frequency: Every diaper change for first 24 hours
At first review (24-72 hours) and second review (5-10 days)
At each feed for first 24 hours

investigations.name: MRI Spine (>3 months or if US unavailable)
Pediatric Neurology / Neurosurgery referral if abnormal

counselling: Most sacral dimples are benign and harmless — अधिकतर सैक्रल डिम्पल सामान्य और हानिरहित होते हैं
Small, midline dimples with visible base usually do not need tests — छोटे, बीच में स्थित गड्ढे जिनका तल दिखता है, आमतौर पर जांच की ज़रूरत नहीं
Certain features may require imaging to rule out spinal problems — कुछ विशेषताओं में रीढ़ की समस्या जांचने के लिए स्कैन ज़रूरी हो सकता है
Observe for warning signs like limb weakness or urinary issues — पैरों की कमज़ोरी या पेशाब की समस्या जैसे लक्षणों पर ध्यान दें
Early detection ensures better outcomes — जल्दी पहचान से बेहतर इलाज होता है
warning_signs: Limb weakness or asymmetry — पैरों में कमज़ोरी या असमानता
Bladder or bowel dysfunction — पेशाब या मल त्याग में समस्या
Growing lesion or discharge from dimple — गड्ढे का बढ़ना या उसमें से रिसाव
Hair tuft or hemangioma near dimple — गड्ढे के पास बालों का गुच्छा या लाल धब्बा
expected_course: Simple sacral dimples that are small (<5 mm), midline, with visible base and no associated skin findings are benign and require no further workup. Spontaneous resolution is expected. High-risk dimples require imaging within 3 months of age (spinal ultrasound) or MRI if older.
key_clinical_points: Differentiate benign from high-risk sacral dimple at first exam
Spinal ultrasound is best done before 3 months (open acoustic window)
Distance >2.5 cm from anus increases suspicion for occult spinal dysraphism
Normal neurological exam is reassuring but does not exclude deep pathology
Document size, location, base visibility, and associated findings at each visit
